A milk float goes past the bottom of Beacon Hill and the man driving it lifts his hand at us without looking and we both, without discussing it, lift ours back. "Your jumper smells like your house," Nora says. "Sorry." "Not a bad thing. It smells like toast." "That's Gary." "Is Gary alright." "Gary's alright." "Good." She doesn't ask for her phone. I don't give her her phone. Her phone is in my pocket next to mine and every so often I feel the two of them knock and I think *I'll give it to her at the next lamppost*, and then at the next lamppost I don't, and then I think *at the postbox*, and at the postbox I don't. I can feel my own phone, the live one, the awake one, buzzing every so often. I don't look. We talk about a programme. This is what we do for about twenty minutes, as we walk down the back way towards hers — we talk about a programme she's been watching, which I haven't, about a woman in it who she doesn't like, about what the woman said in episode four, about whether the woman is going to end up with the other woman, about the actress who plays the woman, who Nora thinks she recognises from something else, and who I, it turns out, also recognise from something else, and we work out between us what the something else is — an ad, for a bank, from years ago — and it takes us four streets to get there and it is, for those four streets, the easiest thing we have done together in months. Then she stops talking about the programme and there is a silence and the silence fills up. "Til." "Yeah." "Did you tell anyone you were coming to find me." "Jess." "Right." "And Finn." "You went to Finn's?" "Yeah." "Jesus Christ, Tilly." "I didn't know where you were." "You went to *Finn's.*" "Yes." "At midnight." "About half twelve." "Oh *half twelve.* That's fine then." We laugh. Both of us. It's a small laugh and it comes at the same time and I want to hold onto it. I can't. It's gone after two seconds and then we're just walking again. "He said you broke up week Tuesday." "Yeah." "You didn't tell me." "No." "Why." She thinks about it. I watch her think about it. Her face does the thing where she's not performing anything — which she does about twice a year, usually when she's extremely tired — and I am briefly allowed to look at the actual Nora, the one underneath the Nora I have built, and the actual Nora is just a girl who hasn't got a good answer. "I don't know," she says. "I meant to. I was going to. Then you'd do the voice about it." "What voice." "The Finn voice. The *oh, Finn* voice. And I was tired." "Right." "Sorry." "It's fine." "It's not fine, but thanks." We walk. We get to her road. The sky over the houses is doing the thing where it's not dark anymore but it's not light yet either; it's a non-colour, a waiting colour. There's a bird going somewhere, one bird, testing it out. A cat on someone's wheelie bin watches us the way the other cat watched me earlier; maybe it's the same cat. Maybe Saltmere only has one cat. At the gate she stops. She puts her hand on the gate and she doesn't push it. "Don't come in," she says. "I wasn't." "Just — I'm going to go in and I'm going to go to bed and I'm going to tell my mum I was at yours if she asks." "Okay." "Okay." "I'll text — " "My phone's dead." "Right." "I'll text you tomorrow. Later. Today. I'll text you today." "Okay." She does not hug me. We stand two feet apart at her gate and the hug does not happen and I understand that it is because she does not have the energy for it and not because she doesn't want to and I understand also that I will, in about ten minutes, start telling myself it was because she didn't want to. She goes up the path. She lets herself in. The door closes. I stand at the gate for about thirty seconds. Her phone is in my pocket. I take it out. I look at it. The screen is still black. I lift the flap of the letterbox with one finger and I post it through, and I hear it land on the mat inside with the small definite sound that a phone makes landing on a mat, and I let the flap go, and I walk away. I get on the bike. I don't look at my own phone until I'm on Mercer Road, and even then I only look for a second. Seventeen notifications. I put it back in my pocket. Gary's in the kitchen when I get in. He's making toast. He looks up. "Alright, love." "Alright." "Don't tell your mum I was up. She'll think I've got that heart thing again." "I won't." "Toast?" "No. Thanks. Going to bed." "Right you are." I go up the stairs in my clothes. I get into bed in my clothes. I don't take the eyeliner off. I lie on my back and I look at the ceiling and I feel the bed tilt very slightly the way it does when you're drunk or have been biking for hours, and I don't cry, and I don't sleep, for quite a long time.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a quietly accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and failings systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER The chapter maintains Tilly's voice with considerable discipline. The true-crime cadence is present but restrained here — this is appropriate, since the plan called for the voice to drop in moments of genuine feeling, and the chapter is mostly in that register. The small embarrassed lies and self-deceptions are rendered through action rather than announcement: "I understand also that I will, in about ten minutes, start telling myself it was because she didn't want to" is exactly the kind of self-aware-but-still-going-to-do-it psychology the character profile demands. This is the chapter's best line and earns its place. The phone business — "I'll give it to her at the next lamppost, and then at the next lamppost I don't" — is well-handled. It's a small, specific, recognisable form of passive dishonesty that characterises Tilly without announcing it. The decision to post it through the letterbox rather than hand it over is the right ending for that thread: cowardly, practical, and slightly kind all at once. Nora is rendered correctly. She is not luminous. She is tired, slightly irritable, not particularly grateful, and the exchange about Finn ("You went to Finn's?" / "Yeah." / "Jesus Christ, Tilly.") has the right texture — funny, a bit mean, not a big moment. The detail about the Finn voice — "And I was tired" — is good characterisation: it's a real reason, not a dramatic one, and it implicates Tilly without exonerating Nora. DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange about the programme is particularly well-executed — it does exactly what the plan calls for, being genuinely easy and warm for four streets, and then stopping. The rhythm of the short exchanges ("Is Gary alright." / "Gary's alright." / "Good.") is pitch-perfect: the period instead of question mark is a nice touch, suggesting the flatness of exhausted speech. The Finn exchange lands. The gate exchange is appropriately awkward without being overwrought. Gary's cameo is brief and exactly right. "Don't tell your mum I was up. She'll think I've got that heart thing again" is the character in two sentences. STRUCTURE AND PACING The chapter moves at the right speed. The milk float detail is good — specific, slightly surreal, not laboured. The cat callback ("maybe it's the same cat. Maybe Saltmere only has one cat") is a small joke that earns its place without straining. The sky description — "a non-colour, a waiting colour" — is the chapter's one moment of mild purple prose, though it's brief enough not to damage anything. The ending is correctly small. Tilly in bed, not crying, not sleeping, eyeliner on, is the right note. It doesn't announce itself as meaningful. WEAKNESSES The chapter is almost too well-behaved. The plan called for Tilly's phone buzzing with notifications from the thing she posted — this is present (seventeen notifications, pocket it) but handled so lightly it barely registers. This is a deliberate choice and defensible, but it means the chapter's emotional stakes are almost entirely in the texture of the walking-and-talking rather than in any forward pressure. For a chapter that's supposed to carry the weight of the unresolved petty cruelty, the notifications feel slightly underplayed. The "non-colour, a waiting colour" construction is the kind of slightly reaching poetic gesture the plan explicitly wanted to avoid (pathetic fallacy, postcard imagery). It's minor but present. The hug-that-doesn't-happen is well-observed but the explanation — "I understand that it is because she does not have the energy for it and not because she doesn't want to and I understand also that I will, in about ten minutes, start telling myself it was because she didn't want to" — is slightly over-explained. The insight is good; the sentence is a touch long and loses some of its punch by the end. OVERALL This is confident, controlled writing that follows its plan faithfully and delivers on the character work. It's not flashy, which is correct. The dialogue is the best thing in it. The ending is earned. The weaknesses are minor: one slightly purple sky, one slightly over-explained insight, one slightly underplayed consequence. The chapter does what it sets out to do with skill and restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: Tuesday I don't go in on Monday. I tell my mum I feel sick and it isn't a lie — I've slept four hours in my clothes and I've got the specific hangover of a night without alcohol, which is somehow worse, and also I cannot, I genuinely cannot, face the canteen. Mum puts her hand on my forehead in the doorway and says *you're not hot,* and I say *I know,* and she looks at me for a second longer than she needs to and then goes to work. Gary leaves me a plate of toast on the landing. I don't eat it. It's still there when I come out to use the loo at two in the afternoon. I eat it cold. I look at my phone seventeen times and then I put it in the drawer. Tuesday I go in. The thing I posted has done what it was going to do. It isn't the end of the world. Hannah from Psychology has blocked me, which I notice between second and third period, and a girl called Ailsa who I have never properly spoken to gives me a look in the corridor that is not quite a look but is enough of a look that I clock it. Jess does not look at me at all. Jess walks past me in the corridor outside the library and her eyes go through me like I'm a window. At lunch I go to the canteen anyway because I have to eat and because not going would be worse. Nora is already at our table, alone, eating a Twix and a banana and nothing else. She sees me. She lifts her hand about three inches off the table in a wave that could, if you didn't know her, be mistaken for swatting a fly. I sit down opposite her. "Alright." "Alright." "You been in yesterday?" "No." "Me neither." "I know." We eat. She eats her Twix. I eat the sandwich I have bought from the machine, which is egg, which I don't like, which is what was left. We do not talk about Sunday night. We do not talk about the shed. We do not talk about the phone, which, when I asked — I texted her on Monday afternoon, once, a *you okay* with no kisses — she replied to with *yeah x*, and that was all. A boy from the year below, Daniel someone, sits down at the end of our table without asking and says, to no one, to both of us, "Is this the bin table." "What?" "The bin. Is this the bin table. There's a bin here, isn't there." He is pointing at the bin at the end of our table, which yes, obviously, is a bin. "Yes, Daniel," Nora says. "This is the bin table. Well done." "Cheers," he says, and drops his tray on our table and gets up again and walks off, leaving the tray, and Nora looks at me across the tray and says *what a prick* and laughs her loud sudden laugh, and I laugh, and for four seconds it is fine, it is just fine, it is the two of us at our table in the canteen laughing about a prick called Daniel, and I think *oh, okay, we are going to be okay,* and then the laugh ends, and we sit there, and we are not okay. She eats the banana. I try to ask her. I get the words lined up in my mouth — *Nor, what did the text mean, actually, was it the period or was it the row or was it — was it what I thought it was for a bit* — and I can feel them there, the shape of them, and then I look at her face, which is closed in the specific way her face closes when she wants you to not ask something, and I don't ask. I don't think I ever will. I think, sitting there watching her finish the banana, that the three drops are going to be one of the things about Nora that I don't get to know. That there's going to be a list of them, now — things I don't get to know — and it starts with Finn and the allotment and the row about the toaster, and it goes on from there, and it has probably been going on for longer than I realised, and the list is going to keep getting longer for as long as we know each other, which might be forever and might be until the end of Year 13. Jess walks past our table with Mira and does not stop. Nora watches her go. "She told you, didn't she," she says. "Told me what." "About the shed. That I go there." "Yeah." "Right." She puts the banana skin on Daniel's tray. She stands up. "I've got to go to Art." "Okay." "See you." "See you." She goes. I sit at the bin table on my own for a bit, and then I get up too. --- After school I walk home. I don't get the bus. I walk the long way, out past the primary school, along the track by the allotments, past ST MARTIN'S ALLOTMENT SOCIETY and the green peeling sign and the gate with the chain that isn't locked. I stop at the gate. I look down the path between the sheds to the second row. I can't see Ray's shed from the road — the angle is wrong — and I don't go in to look. I keep walking. The sky is doing an ordinary grey thing. The wind has the sea in it a bit. A woman goes past me pushing a pram with a kid in it who is chewing on the strap of its own hat. The kid looks at me. I look at the kid. The kid does not wave. I get to the roundabout at the top of Mercer Road and I stop, and I look at the wet pavement, and I say, out loud, to no one: "The subject was last seen alive at the allotment gate." It doesn't come out right. It comes out flat. The Marcie voice has gone; I can't find it. I try it again, different, trying for the laugh. "The subject was last seen alive at the allotment gate, wearing a — " I can't finish. I stand there. A car goes past. I walk home.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is a quietly devastating piece of work that largely fulfills its own ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully. The chapter opens with Monday's absence rendered in precise, unglamorous detail: the cold toast on the landing, the phone in the drawer, the specific hangover of a sleepless night. This is exactly the register the plan called for — small, true, unadorned. The detail of eating the cold toast at two in the afternoon is the kind of thing that lands without announcing itself. The canteen scene is the chapter's centerpiece, and it works. The Twix and banana lunch, the wave that "could, if you didn't know her, be mistaken for swatting a fly" — this is Tilly's voice at its best, observational without being showy. The Daniel scene is a small masterstroke: a genuinely funny, pointless interruption that produces a moment of real warmth between the girls, followed immediately by its collapse. The plan called for "a boy Tilly barely knows says something stupid at their table and Nora laughs, and Tilly laughs, and for a second it's fine. Then it isn't." The execution is faithful and earns the emotional beat without sentimentalizing it. The passage where Tilly lines up the words to ask Nora about the text and then doesn't is excellent. The list of things she doesn't get to know — Finn, the allotment, the row about the toaster — is the chapter's most emotionally resonant moment, and it's earned because it's been built across the whole story. The toaster detail is perfect: specific, mundane, suggesting a whole argument we never see. The Jess walk-past is handled with appropriate economy. The "eyes go through me like I'm a window" is the chapter's one slightly self-conscious simile, but it's not egregious — it's the kind of thing Tilly would think, and it doesn't strain. The ending is exactly what the plan promised: small, unresolved, slightly pathetic in the best sense. Tilly trying to do the Marcie voice alone on a roundabout and failing — "It comes out flat. The Marcie voice has gone; I can't find it" — is the right note. The plan specified she'd try the joke twice, slightly different, and it wouldn't land. The execution is slightly compressed (she only gets partway through the second attempt before stopping), but this actually works better than the plan's version — the inability to finish is more honest than a failed punchline. The dialogue throughout is spare and functional in a way that feels deliberate rather than lazy. "Is this the bin table" is genuinely funny. The exchange between Tilly and Nora — "Alright / Alright / You been in yesterday? / No / Me neither / I know" — captures the specific texture of a friendship under strain without over-explaining it. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "eyes go through me like a window" simile is the one moment where the prose reaches for effect in a way that feels slightly imported. The revelation that Jess told Nora about the shed is a small continuity question — the plan doesn't quite establish this, and it's a little convenient as a way to give Nora a line. The chapter also doesn't quite resolve what "the thing Tilly posted" was — the plan was deliberately vague about content, and the chapter maintains that vagueness, which is mostly right, but the damage (Hannah blocking her, Ailsa's look, Jess's silence) is rendered efficiently enough that we don't feel cheated. The characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Nora is exactly as described: closed, slightly mean, not luminous, eating a Twix. Tilly is exactly as described: performing, missing things, loving Nora in a way she can't examine. The true-crime voice appears at the end in its broken form, which is the right structural choice for a final chapter. The plan called for Tilly to walk past the allotments and keep walking, then try the joke twice. The execution is faithful. The chapter ends without ceremony, as promised. Minor quibbles: "literally" doesn't appear in this chapter, which is fine for a final chapter but worth noting as a verbal tic that's been established. The chapter is perhaps slightly short on the "small talk with long silences" that was meant to characterize the Tuesday lunch — but the Daniel scene compensates. Overall, this is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with considerable skill. It's funny, uncomfortable, emotionally precise, and ends exactly where it should. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction. The author has constructed a voice-driven narrative that sustains itself across eight chapters with impressive consistency and tonal control. Let me examine it critically across the requested dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The first-person narrator, Tilly, is rendered with genuine sophistication. The "Marcie and Bree" true crime podcast framing device is not merely a gimmick — it functions as a psychological portrait of a girl who has aestheticized her own life to manage its disappointments, and the story's emotional climax is precisely the moment when that voice fails her ("The Marcie voice has gone; I can't find it"). This is genuinely earned. Nora is more opaque, which is the point — the story is about Tilly's discovery that she doesn't actually know Nora as well as she thought — but she's rendered with enough specificity (the made bed, the Twix, the shed, the "I was tired" explanation) to feel real rather than symbolic. The secondary characters are efficiently sketched: Finn's flat tidiness and his "literally" without self-consciousness, the woman in the bus shelter with her chips and her Denise-and-Michelle anecdote, Gary making toast. These are not deep characters but they don't need to be, and they're drawn with economy and wit. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the piece's genuine strengths. It's naturalistic, often funny, and carries subtext without announcing it. The exchange with Finn — "I thought you knew" — and the repetition of that phrase with Jess creates a structural motif that lands. The bus shelter woman is a small comic gem. The canteen scene with Daniel is perfectly calibrated. Weak dialogue is not a problem here. PACING: The pacing is largely excellent. The story moves through the night with appropriate urgency, and the chapters have distinct tonal registers. The one area where pacing slightly falters is the Jess's kitchen scene, which introduces Mira and the Cam's-boyfriend-shoes detail somewhat abruptly, and the screenshot-sending moment (Tilly sending the mean screenshot to the small group chat) is the story's most interesting and darkest beat — but it's introduced and then almost immediately dropped. This is both a strength (the story doesn't moralize) and a slight weakness (it's not fully integrated into the emotional resolution). THE SCREENSHOT MOMENT: This is the piece's most daring choice. Tilly, in a moment of petty jealousy and hurt, sends a screenshot of Nora saying something mean about Hannah. This is genuinely dark, genuinely believable, and genuinely uncomfortable. The story doesn't resolve it — Jess blanks Tilly on Tuesday, Hannah has blocked her — but it's handled with admirable restraint. The piece trusts the reader to understand what happened without spelling it out. This is sophisticated. FORCED POETRY/METAPHOR: The piece is largely clean of this. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly — "the sky is doing an ordinary grey thing" is fine, "a non-colour, a waiting colour" is slightly precious but earns its keep. The cat appearing twice ("maybe Saltmere only has one cat") is a nice comic touch. The metaphors that do appear tend to land: "like a song you've learned wrong," "the specific hangover of a night without alcohol." There are no egregious non-sequiturs or purple passages. TELL-DON'T-SHOW: The piece shows rather than tells with impressive consistency. The made bed tells us everything. The two phones knocking together in the pocket. The hug that doesn't happen. These are all well-executed. COHERENCE: The story is highly coherent. The timeline is tracked carefully, the geography of Saltmere feels consistent, and the emotional logic holds throughout. UNEARNED TRANSFORMATIONS: The ending is deliberately unresolved, which is the right choice. Tilly and Nora are not reconciled; the friendship is damaged in ways that may or may not be repairable. The final image — Tilly trying and failing to do the voice — is genuinely moving and earned. There are no false resolutions. WEAKNESSES: The piece is not flawless. The screenshot subplot is introduced and then somewhat underplayed — it's the most morally complex thing Tilly does and it deserves slightly more weight in the final chapter. The UCAS personal statement opening ("I have always believed that literature is a form of listening") is a nice touch but the irony isn't quite developed. The Mira character is introduced with some suggestion of significance (Jess and Mira in the kitchen at 1am) but this thread is dropped. These are minor complaints against a piece that is, overall, genuinely impressive. DARKLY COMIC: The piece successfully walks the line between comedy and genuine emotional weight. The bus shelter scene, Gary's toast, Daniel and the bin table — these are funny without undercutting the emotional stakes. The darkness (the three blood drops, the "probably not" when Tilly asks if Nora was going to do anything, the screenshot) is handled with appropriate seriousness. FAITHFUL TO PROMPT: The prompt asked for darkly comic British YA fiction with the text "🩸🩸🩸" as the inciting incident. The piece delivers on all counts. The British specificity (Saltmere, Costa, Pret, UCAS, the Co-op, Vauxhall Corsa) is well-observed and never feels like a checklist. Overall this is a high-quality piece of YA fiction that demonstrates genuine craft, a distinctive voice, and emotional intelligence. Its flaws are minor relative to its achievements. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 16 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 84.4/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 84.2 | Final: 80.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Setting possibilities:** - Dusty frontier planets with saloons, gunslingers on horseback-analogues, but with plasma pistols and starships landing at the edge of town. Think Firefly, or Outer Worlds, or The Mandalorian. - A lone hauler ship (think Serenity-sized) traveling the fringes of settled space, doing odd jobs, smuggling, sometimes legitimate work. - A backwater moon colony, frontier-style: lawmen with badges, indigenous alien populations, company towns run by corporate overlords (the "railroad barons" of space). **Crew / characters:** - Captain — pragmatic, a bit world-weary, maybe a former soldier or lawman with a past. Could be a woman, which would be a nice subversion of the classic space-cowboy archetype. Name ideas: Captain Reyes, Captain Mira Vance, Captain Holloway. - First mate / gunslinger type — wisecracking, handy with a weapon. - Engineer — maybe an alien, or a young prodigy. Could be the one to first notice the crate moving. - Doc — grizzled medic, dispenses wisdom and whiskey. - A kid or passenger — could become relevant. **What's in the crate?** Several directions: 1. A child. A runaway, a clone, a bio-engineered "product," a princess, an experimental being. Classic and emotional. 2. An alien creature — dangerous, or misunderstood, or both. Like the xenomorph, but more sympathetic. Or a baby. 3. A cryo-preserved person from long ago (an Earth-origin human? A legendary outlaw? A scientist with dangerous knowledge?) 4. An android/AI that believes it's human. 5. A dangerous weapon that's bonded to a host — organic tech. 6. Smuggled indigenous alien sacred creature or being — touches on colonial exploitation themes. 7. A bounty — someone they were supposed to deliver, but things get complicated. I think option 1 or 6 (or a blend) has the most emotional and thematic meat. A child who was being trafficked / an experimental subject being shipped to some corporate lab — the crew has to decide whether to finish the job or do the right thing. **Plot arcs:** - Crew picks up crate as simple cargo haul, doesn't ask questions (typical for their line of work). - Something stirs in the crate — they open it. - Find a child / young person / being inside. - Realize they're in over their heads — whoever sent/is expecting this cargo will come looking. - Moral conflict: deliver and get paid, or protect and face consequences. - Confrontation with pursuers / the buyer. - Resolution: probably they choose the right thing, at great cost, frontier-justice style. **Tone:** - Laconic, sun-baked, wry humor, with moments of tenderness. Think Cormac McCarthy meets Firefly, with a touch of True Grit. Dialogue heavy. Lots of atmosphere: dust, engine hum, starlight through port windows, whiskey in tin cups. **Chapter breakdown ideas (rough, 8 chapters):** Ch 1: Setup — the ship, the crew, the job. Pick up the crate at some dusty backwater. Hints of unease — the broker was evasive, the pay too high. Ch 2: In transit. Engineer or crew member notices something moving. "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." Debate whether to open it. Ch 3: Open the crate. Discover a child (let's say a girl, maybe 8-10 years old, or a teenager) — possibly with strange features (an engineered human, or part-alien). She's scared, mute, or guarded. Ch 4: Crew tries to get information from her. Learn she was being sent to some research facility. Learn who's pursuing them. Meanwhile, comm chatter suggests they're being hunted. Ch 5: Pit stop on another frontier world. Near miss with pursuers. Character development — a crew member (Doc?) bonds with the kid. Decide they can't hand her over. Ch 6: Pursuers catch up. Ship damaged or forced to ground. Standoff setup. Ch 7: Climactic confrontation — shootout, negotiation, sacrifice. Someone dies or is badly hurt. Ch 8: Aftermath. Finding her a safe haven (maybe with an old friend of the Captain, or a hidden settlement). Bittersweet ending — they let her go, ride off into the black. **Alternative darker ending:** They lose, she's taken — but the captain swears vengeance, sets up a sequel-feeling close. Probably too bleak for the arc I want. **Alternative twist:** What if she's not a child, but an adult in stasis? Or what if she's dangerous — she IS the weapon? That adds nuance. Maybe she's an engineered assassin/soldier, a child in body but with lethal abilities, and they see her transformation firsthand. Could be interesting. **Names/worldbuilding flavor:** - Ships: "The Black Sparrow," "The Remittance," "The Lark," "The Coyote" - Planets: Junction, Perdition, New Amarillo, Cinder, Redlight - Corporations/villains: Halcyon Bioworks, the Consortium, Meridian Holdings - The villain could be a calm, terrifying corporate fixer — not a cartoon baddie. # Reflection The prompt "Captain, something's moving in that crate we picked up" immediately evokes a specific genre moment — a crew discovering unexpected cargo. The Space Western framing invites Firefly/Mandalorian comparisons, and while those are well-trodden, they're well-trodden because the formula works. The key will be executing with specificity, voice, and emotional weight rather than trying to reinvent the genre. Of the possibilities for the crate's contents, a child feels strongest for an 8000-word story. It creates immediate moral stakes, allows for tender character moments among hardened characters (which is the emotional heart of the Western genre), and plays against the ominous "moving" implication in the prompt — readers expect a monster, we give them something more vulnerable, which is a reversal that hits. The Mandalorian has done this to great success, but I can do it with a different sensibility — grittier, more morally complex, less precious. I like the idea of the child being somehow "engineered" or "modified" — this makes her both vulnerable AND potentially dangerous, which avoids making her a pure MacGuffin. It means the crew's protective instincts are complicated — is she what she seems? Does she pose a threat? This gives more narrative texture than "just" a kidnapped orphan. I want to resist making her a super-soldier cliché though; better if her "difference" is subtle and unsettling rather than action-movie flashy. An adult woman captain works well — sidesteps the macho cliché, and a maternal/reluctant-protector dynamic with the child carries more freight than a male captain would in the same role (which would read more as standard Mando-coded). A small crew — three or four people — is manageable within 8000 words. Too many and no one gets room to breathe. The tone I'm drawn to is laconic, atmospheric, McCarthy-adjacent but with dialogue warmth. Dust, heat, long silences, sudden violence. Not quippy. The prose should feel like it has weight and grit. I want to avoid Whedon-style banter because it would cheapen the Western mood; I want something closer to True Grit or Deadwood in feel. The 8-chapter breakdown I sketched feels right in shape. I should make sure the middle doesn't sag — often in short stories, chapters 4-5 get loose. Keeping tension through pursuit and moral choice, with a grounded character moment (Doc or Captain with the kid) will anchor the middle. The climax needs real cost — someone should die or be permanently changed for the ending to land. Ending-wise: bittersweet is right for Western. They save her, but they lose something. The frontier doesn't give without taking. Possibly the Captain finds her a home with someone from her past (a former comrade, a settlement). The ship rides on, smaller than before. I should commit to a specific voice early — first line matters enormously in establishing tone. The prompt line itself could be the Chapter 2 opener, which hooks well. Chapter 1 should establish the world and crew before the inciting moment, so the line hits with context.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a Space Western in the grittier, more atmospheric register — closer to *True Grit* and *The Sisters Brothers* than to *Firefly*'s quippy banter. The prose should be laconic, weighted, with long silences and sudden violence. Dialogue should feel lived-in, regional, with dropped consonants and sparse construction. I want to trust the reader — imply rather than explain, let silences do work. **Plot:** A small hauler crew picks up an unmarked crate on a frontier moon. In transit, the engineer realizes something inside is alive. They open it and find a girl — maybe ten years old, thin, strange-eyed, mute — who was being shipped to a corporate bioresearch facility. She is engineered in some way, subtly; not a super-weapon, but something *off*. The crew must decide whether to complete the delivery or protect her, knowing the client (Halcyon Bioworks) will send people after them. They run, they're caught, there is a confrontation, and at the end they deliver her to a safe place at real cost — a crew member dies. **Characters (keeping cast small):** - **Captain Mira Vance** — late 40s, former colonial marshal, dry, guarded. Has a past that makes the girl cut close. Our central POV anchor. - **Doc Alabaster** — ship's medic, older, bookish, the moral conscience. Bonds with the girl. - **Rook** — first mate/gunhand, laconic, loyal, handy with a sidearm. The one who'll pay the cost. - **Teo** — young engineer, the one who first notices the crate moving. Nervous, observant. - **The girl (Wren)** — mute at first, watchful. Modified: slightly wrong pupils, unnatural calm, an uncanny quality. Not dangerous in a cinematic way, but *not quite human* in a way that unsettles. - **Sable Kade** — the corporate fixer pursuing them. Calm, courteous, terrifying. A Chigurh/Anton type, not a cartoon villain. **Voice & style choices:** - Third-person limited, mostly Mira's POV, occasional drift. - Sparse, image-heavy prose. Weather, dust, machinery, light. - Dialogue in dialect — dropped g's, frontier cadence — but not overdone. - No technobabble. Tech is background, worn, familiar to the characters; never explained. - Short chapters with clear shapes; avoid plot sag in the middle by keeping each chapter a scene with its own tension. **What I'm avoiding:** - Whedon-style quippy banter. - Over-explained worldbuilding or technology. - Making the girl a secret super-weapon who solves the plot with powers. - A fully happy ending. The frontier should take its due. - Making Kade a sneering villain. He should be polite and terrible. **What I'm aiming for:** - A story that feels like dust on your teeth. - Emotional weight from restraint, not melodrama. - The central image: hardened people making a choice about a child. - An ending that's earned, bittersweet, and leaves the ship smaller. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Pickup (Junction)** Establish the ship *Remittance* and crew at a frontier port on the moon Junction. Mira meets a broker in a saloon; job is simple — haul a sealed crate to Perdition Station, no questions. Pay is too good. Broker is twitchy. Load the crate. Ship lifts off. Atmosphere-heavy, world-establishing. End with the crate in the hold, humming faintly, the crew not yet alarmed. **Chapter 2 — Something Moving** Transit. Teo, the engineer, is doing rounds in the hold and hears something shift inside the crate. He comes to the bridge: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." Debate among crew. Mira resists opening — the job is the job. Doc pushes. Rook neutral. They descend to the hold. End on Mira's hand on the release latch. **Chapter 3 — What Was Inside** They open the crate. Inside: a girl, maybe ten, thin, pale, with oddly pale or unnaturally still eyes. She doesn't speak. Doc examines her. She's been drugged for transit, now waking. There are marks on her wrists, a barcode-tattoo at the nape. Mira reads the manifest more carefully — it's Halcyon Bioworks cargo. Doc: "She's a child, Mira." Mira: "She's cargo." But even as she says it, something shifts. End with the girl reaching for Doc's hand. **Chapter 4 — The Weight of Her** Aboard ship, traveling toward Perdition. The girl (Doc names her Wren) slowly warms, still won't speak. She does uncanny things — catches a dropped cup before it falls, holds her breath impossibly long, stares through walls as if listening. Doc finds records on the black net about Halcyon's "pediatric subject" program. Mira has a flashback or memory implied — a previous child she failed. Comm intercept: Halcyon already knows the ship is off-course. Kade is coming. Decision point: deliver or run. They choose to run. **Chapter 5 — New Amarillo** They put down on the dustbowl town of New Amarillo to refuel and find a contact who might help — an old comrade of Mira's, a former marshal turned homesteader named Ruth Ainsley. Tense interaction: Ruth is reluctant; she has her own family to protect. She names a place — a hidden settlement in the canyons called Little Grace — where lost children have been taken before. Meanwhile, Rook sees two strangers at the edge of town watching the ship. Close call departing; a firefight at the pad, Teo is grazed. They escape into the black, course set for the canyons. **Chapter 6 — Kade** Kade is introduced in full — he boards a comm channel and speaks to Mira directly. Polite, almost warm. Offers her triple to turn around. Explains, with quiet reason, what Wren is and why Halcyon needs her back — not a weapon, but a *proof of concept*, years of research, millions invested. Makes it sound reasonable. Mira ends the call. Wren speaks for the first time, a single sentence — something that unsettles everyone. Ship drops into the canyon world's atmosphere. They are damaged — Kade's ship has tracked them. They crash-land short of Little Grace. **Chapter 7 — The Canyons** On foot through red canyon country toward the hidden settlement, hauling what they can. Kade and two men pursue. A running gunfight in the rocks. Rook holds a pass, tells Mira to run with the girl and Doc. Rook kills one pursuer, is killed by Kade. Mira, Doc, Teo, and Wren reach a ridge overlooking Little Grace — low buildings in a hidden arroyo. Kade comes up the ridge alone. Confrontation. Mira kills him — not clean, not heroic. She is wounded. **Chapter 8 — Little Grace** Aftermath. They bring Wren into the settlement — a quiet commune of the damaged and the rescued. Doc treats Mira's wound. They bury Rook on the ridge. Wren gives Mira something small — a gesture, a word, something kept. Mira leaves her there. The *Remittance* limps off-world at dusk, three where there were four. Final image: Mira at the helm, the stars coming on, the hold empty but not quite.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer tackling this prompt — especially one writing literary Space Western — would likely start from a place of **feeling** rather than structure. They might have an image burned in their mind: a woman's hand on a crate latch, dust on her boots, something breathing inside. The story would grow outward from that image. The plot would serve the image, not the other way around. A human would probably: - **Be willing to be weird.** Leave things unexplained. Let a scene end on an odd note. Trust that a reader will sit with ambiguity. - **Be uneven on purpose.** Some chapters longer, denser; some just a single scene, almost a vignette. Some dialogue-heavy, some silent. - **Have a hobby horse** — some theme they keep circling because it matters to *them*: maybe it's about the cost of complicity, or about the failures of mothers, or about what we do to children in the name of progress. The story would be secretly *about* this, not just contain it. - **Leave in the weird sentence.** The one that sounds wrong but is right. The rough edge that sands itself smooth in the reader's head. - **Withhold.** Never explain the tech. Never explain the girl. Let the reader feel like they walked in late on a conversation already in progress. - **Be willing to disappoint.** Not every character arc resolves. Rook might die stupidly, off-page almost, and that would hurt more than a heroic last stand. # Telltale LLM Approaches That Would Hurt This Piece **1. Symmetry and closure.** LLMs tend to close every loop: every character introduced gets a moment, every setup gets a payoff, every emotional beat is paid off on-screen. Real short fiction, especially in this register, *leaves things broken*. Chekhov's gun is real, but so is the fact that sometimes a gun is just there, part of the furniture of a life. **2. The "demonstrative" sentence.** LLMs write sentences that demonstrate their own craft — a well-placed em dash, a metaphor that lands. The problem is when *every* sentence is doing this. Real prose has flat passages, connective tissue, moments of plainness that make the striking moments hit. LLM prose is often all peaks. **3. Overwrought atmosphere.** LLMs asked to write "atmospheric" tend to cake it on — dust, sweat, sun, wind, grime, all in one paragraph. A human writer would pick *one* sensory detail and trust it. **4. The emotional lampshade.** LLMs frequently explain what a character is feeling right after showing it, or have characters speak their subtext aloud. "She felt the old grief stirring in her — the grief of the daughter she'd lost." A human writer would let the moment be and trust the reader. **5. Competent dialogue that sounds like nobody.** LLM dialogue often has no idiolect — everyone speaks clear, grammatical, thematically-relevant English with a light dusting of "frontier" diction. Real characters have verbal tics, repeat themselves, say stupid things, don't finish sentences, interrupt. **6. Safe choices.** LLMs avoid ugliness, moral complexity, scenes that don't "earn their keep." They tend toward redemptive arcs. They make characters do the right thing. They don't linger in cruelty or let a protagonist be genuinely unlikeable. **7. The montage / telling passage.** When an LLM needs to cover time, it writes a little montage paragraph: "The days passed. Teo tended to his wound. Doc taught Wren to play cards. Mira kept to herself." This is efficient but dead. A human would pick a single scene and let it stand for the time. **8. Wrapping with a bow.** LLM endings tend toward bittersweet resolution with explicit thematic statement. A final image that *explains itself.* The ship flying into the stars is fine; having Mira *think* about what it all meant is not. **9. The thematic hand-hold.** LLMs often have characters articulate the theme out loud. "Sometimes doin' the right thing costs you everything." A human writer would cut that line even if they wrote it. **10. Over-named emotions and physical cues.** "Her jaw tightened." "His eyes went cold." "Her throat closed." These are the stock shorthand of LLM prose. Humans use them too, but sparingly, and mixed with weirder, more specific observations. # Deep Dive Critique of My Plan **On the whole:** The plan is *very* well-shaped. Too well-shaped. It reads like a professional pitch — every chapter has a clear function, every beat is purposeful, everything tracks. This is both a strength and a suspicious sign. Real short fiction is often *lopsided*. The plan has almost a TV-writer's-room cleanness to it that would make the actual prose feel like filling in blanks. **Specific concerns:** **The villain, Sable Kade.** I said "polite and terrifying, Chigurh-type." But this is itself now a cliché — the urbane villain is as tired as the sneering one. LLMs reach for Chigurh because it's a recognizable template for "sophisticated villain." Real danger: Kade will give a reasonable monologue in Chapter 6 that articulates the story's themes, which is exactly the problem. I should consider not giving him a monologue at all. Maybe he barely speaks. Maybe he isn't even named until after he's dead. Maybe we never get his POV. Or — riskier — maybe he's not that interesting, just a professional doing a job, and the horror is in his ordinariness, not his eloquence. **The girl Wren.** Making her "uncanny" — catching dropped cups, holding her breath, staring through walls — is exactly the kind of thing an LLM does to make a mysterious child "feel special." It risks becoming twee. The Mandalorian's Grogu uses his powers; Stephen King's children do weird things. I'm falling into an established groove. A more human choice: her strangeness is harder to pin down. Something about how she *looks at things*. Something about her silence. Maybe she does one genuinely unsettling thing, once, and we never get an explanation or a second demonstration. **The naming of Wren.** Doc naming her "Wren" is — I'll just say it — schmaltzy. LLM move. "Little bird." Precious. A real writer might refuse to name her at all. The crew might just call her "the girl" the whole story. Refusing the sentimental name is itself a statement. **The "previous child Mira failed" backstory.** This is the LLM emotional-logic move: the protagonist must have a past wound that *parallels* the current situation, so the current stakes mean something to her personally. This is screenwriting logic. It's efficient. It's also cheap. Mira's motive for protecting the girl doesn't need to be about a dead daughter. It can be messier — fatigue, disgust, a moment's decision she can't explain even to herself. The whole point of frontier fiction is that people act without articulable reasons. **The ending — "Little Grace."** A hidden settlement of rescued children run by kind people where Wren will be safe. This is a *very* clean solution. It's the "farm upstate" of this story. It lets the crew off the moral hook. A more honest ending might leave Wren's fate more uncertain — they leave her in a place that *might* be safe, with people they can't fully vouch for, and they have to live with not knowing. Or — darker — the settlement isn't quite what they hoped; it's something stranger or sadder. **Rook's death.** Holding a pass, heroic sacrifice, telling the captain to run — this is the most stock Western beat there is. "Go on without me." It will write itself, and that's the problem. It'll feel inevitable in a bad way. Alternatives: Rook dies suddenly, without speech. Rook dies off-page and Mira finds the body. Rook doesn't die at all, but is maimed or broken in a way that's worse than dying. Or a different crew member dies, which would subvert expectation — Doc's death would hurt more because we've seen him bond with the girl. **The final image.** "The hold empty but not quite." This is me writing the last line already, and it's exactly the kind of LLM move: a poetic, paradoxical phrase that signals closure while pretending to ambiguity. Cut. The actual last image should be more specific, more concrete, possibly weirder. Something physical, not metaphorical. **Chapter 6's Kade phone call.** This is the single most dangerous chapter — it's where the villain explains the stakes to the protagonist in a reasonable voice and she hangs up on him. It's a scene from a hundred movies. And it's where Wren speaks her first line, which is high stakes for the voice I've planned. LLMs love this kind of "centerpiece" scene. Consider: skipping the phone call entirely. Kade doesn't negotiate. He just pursues. The reader has to infer what he is from how he acts. **Everyone gets a character moment.** Teo gets grazed in Chapter 5. Doc bonds with the girl in 4. Rook dies in 7. Mira kills Kade in 7. Everyone has a function and a beat. This is TV writers' room stuff. In real short fiction, someone probably gets short-changed, and that's fine — maybe Teo is barely there, almost silent, and that's his character. **The dialect.** "Dropped g's, frontier cadence" — this is risky. LLMs doing dialect tend to dial it up as a costume ("ain't," "reckon," "them canyons") rather than finding a real voice. Better to have a sparse, rhythmic register that *implies* the frontier without putting it in the apostrophes. McCarthy rarely uses eye-dialect; he uses sentence rhythm. # Ideas to Make It More Human - **Withhold the girl's origin.** Don't read Halcyon files. Don't explain the research program. Let it stay a mystery. The crew never knows exactly what she is, and neither does the reader. - **Refuse the parallel wound.** No dead daughter for Mira. Her reasons stay opaque, even to her. - **Don't name the girl.** She's "the girl" throughout. Or she names herself, once, and it's not a name anyone would choose. - **Kill the wrong character.** Doc, not Rook. The medic, the moral heart, dies. Rook lives, changed. - **Skip the villain monologue.** Kade barely speaks. He's just there, polite, doing the job. Maybe we don't see his face clearly. - **Let a chapter be small.** One chapter should be almost nothing — a meal, a silence, a single exchange. Break the symmetry. - **Cut the safe haven.** The ending is more uncertain. They leave her somewhere. They drive away. They don't know. - **Use one specific sensory detail instead of cascading atmosphere.** A broken seal on a cargo door, a smell of engine grease, a boot print in red dust. One thing at a time. - **Let characters not finish conversations.** Let someone change the subject. Let a question go unanswered. - **One weird sentence per chapter.** Something that doesn't quite make sense, that a reader has to read twice. A real writer's fingerprint. - **No thematic summary line.** Delete any line where a character says what the story is "about." - **The girl does ONE uncanny thing, not a catalog.** And maybe even that thing is deniable — could have been coincidence. - **Leave Rook's wound/death ambiguous in its heroism.** Maybe it's not a heroic sacrifice, just a stupid death. Maybe he dies reloading. - **Be willing to write an ugly scene.** Something mean or small about one of the protagonists. Mira does something unkind to the girl at some point. Not huge, but real. - **Cut some scenes altogether.** Do we need the New Amarillo stop? Could they just be *on the run*, and we compress? Or conversely — could we spend two chapters at New Amarillo and make it the heart of the story, and compress the ending? - **Consider an odd structural choice.** What if Chapter 1 starts *after* the pickup, and we only learn about it through fragments? What if one chapter is from the girl's POV, briefly, and it's disorienting? - **Resist the final image being a metaphor.** End on something physical. A hand. A door. A kettle.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a Space Western short story in a grittier, McCarthy-adjacent register — laconic, sparse, atmospheric without being cluttered. The prompt "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up" is the hinge the story turns on, and will land as the closing line of Chapter 1, not Chapter 2 — I want it to hit earlier and harder, ending a quiet opener with a jolt. **Plot (revised):** A hauler crew takes a sealed crate from a frontier moon. Inside they find a girl, maybe ten, modified in ways that are hard to pin down. A corporate fixer pursues them. The crew chooses to protect her, at real cost. They leave her somewhere that *might* be safe. The ship goes on. **Key changes from prior plan:** - **No parallel backstory wound for Mira.** Her reasons for protecting the girl stay opaque, even to her. No dead daughter, no failed case. She just does it. - **The girl is not named** by the crew. She's "the girl," mostly. Late in the story she may give a name, or a word; it won't be pretty. - **Doc dies, not Rook.** The bond-and-death with the moral heart of the crew. Rook survives, silent, changed. This subverts the "tough guy takes the bullet" beat. - **Kade barely speaks.** No villain monologue, no phone negotiation. He's a man doing a job. We see him briefly, mostly from a distance. His horror is his ordinariness. - **No hidden sanctuary settlement.** The ending is more uncertain. They leave the girl with someone — Ruth, Mira's old comrade — and fly off not knowing if it will hold. - **The girl does one genuinely uncanny thing, not a catalog.** Deniable. Never explained. - **Mira does something unkind.** At least one scene where she's hard on the girl in a way that isn't redeemed or apologized for. - **Withholding.** Never explain Halcyon's research. Never explain what the girl is. The reader knows only what the crew knows, and the crew never knows much. - **Structural asymmetry.** Some chapters are full scenes; at least one is a small, quiet vignette. Not every character gets a "moment." **Voice & style:** - Third-person limited, primarily Mira's POV, with restraint — we don't always get her interior. - Rhythmic, sparse sentences. Dialect through cadence, not eye-dialect. No "ain't"-costume. - One concrete sensory detail at a time. No atmosphere cascades. - Flat connective prose between struck moments. Not every sentence working hard. - Cut any line a character might speak that summarizes the theme. - End on a physical image, not a metaphor. **What I'm avoiding:** - Emotional lampshading (telling us what the felt moment means). - Neat symmetries and paid-off setups. - The girl as a super-weapon or magical-child trope. - Kade as eloquent-villain trope. - Heroic sacrifice dialogue ("Go on without me"). - Thematic summary at the end. - A name like "Wren." # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Junction** A dusty port moon. Saloon, broker, handshake. Mira takes the job — haul a sealed crate to Perdition Station. Pay is too good; she takes it anyway. We meet the crew in glances: Rook loading the crate, Teo doing preflight, Doc reading a book in the galley. The crate goes in the hold. The ship lifts. In the last scene, Teo is alone in the hold doing a post-lift check, and hears something shift inside the crate. He climbs the ladder to the bridge. Last line of the chapter: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." **Chapter 2 — The Latch** Debate in the galley. Mira wants to finish the job. Doc pushes to open it. Rook silent. Teo skittish. We get a sense of the crew's shape through how they disagree. Mira ultimately goes down to the hold alone. She stands at the crate a long time. She opens it. A girl, maybe ten, pale, very still, eyes not quite right. Mira closes the lid again without calling anyone. She sits with it for a while. End of chapter: she calls Doc down. **Chapter 3 — The Hold** The girl is out of the crate, in the infirmary. Doc tends her. She won't speak. A small tattoo or brand at the nape of her neck. The crew gathers, uncertain. Mira is sharp with the girl at one point — tells her to sit still in a way that's too hard; the girl doesn't flinch, which is almost worse. Doc reads out the manifest code — Halcyon. Nobody explains what that means; Rook spits. They have to decide. Mira doesn't decide out loud. She goes to the bridge and changes course. Teo notices, says nothing. **Chapter 4 — Running** Transit. A small chapter. Not much happens. Doc feeds the girl soup. She eats slowly, methodically. Rook cleans his sidearm at the galley table. Teo picks up a comm intercept — their ship's transponder signature has been flagged on a public board. They're being hunted, quietly, by someone willing to pay for sightings. Mira stands in the hold looking at the empty crate. The one uncanny moment in the story: a cup of water on the galley table. The girl is looking at it. It tips. Nobody touches it. It could have been the ship. Nobody says anything. **Chapter 5 — New Amarillo** Dustbowl town. Mira goes alone to find Ruth Ainsley, an old comrade from her marshal days, now running a small holding on the edge of town. Ruth is hard-eyed, careful. Long conversation — sparse, not expository. Mira asks if Ruth can take the girl. Ruth says she'll think on it. Meanwhile, back at the ship, Doc sits with the girl on the loading ramp in the dusk. She says one word — not her name, something else, something strange, maybe ugly. Doc doesn't react much. End of chapter: Rook on the roof of the ship spots two men at the edge of town, watching. He climbs down without a word. **Chapter 6 — The Pad** Departure goes wrong. A brief, ugly firefight on the pad as they lift. Kade is glimpsed, not named, at the far end of the lot — a man in a long coat giving an order, not firing himself. Doc is shot. They get airborne. Doc is bleeding in the infirmary. The girl sits by him. Teo is at the helm; Mira is with Doc. Doc doesn't make a speech. He asks Mira something small — about a book he was reading, whether she'd finish it. He dies before she answers. Rook, at the galley door, doesn't come in. End of chapter: they set course back toward Ruth's holding. **Chapter 7 — Red Country** The ship is damaged; they put down in the canyon country outside Ruth's land. They walk the last stretch, carrying what they can. Kade and one other man come through the rocks. Gunfight — brief, not choreographed, confused. Mira kills Kade. It isn't clean. Rook is hit, not fatally. The other man runs. Mira sits by Kade's body for a moment, going through his pockets — finds nothing interesting. A company ID. The mundanity of it. They walk on. The girl walks between them. She holds Rook's hand, not Mira's. **Chapter 8 — Ainsley's** They arrive at Ruth's. Ruth is on the porch with a rifle across her knees. She sees them. She takes the girl in without a word, a hand on her shoulder, into the house. Mira and Rook sit on the porch. Ruth brings out two cups of coffee. Mira asks if she'll keep her. Ruth says she'll try. That's all. Mira and Rook walk back to the ship at dusk. Teo has the engines warm. They lift. Final scene: Mira in the pilot's seat; Rook behind her, silent; Teo asleep against the bulkhead. The hold below them is empty. Mira looks at a small object on the console — something the girl left, or something of Doc's, a concrete object, not named as symbolic. She sets the course. The ship goes on.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Captain Mira Vance **Background & personality.** Fifty-one years old. Was a colonial marshal on three different moons over fifteen years before something — she doesn't discuss it and the crew has learned not to ask — put her off the badge. Bought the *Remittance* with severance and a loan she's still paying. Runs cargo, legal when she can, grey when she has to. Doesn't do guns-for-hire work and doesn't do slaves, which in her business counts as principles. She is not warm. She's not cold either, which would be simpler. She's *withheld*. She makes decisions and then doesn't explain them, not out of mystery but out of a deep belief that explanation is a weakness. Her crew reads her by her back and shoulders more than her face. She once went four days without speaking after a run went bad, and nobody asked her about it; she just came out of it eating breakfast one morning like nothing. Her flaws are mostly the flaws of her virtues. She's hard on people who are weaker than her, in a way she doesn't see as cruelty — she expects everyone to absorb difficulty the way she does. She has little patience for the girl's silence, even while protecting her. She will be sharper with the girl than with the grown men around her, which is a kind of unfairness she can't quite name. **Idiosyncrasies.** Drinks her coffee scalding, out of habit from her marshal days when you drank it before it got cold or not at all. Cleans her sidearm every third day whether it's been fired or not. Reads manifest paperwork on actual paper, printed out, with a pen in her hand — says screens make her eyes tired, but really she just likes the weight of it. Chews the inside of her cheek when she's deciding something. Has a small scar through her left eyebrow she doesn't remember getting. **Physicality.** Tall, lean, narrow through the shoulders. Hair cut close, grey coming in fast at the temples, still mostly dark. Skin weathered, brown from years under sun lamps and real suns. She moves without wasted motion — stands with her weight even on both feet, walks with her hands loose, never puts her hands in her pockets. Her face is not expressive. Her eyes do the work. **Motives & allegiances.** Loyalty to her crew, absolute, unsentimental. Loyalty to an idea of decent conduct she couldn't articulate if asked. No family anyone knows about. Had a partner once on Junction — a man, maybe a woman, nobody's sure — who isn't in her life anymore. Owes money to a broker on Perdition. Owes a favor to Ruth Ainsley she'd rather not call in. Takes the girl because she takes the girl; if asked why, she would look at you like you'd asked a stupid question. **Voice samples.** *"Ain't our business what's in it. That's the whole point of a sealed crate, Doc."* *Looking at the girl in the infirmary: She didn't look like a child. She looked like something a child had been made into. Mira couldn't hold the thought steady and didn't try.* *"Shut the door, Teo. Either shut it or come in."* # Doc Alabaster **Background & personality.** Sixty-three. Trained as a surgeon on Earth, a long time ago, at an institution he doesn't name. Lost his license, or walked away from it — again, the story shifts depending on how drunk he is telling it. Has been aboard the *Remittance* eleven years, which is longer than anyone except Mira. Reads constantly — actual books, bound paper, which is absurd to haul around in cargo weight but he does anyway. He is the warmest of the crew, but his warmth is the warmth of a man who has decided, as a matter of policy, to be kind. It's not natural to him; it's something he built. He is kind to the girl because he made that decision about kindness a long time ago. He has a dry, slightly cruel sense of humor he uses mostly on himself. His flaws: he drinks a little too much in the evenings. He is sentimental in ways he doesn't admit. He talks more than he should when he's nervous, which means the crew can always tell when he's nervous. He has a tendency to make things his responsibility that aren't — he'll take on another person's moral weight without asking if they wanted to give it to him. **Idiosyncrasies.** Reads with his glasses pushed up on his forehead, close to the page. Won't use the infirmary's med-scanner if he can avoid it — prefers to put his hands on a patient, calls it "the old way." Keeps a bottle of actual brandy, not synth, in a locker he thinks nobody knows about. Names things — the coffee pot is Agnes, the galley table is Earl. The ship itself he just calls "the ship." **Physicality.** Short, round-shouldered, a soft belly. Bald on top, white hair around the sides, a short white beard. Hands surprisingly delicate — surgeon's hands, long fingers, clean nails. Moves slowly, with a slight limp in the left knee he refuses to have looked at. When he laughs, which is often, it comes out as a single low huff, like a small engine starting. **Motives & allegiances.** Loyal to Mira, whom he respects without liking all the time. Fond of Rook, who reminds him of someone he won't name. Protective of Teo, who he thinks is too young for this life. He takes the girl on as his own from the moment he sees her, not dramatically but completely — the decision is made by his hands, reaching for her, before his head catches up. **Voice samples.** *"She's dehydrated. She's malnourished. She's a child, Mira. I don't care whose crate she came out of."* *To the girl, quietly, not expecting an answer: "You don't have to talk. I talk enough for the both of us. Ask anyone."* *"Agnes is acting up again. I think she's dying. Don't tell her I said so."* # Rook **Background & personality.** Late thirties, maybe forty, nobody's sure and he doesn't volunteer. His first name, if he has one, doesn't come up. Came aboard six years ago after a job on a moon called Tess where he and Mira were briefly on opposite sides of something; she hired him after, which none of the crew has ever gotten the full story on. Rook is quiet in a way that isn't shyness. He just doesn't see the point of saying what doesn't need saying. When he does speak it's usually short and to the point and sometimes funny in a deadpan way that Teo is still learning to catch. He is the crew's gunhand, though Mira is probably a better shot; Rook is just more willing. His flaws: he is too loyal, which is a flaw in his line of work. He will follow Mira into something stupid because she's asked him to, and then resent her for it in silence for weeks. He has violence in him he keeps on a short leash and is tired of keeping on a short leash. He doesn't like children — not in a cruel way, just in the way that some people aren't built for them. **Idiosyncrasies.** Rolls his own cigarettes, smokes two a day, always at the same hours. Wears the same three shirts in rotation, all dark. Sleeps with his boots beside the bunk, laces loose, ready to pull on. Has a habit, when thinking, of tapping his front teeth with his thumbnail — a small, private tic. **Physicality.** Medium height, wiry, hard. Black hair going grey, cut short. A face that looks like it's been broken once and healed wrong, though it hasn't — it's just a face like that. Eyes dark, deep-set. Moves like a man who knows where every exit is in every room he walks into. Sits with his back to walls. When he smiles, which is rare, it goes up on one side only. **Motives & allegiances.** Loyal to Mira, first and last. Fond of Doc in a way he wouldn't admit. Tolerates Teo. Toward the girl he is guarded, watchful, not unkind but not warm either — until late in the story, when she takes his hand and he doesn't pull away, and it surprises him more than anyone. **Voice samples.** *"Two men at the north edge of the lot. They've been there long enough."* *After Mira changes course for the third time: "Not asking."* *To the girl, once, quietly, after she's done something strange: "Don't do that again. Not where people can see."* # Teo **Background & personality.** Twenty-three. Grew up on a farming settlement on a moon called Hollis, left at seventeen, has been crewing small ships ever since. Three years aboard the *Remittance*, his longest posting. Good with engines in a way that is almost a feel rather than a knowledge — he'll stand in a mechanical room with his hand on a bulkhead and tell you what's wrong with something two decks away. He is the youngest of the crew and the most talkative, though not by much in this crew. He asks questions. He doesn't yet have the older crew's instinct for what not to ask. He is also the most visibly scared of any given situation, which Mira has decided is a feature rather than a bug — he notices things the others have stopped noticing because he hasn't made peace with them yet. His flaws: he tries too hard to please. He is still, in some part of himself, a farm kid, and he has a farm kid's embarrassment about that fact and overcompensates. He talks when he should be quiet. He is loyal but not yet useful in a fight. **Idiosyncrasies.** Bites his fingernails, is embarrassed about it, tries to hide it. Wears a pendant on a cord under his shirt — something from home, he won't say. Eats fast and then is embarrassed he's finished before everyone else. Laughs at his own jokes before he finishes telling them. **Physicality.** Slight, not tall. Sandy hair that needs cutting. A narrow, open face with big dark eyes that give him away every time. Hands always a little dirty from engine grease no matter how much he washes. Walks quickly, half a step faster than anyone else aboard. Sits on the edges of chairs. **Motives & allegiances.** Devoted to Mira in a way that's half apprenticeship, half something like love he hasn't named to himself. Admires Rook, is a little scared of him. Likes Doc; Doc is the one who talks with him. Toward the girl he is uncertain — fascinated, a little frightened, kind without knowing how. **Voice samples.** *"Captain — Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up."* *"I don't know what I'm hearing. I'm just telling you I'm hearing it."* *Muttering to himself in the engine room: "Come on. Come on, sweetheart. Don't do this to me right now."* # The Girl **Background & personality.** She is maybe ten. Nobody knows, including her. She has a small numerical tattoo at the nape of her neck and scars on the insides of her elbows from long-term intravenous access. She has been in the care of Halcyon Bioworks since before she can remember. What they did to her is not described and will not be. She does not know the word "mother." She knows the word "subject." She is watchful. She does not cry. She eats when food is placed in front of her and stops when half of it is gone, as if that's a rule she has internalized. She sits very still. When she is told to do something she does it, the first time, without question, which is in its own way unsettling to people who grew up being told to do things. She is not supernatural. She is not a weapon. She is a child who has been made slightly wrong by people who thought they were doing something important. Whatever she can do — if she can do anything — is small, deniable, and never demonstrated twice. **Physicality.** Small, thin, pale in a way that looks like she hasn't seen proper sun. Dark hair, straight, cut in a practical bob by someone who didn't care how it looked. Her eyes: the pupils are slightly too large, or slightly the wrong shape — it's hard to say exactly. People find it hard to look at her directly for long. She moves quietly. She doesn't fidget. **Motives & allegiances.** She has none of the usual ones, because she hasn't had the usual life. She attaches to Doc first because he touches her gently, which is a novel experience. She is wary of Mira. She watches Rook. She likes Teo because he is afraid of her and she recognizes the feeling. What she wants, to the extent she wants anything, is to not be put back in the crate. **Voice samples.** *[She doesn't speak for most of the story. When she does, in Chapter 5:]* *"They said I would sleep."* *[And later, once, to Rook, when he asks if she's cold:]* *"I don't know."*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — Junction The broker's name was Hensley and he was sweating in a room where the climate ran cold. Mira watched him across the small table and did not help him with the silence. "It's a straight run," Hensley said again. "Perdition Station. Dock eleven. They'll meet you." "Who's they." "Halcyon men. They'll have the paper." She turned the offer card over in her fingers. The pay was on the back, printed in small honest numerals, and the number was wrong. It was the kind of number you put on a job when you wanted somebody who wouldn't think about it. "Cargo." "One crate. Sealed. Temp-controlled. Don't open it, don't scan it, don't put it near the reactor. Standard conditions, it's all on the sheet." "How heavy." "Ninety kilos." She let that sit. Ninety kilos was a person. Ninety kilos was a lot of things. She watched Hensley's face for the flicker and he gave it to her, a little tightening at the corner of his mouth, and looked away. "Mira." "I'm thinking." "It's not —" "I said I'm thinking." Outside the saloon window the port of Junction moved in slow brown weather. Dust came in off the flats and got into everything. The standing lights were on though it wasn't yet dark; the sun here was a small white thing that never quite committed. She watched a freighter lift on its under-burners across the lot and the dust went up around it in a wide skirt and came down again. She thought about the loan. She thought about the hydraulic seal on the starboard cargo door that wanted replacing before winter side. She thought about Doc's reader that he'd been wanting and wouldn't buy for himself. She thought about none of the things she should have been thinking about. "All right," she said. Hensley exhaled. "Don't," Mira said. "Don't do that. Just give me the sheet." --- She walked back to the *Remittance* with the sheet folded into her inside pocket and a taste in her mouth she didn't like. The ship sat in bay nine at the low end of the field, an old Corvid-class hauler, grey where she'd been grey and rust-colored where she hadn't. The starboard door was propped open and Rook was on the ramp with his arms crossed, watching two loaders wheel a crate down toward him on a pallet jack. "That it?" he said. "That's it." He looked at the crate. It was matte black, unmarked except for a small Halcyon cipher stamped in the corner and a row of seal-tabs down the side. About the size of a coffin, which was a thought she didn't need and had anyway. "Temp line?" "Internal. Don't plug it. Don't scan it." Rook looked at her. He didn't say anything. After a moment he turned and waved the loaders up the ramp. She stood at the bottom of the ramp and watched them take it in. The wheels of the jack chattered on the metal grating. Inside the hold Teo was waiting with the tie-downs, and she heard him say something to the loaders, and one of them laugh, and then the sound of straps going taut. She did not go up. Doc came down the ramp instead, a book in one hand and his glasses pushed up on his forehead. "We lifting tonight?" "We're lifting tonight." "Good. This town makes my knee hurt." "Everything makes your knee hurt." "Not equally." He looked past her at the weather. "Where are we bound." "Perdition." "Oh, lovely." He huffed his small laugh. "What's in the box." "Don't know." "Don't want to know?" "Not particularly." He looked at her then, longer than was comfortable, and she looked back at him until he shrugged and limped off down the ramp toward the commissary. He did that sometimes. Looked at her like he was waiting for something. She had decided years ago not to ask what. --- They lifted an hour past sundown. Junction fell away under them in a slow brown turning and then the dark came up all at once the way it did out here, without ceremony, and the stars were there where they had always been. Mira sat the pilot's chair and took the ship up through the thin atmosphere and out into the black and then she handed it off to autonav and sat a while longer with her hands on the yoke, not flying, just sitting. In the galley Doc would be making coffee. In the engine room Teo would be doing his post-lift, running his hand along the bulkheads, listening. Rook would be somewhere, he was always somewhere.
