Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — The Thing His Body Did She went into town in the morning. She did not say for what. She took her canvas bag and her keys and she said *back by lunch* and she did not kiss me, which was not unusual anymore, and I stood at the window and watched the car go down the drive until the trees took it. The house was very quiet. I made coffee. I didn't drink it. I sat at the table and opened the laptop and closed the laptop and got up and walked into the living room and looked at the bookshelf and walked out again. I did this twice. The second time I noticed I was doing it and stopped in the hallway and stood there and tried to remember what I had been going to do. I went upstairs to brush my teeth. That was what it was. I had not brushed my teeth. The bathroom is small. There is a window high up on the wall that gives the room a watery light in the mornings and a pedestal sink Maren picked out at a salvage place that I never liked. The mirror is round and hangs on a chain and is always a little crooked. I straightened it. I put toothpaste on the brush. I ran the water. I looked at myself in the mirror while the water ran. I was smiling. I do not mean that I noticed I was smiling and then stopped. I mean that I saw, in the mirror, a man smiling, and the man was me, and I was not the one doing it. I want to be precise. The muscles of my face were arranged in a smile. The corners of my mouth were up. My eyes were doing the thing eyes do in a real smile, the small gather at the outer corner. It was not a grimace. It was not a twitch. It was the face I made at my nephew when he told me a joke. It was a warm smile. It was the smile of a man who was pleased. I was not pleased. I stood there with the toothbrush in my hand and the water running and I watched the smile and I waited for it to stop. It did not stop. I tried to stop it. I tried, I mean, to move my face — to frown, to open my mouth, to do anything — and for a long second nothing happened, and the man in the mirror kept smiling at me, patient, fond, and then the something let go and my face fell, all at once, like a dropped thing. I sat down on the edge of the tub. I want to say I thought about many things. I did not. I thought about the smile. I thought about the way it had felt, from inside, which was the worst part — which was that it had not felt like anything. It had not been a struggle. It had not been a possession the way I understood possession, which was a word I was already trying not to use, even in my head, because using it would have made this a thing I knew the shape of, and I did not want to know the shape of it. It had been easy. It had used my face the way I used my face. It had known how. I sat on the tub a long time. The water ran. Eventually I got up and turned it off and looked at myself again, carefully, and my face was my face, tired and a little grey around the mouth, and I rinsed the toothbrush without using it and put it back in the cup. I did not brush my teeth. I could not bring myself to put anything in my mouth. I went downstairs. I sat at the table. I did not open the laptop. I put my hands on the wood and looked at them. They were steady. They had not been this steady in a while. I had been, without really admitting it to myself, a man with a slight tremor for months now, the kind you notice when you lift a mug. The mug had not shaken this morning. My hands lay on the table like somebody else's hands, clean, still, competent. The car came up the drive a little before one. I heard it before I saw it. I had time, I realised, to arrange myself, and I did. I straightened. I got up. I put the kettle on, so there would be a reason for me to be in the kitchen and a reason for my back to be to the door. She came in with the bag and the keys and a smell of outside on her coat. "Hi," she said. "Hey." "I got — " she said, and then she stopped. I heard her stop. I heard the pause in it. She was looking at me. I did not turn around. I poured the water into the cup very carefully. I watched my hand, which did not shake. "Daniel," she said. "Mm." "Look at me." "In a second. Hot water." "Daniel." I turned. I did it slowly. I arranged my face first. I did it the way I had, apparently, been arranging my face for a long time without knowing, and I thought, as I turned, *I have been practicing for this my whole life.* She looked at me. She did not ask. That was the thing. She looked at me and her mouth opened and then closed and she did not ask. She set the bag on the counter. She took her coat off. She hung it on the hook by the door. She did all of this without looking away from me, and then, finally, she looked away, and she looked at the floor between us, and she said, in a voice that had no shape to it, "Okay." "Okay what," I said. "Nothing," she said. "Okay. I'm going to — I'm going to put these away." She put them away. I drank the tea. We did not speak for the rest of the afternoon. At dinner I tried to say something — I had a sentence in my mouth, I was going to say it, I had chosen the words — and when I opened my mouth the sentence was gone. Not forgotten. Taken. The place where it had been was smooth. I closed my mouth. She watched me close it. She did not ask what I had been going to say.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of horror writing that earns its effects through restraint and precision rather than escalation. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Voice** The prose is genuinely accomplished. The narrator's voice is consistent with the established profile — avoidant, self-aware about his avoidance, precise in a way that reveals rather than conceals. The line "I want to be precise" before the description of the smile is a perfect character beat: a man who uses precision as a form of control, trying to apply that control to something that defeats it. The observation that the smile "had not felt like anything" from inside is the chapter's most chilling moment, and it lands because it's earned through the careful setup of the mirror scene. The sentence "It had used my face the way I used my face" is the kind of line that justifies the whole enterprise — it's not reaching for poetry, it arrives from the logic of the scene and the character. **The Mirror Scene** This is the chapter's centerpiece and it works. The horror is specific and physical without being generic. The detail about the "small gather at the outer corner" of the eyes — the Duchenne marker of a genuine smile — is exactly the kind of precise, non-occult detail the plan called for. The narrator's attempt to stop the smile and the "something let go" moment is genuinely unsettling. The face "fell, all at once, like a dropped thing" is a strong image that doesn't overreach. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and evasive as planned, and it works. Maren's "Okay" — her not-asking — is the chapter's second major horror beat, and it's handled well. The exchange is minimal but loaded. The plan called for "the not-asking is the most frightening thing that has happened to him" and the chapter delivers this without stating it directly. **The Ending** "Not forgotten. Taken. The place where it had been was smooth." This is the chapter's final beat and it's excellent — the smoothness echoing the steadiness of the hands, the ease that the entity brings. The plan called for him to forget a sentence "as if the sentence was taken out of him" and the execution improves on the plan by adding "smooth," which connects to the broader texture of the entity's presence. **Weaknesses** The paragraph beginning "I want to say I thought about many things" is slightly over-explained. The narrator telling us he was "trying not to use" the word possession is a moment where the writing becomes a little too self-conscious, a little too writerly in its meta-commentary. It's not a serious flaw but it's the chapter's weakest passage. The line "I had been practicing for this my whole life" is strong but risks being too on-the-nose given the character profiles — it's almost too neat a summary of Daniel's psychology. It works in context but it's the moment where the writing comes closest to explaining itself. The steadiness of the hands as a detail is good but the paragraph dwelling on it is slightly over-extended. The point is made; the extra sentences about the tremor are redundant. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and improves on it in several places. The plan called for "something the body does that the mind can't lie about" and the smile delivers this. The plan called for Maren's not-asking to be "the most frightening thing" and the chapter handles this with appropriate understatement. The plan's note about him arranging himself before she enters is executed well — the kettle as cover is a nice specific detail. **Character Consistency** Daniel is entirely consistent with his profile: the practiced arrangement of his face, the use of a task (pouring tea) to avoid eye contact, the "I have been practicing for this my whole life" thought. Maren's "Okay" and her not-asking are consistent with her profile's description of her as someone who "stands very still when she's upset, which most people misread as calm." **Overall Assessment** This is high-quality literary horror writing. It's not perfect — there are a few moments of slight over-explanation — but the core of the chapter is genuinely accomplished. The horror is specific, physical, and earned. The characters behave consistently. The prose is controlled without being cold. The chapter does what it sets out to do and does it well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 — What She Almost Says The next day was a Thursday. I remember because I had a meeting on the calendar I did not take. I watched the little notification come up on the laptop and I watched the minute tick over and I watched the meeting not happen and nobody called to ask where I was, which said something about my job and something about me, and I did not want to look at either of those things, so I closed the laptop and went to find her. She was in the living room on the couch with her feet tucked under her and a book open on her knee. She was not reading it. The page had not turned in the twenty minutes I had been aware of her being there. I sat down on the other end of the couch. I did not touch her. I put my hands on my knees like a man at an interview. "Have you been sleeping?" I said. She looked up slowly, like she had to find me. "Not really." "I thought so." "Mm." "Are you all right?" She closed the book. She did not mark the page. She set it on the arm of the couch and she looked at me for what felt like a long time, and her face did something I could not read — a brief movement, gone before I caught it, the way a fish turns under the surface of a pond. "Are you," she said. It was not a question. Or it was, but not the kind. She said it flat, without the lift at the end. She said it the way you say *here* when somebody asks if you're there. "Yes," I said. "Okay." "I am." "I said okay." She picked the book up again. She did not open it. She held it on her knee. I sat there. I had come in with things to say and the things had gone out of me when she looked at me, the way the sentence had gone at dinner, and I did not know if it was her or it was the other thing, and not knowing was, I was finding, a whole new category of tired. "Maren." "Mm." "I don't — " "Don't," she said. "Not yet. Please." "Okay." "I'm not — " She stopped. She pressed the heel of her hand into her eye, quickly, and took it away. "I'm sorry. I can't. Not right now. I will. Just not right now." "Okay." "Okay?" "Okay, Mar." She nodded. She opened the book. The page did not turn. — In the evening I did the dishes. I had not done the dishes in a while; she had been doing them, the way she had been doing most things, and I was aware of this and had chosen not to be aware of it, and tonight I got up and ran the water and rolled my sleeves and did them. It felt like a small confession. It was a small confession. She sat at the table behind me and I could feel her watching my back. I was halfway through the second plate when she said, into the air, not quite to me: "I used to hum a song when I couldn't sleep. My mother taught it to me. I don't remember it anymore." I kept washing. I don't know why. I had the plate in my hand and I kept washing it, around and around, long past clean. "What song," I said. "I don't remember." "What was it about?" "I don't remember." "Your mother taught it to you?" "My grandmother taught it to her. I think. I'm not sure. It went — " She made a small sound in her throat, not a hum, the shape of a hum without the tune. "It went something like that. I think. I used to know it." I rinsed the plate. I put it on the rack. I picked up another one. "When did you stop remembering it?" I said. She was quiet a long time. Long enough that I thought maybe she wasn't going to answer. Then she said, very quietly: "When I didn't need it anymore." I did not turn around. I could not. I felt her behind me at the table and I felt the kitchen around us and I felt the house around the kitchen and I washed the plate I was holding and I rinsed it and I put it on the rack and I picked up the next one. "Mar," I said. "Mm." "I don't — " "I know." "No, I mean, I don't — " "I know, Daniel. It's okay. Finish the dishes." I finished the dishes. When I turned around she was not at the table. She had gone upstairs. I had not heard her go. — That night she watched me sleep and I did not pretend. I lay on my back and I let my eyes be closed but not the way you close them for sleep, and I watched her through my lashes, and she did not know I was watching, or she knew and did not care, and her face was not composed. Her face was wrecked. She had been holding it all day. She let it go now, in the dark, where she thought nobody could see. Her mouth was open a little. Her eyes were wet. She was not crying — she was past crying — she was doing the thing you do after, the long looking at a thing you cannot fix, and I understood, lying there with my eyes almost closed, that she had been doing this for a long time. That the face she wore in the day was a kindness. That she had been sparing me. I could have opened my eyes. I could have said her name. I could have sat up and pulled her down against me and put my hand on the back of her neck, under the hair, and held her there while she made whatever sound she had not been making all these months. I closed my eyes. I am trying to tell you the truth. I closed my eyes and I let her watch, and after a while she lay down, and after a while her breath went long, and I did not know if she was asleep, and I did not check.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine literary craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Execution** The narrator's voice is consistent and convincing throughout. The self-awareness is calibrated precisely — he knows what he's doing wrong, names it, and does it anyway, which is far more damning than simple obliviousness. The line "I had come in with things to say and the things had gone out of me when she looked at me" captures his particular brand of passivity without editorializing. The confession at the end — "I am trying to tell you the truth. I closed my eyes" — is the chapter's emotional peak, and it earns its weight because the whole chapter has been building toward that specific cowardice. **Dialogue** The dialogue is exceptional. "Are you." delivered flat, without the lift, is a devastating choice — it transforms a question into something closer to an accusation or a verdict. The exchange about the song is handled with real skill: Maren's fragmented, trailing sentences ("It went — " followed by a non-hum) convey something enormous without stating it. The repetition of "I don't remember" is not lazy; it accumulates. The "Okay / I am / I said okay" exchange captures the specific texture of a couple who have learned to talk around each other. There is no false note in the dialogue. **The Song Passage** This is the chapter's most important sequence, and it largely succeeds. The plan called for Maren to try to tell him something enormous that he fails to hear, and the execution delivers this. The reader understands — through the grandmother detail, the honey connection, the timing of when she "stopped needing it" — that she is confessing something about the entity's history in her family. Daniel's response (washing the plate "around and around, long past clean") is a good physical correlative for his avoidance. The "when I didn't need it anymore" line lands with genuine weight. **The Final Scene** The vigil scene inverted — now he watches her watching him — is handled with restraint. The description of her face as "wrecked" rather than reaching for more elaborate language is the right call. "She was past crying — she was doing the thing you do after, the long looking at a thing you cannot fix" is the chapter's best sentence, precise and true without being showy. The final choice — closing his eyes — is the chapter's moral center, and the narrator's direct address to the reader ("I am trying to tell you the truth") is a risk that pays off because it's used once and not again. **Weaknesses** The fish metaphor ("the way a fish turns under the surface of a pond") is the chapter's one moment of reaching — it's not bad, but it's slightly more decorative than the surrounding prose warrants, and it doesn't quite land with the precision of the rest. The section break structure (three scenes) is clean but the middle scene (the dishes) is slightly longer than it needs to be; the repetition of "I don't —" / "I know" / "I don't —" / "I know, Daniel" is effective but risks feeling slightly schematic. These are minor complaints. **Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The conversation about sleeping, the "are you" exchange, the song passage, the final vigil with eyes open — all present and executed. The plan's note that "the reader should feel, without being told, that she has just tried to tell him something enormous and he has failed to hear it" is achieved. The plan's final beat — "he closes his eyes anyway" — is rendered with the direct address that makes it more powerful than a simple action. **Overall Assessment** This is genuinely accomplished literary horror writing. It understands that the horror of this chapter is entirely domestic and psychological, and it trusts that understanding. The prose is clean, the characters are consistent with their profiles, the dialogue is among the best in the genre of "people talking around things," and the emotional arc is earned rather than manufactured. The chapter does what the best horror does: it makes the reader complicit in the narrator's cowardice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# Chapter 6 — The House In the morning I told her I was going into town. "We need things," I said. "I'll get a list." "There's a list," she said. "On the fridge." "Right." "It's been on the fridge." "Right." She was at the sink. She did not turn around. I took the list off the fridge — her handwriting, short strokes, things like *eggs, oats, tape* — and folded it into my pocket and stood in the kitchen looking at her back. She was washing a cup that was already clean. She had been washing it since I came in. "Mar." "Mm." "I'm going." "Okay." I waited. I thought she might turn. She did not turn. She ran the water over the cup and set it on the rack where it joined the other clean cups and she picked up a second one and started again. "I'll be back in a couple hours," I said. "Okay." "Do you want anything. Anything not on the list." "No." "Okay." I stood there another second. I do not know what I was waiting for. I went. — The car started on the first try, which surprised me, because it had been doing a thing in cold mornings and the morning was cold. I backed down the drive and turned onto the road and drove. I drove toward town. The road does a long slow bend around a field where somebody keeps three horses, and I passed the horses, and I passed the place where the tree fell over last winter that nobody has cut up yet, and I passed the turn for the reservoir, and I kept going. I do not remember the next part. I do not mean I was distracted. I mean I do not remember it. I remember the horses and the tree and the turn and then I remember sitting in a parking lot with the engine off and my hands on the wheel and the radio on. The parking lot was behind a hardware store I had been to twice. The dashboard said it was 11:40. I had left the house at 9:15. The radio was playing a song I didn't know the name of, something slow, a woman singing. I sat and listened to it and I realised, in a way that arrived gently, like a person stepping into a room, that I had been humming along. I stopped humming. I turned the radio off. I sat a long time with the engine off. I watched people come out of the hardware store with things. A man with a bag of salt. A woman with a length of pipe. A kid, maybe twelve, carrying a bucket with a rope coiled in it. The parking lot had that quality some parking lots have, where the light seems to come from the ground rather than the sky, and I sat in it and tried to think about what I was going to do and could not think about it. I did not go into the hardware store. I did not go to the grocery. I did not get the eggs or the oats or the tape. I sat. When the dashboard said 12:20 I started the car and drove home. — She was at the kitchen table when I came in. She was not doing anything. She was not reading. She was not on her phone. She was sitting at the table with her hands on it, palms down, fingers a little spread, as if she were steadying the table or the table were steadying her. She looked up when I came in and her face did something complicated and she said: "You came back." I set the empty canvas bag on the counter. "I didn't get anything," I said. "I see." "I'm sorry. I — " "It's okay." "I was going to." "It's okay, Daniel." I stood with my back to the counter. She sat at the table. The distance of the kitchen between us was not large and felt very large. "I wasn't sure you would," she said. "Would what." "Come back." I looked at her. She looked at me. Her hands stayed flat on the table. "I was getting groceries," I said. "Okay." "I was." "Okay, Daniel." She said it softly. She was not arguing. She was not going to argue. She had been sitting at this table for three hours with her hands flat and her phone nowhere near her and she had been waiting, I understood, not to find out whether I was coming back with eggs. She had been waiting to find out whether I was coming back. I thought about this. I let myself think about it, for a second, straight on. I thought: she has been letting me go every morning for a long time now. I thought: she has been sitting at this table every time. I thought: I did not know. I said, "How long." She did not pretend not to understand. "A while," she said. "How long." "I don't know, Daniel. A while." "Every time I go out." "Not every time." "Most times." "Yes." We were quiet. She took her hands off the table and put them in her lap. She did it carefully, as if she were moving something breakable. "What would you do," I said. "If I didn't." She looked at me. Her face was very tired. It was the face from last night in the dark, the wrecked one, and she was not hiding it now, and I understood she had stopped being able to hide it, or had stopped being willing, which was close enough to the same thing. "I don't know," she said. "Maren." "I don't know, Daniel. I would sit here. I don't know after that. I haven't — I haven't gotten that far. I sit here. That's as far as I've gotten." I did not answer. I could not. I went upstairs. I lay down on top of the covers in my clothes in the afternoon light and I tried to cry and I could not cry, and after a while I stopped trying, and I lay there, and the house made its small sounds around me, and downstairs, faintly, I could hear her still sitting at the table.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a quietly devastating piece of domestic horror that operates almost entirely through restraint. The chapter follows Daniel's failed attempt to leave — he drives to town, loses two and a half hours, returns with nothing — and culminates in Maren's revelation that she has been waiting, each time he leaves, to find out if he'll come back. The final image of Daniel lying on the bed unable to cry while hearing her "still sitting at the table" is genuinely affecting. **Strengths:** The dialogue is exceptional. The exchange at the sink — "It's been on the fridge." / "Right." — establishes the texture of a marriage in quiet collapse without a single false note. The kitchen confrontation after his return is even better: "You came back" lands with enormous weight because it's so plainly stated, and Maren's "I sit here. That's as far as I've gotten" is one of the most honest lines in the chapter. The dialogue never over-explains, never reaches for profundity, and yet consistently carries emotional freight. This is difficult to do and the model does it well. The horror element — the lost two and a half hours, the humming along to a song he doesn't know — is handled with exactly the right touch. It's not announced. It arrives "gently, like a person stepping into a room," which is a metaphor that actually earns its place because it mirrors the entity's mode of operation as established in the character profiles. The detail of humming along and then stopping is genuinely unsettling precisely because it's so small. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Daniel drives, loses time, returns with nothing, Maren says "you came back," the revelation that she's been waiting, Daniel going upstairs unable to cry. Every beat from the plan is executed, and executed well. Character consistency is strong. Daniel's passivity — standing in the kitchen waiting for her to turn, saying "I was going to" about the groceries — is perfectly in line with his profile. Maren's flat hands on the table, her refusal to argue, her exhausted honesty, all track with her established character. The detail of her washing an already-clean cup is a small, precise observation that shows rather than tells. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is almost too controlled. The plan called for Daniel to understand "for the first time, that she has been waiting for him to not come back" — and this is delivered, but perhaps too cleanly. The emotional revelation is clear and well-executed, but it doesn't surprise. The reader has been ahead of Daniel for some time, and while that's partly the point (his obliviousness is his character), the chapter doesn't quite find a way to make the revelation land with the force of genuine discovery. The parking lot scene, while effective, has one slightly over-worked moment: "the light seems to come from the ground rather than the sky." This is the kind of atmospheric detail that gestures toward significance without quite earning it — it's not wrong, but it's the chapter's one moment of reaching. Similarly, "like a person stepping into a room" is good but slightly self-conscious. The ending — "downstairs, faintly, I could hear her still sitting at the table" — is strong but "hear her still sitting" is a slightly odd construction. You can hear someone sitting only if they're making noise, and the chapter hasn't established that she is. It's a small logical wobble in an otherwise precise piece. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes. The middle section in the parking lot, while atmospheric, could be tightened. The repetition of "I did not go into the hardware store. I did not go to the grocery. I did not get the eggs or the oats or the tape" is effective anaphora, but the chapter has a few moments where it lingers slightly past the point of maximum impact. Overall, this is high-quality literary horror writing — restrained, precise, emotionally intelligent, and faithful to both the plan and the characters. Its weaknesses are minor and mostly matters of degree rather than kind. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 — Honey I came downstairs a little after dark. I had not slept. I had lain on top of the covers with my hands folded on my stomach like a man laid out, and the light had gone from the window, and the room had gone blue, and then grey, and then the grey had thickened into the particular dark of that bedroom, which has a weight to it because of the trees on that side of the house. I had lain in it and noticed, with the attention a sick person brings to their own body, that my hands were steady. That my chest did not feel tight. That the small headache I had been carrying behind my eyes for weeks was gone. I felt good. That is the honest word. I felt good. I got up and I went downstairs because I was afraid to lie there any longer. The kitchen light was on. She was at the table. She had not, I think, moved. There was a cup in front of her that looked as if it had been there some time. She looked up when I came in and she did not look surprised and she did not look relieved. She looked at me the way you look at weather you have been watching come in across a field. I sat down across from her. We sat. After a while I said, "It was you first." She did not answer. "It was you," I said. "Wasn't it." "Yes." "For how long." "A long time." "How long, Mar." "Since I was little." I nodded. I had known for — I don't know. A day. An hour. Longer. I had known the way you know things you have not let yourself say. I looked at her across the table and she was very small in the kitchen light and her hair was down and she looked like herself, entirely like herself, and I thought: *she has been carrying this since she was a child and she has never once looked like anything but herself.* "You knew," I said. "About me." "I hoped not." "Maren." "I hoped. For a long time. I kept — I kept thinking I was imagining it. I kept thinking it was me still. That I was the one feeling it and putting it on you. I wanted that. I wanted it to be that. And then it wasn't." "When did you stop hoping." She thought about this. She did not rush. "April," she said. "Maybe March. I don't know. Sometime in there." "That's a long time ago." "Yes." "You didn't say anything." "No." "Why." She looked at me. Her eyes were dry. Her mouth was a little open. She looked like she was trying to find an answer she could stand behind, and not finding one, and saying the true one instead. "I didn't know if telling you would make it faster," she said. "I thought maybe if you didn't know, it would go slower. I was — I was making it up, Daniel. I didn't know anything. I didn't know what to do. I made it up. I fed you the honey because my grandmother fed it to me and I don't know if it did anything, for her or for me, and I don't know if it did anything for you. Probably it didn't. I just. I couldn't not do something." "The honey." "My grandmother kept a jar by the bed." "For what." "For something. She never really said. She said it was for the dark patch. She said eat your honey and she watched me eat it. I ate it every morning until I was eighteen. Then I stopped and I didn't think about it for ten years. Then we came here and I started again. I started doing it for you. I don't know why I did. I didn't decide. I just — I put the spoon down one morning and you ate it, and then the next morning I put it down again." "Does it work." "I don't know." "Did it work on you." "I don't know, Daniel. I don't know. It might have. It might not have. Something let go of me for a while. Maybe that was the honey. Maybe that was you. Maybe it was tired of me. I don't know." We were quiet. I thought about the honey. I thought about her standing in the kitchen in the mornings with the jar, and the spoon, and the small ritual of it, and me eating it without asking, and the fact that I had not asked was the whole shape of our marriage, was the exact shape, and I had not understood until now. I said, "Can it go back." She did not answer. "Maren. Can it go back into you." "I don't know." "If I — " "Daniel." " — if I asked you to — " "Daniel." " — to take it back — " "I can't do that for you." She said it very quietly. She said it the way you say a thing you have been saying to yourself for a long time in preparation for saying it out loud. "Please," I said. "I can't." "Mar." "I can't, Daniel. I can't. I carried it for twenty years and I can't. I'm sorry. I am so sorry. I can't." I nodded. I was nodding before she finished. I understood. I had known, before asking, that she would say no, and I had asked anyway, because asking was the last thing I had, and now I had asked it and it was gone. "Then what," I said. "I don't know." "Mar." "I don't know. I don't know. I don't know." We sat. After a while she reached across the table and put her hand on mine. Her hand was very cold. I turned mine over under hers and held it. We sat like that a long time without saying anything. Later we went upstairs. She lay down with her back to me and she did not watch me and I understood this as a kind of giving up, and I was grateful for it, and I hated the gratitude, and I lay there with the gratitude in my chest like a warm stone until I slept.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of literary horror fiction that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Execution** The narrator's voice is consistent and well-calibrated. The prose is plain where it needs to be and precise where it counts. The line "I felt good. That is the honest word. I felt good." is an excellent example of the story's method: the horror is in the admission, not in any gothic dressing. The repetition of "I felt good" earns its weight because the reader understands why feeling good is the worst possible thing. The chapter follows the plan closely. The key beats are all present: Daniel coming downstairs, the conversation about the honey and Maren's grandmother, the revelation that it was in her first, the "please" exchange, and the ending with her back turned and his gratitude at her giving up. The plan is executed with fidelity and intelligence. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. It is sparse, evasive, and emotionally precise. The exchange "Can it go back" / "I don't know" / "Maren. Can it go back into you" / "I don't know" / "If I — " / "Daniel." / " — if I asked you to — " / "Daniel." / " — to take it back — " / "I can't do that for you" is particularly well-handled. The interruptions feel real, the rhythm of someone trying to say something they know they shouldn't have to say. Maren's explanation of the honey — "I didn't decide. I just — I put the spoon down one morning and you ate it" — is exactly right for her character: specific, honest, slightly self-accusing. **Character Consistency** Both characters are consistent with their profiles. Daniel's passivity is present even in his most active moment — he asks her to take it back, which is still asking someone else to solve his problem. His gratitude at her giving up, and his shame at the gratitude, is precisely the kind of self-aware-but-still-passive response the profile establishes. Maren's "I don't know" repeated three times at the end is consistent with her profile's note that she would rather say nothing than say something approximate — here she has nothing approximate to offer, only honest uncertainty. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is genuinely moving. The image of Maren feeding him honey every morning because her grandmother did it, not knowing if it worked, "just couldn't not do something" — this is the emotional core of the chapter and it lands. The horror of the piece is relational, not supernatural, and this chapter makes that explicit without deflating it. **Weaknesses** There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "She looked at me the way you look at weather you have been watching come in across a field" is a good metaphor but it's the kind of thing that calls attention to itself in a chapter that otherwise earns its effects through restraint. The line "I had known the way you know things you have not let yourself say" is slightly tautological — it tells us something we already understand from context. The ending — "a warm stone" — is a minor stumble. It's not a bad image, but it's the most conventional metaphor in the chapter, and it comes at the most important moment. The plan called for the ending to be "his, and it is not the first line inverted; it is something smaller and worse." The execution is close but the warm stone image softens what should be a colder, more unsettling final note. The chapter is also almost entirely dialogue and interiority, which is appropriate to the plan but means the physical wrongness that the plan promised ("at least one moment of physical wrongness that is not ambient") is only gestured at in the opening paragraphs (the steady hands, the absent headache). This is present but understated to the point of near-absence. **Overall** This is accomplished literary horror writing. It is not a masterpiece — the ending image is slightly soft, one or two metaphors are conventional, and the physical horror element is underdeveloped — but it is consistently good, emotionally honest, and faithful to both the plan and the characters. The dialogue is genuinely strong. The restraint is earned rather than evasive. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 — What She Did I woke and the bed was cold on her side. I lay a moment with my eyes closed and I listened. The house was doing its morning sounds, which are not the same as its night sounds — the pipes, the small tick of the radiator, a bird in the eaves. I could hear the fridge. I could not hear her. I got up. I went downstairs in my socks. The stairs were cold. The kitchen light was on. She was at the table. She had a canvas bag on the chair beside her and her coat was on the back of the chair and her keys were on the table in front of her with her hand resting next to them, not on them, next to them, as if she had put them down and then not been able to pick them up again. I stopped in the doorway. "Hi," she said. "Hi." She looked at me. Her face was the face from the other night and also not — it had been arranged, a little, the way you arrange your face for a thing you have to do. Her eyes were clear. "I'm going," she said. "Okay." "I should have gone a year ago." "Okay." "Before the house. Before any of this." "Okay, Mar." She nodded. She did not cry. Her voice was steady in the way a voice is steady when a person has used up the part of themselves that cries. "I don't know what it does," she said. "If I leave. I don't know if it comes with me. I don't know if it stays with you. I don't know if it — if it splits. I don't know. It might do any of those. I don't know which would be worse. I've been thinking about it for a long time and I don't know." "Okay." "I'm going to find out." "Okay." "I'm sorry, Daniel." "It's okay." "It isn't." "No," I said. "It isn't. But you can say it. You can say it's okay. I'll say it back. It's okay." She looked at me a long time. She was looking for something in my face. I don't know what she was looking for. I don't know if she found it. Something in her eased, a little, and she nodded once, small. "Okay," she said. I stood in the doorway. I did not go to her. I had the thought that I could — that I could cross the kitchen and kneel by her chair and put my face in her lap and ask her not to. I had the thought clearly. I watched myself have it. And I watched the part of me that was still me consider it, and I watched the other part, the easier part, the part with the steady hands, consider it too, and I understood that I could not tell, anymore, which of them wanted it more. Which of them would use it. I stayed in the doorway. She stood up. She put her coat on. She picked up the keys. She picked up the bag. She came across the kitchen toward me and I thought for a second she was going to go around me, out the door, without — and then she stopped in front of me and she put her hand up and cupped the side of my face. Her hand was very cold. She looked at me, and she did the thing she used to do a long time ago when we were first married, which was to look at me as if she were taking my picture — holding the look a beat longer than was comfortable, making a record of it. She had not done this in years. I had forgotten she used to do it. I remembered, now, standing in the kitchen doorway with her palm cold on my cheek, that she had used to do it every time she left the house. She leaned up and kissed me on the forehead. It was the kiss you give a child. It was the kiss you give a body at a wake. It was both at once and she knew it and she did it anyway, and then she took her hand down, and she went past me into the hall, and I heard the front door, and I heard the car, and I heard the car go down the drive, and I heard it get smaller, and I heard it gone. I stood in the doorway of the kitchen. The house was quiet. The fridge ran. The radiator ticked. A bird did something in the eaves and stopped. I was aware, in a distant calm way, that I should be feeling something, and I was not feeling it, or I was feeling it at a remove, as if through a wall. I noticed this. I noticed, too, that I was noticing — that there was a small gap now between me and the noticing, a thin clear space, and the space was watching. The space was patient. The space had all the time it needed. I thought about going upstairs. The bed would still be warm on her side. I could lie down in the warm place and put my face in the pillow and breathe her in, which would be the thing a man would do, the thing I would have done, a year ago, a week ago, yesterday. I thought about doing it. I did not move. The honey jar was on the counter where she always left it. The spoon was beside it. She had set them out before she went. She had done it without telling me. She had done it the way she had been doing it every morning for a long time now, which was without deciding, which was because she could not not do it. I looked at the jar. I looked at the spoon. After a while I went and I ate the honey. It was sweet. It stayed in the roof of my mouth. I stood at the counter with the empty spoon in my hand and I thought: *she left it for me. She left it and she went.* And I thought: *I ate it.* And I did not know, standing there, which of us had done the eating. I put the spoon in the sink. I ran the water over it. I watched my hand, which did not shake, and which went on not shaking, and I stood at the sink a long time after the spoon was clean.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror writing that earns most of what it reaches for. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with a classic horror beat — the cold side of the bed — but immediately distinguishes itself by refusing to sensationalize. The prose is controlled, plain in the best sense, and the repetition of "Okay" throughout the dialogue scene is a masterstroke. It functions simultaneously as Daniel's emotional shutdown, his passivity, his inability to meet the moment, and — crucially — as something that might not entirely be him anymore. The dialogue is sparse and evasive exactly as planned, and it works. Maren's speech about not knowing what the entity does when she leaves ("I don't know if it comes with me. I don't know if it stays with you. I don't know if it — if it splits") is the closest the story comes to exposition, and it's handled with appropriate uncertainty and fragmentation. The moment where Daniel watches himself consider going to her — "I watched myself have it. And I watched the part of me that was still me consider it, and I watched the other part, the easier part, the part with the steady hands, consider it too" — is the chapter's most precise articulation of the possession's nature. It doesn't announce itself; it's a doubling of perspective, a gap between the watching and the doing. This is exactly what the plan called for and it's executed with real skill. The forehead kiss is handled well. The plan noted it should feel like "the kiss you give a child or a corpse" and the text delivers this directly, even naming it: "It was the kiss you give a child. It was the kiss you give a body at a wake." This is slightly on-the-nose — the text tells us what the kiss means rather than fully trusting the image — but it's not egregious, and the follow-up ("she knew it and she did it anyway") adds a layer of Maren's agency that saves it. The honey ending is the chapter's most interesting choice and its most debatable. The plan called for "something smaller and worse" as a final note, and the honey delivers this. The ambiguity — "I did not know, standing there, which of us had done the eating" — is genuinely unsettling because it's so quiet. The act of eating the honey she left for him, after she's gone, is both a gesture of love toward her absence and a demonstration that the thing inside him will use even that love. The final image of the hand that doesn't shake, held under running water long after the spoon is clean, is strong. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "small gap now between me and the noticing, a thin clear space, and the space was watching. The space was patient. The space had all the time it needed" passage is the most explicit articulation of the entity in the whole story, and while it's earned by this point, it edges toward the kind of atmospheric declaration that the plan was trying to avoid. It's not purple prose exactly, but it's the chapter's most "literary register" moment and it arrives a beat too early — before the honey ending, which is quieter and better. The dialogue is genuinely good. "It isn't." / "No. It isn't. But you can say it. You can say it's okay. I'll say it back. It's okay." — this exchange is the chapter's emotional peak and it's earned. It shows Daniel's passivity while also showing something almost tender in it, a willingness to give her the ritual of the words even when the words are hollow. Maren is consistent with her profile: steady voice, cold hands, the photograph-look she used to do when leaving. That detail — the photograph-look — is beautifully deployed. It's specific, it's character-consistent, it's been established in the profiles, and its return here carries genuine weight without being telegraphed. The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The bag, the coat, the keys on the table, the forehead kiss, the entity's patience at the end — all present. The plan's note about "a small distance now between the watching and the standing" is rendered as "a small gap now between me and the noticing," which is a slight variation but captures the same thing. One minor criticism: the repetition of "Okay" is effective but the chapter leans on it perhaps one beat too long. By the fifth or sixth "okay" the technique is fully established and the subsequent ones add diminishing returns. A single deviation — one moment where Daniel says something other than okay — might have sharpened the effect. Overall this is a high-quality chapter that executes its plan with intelligence and restraint. The horror is genuinely uncomfortable in the right way — not through spectacle but through the recognition of how a person can be absent from themselves. The ending is earned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of horror fiction that operates almost entirely through implication, restraint, and psychological precision. The story takes the prompt — a woman watching a sleeping man — and builds it into something genuinely unsettling about possession, marriage, and the slow erosion of self. Let me examine it carefully across the requested dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose is consistently controlled and earned. The writer has a distinctive voice that uses repetition structurally ("I did not... I could not... I would not") without it becoming a tic. The rhythm of the sentences mirrors the psychological state of the narrator — careful, watchful, holding still. This is not superficial stylistic flourish; it serves the story's central concern with performance and concealment. The horror is genuinely original. Rather than conventional possession tropes (violence, grotesquerie, demonic imagery), the story locates its horror in the uncanny valley of the self — the smile that appears without the narrator's volition, the missing hours, the steady hands. The honey as a folk remedy/ritual object is beautifully handled: ambiguous, domestic, never over-explained. The grandmother's song that Maren no longer needs is one of the story's finest moments. Character work is strong. Both Daniel and Maren are rendered with real complexity. Maren's vigil — watching him sleep, feeding him honey, sitting at the table waiting for him to return — is heartbreaking and strange simultaneously. The revelation that she carried this thing first, that she has been trying to protect him with inadequate folk remedies, recontextualizes everything that came before without feeling like a cheat. Daniel's passivity is itself a symptom, and the story is smart enough to make us uncertain whether his failures of action (not touching her neck, not opening his eyes, not crossing the kitchen) are his or the thing's. The dialogue is excellent — sparse, loaded, doing enormous work. "You came back" is devastating. "I sit here. That's as far as I've gotten" is perfectly calibrated. The conversation in Chapter 7 where Maren explains the honey is the story's emotional and expository peak, and it lands because it's been so carefully prepared. The ending is genuinely ambiguous and earned. The honey jar left out, Daniel eating it, not knowing which of them is doing the eating — this is the right note to end on. It refuses resolution without feeling evasive. WEAKNESSES AND CONCERNS: The pacing in the middle chapters (3-5) is slightly slack. Chapter 3's dinner scene, while well-written, covers familiar literary-fiction territory (the failing marriage, the old wound surfaced at dinner) and the horror element recedes almost entirely. The story risks becoming a literary marriage drama with horror elements rather than horror fiction with literary depth. This is a minor complaint because the accumulation serves the final chapters, but a reader could lose the thread. Some of the metaphors, while generally landing, occasionally tip toward the over-familiar: "the way a lamp stops working, some filament somewhere" is a slightly tired image for a relationship's physical distance. "Like a fish turns under the surface of a pond" is more original. The "burning house" image recurs (she watches him like someone watching a house burn) and while it's used deliberately, it's not the freshest vehicle. The world-building is deliberately minimal — this is a domestic horror story and the house, the yard, the fog are all functional rather than richly realized. This is a choice, not a failure, but it means the story's power is almost entirely character-dependent. The transformation — the revelation of what's happening — is handled with admirable restraint, but the story never quite commits to explaining its own mythology. This is largely a strength (ambiguity is appropriate) but the honey's mechanics, the nature of the possession, and what "it" actually is remain so vague that some readers may feel the story is withholding rather than being genuinely mysterious. The grandmother's lineage suggests a folk horror tradition the story gestures at but doesn't develop. The "unearned transformation" question: Maren's departure in Chapter 8 is the story's most significant plot event, and it is earned — we've watched her sitting at the table, waiting, for the whole story. Daniel's acceptance of it is also earned by his passivity throughout. The final image of eating the honey is earned. So the transformations, such as they are, feel justified. OVERALL: This is a high-quality, genuinely literary horror story with a distinctive voice, strong character work, and real emotional and psychological depth. Its restraint is its greatest virtue and occasionally its limitation. The prose is controlled without being cold, and the horror is the more effective for being located in the domestic and the intimate. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 14 World Building: 12 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 81.2/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 82.0 | Final: 80.7)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Initial Prompt Interpretation** "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" — pure pulp. Think Grindhouse, Tarantino, early Rodriguez, the babysitter-as-avenger subgenre. The phrase promises genre violence, dark humor, a protagonist whose appearance belies their capability. The babysitter is underestimated — that's the core irony that powers pulp revenge. **Character ideas for the babysitter:** - Ex-cartel enforcer trying to live quiet life, hiding out in suburbia - Former child soldier turned nursing student - Ex-Mossad agent in witness protection - A retired assassin, now 50s, taking side gigs - A teenage girl who's secretly something else — reformed serial killer, witch, revenant - A trans woman who was a Marine Recon sniper - Nun who left the church after doing "wet work" for the Vatican - A quiet 19-year-old whose father was a hitman and taught her everything - Someone hiding from a past gang; the past comes knocking I like the 19-year-old angle best — it maps to the "babysitter" role believably. Or late 20s grad student vibe. **The kids:** - One kid, a precocious 8-year-old boy who becomes her partner-in-survival - Two kids — older girl and little brother - A single child with a medical condition (gives stakes/tension) - Kids of a wealthy family — maybe the parents are the reason trouble comes - Kids whose father is a crooked DA, judge, mob accountant **The antagonists:** - Home invasion crew — wrong house, actually there for something specific - Kidnappers targeting the kids for ransom - Hitmen who found her cover identity - Local meth gang who doesn't know what they walked into - Dirty cops - A cult **Setting:** - Isolated lake house in winter - Suburban McMansion - Desert compound - Small Texas town - Rainy Pacific Northwest - New Mexico, monsoon season I like isolated + one night, classic unity of place and time. **Plot structures (8 chapters, ~1000 words each = 8k words total):** *Version A: Hidden Past* Ch 1: Setup — babysitter arrives, meets kids, establishes normalcy, hints of her past Ch 2: Intruders arrive, situation escalates fast Ch 3: First kill, she surprises them, kids hidden Ch 4: Cat and mouse through house Ch 5: She's hurt, captured or cornered, reveals more backstory Ch 6: Turns tables, hunts them Ch 7: Confrontation with the boss — reveals why they came Ch 8: Aftermath, new normal, dark coda *Version B: The Parents Did It* Parents are involved in something bad, babysitter uncovers it while protecting kids. *Version C: Revenge Proper* They hurt one of the kids first — now it's revenge not defense. **Tone:** - Pulpy but not cartoonish - Sharp prose, punchy chapters - Some dark humor, some genuine menace - Tarantino-esque dialogue mixed with Cormac-lite violence - Could lean toward Jack Ketchum/Gillian Flynn in its meanness **Possible title beats / chapter titles:** - "The House on Marigold Lane" - "Rules of the House" - "Broken Window" - "The First One Down" - "What She Was Before" - "Basement Work" - "Meet the Boss" - "Morning" **Specific ideas I'm excited about:** - Babysitter has a tattoo she hides; kids notice it - She established three rules at the start that pay off later - A Chekhov item introduced innocuously (knitting needles, a hair pin, nail file) - The kid becomes her accomplice in a small, unsettling way — takes agency - One villain underestimates her right up to the last second - A phone call to someone from her past for "cleanup" - Final image: her baking cookies with the kids the next morning, casual, a bandage somewhere **Name ideas:** - Babysitter: Jules, Cora, Mira, Rook, Sadie, Wren - Kids: Eli and Maddie; or just Sam; Theo - Villain: Dutch, Marco, someone with a title like "The Preacher" # Reflection The prompt is inherently pulp, so leaning into genre feels right. The challenge with 8k words is pacing — I need to resist front-loading backstory and instead earn revelation through action. The phrase "fucked with the wrong babysitter" is a punchline; the story is the setup to that punchline. What works: - The single-night, single-location structure has built-in momentum and forces every chapter to advance - A young-looking protagonist with a hidden lethal past is the most genre-satisfying version; it creates immediate irony - Two kids (older sister, younger brother) lets me have one who's perceptive and one who provides vulnerability/stakes. Or just one kid for tighter focus. - Home invasion that's at the *wrong* house feels fresh and plays directly with the prompt — the antagonists literally fucked with the wrong one - Alternatively: antagonists at the *right* house (coming for her, or for something the parents have) creates a deeper mystery What doesn't work as well: - Too much backstory exposition will kill pace; I need to show her competence more than explain it - Making her a former government spook feels overdone; something messier is more interesting — cartel enforcer's daughter, or she was the one hired when hitmen needed hitmen - Supernatural angle (witch, revenant) could be fun but might dilute the grounded pulp feel - A cult would require too much setup for 8k words - Overly comedic tone could undercut the "revenge" element — I want menace with flecks of humor, not parody On protagonist: I think a woman in her early 20s, quiet, polite, hiding in plain sight. Maybe she grew up in a criminal family and got out, or did something terrible young and is atoning. Her competence should feel earned, not superhero-ish. She should get hurt, be scared, make mistakes. On antagonists: Three or four works best for pacing. A crew with distinct personalities — the leader who's smart, the brute, the loose cannon, the reluctant one. Classic but effective in 8k. On the twist/reveal: Best if the reason they came isn't random — it connects to either the parents' secret OR the babysitter's past. The latter is more satisfying for the prompt: they came for her, thinking she'd be easy. They were wrong. That IS the prompt, literalized. Pacing across 8 chapters: I want Ch 1 to be deceptively gentle and domestic, Ch 2 to shift the air, Ch 3 to deliver first violence. Middle chapters (4-6) should be the hunt and reversal. Ch 7 is climax/confrontation with the boss who must know her. Ch 8 is the morning after — cookies, kids, police not called, someone gets buried. A dark coda that recontextualizes her. Tone target: Noir-pulp with a cold, spare voice. Short sentences. Dialogue that bites. Violence that's ugly, not cool. A protagonist we root for despite — because of — her darkness. Think *You Were Never Really Here* meets *The Babysitter* meets a Jordan Harper short story. I'm drawn to the version where the crew is after her specifically — her past caught up. The parents being out at some benefit gala, the kids sleeping upstairs, and four men showing up to collect a debt. She has to protect the kids, yes, but she also has to become what she was again — and we watch her decide she's okay with that. That's the juiciest thematic meat.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a pulp revenge story that takes its prompt seriously as both premise and punchline. The phrase "they fucked with the wrong babysitter" is the shape the whole piece pours into: a setup of deceptive domesticity, a collision with violence, and a protagonist whose competence is the grim joke that pays off the title. **Protagonist:** A young woman in her early twenties — I'll call her Wren — who grew up inside a criminal world and got out. She babysits for a suburban family in a quiet town because it's the opposite of what she was. The men who come for her tonight are from her past. The kids are incidental to their plan but central to hers. **Antagonists:** A crew of four, distinct: a cold leader who knows who she was, a brute, a jumpy young one, and a reluctant middle-aged wheelman. This lets me vary the kills and the tension, and gives me someone whose fear foreshadows her capability. **Structure:** Single night, single house (with brief spillover to the yard/garage). Unity of time and place, classic pulp containment. Eight chapters of ~1000 words, each a clear beat. I want the first chapter to feel almost like a different genre — domestic, warm, slightly melancholy — so the turn lands harder. **Voice & style:** Close third on Wren. Short sentences. Spare, noir-adjacent prose with a cold undercurrent. Dialogue that cuts. Violence that's ugly and physical — not balletic, not cool. Some dark, dry humor, but no winking. I want the reader to feel the weight of what she's capable of, not cheer it like a video game. **What I'm aiming for:** - Earned menace through action and small specifics, not backstory monologues - A protagonist whose darkness is real — she's not purely heroic, she's efficient, and that's unsettling - Kids who feel like actual children: scared, clever, a little weird - A reveal (the boss knows her) that reframes the fight as personal - A morning-after coda that's almost tender, almost sweet, and completely wrong **What I'm avoiding:** - Long flashback exposition dumps; backstory in drips, through objects and dialogue - Supernatural elements - Parody tone or quippy action-movie banter - Making her invincible; she gets hurt, scared, and makes at least one mistake that costs - Redemption arc cliché — she doesn't need to be saved, and she doesn't quite get saved here **Key motifs/objects to seed early and pay off:** - A set of three "house rules" Wren tells the kids (paid off in reversal) - Knitting she brings along (needles later used; the half-finished scarf as image) - A phone number memorized, never dialed in years — used at the end for cleanup - A tattoo on her ribs the little girl glimpses - Chocolate chip cookies prepped in Ch 1, baked in Ch 8 # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Rules of the House (~1000 words)** Wren arrives at the Mercers' house on a Friday night. The parents — a lawyer and his wife — are going to a charity gala. Two kids: Maddie, nine, sharp-eyed; Eli, six, clinging. Establish the suburban calm, the big house, the woods behind it. Wren is polite, quiet, mid-twenties, knits, brings her own bag. She lays out three rules for the kids: *don't open the door for anyone, stay upstairs if I say so, and if I tell you to run, you run and don't look back.* Maddie notices a tattoo on Wren's ribs when she reaches up. They bake cookies, read a book. Parents call in. A car slows on the street outside; Wren notices. End on her locking the back door twice. **Chapter 2 — Headlights (~1000 words)** Kids are in bed. Wren knits in the living room, TV low, but her attention is on the windows. A black SUV parks two houses down. Four men get out. She watches for thirty seconds, then moves — wakes Maddie, not Eli. Tells Maddie to take her brother to the attic crawlspace, lock it, and not come out no matter what she hears. Maddie asks why. Wren says, "Because I told you to run." Wren goes downstairs, phone in hand. She doesn't call 911. She dials a memorized number, leaves a clipped message, and hangs up. The doorbell rings. She opens a drawer in the kitchen. End on her walking toward the door. **Chapter 3 — First One Down (~1000 words)** She doesn't answer the door. The men try the handle, then go around. A window breaks at the back. Wren is already in the mudroom. The jumpy young one (Denny) comes through first — she takes him with a meat mallet and a kitchen knife, fast and ugly, before he can shout. He dies noisy anyway, and she knows the others heard. She drags him behind the island. The brute (Marco) calls his name; no answer. The leader's voice — calm, familiar — says, "Wren. Come on." She freezes at her own name. So they know. End on her recognizing something in that voice. **Chapter 4 — What She Was Before (~1000 words)** Cat and mouse in the dark house. Wren cuts the breakers. She moves room to room, using the geography she's memorized. A flashback in flickers only: a man teaching her to hold a knife at thirteen, her walking out of a house at nineteen with blood on her shoes, a number written on the inside of a matchbook. She takes the brute next in the laundry room — a bad, grappling kill, and she gets cut badly across the forearm. She binds it with a dishtowel. The reluctant wheelman (Ray) is in the hall calling her name too, saying he didn't want this, he was just driving. She almost believes him. Almost. End with her upstairs, bleeding, listening to Eli cry faintly through the attic floor. **Chapter 5 — The Wheelman (~1000 words)** Ray finds her — or thinks he does. They talk through a wall. He tells her the boss is Holloway — and the name lands. Holloway was the man she left for dead four years ago. He survived. He's been looking for her ever since. Ray says he has a daughter, he just wants to walk out. Wren listens. She lets him think he's convinced her. When he comes around the corner with his hands half-raised, she kills him with the knitting needles — clean, through the throat. She sits beside his body a moment. This one she feels. The kids are still crying above her. She wipes her face and goes to find Holloway. **Chapter 6 — Basement Work (~1000 words)** Holloway is patient; he's waited four years. He's in the basement, where he went down to cut the phones — not realizing she'd already cut them from the attic wiring she knows this house because she's babysat here fifty times. He expects her to come from the stairs. She comes from the bulkhead. It goes wrong — he's faster than she remembered, older and meaner. He gets her down. He talks while he hurts her: about the man she killed, about what he taught her, about how she was the best thing he ever made. She lets him talk. Her hand finds the box cutter in her back pocket. End on a short, decisive motion and the wet sound that follows. **Chapter 7 — Morning Will Come (~1000 words)** Holloway is alive, barely, zip-tied to a pipe. She doesn't kill him yet. She climbs the stairs, her arm in ribbons, and calls up to Maddie in the attic. The girl opens the hatch, Eli behind her. Wren tells them everything is okay now, that some bad men came to the wrong house, and she made them leave. Maddie looks at the blood on her shirt and says nothing. Wren cleans up what she can, puts the kids in her own bed, sits outside the door. The number she called in Ch 2 calls back: a woman's voice, asking for an address. She gives it. Two hours until dawn. She goes back down to Holloway. They talk, quietly, for the last time. She finishes it before the sun comes up. **Chapter 8 — Cookies (~1000 words)** Dawn. A plain van with no plates. Two women in coveralls who do not speak to her beyond necessities. The house is cleaned in ninety minutes — a thoroughness that should frighten anyone who sees it. Wren showers, changes into clothes from her bag, bandages her arm beneath a long-sleeved henley. She bakes the cookies she and Eli mixed the night before. When Maddie comes down, Wren pours her orange juice. Maddie asks, quiet: *Were you always a babysitter?* Wren says: *No. But I am now.* The parents come home at nine, flushed from champagne and a late breakfast, apologizing. Eli runs to his mother. Maddie looks at Wren a long time. Wren smiles. She gets paid in cash. She walks out past the neighbor watering his lawn and nods good morning. End image: her on a bus, scarf half-knit in her lap, picking up a stitch she dropped in the dark last night.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer tackling "they fucked with the wrong babysitter" would probably start with *attitude*. The prompt is a brag, a tagline, a bumper sticker of a sentence. It has swagger. A human writer would decide early how to relate to that swagger: embrace it fully (go lurid, go bloody, go fun), ironize it (make the "revenge" feel hollow), or undercut it (make the violence genuinely sickening). The worst move is to do all three at once tentatively — which is exactly what an LLM tends to do, hedging its bets. A human would probably also write from some specific seed: a real babysitting gig they remember, a woman they knew who seemed too calm, a house they've been in. Something grounding. They'd bring weird specific details — the smell of a particular brand of cookie dough, the way a specific kind of screen door sounds, a song stuck in someone's head. These idiosyncrasies are what make prose feel lived-in rather than assembled. A human might also write messier. They'd leave threads hanging. They'd give a character a weird quirk that isn't "justified" by plot. They might start a chapter in the middle of a thought. They'd trust the reader to catch up. They'd let a scene go longer than it "should" if the texture was interesting, or clip a scene brutally short if the point had been made. Crucially, a human writer doing pulp knows pulp has *meanness* and *appetite* in it. It's not polite. It doesn't apologize. It's okay with being ugly, with being unfair, with leaving a bad taste. An LLM almost always wants to be liked. # General LLM Failings That Would Hurt This Piece **1. The Safety Reflex.** LLMs soften violence, soften language, soften moral ambiguity. They'll describe a stabbing in metaphor rather than physicality. They'll have characters feel bad in ways that editorialize the reader's response. For a pulp revenge tale, this is death. The violence needs to be ugly and specific. The protagonist needs to be allowed to be cold without the prose apologizing for her. **2. The Redemption Reflex / Bow-Tying.** LLMs love neat endings. They love a moral. They love growth arcs. Pulp revenge doesn't want this. The ending should leave residue. The "morning after cookies" scene I planned is good, but I can see the LLM version of it — where Maddie's question becomes a tidy emotional beat and Wren has a moment of quiet self-knowledge. That would ruin it. It should be *strange*, not resolved. **3. Impressive-Sounding Prose.** LLMs reach for the cinematic, the operatic, the weighty sentence. "The night pressed in like a held breath." "Violence had always been her native tongue." This kind of thing is a tell. Good pulp prose is often boring on the sentence level — it's about image, rhythm, and restraint, not ornate metaphor. Overwriting kills pulp dead. **4. Over-Explanation.** LLMs explain what a character is feeling, then describe how they show it, then have another character notice it. Triple redundancy. A human trusts one of those three to do the job. **5. Symmetry and Symbol-Stacking.** LLMs love to tie motifs together too tightly — the tattoo, the knitting, the three rules, the cookies, the phone number — each paid off with a satisfying click. I've already planned five motifs that all get paid off neatly. That's too many clicks. Real writing leaves some motifs unresolved or lets them just be texture. **6. Generic Character Voices.** LLMs make tough guys sound like tough guys from movies. They speak in lines, not dialogue. "Come on, Wren." "Don't make this harder than it has to be." A human would make at least one of these men speak weirdly, off-model, memorably. **7. Pacing Evenness.** LLMs spread action evenly across planned beats. Humans let scenes breathe or strangle. A fight might take a paragraph; a silence might take a page. **8. The Competent Woman Archetype.** This is a specific LLM trap — the Cool Assassin Lady who is calm, measured, efficient, and makes grim one-liners. She's a Pinterest board. A real character would have a tic, a fear, an irrational behavior, a weakness that isn't plot-relevant. **9. Sanitized Interiority.** LLMs give protagonists thoughts that are morally presentable. Wren would probably have at least one thought in this story that is genuinely ugly — enjoyment, contempt, cruelty — and an LLM would flinch from writing it. **10. Ending Benediction.** LLMs almost can't help closing on a resonant, slightly poetic image that gestures at theme. My "picking up a stitch she dropped in the dark last night" ending is… exactly that. It's a tell. # Deep Dive Critique of the Plan **The Protagonist is Too Clean.** Wren as planned is a genre type — the quiet lethal woman who got out and has rules. I gave her knitting (cozy), a tattoo (mysterious), and a hidden past (tropey). She needs something that doesn't fit — a mean streak the book doesn't excuse, a pettiness, a weird fixation, a specific bad habit. Does she steal small things from the houses she sits at? Does she hate one of the kids a little? Does she have a crush on the dad she doesn't act on but notices in herself with disgust? Something human and unflattering. **The Kids are Furniture.** Maddie "perceptive" and Eli "little and scared" — these are placeholders. They have no texture. Real kids are weirder. Eli should have an obsession (a specific toy, a specific word). Maddie should have a thing she's lying about. They should be annoying in places, not just endangered. **The Crew is a Checklist.** Cold leader, brute, jumpy one, reluctant wheelman — this is literally the Four Guys of pulp cliché. At least one of them needs to surprise me. Maybe the brute isn't a brute — he's quiet and sad. Maybe the jumpy young one is the most competent. Maybe the leader isn't the scariest. **The Plot is Too Neat.** Each chapter has its designated kill, its designated reveal. Ch 3 kill #1, Ch 4 kill #2, Ch 5 kill #3, Ch 6 the boss. This is videogame structure. Human writing would break this rhythm — maybe two kills happen fast and close, maybe one kill happens offstage, maybe she tries to kill someone and fails, maybe the order gets confused, maybe one of them just runs away and lives. **The Backstory is Too Legible.** "Holloway is the man she left for dead four years ago" — this is a mechanical reveal. It clicks into place. Better if her history is murkier, partially withheld even from the ending. The reader should feel the weight of it without getting the full account. **The Motifs are Over-Engineered.** Three rules, tattoo, knitting, phone number, cookies, scarf, matchbook — I'm stacking Chekhov's items. A human writer would seed two, max, and let them do heavy lifting. Some of these need to just be texture, or disappear. **The Ending is Too Graceful.** The cookies scene is strong conceptually but I've written it too nicely in the plan.
