The cleanup crew arriving, the bandaged arm, the cash payment, the bus, the scarf — it's all coherent and symbolic. A human would disrupt this. Maybe she's still bleeding through the shirt. Maybe Eli says something awful and funny. Maybe she doesn't take the bus, she just walks. Maybe the neighbor is suspicious. Maybe she forgets something at the house. **Dialogue Beats are Movie-Dialogue.** "Because I told you to run." "Come on, Wren." "I just want to walk out." These are all fine, and all from the LLM dialogue handbook. At least some lines need to be off-rhythm, accidental, even dumb. **The Moral Temperature is Too Stable.** Wren is sympathetic throughout. The plan never really asks the reader to be disturbed by her. For the piece to have teeth, there should be at least one moment where we recoil — not at the violence, but at her. **Chapter 6 (Holloway monologues while hurting her) is Classic Villain Monologue.** This is the most LLM thing in the plan. Villains explaining themselves to captive heroes is pulp, yes, but the specific texture I've planned — "about how she was the best thing he ever made" — is generic. It should be more specific, weirder, or cut entirely. **The Cleanup Crew is Too Cool.** Two women in coveralls, ninety minutes, efficient. This is a setpiece from *Pulp Fiction* / *Mr. & Mrs. Smith* / every show with a fixer. It's the lazy choice. If I keep it, it needs to be weirder — older women, maybe one is chatty, maybe they don't come and she has to do it herself, maybe only one comes and she's scared of Wren. # Ideas to Make it More Human - Give Wren a specific, unflattering detail: she's been skimming hours on her timecard at another job, or she's sleeping with someone married, or she genuinely dislikes one of the kids she's sat for (not these ones, but she's thought it about kids before). - Let one of the men live. Not as a mercy — as a problem. Loose ends. - Don't reveal all the backstory. Let Holloway call her a name that isn't Wren and leave that hanging. - Make one kill go *badly* in a non-cool way — she throws up, or her hand won't stop shaking, or she kills the wrong person (a neighbor who came to check on a noise — this would be devastating and real). - Cut at least one motif. Pick the two strongest and drop the rest, or demote them to background. - Write at least one paragraph that is deliberately flat, almost reportorial, as contrast to more charged prose. - Let a chapter open mid-action without orientation. - Give Eli a specific toy he keeps talking about. Let that toy be the last thing seen, not some grand symbolic image. - Let Maddie be actively unhelpful at a key moment — panic, make noise, do the wrong thing. Kids do that. - Have Wren make a real mistake with consequences, not just "get hurt" as a cost of combat. - Let the ending be anticlimactic in some specific way. She doesn't get on a bus to new adventures. She goes back to her shitty apartment and the dishes are still in the sink from yesterday. - Allow ugliness in her interiority — a thought about one of the kids that's uncharitable, a flash of enjoyment during a kill, boredom during the boss's speech. - Let the prose be ruder. Let it swear. Let it be sour. - Consider first person for one chapter, or a letter, or some disruption to close-third uniformity. Maybe not — but consider it. - Don't make her competence feel like a superpower. Make it feel like a disease.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a pulp revenge story and I'm going to let it be one — bloody, mean, with appetite. The prompt is a brag and I'm going to earn the brag, but I'm going to resist the LLM instinct to make the protagonist admirable, the violence stylish, or the ending resonant-in-a-poetic-way. I want the reader to finish the story a little unsettled by how much they rooted for her. **Protagonist.** Wren, early twenties, was raised inside something bad and got most of the way out. She babysits because it pays cash and nobody asks questions and because she likes kids better than adults most days. She is not a Cool Assassin Woman. She's tired. Her apartment is a mess. She has a specific unflattering habit — she reads the parents' medicine cabinets, sometimes takes a pill, sometimes pockets a twenty off a dresser if it's loose. Small thefts. She's aware this is petty and does it anyway. During the violence, she will feel things we're not supposed to admit characters feel: focus that edges into pleasure, boredom during a monologue, a flash of contempt for someone begging. I'm going to write those, not flinch. **The kids.** Maddie is nine and lying about something small all evening (she broke something, she has a crush, she snuck a video — I'll decide in scene). Eli is six and obsessed with a plastic triceratops named Gary. He talks about Gary a lot. Gary is going to end the story, not a scarf. **The crew.** Four men, but I'm breaking the checklist. The "brute" is quiet and kind of sad. The "jumpy one" turns out to be the most dangerous. The leader isn't the scariest. One of them is going to survive. That's the problem she carries out. **Plot shape.** Same bones as before — single night, single house, past catching up — but I'm disrupting the tidy one-kill-per-chapter rhythm. One kill will be ugly and go wrong in a non-cool way (she kills a neighbor who came to check on the noise; this happens and isn't undone). One antagonist walks out alive. The backstory stays partial — we get enough to understand, not enough to close. **Voice & style.** Close third, past tense, rude when it needs to be. Short sentences, but I'll let a paragraph sprawl if the texture wants it. Some deliberately flat reportorial prose as contrast. Dialogue that's sometimes dumb, sometimes off-rhythm, not always a line. I'll open at least one chapter mid-action without orientation. I will not write "the night pressed in" sentences. I will not end on a poetic image that gestures at theme. **What I'm cutting from the previous plan.** The knitting needles as kill weapon (too cute). The memorized phone number and cleanup crew (too *John Wick*). The three rules as a symmetrical payoff structure. The scarf-on-the-bus ending. Holloway's villain monologue about having made her. The tattoo reveal. **What I'm keeping.** Suburban house, one night, crew of four, past-coming-for-her premise, the cookies, Maddie's perceptiveness, the morning after. **What I'm aiming for.** A protagonist who is compelling because she's a little wrong. Violence that costs. An ending that doesn't absolve. The prompt's swagger delivered, but with residue. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Mercers (~1000 words)** Wren arrives at the Mercers' at seven. Establish house, kids, parents, the evening. Mrs. Mercer is a little drunk already and over-explains the microwave. Mr. Mercer is on his phone. Eli immediately introduces Gary the triceratops. Maddie is on a tablet and barely looks up; Wren can tell she's hiding something on the screen. Wren takes the cash advance, notes the alarm code, notes the back door doesn't latch right. After the parents leave she does what she always does — walks the house slow, opens a few drawers, reads the labels in the medicine cabinet, takes one Ativan and pockets it for later. Not tonight. She's working. Makes cookie dough with Eli, who narrates Gary's opinions on chocolate chips. Maddie stays on her tablet. A car passes twice on the street. Wren clocks it but doesn't react yet. End: she puts the kids to bed, sits on the couch, and sees the car again, parked now, lights off, three houses down. **Chapter 2 — Dogs (~1000 words)** She watches for a long time before moving. Four men get out. She recognizes the walk of one of them before she can see his face, and something in her stomach goes cold and familiar. She goes upstairs. Wakes Maddie first. Tells her: take your brother up to the attic and stay there, don't come down, don't make noise, I'll come get you. Maddie asks why. Wren says, "Because there are bad men." Maddie doesn't cry. She asks if she can bring the tablet. Wren says yes. Eli wakes asking for Gary; they find Gary; they go up. Wren doesn't call 911. She could. She doesn't, and she registers that about herself. She goes to the kitchen and takes the good knife out of the block, and a smaller paring knife, and slides the paring knife into her back waistband. The doorbell rings. Then again. Then the sound of someone walking around the side of the house. End: glass breaking at the back. **Chapter 3 — Denny (~1000 words)** The jumpy young one, Denny, comes through first — she expects the brute and is surprised; the surprise almost gets her killed. They struggle in the mudroom and he's fast, faster than she is, and stronger than he looks, and for thirty seconds it's a real question. She wins it ugly — slips on a boot, goes down, comes up under his jaw with the knife. He bleeds out on the tile making a sound she doesn't want to remember. Her hand is shaking. She makes herself stop it. The others heard — they had to have — and she waits for them to come and they don't. Silence. Then the leader's voice from somewhere in the house: *"Wren. Sweetheart. We just want to talk."* She doesn't know the voice. She knows the word sweetheart. End: she hears someone upstairs. **Chapter 4 — Neighbor (~1000 words)** Someone is moving on the second floor. She goes up in the dark, paring knife out, and at the top of the stairs she sees a figure in the hallway and she moves on it fast and quiet and puts the knife into its neck before she understands what she's looking at. It's Mr. Halvorsen from next door. Bathrobe. Flashlight. He must have heard the glass. He makes a sound and goes down. She catches him so he doesn't thump. She kneels there with him for a second that lasts longer than it should. She does not throw up. She wants to. She drags him into the linen closet and closes the door and her hands are wet and she wipes them on a towel that was folded nicely. When she comes out she is different. The prose should register this without saying it. From downstairs: *"Wren? You okay up there?"* The leader is fucking with her. She goes down. **Chapter 5 — Ray (~1000 words)** The middle-aged one — Ray — is in the kitchen, sitting at the island with his hands flat on the marble like someone waiting for a meal. He's unarmed, or pretending to be. He says he's out. He says he knew Denny was going to get himself killed. He says the leader — he calls him Cope, short for something — isn't right, hasn't been right for a long time, and he, Ray, has a daughter, and he's just driving tonight, he was just driving. Wren stands in the doorway with the knife and listens. She believes him about the daughter. She doesn't believe him about being out. She tells him to leave out the front door and not come back. He goes. She lets him. She doesn't know why and she tells herself it's because he didn't do anything yet and she knows that's a lie. Through the window she watches him walk to the SUV and get in and drive away. That's the man who lives. End: footsteps in the basement. And another set — the brute, Marco — somewhere on the main floor. **Chapter 6 — Marco (~1000 words)** She finds Marco in the laundry room. He is huge and he is crying, quietly, while he looks for her. He's been crying for a while. She doesn't understand it and doesn't want to. He sees her and says, "I'm sorry," and comes at her anyway. It is the worst fight of the night. He gets her against the dryer and she thinks *this is it* with a clarity that surprises her, and then her hand finds the iron on the shelf and she hits him with it until he stops. She keeps hitting after he stops, once, twice. That's the ugly beat I'm not flinching from. She sits on the floor next to him afterward for maybe a minute. Her forearm is opened up; she binds it with a dishtowel. Upstairs Eli is crying — she can hear it faintly through the vents. End: she goes to the basement door. Opens it. Goes down. **Chapter 7 — Cope (~1000 words)** Cope is waiting for her in the finished basement, sitting in the Mercers' recliner like he lives there. He is not what she expected. Older. Unimpressive. He says her name — not Wren, the other one, the one from before, and I'll leave that name unspoken on the page or mostly so. He doesn't monologue. He asks her how she's been. He asks about her mother. He tells her two or three specific things that place him in her life — small, domestic, wrong things, the kind of detail that says *I was there once and you didn't know me then.* She realizes he isn't the man she thinks should have come for her; he's someone lower down who volunteered. He wants the job because finishing her is a promotion. This is worse, somehow, than if it had been personal. He has a gun and he uses it and he misses once and hits her in the thigh the second time and she gets to him anyway because the basement is small and she is furious now in a way she hasn't let herself be all night. She kills him with her hands and the paring knife and it takes a while. She does not feel good afterward. She does not feel bad. She feels like she has finished a chore. End: she climbs the stairs. **Chapter 8 — Gary (~1000 words)** Dawn is coming but not here yet. She does not call a cleanup crew because she does not have a cleanup crew. She has a phone and 911 and a version of the story she starts assembling while she washes her hands: a home invasion, a neighbor who heroically came to help and was killed, a babysitter who fought back. It is not a perfect story. Some of it will hold. She will not be Wren anymore after tonight; she knows that. She goes upstairs and gets the kids down from the attic. Eli has Gary. Maddie looks at the blood on her and asks, flat, "Are they gone?" Wren says yes. Maddie nods like she's been told the weather. Wren puts them on the couch with a blanket and the TV on and goes to the kitchen and bakes the cookies they made last night, because she needs the house to smell like something else when the cops come, and because Eli will want one. When they come out of the oven Eli eats two and gives one to Gary. Maddie doesn't eat. She watches Wren. She says, "I won't tell." Wren doesn't ask what she means. She calls 911 at seven-fourteen. The parents come home to a police line. In the noise of it Wren slips away before they can find her to thank her — out the back, across the yard, through the gap in the hedge she noticed earlier. She walks. She doesn't take a bus. Her thigh is bleeding through the bandage. Somewhere a few blocks away she sits on a curb and realizes she's still holding Gary. Eli pressed him into her hand at some point and she didn't notice. End: she sits on the curb with a plastic dinosaur in her lap and tries to figure out what to do with him. That's the last image. Not resonant. Just true.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Wren **Background.** Her real name isn't Wren. She was born into something — not a cartel, not a mafia, nothing with a name that would mean anything to a cop. A crew. Her stepfather ran it, and her mother kept the books and the quiet. Wren grew up in a house in the desert outside Yuma where men came and went and sometimes didn't come back, and by twelve she knew what the crawlspace under the kitchen was for. She started helping at fifteen, not by choice exactly — by gravity. She was good at it. She was good at it in a way that made her stepfather proud, which was the worst thing about it. At nineteen something happened that she doesn't narrate to herself anymore — she left in the middle of the night with a split lip and eleven hundred dollars from the safe, took a bus to Phoenix, then another to wherever, then another. She's been Wren for almost four years now. She babysits, does some cash under-the-table at a vet clinic cleaning kennels, rents a studio where the shower drains slow. She hasn't killed anyone in three years and eight months. She knows the exact number and doesn't like that she does. **Personality.** Quiet in a way adults read as shy and kids read as listening. Dry. A little mean in her head and almost never out loud. She likes kids because kids don't perform and don't expect her to. She is lonely in a way she has decided not to address. She reads a lot — thrillers, mostly, which she finds funny. She cries at commercials sometimes and is embarrassed by it. **Idiosyncrasies & flaws.** Steals small things from the houses she sits in. Not valuables — a single Ativan from a bottle of thirty, a twenty off a dresser where there's a pile, a nice pen. She tells herself it's a tax on people who don't lock their cabinets. She knows this is bullshit. She reads the mail on the counter. She eats the parents' leftovers and doesn't hide it. She bites her thumbnail when she's thinking and the nail is always ragged. She flirts, sometimes, with dads, in microscopic ways she doesn't act on, and afterward dislikes herself about it for a day or two. She is not a good person who does one bad thing. She is a mostly okay person with a lot of small ugliness and one very large thing in her past. **Physicality.** Five-six. Thin but not delicate — sinew, not grace. Dark hair she cuts herself in the bathroom and it shows. A face that ages depending on the light: seventeen in bad fluorescent, twenty-eight at 3 a.m. Jeans, a henley, beat-up Vans. She moves economically. She doesn't fidget. When she's scared her hands go very still and her jaw sets, which reads as calm to people who don't know better. She has a small scar under her chin from a kitchen knife when she was eleven and a long flat scar on her ribs she doesn't explain. **Motives & allegiances.** Survive. Keep the kids alive. Get through the night without becoming the thing she was at nineteen, and failing that, get through the night. **Voice.** > *She'd read somewhere that you were supposed to narrate yourself through hard things. Pick up the knife. Walk to the door. Turn the knob. It was supposed to keep you present. She thought that was stupid. The trick was not being present.* > "I'm not gonna hurt you, buddy. I just want you to go upstairs and stay with Gary, okay? Gary's scared. He needs you to be brave for him." > *The Ativan was still in her pocket. She thought about it the way you think about a cigarette when you've quit.* --- # Maddie Mercer **Background.** Nine. Third-grader. Good at school, bad at friends. Only child until Eli came and then suddenly middle-management. Her parents love her and don't really see her; she has figured this out and is angry about it in a slow-growing way she doesn't have the vocabulary for yet. She spends a lot of time on her tablet and has recently, in the last two months, started watching things she isn't supposed to watch — a YouTube channel where a woman does true-crime recaps in a sweet voice. Maddie thinks about murder more than a nine-year-old probably should. Not doing it. Just — the fact of it. The way people do it and the way other people find out. **Personality.** Watchful. Withholding. Funny when she wants to be. She tests people — small lies, small provocations, to see what they'll do. She likes Wren because Wren doesn't try. Most babysitters try. **Idiosyncrasies & flaws.** Lies about small things for practice. Tonight she has been hiding a broken figurine from her mother's shelf — she knocked it off Tuesday and glued it wrong and put it back hoping no one would notice. She's been thinking about it all week. She is a little cruel to Eli in ways he doesn't register yet. She bites the inside of her cheek. She is too old for her age in some ways and much younger in others. **Physicality.** Tall for nine, coltish, brown hair in a messy ponytail she did herself. Glasses she pushes up with her knuckle. She holds the tablet like a shield. When she's scared she goes very quiet and very still, like her mother does when her father is mad, which is a thing she has learned without being taught. **Motives & allegiances.** Herself. Eli, reluctantly. She will protect him tonight but she will also resent him for needing it. **Voice.** > "You said bad men. Are they here for us or for you?" > *She knew she was supposed to be more scared. She was scared. But she was also — and she wouldn't have had the word for this — interested.* > "I won't tell." --- # Eli Mercer **Background.** Six. Kindergarten. Loves dinosaurs with a focus that has worried his parents into a specialist appointment they haven't made yet. His whole inner life runs on Gary, a plastic triceratops his grandfather gave him, and Gary's complicated opinions about everything. **Personality.** Sweet, chatty, absent from the main conversation and deep inside his own. Will ask you a question and then answer it himself before you can. Hugs hard. **Idiosyncrasies.** Gary has a voice — higher than Eli's, a little raspy. Gary is grumpy and hates peas. Eli will not go to sleep without Gary on the pillow beside him. Eli knows the names of twelve dinosaurs and will recite them if given any excuse. **Physicality.** Small, soft, pajamas with planets on them. Scab on one knee. Hair that sticks up in the back no matter what. **Motives & allegiances.** Gary. His sister. His mom. Wren, now, a little. **Voice.** > "Gary says the cookies need more chips. Gary says that's the rule." > "Is the lady a nice lady? Gary thinks she's a nice lady but Gary thinks everyone is a nice lady." --- # Cope **Background.** Fifty-one. Middle-rung for twenty-five years in an organization that has no name but has a shape. Never quite made the jump to the inner thing. Watched younger men get brought up while he stayed where he was. Volunteered for the Wren job because he's been told, not in so many words, that if he brings her in he's got a seat at a different table. He is aware this might not be true. He came anyway. He has been looking for her for ten months. **Personality.** Patient. Methodical. Not cruel for sport but willing. Reads westerns. Has a wife in Henderson who thinks he's in freight. **Idiosyncrasies.** Chews sunflower seeds constantly and keeps the shells in a folded paper towel in his pocket. Always. Even tonight. He knew Wren's mother a little, years ago — talked to her twice at a cookout. He remembers her being kind about his tie. **Physicality.** Soft-bodied, unimpressive, the kind of man you don't look at twice at a gas station. Grey at the temples. Wears a Members Only jacket unironically. Small wet eyes. **Motives.** The promotion. Finishing the job. Proving to someone, maybe himself, that he should have been brought up years ago. **Voice.** > "Your mother had a nice laugh. I only heard it the once. Somebody's birthday, I think." > "I'm not gonna talk to you about your feelings, sweetheart. I don't have the time and neither do you." --- # Denny **Background.** Twenty-four. Fast, mean, a natural at it. The one in the crew who scares the others a little. No real backstory needed; he'd resist one if you tried. Dead by the end of Ch 3. **Personality.** A grin that doesn't mean anything. Talks too much when he's nervous, which he always is, which is part of why he's dangerous — the nerves move faster than thought. **Physicality.** Wiry, tattooed knuckles, cheap cologne. Moves like something that's been kicked a lot and learned from it. **Voice.** > "Aw, she's cute. Hey. Hey. Don't. Hey—" --- # Marco **Background.** Thirty-eight. Bouncer, briefly a fighter, a man who has hurt many people for money and has started, in the last year, not to sleep well. His mother died in April. He has not told anyone in the crew. He took this job because it was offered and he needed the money because the funeral cost more than he had. **Personality.** Quiet. Slow. Not stupid; just deliberate. Polite to waitresses. Reads the Bible in a confused, occasional way. **Physicality.** Six-four, thick, a body that has started to ache in the mornings. A soft face on a hard neck. Hands he keeps closed when he isn't using them, because open they look too gentle. **Idiosyncrasy.** Crying tonight, quietly, while he looks for her. He doesn't know exactly why. He thinks it might be about his mother. He thinks it might be about what he knows they're here to do. He is going to do it anyway, which is the part that breaks him. **Voice.** > "I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I'm sorry." --- # Ray **Background.** Forty-six. Driver, fixer, low-level. Has done enough bad things to lose track but none of them with his own hands if he can help it. Has a daughter, twelve, who lives with his ex-wife in Reno and thinks he sells industrial parts. He is not lying about her. **Personality.** Tired. Funny in a dry way he doesn't use with these men. Knows he is the smartest one in the car and knows this is not a thing to advertise. **Physicality.** Average everything. A face that forgets itself. Windbreaker. Keys on a carabiner. **Motives.** Get through the night. Get home. He will walk out when Wren lets him and he will not look back and he will not tell anyone and he will also, eventually, do another job like this one, because that's what he does. She knows this when she lets him go. That's the weight of it. **Voice.** > "I'm gonna put my hands on the counter, okay? I'm gonna keep 'em there. You and me are gonna have a conversation like adults." > "I got a kid. I know everybody says that. I really got one." --- # Mr. Halvorsen **Background.** Seventy-two. Retired high school shop teacher. Widower two years. Has been lonely in a way he is too proud to name. Hears glass break next door at eleven-forty on a Friday and puts on his robe and gets his big Maglite because someone has to check, and because in a small way he wants to be useful to somebody again. **Physicality.** Thin, stooped, slippers. White hair still thick. Smells faintly of Old Spice and basement. **His role in the story.** He is killed by Wren in the upstairs hallway in the dark before either of them understands what's happening. He gets a paragraph of life before he dies and that is the whole point — that he was a person, not a beat. His death is the worst thing she does tonight and she knows it and the story will not redeem it.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — The Mercers The Mercers lived in one of those houses that looked bigger from the street than it was inside. Pale brick, two stories, a lawn kept by someone else. Wren parked her Civic at the curb because the driveway was full and walked up with her bag on her shoulder, bag strap across her chest the way she always wore it, hand on the strap. She rang the bell at six fifty-eight. She liked to be two minutes early. Any earlier and people felt surveilled; any later and they started the evening tense. Mrs. Mercer opened the door already holding a glass of white. She was a pretty woman gone slightly loose at the edges, a caterer's wife with a caterer's husband. "Wren, hi, hi, come in, we're running so late, Greg — Greg, Wren's here — Wren, you look nice, did you do something to your hair?" "No," Wren said. "It looks nice." The house smelled like the candle she knew from the Target by the freeway. Eli came around the corner at a full run in pajamas with planets on them, stopped two feet from her knees, and held up a plastic triceratops. "This is Gary," he said. "Hi, Gary." "Gary says hi back but he's shy." "That's okay." "Gary doesn't like it when people look at him too long." "I'll try not to." Maddie was on the couch with a tablet angled away from the room. She looked up without moving her head, registered Wren, went back to the screen. Wren saw in the second before the glance broke that she had her thumb over whatever was in the corner of the screen, hiding it. Not porn. A kid that age hiding porn wouldn't be that practiced. Something else. "Kitchen," Mrs. Mercer said. "Come. Let me show you the — I know I showed you last time, but Greg redid the — " "Microwave," Wren said. "Microwave, right, right, you have to press clear first or it won't — " "I remember." Mr. Mercer came down the stairs tying a tie and saying something into his phone. He smiled at Wren without looking at her. He was handsome in a way that had begun, very recently, to stop being handsome. He put his hand over the phone and said, "Cash on the counter. Ninety for tonight, we'll be back by midnight-thirty, one at the latest, it's the thing at the Drury." "Great," Wren said. "Kids have eaten. Maddie's not allowed on the tablet after nine." "Okay." "She'll say she is. She's not." "Okay." He went back to his phone. Mrs. Mercer hugged her children like she wouldn't see them for a week, kissed the tops of their heads, left lipstick on Eli. Then they were gone in a wash of perfume and keys and the door shut too hard and the house adjusted around the absence. Wren stood in the front hall and listened. Refrigerator. The tablet. Eli narrating something to Gary in a whisper. A car on the cross street. Nothing else. "Okay," she said, mostly to herself. She did her walk. She did it in every house. She told herself it was so she'd know the layout if something happened, and that was true and also not the whole truth. She went room to room slow, opening the doors she was allowed to open and one or two she wasn't. Master bedroom: unmade on his side, made on hers. A pile of bills by the window. A twenty on the dresser with a receipt under it; she left the twenty. The medicine cabinet in the master bath had the usual — floss, a prescription for something heart-related in his name, Zoloft in hers, a bottle of Ativan with her name on it and thirty in the count. Wren took one. She palmed it and dropped it in the watch pocket of her jeans. Not for tonight. She didn't take things the night she took them. Back of the house: sliding door onto a little deck, then grass, then a line of trees. She checked the lock. The latch rode a quarter inch short of the strike. She lifted the handle and pressed and heard the bolt go. Fine if you knew. Most people didn't know. Front door: deadbolt worked. Alarm panel by the coat closet. The code was on a Post-it inside the cabinet above the microwave, which was its own problem, but not hers tonight. She came back to the kitchen. Eli was on the stool with his elbows on the island and Gary lying on his side next to a bag of chocolate chips. "We're making cookies," Eli said. "Are we." "Gary says we have to." "Well." "Gary says you have to use more than the recipe says. He says recipes are the minimum." "Gary's a strong personality." "Yeah." She got the bowl down and the butter from the counter where Mrs. Mercer had left it softening, and they made dough. Eli measured the sugar with both hands on the cup like it was heavy. He told her Gary's opinions on each ingredient. She let him crack the egg and she picked the shell out of the bowl without him seeing because he would've been upset. Maddie stayed on the couch. At one point Wren looked over and Maddie was looking at her, not at the tablet, with a small flat expression Wren couldn't read, and then the tablet again. They put the dough in the fridge to bake later. Teeth, pajamas, a book. Eli wanted the one about the moon; she read it. Maddie took herself to bed without being asked and closed her door firmly. Wren sat on the couch in the living room with the television low and her bag at her feet. At nine-forty a car came slowly down the street with its headlights on and kept going. At ten-fifteen the same car came back the other way and kept going. At ten-fifty-two it came back and stopped. She watched the headlights go out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on the plan's promises. Let me work through it carefully. The prose voice is controlled and distinctive. The model has found a register that's dry, observational, and economical without being mannered. "A pretty woman gone slightly loose at the edges" is a good line — specific, a little cruel, earned. "He was handsome in a way that had begun, very recently, to stop being handsome" is similarly precise and does character work efficiently. These aren't forced metaphors; they're accurate observations rendered with a slight edge that tells us something about Wren's way of seeing. The chapter plan called for establishing the house, kids, parents, the evening routine, Wren's small thefts, the back door latch, the alarm code, and the car. All of this is executed. The plan is followed faithfully and without mechanical box-checking — the elements are woven into the scene rather than announced. Character work is strong. Eli and Gary are immediately charming without being cloying. The Gary voice ("Gary says recipes are the minimum") is funny and specific and doesn't overstay its welcome. Maddie's watchfulness is established economically — the thumb over the screen, the flat expression, the self-directed bedtime. Wren's small thefts are rendered without editorializing: she takes the Ativan, notes she doesn't take things the night she takes them, and moves on. The plan called for her to be aware this is petty and do it anyway, and the prose delivers that without underlining it. The dialogue is notably good. Mrs. Mercer's flustered over-explanation, Mr. Mercer's phone-distracted efficiency, Eli's Gary-mediated conversation — all of it sounds right and does work. The exchange about the microwave ("I remember" / "Microwave, right, right") is a small perfect beat. The dialogue is not trying to be clever; it's trying to be accurate, which is harder. The ending is effective. The car appearing three times, the headlights going out — it's a clean, unshowy way to close the chapter on threat. The plan called for this and the execution is restrained. No "the night pressed in" sentences, as promised. What's missing or slightly weak: The chapter plan mentioned Maddie hiding something on the screen and Wren being able to tell it's not porn. This is present but underdeveloped — we don't get quite enough of Maddie's interiority or the specific texture of what she's hiding (the broken figurine is mentioned in the plan but doesn't appear here, which is fine for chapter one, but Maddie feels slightly thinner than her profile suggests she should). The plan also mentioned Wren flirting microscopically with Mr. Mercer — this is absent, which is probably a good call given the chapter's economy, but it means one of Wren's noted flaws doesn't appear. The prose is occasionally slightly flat in a way that reads as intentional (the reportorial mode the plan describes) but occasionally tips into just being flat. "She did her walk. She did it in every house" is good. Some of the middle section of the house-walk is functional rather than interesting. No purple prose. No forced metaphors. The lightness of the Eli/Gary scenes is genuinely earned and will make the darkness that follows land harder. The chapter does exactly what a first chapter should: establishes world, character, and threat without rushing any of them. Overall this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that honors its plan and its characters. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — Dogs She watched for a long time before she moved. This was something her stepfather had taught her, although he hadn't put it in words. You watched. You let the other person commit to being there before you committed to seeing them. Amateurs looked and then acted. Professionals looked and kept looking and then, when they had enough, acted once. The SUV was a dark Tahoe, older, three houses down on the opposite side. The driver killed the lights but not the engine, not for a full minute. The engine cut. Nobody got out for another minute after that. Then the driver's door, then the passenger, then the back two almost together. Four men. They stood on the sidewalk for a second arranging themselves the way a crew does — checking who was carrying what, who was going where — and that was what told her, more than anything else, what they were. Civilians didn't stand like that on a sidewalk. The tallest one rolled his neck and it popped loud enough that she could almost hear it through the glass. She knew the walk of the one on the left. Not the face — too far, too dark — but the walk, the way he led with his right shoulder like he was about to turn into a door that wasn't there. She had watched a man walk that way across a kitchen in Yuma for half her childhood. Not him; he was dead. Someone who had learned from the same person, or from him. Something in her stomach went cold and familiar, the way a room you haven't been in for years is still the shape of itself in the dark. She stood up. She did not hurry. Hurry was how you forgot things. Upstairs first. Maddie's door. She went in without knocking and sat on the edge of the bed and put her hand on Maddie's shoulder and Maddie came awake all at once, eyes open, not startled, like she had been waiting to be woken. "Hey," Wren said, quiet. "I need you to listen to me and not ask questions until I'm done." Maddie nodded. "There are bad men about to come in the house. I need you to take your brother up to the attic, the crawlspace off the landing, and close the hatch. You know the one." "Yeah." "You stay up there no matter what you hear. You don't come down. You don't peek. If you hear me and it doesn't sound right, you stay up there anyway. You understand me?" "Yeah." "If I don't come for you — if nobody you know comes — you wait until it's light and then you go out the front and across to the Halvorsens'. Only the Halvorsens'. Not the house with the dogs." "Okay." "Repeat it." "Attic. Stay. Don't come down. Halvorsens' in the morning." "Good." Maddie sat up. She reached for her glasses and got them on. Then she said, "Can I bring my tablet?" Wren almost laughed. "Yes. Bring the tablet. Keep it on silent. Do not turn the flashlight on." "Okay." "Get your brother. Be quiet with him." Eli was harder. He was a deep sleeper and he was six, and he came up fuzzy and whiny, and when Maddie said *come on Eli we're doing a game* he said he didn't want to and Wren crouched by the bed and said, "Buddy. Where's Gary?" "Under the pillow." "Get Gary. Gary has to come." "Why?" "Gary's scared. He wants to go with you." That worked. He clutched Gary to his chest and let his sister take his hand and Wren walked them to the landing and pulled the cord on the attic hatch and the little folding stairs came down and she lifted Eli up first because his legs wouldn't do the climb on their own. Then Maddie. Maddie paused on the third step and looked down at Wren and said, with the same flat voice as before, "Are they here for us or for you?" "Go," Wren said. Maddie went. Wren folded the stairs back up and pushed the hatch closed and wiped a smudge of dust off the ceiling with her sleeve. Then she stood on the landing and did not move for maybe five seconds and thought about the phone in her pocket. She could call 911. The cops in this town were four minutes away. Four minutes was a long time and also not that long. She could call and stand in the hallway with a knife and wait. She could tell them the truth — four men are coming in my employer's house — and they would come, and the men outside would see the cars and leave, and she would be on a report, and her name and the photo from her driver's license would be on a report, and somewhere an office would flag it, and the rest of her life would be a different length than it had been ten minutes ago. She did not call. She registered, distantly, that she hadn't called. She registered that she had known, coming up the stairs, that she was not going to call. She filed this away to think about later, if there was a later. She went down to the kitchen. She pulled the good eight-inch from the block and tested the edge against her thumbnail. It shaved. Mr. Mercer kept his knives sharp; one of the things she liked about him. She took the paring knife too, four-inch, and slid it handle-down into the back waistband of her jeans where the henley would cover it. She killed the kitchen light. She killed the living-room lamp. She left the television on, low, because a dark house without sound would tell them something the television wouldn't. The doorbell rang. She stood very still in the dark hallway and watched the frosted glass beside the door. A shape on the porch. Then the bell again, polite, like a neighbor. Then the sound of a man walking slowly around the side of the house, footsteps in the mulch, casual, in no hurry at all. From the back of the house, glass broke.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable discipline and craft. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Prose Style** The prose is clean and controlled. The model largely avoids the "night pressed in" type of atmospheric cliché it promised to cut. The sentence rhythms are varied well — short declarative sentences alternate with longer observational ones without feeling mechanical. "Something in her stomach went cold and familiar, the way a room you haven't been in for years is still the shape of itself in the dark" is the chapter's most ambitious metaphor, and it mostly earns itself — the spatial memory of a room mapping onto bodily dread is genuinely apt rather than decorative. It's not a perfect image (rooms don't quite "be the shape of themselves" in a way that fully resolves), but it's working hard and mostly landing. The reportorial flatness the plan called for is present: "The engine cut. Nobody got out for another minute after that. Then the driver's door, then the passenger, then the back two almost together." This is effective. It reads like surveillance, which is exactly right for Wren's psychology. **Character Work** Wren's decision not to call 911 is handled with exactly the right touch — she notices it about herself, files it away, doesn't editorialize. This is the plan's intention executed well: showing her moral complexity without announcing it. The detail about Mr. Mercer's sharp knives and her liking him for it is a small, excellent character beat — it reveals her observational nature and her capacity for small appreciations even in crisis. Maddie is very well-rendered. Her flat "Are they here for us or for you?" lands perfectly and is consistent with her profile — watchful, testing, too old for her age. The tablet request is a genuine moment of lightness that doesn't undercut the tension. Eli's Gary-mediated compliance ("Gary's scared. He wants to go with you") is both tactically smart on Wren's part and emotionally true to a six-year-old. **Dialogue** The dialogue is spare and functional, which is the right call. Maddie's repetition of instructions ("Attic. Stay. Don't come down. Halvorsens' in the morning.") is a nice procedural beat. The exchange is efficient without being clipped to the point of unreality. The "Can I bring my tablet?" line is the chapter's best dialogue moment — it's funny, true, and characterizes Maddie without a word of description. **Plot and Pacing** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: watching the crew arrive, recognizing the walk, getting the kids to the attic, the deliberate non-call to 911, arming herself, the doorbell, the glass breaking. The pacing is well-managed — the chapter earns its ending beat because the preceding stillness has been so carefully maintained. The detail about the walk — "the way he led with his right shoulder like he was about to turn into a door that wasn't there" — is specific and good. It does the work of establishing Wren's past without exposition. **Weaknesses** The chapter is quite faithful to its plan, perhaps too faithful — there's little that surprises beyond what was already mapped. The plan called for disrupting expectations, but this chapter is largely executing setup, so that's somewhat appropriate. The "Halvorsens'" instruction is a small piece of dramatic irony given what we know from the plan about Mr. Halvorsen's fate, which is well-placed. The stepfather teaching her to watch without putting it in words is a slight tell-don't-show wobble — the narration explains the lesson rather than just showing the behavior. It's minor. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "rude when it needs to be" voice promised — it's controlled and precise but not particularly mean or raw. This is a small gap between intention and execution. Overall this is confident, disciplined work that serves the story's larger architecture well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — Denny The glass went in two pieces — the first a short hard snap, the second a longer slumping fall as whoever it was reached through and swept the shards out of the frame so they wouldn't catch on his sleeve. Somebody who had done it before. Somebody who hadn't, in her experience, done it very many times. She was already in the mudroom. The mudroom was a narrow run between the kitchen and the back door, hooks on one wall, a bench with cubbies on the other, a washer-dryer stack at the far end, the back door itself centered. There was a window in the back door and that was the window that had gone. She had tucked herself in against the washer with the eight-inch held low along her thigh and her breath pushed down into her stomach where it wouldn't show. She had been expecting the big one. The tall one who rolled his neck. That was how she had placed him in her head — first through the door, because the big ones always were, because the crew sent the body in first and the brain in second. She had placed him in her head and she had placed the knife accordingly, up high under the jaw, because going for the body on someone that size was wasted work. It wasn't him. It was a skinny kid with tattooed knuckles, a cheap-cologne smell that reached her before he did, a black hoodie, a grin already on his face for no reason she could see. He came through the doorframe sideways because he was narrow enough to and because he was quick. He was quick. That was the first thing she understood, and the second thing she understood was that she had miscalibrated, and the third thing she understood was that her body had already committed to the jaw-strike geometry of a different, larger man and was wrong about everything. He saw her and said, "Aw, she's cute." She went for him anyway. The knife went where she had sent it and met nothing — he was not where a bigger man would have been, he was lower, faster, under the swing, and her wrist passed over his shoulder and she was fouled against her own momentum. He came up into her with his forearm under her chin and drove her back into the washer hard enough that the whole stack rocked and a detergent bottle on top fell and bounced. The knife was still in her hand. She felt that. She told herself to remember that. He got a fistful of her hair and yanked and her feet came off the ground for a second and she thought, clearly, *he's going to kill me by accident.* Not by plan. He was going to get too excited and break something he wasn't supposed to break. She kicked the side of his knee. She got it clean. He buckled and she came down with him and they went to the floor together in the narrow space between the bench and the washer and he was still grinning — actually grinning — and saying, "Hey. Hey. Hey, don't — *hey* — " in a voice that was almost laughing. He got a hand on the wrist of her knife hand. This was the part she didn't like. He was stronger than her. Not by a lot. By enough. She felt him start to peel her wrist open and she felt her fingers start to go and she let her arm go slack for half a second, a half second she counted, and he overcommitted — pulled where he didn't need to pull — and she brought her knee up between them and rolled her hip and came out the other side of him, ugly, scraping an elbow on the bench, and now he was the one half-under the washer and she was the one with leverage. She couldn't get the eight-inch around. The angle was wrong. She reached behind her, left-handed, and got the paring knife. He saw it. He had a half-second to see it. In that half-second his face did something that wasn't quite fear — it was closer to surprise, like a kid who has been told a rule he didn't know existed. His grin stayed on his mouth but his eyes left it. She put the paring knife into the side of his neck under the jaw. It went in easier than she remembered. She had forgotten how little resistance there was when you hit the soft part between the muscle and the bone. She had forgotten and she was grateful, for one second, that she had forgotten, and then she was ashamed of being grateful. He made a sound. It wasn't a scream. It was a noise she didn't have a word for — wet, surprised, almost polite, like someone clearing his throat at a funeral. He kicked one leg out and his heel hit the washer twice, *bam bam*, before she got her forearm across his mouth and held it there. His eyes found hers and held on and then stopped holding on and then were just eyes. It took longer than it should have. It always did. When he was finished she pulled the paring knife out and wiped it on his hoodie and sat back on her heels and looked at her hand. Her hand was shaking. It was shaking enough that she watched it for a full breath, the way you watch something that is not yours, and then she set the knife on the floor and put her hand flat on the tile and pressed down until the shaking went into the tile and stopped. She stood up. There was blood on her henley from collarbone to hem on the left side. There was blood on the washer. There was blood on the detergent bottle, which was still bouncing in a slow diminishing arc at the edge of the bench. The house was quiet. The others had heard. They had to have heard the washer, at least. She listened for movement and got none, and that was worse than movement, because it meant they were standing still and listening for her the same way. Then a voice came from somewhere in the front of the house — not loud, conversational, a man in a living room calling to a woman in the kitchen. "Wren. Sweetheart. We just want to talk." She didn't know the voice. She knew the word *sweetheart.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The model promised a fight that goes wrong in a non-cool way, a protagonist who miscalibrates, and a kill that costs something — and it delivers all three without flinching or prettifying. The opening is strong: the glass breaking in two pieces, the detail about sweeping shards so they don't catch on a sleeve, establishes competence in the intruder while grounding us in physical specificity. The prose is doing real work here — not decorating, but informing. The central reversal — expecting Marco, getting Denny — is handled well. The miscalibration is credible and the consequences are immediate. The fight choreography is unusually good for fiction: it tracks spatially, the leverage shifts are believable, and crucially, Wren wins ugly rather than cleverly. The detail of her arm going slack for half a second, counting it, exploiting the overcommitment — this reads as genuine rather than action-movie slick. The prose style is largely what was promised: short sentences, flat reportorial beats, occasional sprawl. "He was going to get too excited and break something he wasn't supposed to break" is a genuinely good line — it characterizes Denny, characterizes Wren's analytical mind under pressure, and carries dark irony. The "almost polite, like someone clearing his throat at a funeral" simile for the death sound is the chapter's best moment: specific, unexpected, and earned rather than decorative. The moment of shame — "she was grateful, for one second, that she had forgotten, and then she was ashamed of being grateful" — is exactly the kind of psychological honesty the plan called for. It doesn't editorialize; it just reports the sequence of feelings. The hand-shaking detail and pressing it flat against the tile is good. The detergent bottle "still bouncing in a slow diminishing arc" is a nice piece of observed detail that earns its place. The ending lands. "She didn't know the voice. She knew the word sweetheart." This is the chapter's closing beat and it works — economical, ominous, character-revealing without explanation. Weaknesses: The chapter is almost too clean in its execution. The plan called for something that "almost gets her killed" and while the fight is genuinely dangerous, it never quite reaches the level of existential terror the plan implied. The "grin that doesn't mean anything" characterization of Denny is present but thin — he's more a physical problem than a person, which is partly intentional but means his death lands less hard than it might. The line "it went in easier than she remembered" is good but the reflection on having forgotten is slightly over-explained — the shame beat works, but the "grateful for one second" construction is a hair too neat. The dialogue is minimal but effective. "Aw, she's cute" and "Hey. Hey. Hey, don't — hey —" are exactly right for Denny: the grin in the voice, the almost-laughing quality, the rule-he-didn't-know-existed face. Cope's closing line is appropriately chilling. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the surprise of Denny first, the real danger of the fight, the ugly win, the shaking hand, the silence from the others, the voice from the front of the house. The paring knife from the plan is used correctly. The chapter ends exactly where planned. One minor concern: "She had forgotten how little resistance there was when you hit the soft part between the muscle and the bone" — this is slightly clinical in a way that works for Wren's character but edges toward the kind of competence-porn the plan was trying to avoid. It's a fine line and the shame beat immediately after corrects it. Overall this is a strong, disciplined chapter that earns its darkness without wallowing, executes its plan faithfully, and maintains a consistent, effective voice throughout. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — Neighbor She did not answer. The voice had come from the front of the house and she stood in the mudroom in the dark and let it sit on the air until it was gone. Answering was what he wanted. He was putting a line out to see where she pulled from. She did not pull. She stepped over Denny without looking at him and went into the kitchen low. The television in the living room was still on, muted blue light pulsing into the hall. She kept the island between her and the front of the house and went to the breaker panel in the pantry and opened it one-handed and put her fingers on the main and stopped. No. The TV was cover. Without the TV the house became a listening chamber and he would hear her moving. Leave the power. She closed the panel. She went back through the kitchen and up the back stairs. The back stairs were narrow and carpeted and came up behind the laundry closet on the second floor, between Eli's room and the bathroom. She went up on the outside edges of the treads where they were tight against the stringer and wouldn't creak. Her mother's trick. Her mother had known every quiet place in every floor of every house they'd lived in and she had shown Wren, not like a lesson, just walking through rooms saying *not here, not here, here's fine.* At the top she crouched. She listened. The front of the house was where the voice had been. The voice had come from near the foyer, she thought, maybe the bottom of the main stairs. That man had a gun or he didn't. She had to assume he did. Denny hadn't. Denny had come in with hands. That meant either Denny was the throwaway or the crew wasn't heavy on firearms or both. She filed it. She moved down the upstairs hall toward the landing. The landing was where the main stairs came up and where the attic hatch was and where — she saw this before she understood she was seeing it — a figure stood with its back to her. A figure with a flashlight. The flashlight was pointed down at the carpet, not up, not swinging, and that was the thing that got her moving before her brain caught up. A man clearing a house swept a flashlight. This one was held down like somebody who didn't want to wake a kid. She crossed the ten feet between them without sound. She had the paring knife in her right hand because the paring knife was what was in her right hand. She put her left hand over his mouth from behind and she put the paring knife into the side of his neck in the same place she had put it into Denny's, because that was the place she knew now, and he made a small noise against her palm and his knees went. He was light. That was what she noticed first. He was light in a way Denny had not been light. Denny had been a hundred and sixty pounds of quick. This man was less than that and softer and his shoulders under her hand were thin and bony and the robe — the *robe* — She knew before she turned him. She held him against her chest and let him down slow because she had taught herself, a long time ago, not to let bodies fall. Bodies falling made noise. She went to her knees with him and laid him on his back on the carpet of the upstairs hall and the flashlight rolled out of his hand and the beam went across his face and she saw him. Mr. Halvorsen from next door. White hair. The robe she had seen him in once in August, watering his tomatoes at dusk. Slippers. One slipper had come off on the stairs, she saw now, looking back; it sat on the third step up from the landing like something a child had dropped. His eyes were open and he was trying to say something and nothing was coming out except a soft wet sound and a lot of blood, a great deal of blood, more blood than she would have thought a man his age had in him. His hand came up and found her wrist and held on. Not hard. Just held. She said, "I'm sorry." She said it out loud. She heard herself say it. His hand stayed on her wrist for maybe six seconds and then it didn't. She sat back on her heels on the hallway carpet and looked at him. The flashlight beam was still pointed at the wainscoting and the wainscoting was lit up in a warm clean circle that had nothing to do with anything. She reached out and clicked the flashlight off. Then she took her hand off it because her hand had left a print on the metal in Denny's blood, and there was nothing to be done about that, and she registered it and filed it and moved on. She did not throw up. She wanted to. She wanted to very badly. She swallowed twice and breathed through her nose and the wanting moved down into her stomach and stayed there like something she would carry for a long time. She put her hands under his arms and dragged him across the hall to the linen closet. He was not heavy. His heels made two long smears on the carpet and she looked at the smears and thought, *that's fine, that's fine, that was always going to happen.* She opened the closet door and pulled him in and folded his legs in after him and closed the door. The towels on the shelf were folded with the rounded edges out. Mrs. Mercer folded towels like that. Wren took one off the top of the stack and wiped her hands on it, both sides, and then put the towel in the closet with him and shut the door again. She stood up. From downstairs the voice came again, patient, pleasant, like a man calling for a cat. "Wren? You okay up there?" She stood in the hallway in the dark with her palms still tacky and the paring knife down at her side and she understood, with a clarity that had not been there before Mr. Halvorsen, that she was going to kill every one of them who did not run. She went down the main stairs.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes one of the most difficult tasks in pulp fiction: making an accidental killing land with genuine moral weight without either sentimentalizing it or brushing past it. The model largely succeeds. The prose is controlled and purposeful. The decision to have Wren move on the figure before her brain catches up is psychologically precise — it's the trained body acting before the thinking mind, which is exactly right for this character. The detail of the flashlight being held downward rather than sweeping — "like somebody who didn't want to wake a kid" — is the kind of specific, earned observation that makes the horror of the mistake feel inevitable rather than contrived. The reader understands why she moved. That's the cruelest part. The Halvorsen paragraph — "He was light. That was what she noticed first" — is the chapter's best moment. The physical detail of lightness as the first register of wrongness is genuinely good writing. It's not poetic in a showy way; it's the body's knowledge arriving before the mind's. The slipper on the third step is excellent: concrete, specific, and devastating without announcing itself as devastating. The mother's trick with the stairs — "not here, not here, here's fine" — is a brief but effective piece of characterization that deepens Wren without stopping the chapter to explain her. It's the right length and the right placement. The towel detail is strong. "Mrs. Mercer folded towels like that" — the domestic specificity against the horror of what she's doing with the towel is exactly the kind of tonal contrast the plan called for. The chapter registers the wrongness of the act through the wrongness of the detail without editorializing. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the line "she understood, with a clarity that had not been there before Mr. Halvorsen, that she was going to kill every one of them who did not run" is the one moment where the prose tells us what it should trust us to feel. The chapter has already shown us this shift; naming it is slightly redundant and slightly too clean for a story that's otherwise resisting clean articulation. The plan specifically noted "the prose should register this without saying it" — and the model almost achieves this, then undercuts it with that final explicit statement. The "Wren? You okay up there?" callback is effective — the leader's pleasantness against what she's just done is genuinely unsettling. The chapter ends on action rather than image, which is the right call. Dialogue is minimal and functional. "I'm sorry" said aloud — the character hearing herself say it — is a small, precise beat. The leader's voice is appropriately chilling in its casualness. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the dark hallway, the figure, the mistake, the linen closet, the folded towel, the shift in Wren. The plan's instruction to give Halvorsen "a paragraph of life before he dies" is honored — the August tomatoes, the robe, the slipper — though it's distributed across the chapter rather than front-loaded, which actually works better. The one structural note: the chapter plan called for the prose to "register this without saying it" regarding Wren's transformation. The final explicit statement is the chapter's one significant failure to execute its own intention. It's not bad writing, but it's less good than what surrounds it. Overall this is a strong chapter — disciplined, dark, and honest about what it's doing. The accidental killing lands. The character is consistent. The prose earns its moments without reaching for them. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 — Ray The main stairs came down into the foyer and the foyer opened into the kitchen on the right and the living room on the left. She came down on the outside edges again and stopped three from the bottom and listened. Living room: empty. The TV light alone. Kitchen: a man at the island. She could see him through the doorway at an angle, just his hands and the near edge of his shoulder. The hands were flat on the marble like someone waiting to be served. She watched them for a ten-count. They did not move. The fingers did not drum. He was not reaching under the counter. He was sitting still on purpose so she would see him sitting still. She came down the last three steps and crossed the foyer and stood in the kitchen doorway with the paring knife at her hip. He was in his forties. Plain face. Windbreaker zipped halfway. Keys on a carabiner clipped through his belt loop. He had turned on the light over the range, not the main lights — a dim warm bulb over the stove. He had done this, she understood, so she could see him without being inside the light herself. A small courtesy. He saw her and did not move his hands. "Hey," he said. She didn't answer. "I'm Ray. I'm gonna put my hands on the counter, okay? I'm gonna keep 'em there. You and me are gonna have a conversation like adults." "Your hands are already on the counter." "Yeah. They're gonna stay there." "Where's the other one?" "Basement, last I knew. Cope's in the basement. Marco was coming up the main. I don't know where Marco is right now. I'm not lying to you about that." "Denny's in the mudroom." Ray exhaled through his nose. "Figured." "You knew it was gonna be him." "I knew he was gonna go first through the door because that's what he does. Did. Yeah." She took a step into the kitchen. He watched her feet, not her face. That was smart. Faces lied; feet told you what someone was about to do. "Talk," she said. "I'm out." "Sure." "I mean it. I'm driving tonight. That's the deal. I drove them here and I'm supposed to drive them back, that's the whole — that's what I'm here for. I didn't come in with hands. Check me if you want, I got a folding knife in my pocket and nothing else, you can have the knife." "I don't want your knife." "Okay." She came another step in. She could see the whole kitchen now. Nobody under the island. The pantry door was open the way she had left it. No boots showing under the pantry. "Why'd you sit down," she said. "Because I was gonna get killed if I didn't." "Correct." "Yeah." "Keep talking." "Cope's not right. I'm telling you this because it's true and also because it's the only thing I got that's worth something to you. He's been wrong for a year. He volunteered for this because somebody told him if he brought you in he'd get moved up, and he believed it, and I don't think the person who told him meant it. I think they sent him because he'd go." "How many of you know my name." Ray's hands were still flat. "He does. Marco doesn't. Denny didn't. I — I know the one he calls you." "Which one." He said a name. Not loud. It landed in the room like a dropped cup. She did not react. She had taught herself this a long time ago and she was grateful, now, that she had. Her face stayed where it was. Her hand on the knife stayed where it was. Inside her chest something cold moved a quarter inch and stopped. "Nobody's called me that in four years," she said. "I know." "Who told him." "I don't know. I don't. I'd tell you if I did. I'm telling you everything I got." "You have a daughter." "Yeah." "How old." "Twelve." "Name." "Kaylee." "You're lying about the name." "I'm not. I'm not lying about the kid and I'm not lying about the name. Everybody says they got a kid and I really got one. She lives with her mom in Reno. She thinks I sell industrial parts." "What do you sell." "Whatever somebody needs moved." "Have you ever hurt a kid." "No." "Think about it before you answer." "I already thought about it. No." She looked at him for a long moment. He looked back only as much as he had to. His eyes kept finding the range hood above his head and coming back to her and finding the range hood again. She recognized the behavior. It was what people did when they were telling the truth and hated how it sounded. "Walk out the front," she said. He didn't move. "Go," she said. "You're not gonna — " "If you turn around I will. If you come back I will. If I ever see you again, Ray, anywhere, a gas station in another state, I will. You go home to Reno and you tell your daughter you sell industrial parts and you keep selling them, whatever that means, and you stay out of my weather." "Yeah." "Get up slow." He got up slow. He kept his hands visible the whole way. He walked past her with a yard of space and did not turn his back until he had to, at the front door, and then he turned it and opened the door and stepped out onto the porch and pulled the door shut behind him with a quietness that was its own apology. She went to the window beside the door and watched him walk to the Tahoe. He walked like a man who was trying not to hurry and failing a little. He got in. He started it. He pulled away from the curb without the headlights on and turned them on at the end of the block. She stood at the window and watched until the taillights were gone. She knew, watching them go, that she had just let a man who did this for a living walk back out into the world to do it for a living. She knew what that meant. She filed it with Mr. Halvorsen and the towel and the smears on the carpet, on the shelf inside her where these things went. From the laundry room, at the back of the house, she heard a man crying.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a taut, controlled piece of writing that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter's central scene — Wren and Ray at the kitchen island — is the whole chapter, and it earns its space. The writing is disciplined. The prose is spare without being skeletal, and the author resists the temptation to over-explain or over-poeticize. The detail of Ray turning on the range hood light rather than the main lights is excellent: it's a small, specific gesture that does real character work without announcing itself. The observation that he watches her feet rather than her face, and Wren's recognition of this as smart, is similarly efficient — it tells us about both characters simultaneously. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It has the off-rhythm quality the plan called for. Ray's lines feel like a man who has rehearsed this conversation in the car and is now delivering it under pressure — slightly too prepared, slightly too calm, but not implausibly so. "I got a kid. I know everybody says that. I really got one." lands exactly as it should. The exchange about the daughter's name — Wren accusing him of lying, Ray insisting he isn't — is a nice piece of psychological texture. It shows Wren testing him, and it shows Ray holding his ground on the one thing he won't let her take from him. The name-drop moment — Ray saying the name she used to go by — is handled with real restraint. "It landed in the room like a dropped cup" is the one metaphor in the chapter, and it's a good one: domestic, sudden, not dramatic. The decision to leave the name itself unspoken on the page (consistent with the plan) is correct. Wren's non-reaction, and the narration noting she taught herself this, is efficient backstory delivery that doesn't feel like backstory delivery. The moral weight of letting Ray go is handled well. The plan called for Wren to know she's making a bad choice and to file it alongside Mr. Halvorsen, and the chapter delivers: "She filed it with Mr. Halvorsen and the towel and the smears on the carpet, on the shelf inside her where these things went." This is the chapter's most ambitious sentence and it mostly works — "the shelf inside her" is a slightly tired metaphor, but the accumulation of specific objects (the towel, the smears) saves it from being purely abstract. The ending — the sound of a man crying from the laundry room — is a clean, effective transition. It doesn't try to be more than it is. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the interrogation structure is familiar. Question, answer, question, answer. The plan called for dialogue that's "sometimes dumb, sometimes off-rhythm, not always a line," and while the dialogue is good, it's also quite clean and functional — it doesn't have the roughness or the occasional non-sequitur the plan promised. Ray is perhaps a little too articulate, a little too perfectly positioned as the moral counterweight. The plan noted he should be the man who lives and that this is "the weight she carries out," and the chapter does establish this, but Ray himself is slightly too neatly drawn — his "I got a kid" speech, his careful hands, his quiet exit. He's convincing but not surprising. The plan also called for Wren to "not know why" she lets him go and to tell herself it's because he didn't do anything yet and know that's a lie. The chapter delivers this, but briefly — it could have sat with the ambiguity a beat longer. The self-knowledge is there but it's stated rather than dramatized. Coherence is strong. The chapter fits cleanly into the larger narrative, references Denny's death naturally, and sets up Marco and Cope without over-explaining. The physical geography of the house is maintained. No purple prose. No forced metaphors beyond the dropped cup, which lands. The prose is occasionally flat in a way that's intentional and correct for the voice. The chapter is not amateurish. It's the work of someone who knows what they're doing. The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully — Ray's hands on the counter, the daughter named Kaylee, the front door exit, the watching of taillights, the sound of crying at the end. The plan's beats are all hit. Overall: a strong chapter, perhaps the most controlled in the sequence. Its weakness is that it's almost too controlled — the plan promised some roughness and surprise that the chapter doesn't quite deliver. Ray is a little too perfectly the "tired, dry, smart" man the profile describes. But these are minor complaints against a chapter that does its job with real craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 — Marco The crying was quiet. That was the thing about it. It wasn't loud enough to be a trick and it wasn't soft enough to be private. It was a man trying not to cry and failing at a low volume, the way a man cries when he has been crying for a while and has given up expecting it to stop. She moved along the back hall toward the laundry room with the eight-inch in her right hand and the paring knife in the back of her waistband again. She had rinsed the paring knife in the kitchen sink while she watched Ray's taillights. She had done it without thinking about it and only afterward registered that she had done it, and the registering had made her feel something she set aside to feel later, if later came. The laundry room was at the end of the back hall, past the mudroom where Denny was. The door was open. The light inside was on — the overhead fluorescent, the cheap one that buzzed. She could see his shadow moving across the light under the door crack before she got close enough to see him. A big shadow. Slow. She stopped three feet from the doorway. She listened. The crying. His breath. A small sound that she took a second to place and then placed: he was sniffing, trying to clear his nose, quietly, again and again. Like a man on a pew. She stepped into the doorway. He was standing in the middle of the little room with his back to her and his head down and he was not looking for her at all. He was just standing there. His shoulders were as wide as the space between the washer and the folding counter. He had a tire iron in his right hand, down at his thigh, and his left hand was up at his face, under his nose, and he was wiping his nose with the back of his wrist in a slow methodical way. She said, "Turn around." He turned around. His face was red and wet and his eyes were very small in it. He was older than she had expected. Not by a lot. By five or six years. The kind of five or six years that showed in the way a body held itself more than in the face. His mouth had a shape to it that wasn't a sneer and wasn't a grimace. It was the mouth of a man who had known, walking into a room, that he was not going to walk out of it the same, and who had walked in anyway. He said, "I'm sorry." "Put it down." "I can't." "Put it down, Marco." "I can't. I'm sorry. I can't." He came at her. He came at her slow for the first step and fast for the second, and she was already moving back out of the doorway because she had known he would, because the *I'm sorry* had been a man telling her what he was about to do and asking forgiveness for it in advance.
