She backed into the hall and he came through the doorway sideways because he had to and swung the tire iron low, at her knee, and she got the leg back and the iron passed and hit the doorframe and took a chunk of molding off the jamb. He was strong. That part she had been right about. The way he moved after a swing was not the way a big slow man moved; it was the way a fighter moved, a man who had once made his living with his hands and had not forgotten the geometry. He recovered the iron and stepped into her and she had to give ground, and giving ground took her back down the hall into the kitchen and the kitchen was where it went wrong. She tried to put the island between them. He was faster around the island than she had counted on. He cut the corner. He got a hand on the collar of her henley and she felt the seam give at the shoulder and then his other hand, the one with the iron, came around and she got her forearm up and the iron hit her forearm instead of her temple and the forearm opened along the bone from elbow to wrist in a long bright line that did not hurt yet and would hurt in about four seconds. He drove her back. She hit the range with her hip and her hand went out for balance and found the handle of a skillet on the back burner and closed on it without her deciding, and she swung the skillet up into the side of his head with everything she had in her shoulder. It was a cast-iron skillet. Mrs. Mercer seasoned it on Sundays. It connected with his temple and the sound it made was not a sound she wanted. He went sideways into the refrigerator and the refrigerator rocked and a magnet fell and a child's drawing came with it. He stayed on his feet. He stayed on his feet and turned back toward her with his eyes not quite finding her and the tire iron still in his hand and she understood that the skillet had been the best hit she had in her and it had not been enough. She hit him again. She hit him in the same place and this time his legs went and he came down against the cabinets and slid. The tire iron left his hand and hit the floor and rolled under the oven. He was breathing. He was looking up at her. His mouth was working around words that weren't coming. She hit him again. With the skillet. In the head. He stopped looking up at her. She hit him once more after that. She did not need to. She registered, as the skillet came down, that she did not need to, and she brought it down anyway, and she felt, in the second her arm was at the top of the swing, something that was not satisfaction and was not anger and was not relief. It was close to all three and was none of them. It was the thing she had carried out of Yuma and pretended she had left behind. She set the skillet on the stove. She sat down on the kitchen floor next to Marco with her back against the cabinet and she looked at her forearm. It was open from the elbow to the wrist and the blood was coming in a steady unhurried pulse that told her she had missed the artery by a finger's width. She pulled the dishtowel off the oven handle and put it against her arm and held it there with her other hand and her teeth. From the vent above the stove, faint, carried down through the ductwork from the attic two floors up, she heard Eli crying. She sat there another five seconds and then she got up and went to the basement door.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The model set out to write a fight scene that goes wrong in a non-cool way, to show Wren doing something she doesn't need to do (the extra hit), and to register the thing she carried out of Yuma without naming it. It delivers on all three. The opening paragraph on Marco's crying is the chapter's best piece of writing: "It wasn't loud enough to be a trick and it wasn't soft enough to be private." This is precise and earns its place. The description of him as "a man trying not to cry and failing at a low volume, the way a man cries when he has been crying for a while and has given up expecting it to stop" is genuinely good — specific, unsentimental, and character-revealing without being showy. The fight choreography is competent and grounded. The model avoids the trap of making it stylish. The cast-iron skillet is a better choice than a knife because it's domestic and ugly and requires repeated effort. The detail that "Mrs. Mercer seasoned it on Sundays" is a small, well-placed intrusion of the ordinary into the violent — it works without being precious about it. The moral ugliness is handled correctly. Wren hits Marco after he's down, registers that she doesn't need to, and does it anyway. The prose then names what she feels without romanticizing it: "It was close to all three and was none of them. It was the thing she had carried out of Yuma and pretended she had left behind." This is the chapter's thematic payload and it lands cleanly. The model doesn't flinch, as promised in the plan. The dialogue is minimal and functional. "I'm sorry. I can't." is exactly right for Marco — it fulfills his character profile (the apology in advance, the man who knows what he's about to do and does it anyway). The exchange is brief enough not to overstay its welcome. The forearm wound is handled well — "the blood was coming in a steady unhurried pulse that told her she had missed the artery by a finger's width" — clinical, not dramatic, which is the right register. The ending with Eli crying through the vent is effective. It doesn't editorialize. It just places the child's sound against what she's done and lets the reader sit with it. Weaknesses: The chapter is slightly mechanical in its fight sequencing — the back-and-forth of the struggle reads competently but not brilliantly. Some of the physical choreography ("she got the leg back and the iron passed") is functional rather than vivid. The line "a child's drawing came with it" when the magnet falls is a small reach toward pathos that is slightly too deliberate — it's the one moment where the prose is trying a little too hard to underline the domestic-versus-violent contrast. The phrase "the geometry" in reference to Marco's fighting instincts is a mild affectation. These are minor complaints against an otherwise disciplined chapter. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Marco is crying, he says "I'm sorry" and comes anyway, the fight is the worst of the night, she gets her forearm opened, she hits him after he's down, she sits on the floor, she hears Eli through the vents, she goes to the basement door. Every beat is executed. The character is consistent with her profile. The darkness is well-earned rather than gratuitous. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 — Cope The basement door was in the back hall between the laundry room and the powder room. It was already open an inch. He had done that. She stood in front of it with the dishtowel tied around her forearm by its corners, pulled tight with her teeth and her good hand, and she put the eight-inch on the hallway sideboard because the eight-inch was too long for a staircase. She kept the paring knife. She went down. The stairs were carpeted and turned once at a landing. She took them slow. Light from below, not overhead — a lamp. He had turned on a lamp. She could smell him before she saw him. Sunflower shells and Old Spice aftershave and something sourer underneath, maybe sweat, maybe just old man. She came off the last stair into the finished half of the basement. He was sitting in the Mercers' recliner. The recliner was angled at the foot of the stairs so that whoever came down came down into his line of sight. He was sitting in it with his legs crossed at the knee and a paper towel folded on his thigh and a pistol resting on the paper towel, not pointed at her, not in his hand, just sitting there where she could see it. His other hand had sunflower seeds in it. He was working them. One at a time. The shells went into a second folded paper towel on the armrest. He was smaller than she had expected. Softer. Fifty-some, grey at the temples, a Members Only jacket zipped to the sternum. A face you'd forget at a gas station. He said, "There you are." She didn't answer. "Sit down. There's the ottoman." "No." "Suit yourself." He cracked a seed. He spit the shell into the paper towel. He did not look at the pistol and did not look at her hand with the paring knife. He looked at her face, patient, the way a man at a bus stop looks at the bus he expects. "I knew your mother a little," he said. She stayed where she was. "Not well. I want to be clear about that. I met her twice. Somebody's birthday. Ramirez's kid, I think. You would've been nine, ten. I talked to her about a tie. I had on a tie I didn't like and she said something nice about it and I remembered that because I was having a bad day and she didn't know it and she was kind about the tie anyway. That's all. I'm not claiming I knew her." She said, "Is this where you tell me she talked about me." "No. She didn't. I don't know what she talked about when she wasn't talking about a tie." "Good." He nodded like she had scored a point fairly. He cracked another seed. "I'm not here to have a conversation with you about your feelings, sweetheart. I don't have the time and neither do you. I'm just telling you why I recognized the name. When it came up I recognized the name and I put my hand up for it." "You volunteered." "Yeah." "For a promotion." "That's right." "You think they're gonna give it to you." He considered that. He cracked a seed. "I think there's a chance," he said. "I think the chance isn't as big as the man who told me implied. I think I came anyway because I'm fifty-one and the chance of anything is smaller every year and a small chance is still a chance. I think you already knew all of that before I said it." "Yeah." "So." He uncrossed his legs and recrossed them the other way and picked up the pistol off the paper towel. He did it slow. He did not point it at her. He held it loose in his lap with the muzzle at the carpet. He said her other name. Not loud. He said it the way he'd said *tie.* A small domestic word he happened to know. He said it and watched her face to see what happened to it and nothing did, and he nodded, a little, like he had expected that and was confirming it. "I don't need you to come quiet," he said. "I want to be clear. I'm not asking." "Okay." "I just wanted to say the name out loud once before we did this. I've had it in my head for a while." "Okay." "All right." He raised the pistol. She was already moving. The basement was small and the recliner was four steps from the foot of the stairs and four steps was nothing, four steps was a held breath, and she closed two of them before the first shot and the first shot went past her left ear and into the drywall behind her and the second shot came as she was inside his arm and it went through the meat of her thigh, the outside of it, and she felt it go in and out, a bright punched feeling, and her leg kept working because adrenaline is a liar that tells useful lies. She got the paring knife into his hand that had the pistol. Through the back of it, into the meat between the knuckles, and he made a noise and the pistol went sideways and she got her other hand on his throat. He was not a big man. She had been a big man's daughter and she knew what a big man felt like under a hand and he did not feel like one. She put her weight down through her palm into the soft under his jaw and she leaned and she did not let up. He hit her. He hit her in the ribs twice and in the side of the head once and the hits were the hits of a man whose hands had mostly held paperwork for ten years. She took them. She kept leaning. He tried to say something. She did not let him. It took longer than Denny and longer than Mr. Halvorsen and longer than Marco. It took a long time. He looked at her the whole way through and his eyes did not go to fear, which surprised her, they went to something more like disappointment, like a man who had bet on a horse and watched it come in second, and at the end his hand came up one more time and touched her wrist, not hard, almost the way Mr. Halvorsen had, and then it stopped touching her wrist. She stayed on top of him a long moment after he stopped. Then she got off him. She sat on the ottoman and looked at her leg. The jeans were black with it. She untied the dishtowel from her forearm and retied it around her thigh above the hole, high and tight, and used her teeth again. The forearm started bleeding again. She didn't have another towel. The paper towel with the sunflower shells was still folded on the armrest. She left it there. She got up and went to the stairs.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with precise, functional prose — the detail about the eight-inch knife being "too long for a staircase" is exactly the kind of specific, practical thinking that establishes Wren's competence without glamorizing it. The sensory detail of smelling Cope before seeing him (sunflower shells, Old Spice, sweat) is well-executed and consistent with the character profile's emphasis on his seed-chewing habit. The staging of Cope in the recliner is effective. The pistol resting on the paper towel, not pointed at her — this is a good visual that communicates his confidence and his methodical nature without over-explaining. The Members Only jacket detail lands. The dialogue is where this chapter genuinely excels. Cope's voice is exactly right: patient, a little sad, not villainous in any theatrical way. The tie anecdote is a masterstroke — it's specific, domestic, and wrong in the way the plan promised. "She was kind about the tie anyway" is a line that does real work. The exchange about the promotion is clean and honest: "I think the chance isn't as big as the man who told me implied. I think I came anyway because I'm fifty-one and the chance of anything is smaller every year." This is character writing of genuine quality. It avoids the villain monologue the plan explicitly wanted to cut, while still giving Cope dimensionality. The plan called for Cope to say her real name — "not Wren, the other one" — and the chapter handles this well, keeping the name unspoken on the page as planned, using it as a beat rather than a revelation. The fight sequence is appropriately unglamorous. "Four steps was nothing, four steps was a held breath" is a slightly strained metaphor but not egregiously so. The bullet wound detail — "a bright punched feeling, and her leg kept working because adrenaline is a liar that tells useful lies" — is one of the chapter's best lines. It's earned, not decorative. The plan specified that Cope's death should feel like "finishing a chore" and that she should feel neither good nor bad. The chapter delivers this: "his eyes did not go to fear, which surprised her, they went to something more like disappointment, like a man who had bet on a horse and watched it come in second." This is good. The horse-betting simile is a little familiar but fits the pulp register. The hand touching her wrist at the end, echoing Mr. Halvorsen, is a subtle and effective callback that the plan didn't explicitly specify but that feels right. The ending — sitting on the ottoman, retying the dishtowel, the sunflower shells still on the armrest, getting up and going to the stairs — is exactly the kind of non-resonant, just-true ending the plan called for. It doesn't reach for meaning. It just stops. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: "four steps was a held breath" doesn't quite work as a comparison. The prose occasionally flirts with the kind of rhythmic self-consciousness that can tip into performance — "She took them. She kept leaning." is effective, but there are a few moments where the short-sentence staccato feels slightly mechanical rather than earned. The description of Cope's eyes going to "disappointment" is good but the horse-betting simile that follows it is the chapter's most conventional moment. The chapter is faithful to the plan in nearly every particular: the recliner staging, the sunflower seeds, the name spoken aloud, the gun, the thigh wound, the small basement making the fight possible, the chore-like feeling at the end. The character profiles are honored throughout — Cope is exactly as described, unimpressive and patient and a little sad about his own ambitions. The plan's instruction to "not flinch" from the ugliness is honored. The killing takes a long time. She doesn't feel good. She doesn't feel bad. The prose registers this without announcing it. This is confident, controlled writing that earns its darkness without aestheticizing it excessively. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 — Gary She went up the stairs one at a time with her good hand on the rail and the bad leg trailing. Halfway up she had to stop and breathe with her forehead against the wall. The wall was cool. She stayed there longer than she meant to. She was not going to call a cleanup crew because she did not have a cleanup crew. She had never had one. The number she had carried in her head for four years was a number she had never dialed because dialing it meant being found, and she had chosen, every morning for four years, not to be found. She was going to call 911. She worked on the story while she climbed. Home invasion. Four men. The neighbor heard glass and came to help and was killed in the upstairs hall. The babysitter fought back with a kitchen knife and got lucky. The babysitter's name, on the report, would be Wren Adler because that was the name on her license and the license was real enough to pass the kind of look a small-town detective would give it. After tonight she would not be Wren Adler. She knew that. She was already, standing on the staircase, composing the face she would show a paramedic and deciding what would come off it, later, when she threw the face away. She came up into the kitchen. Marco was where she had left him. She stepped around him. She did not look at him long. In the upstairs hall she stood under the attic hatch and pulled the cord. The folding stairs came down and she called up, not loud, "It's me. It's over." Nothing for a second. Then Maddie's face at the edge of the hatch. Glasses on. Tablet under her arm. She looked down at Wren and saw the leg and the arm and the shirt and did not react to any of it. She climbed down the folding stairs on her own and turned at the bottom and reached up for Eli, who came down after her with Gary pressed against his cheek. Eli saw Wren and said, "You're red." "Yeah, buddy." "A lot." "I know." Maddie said, "Are they gone." "Yeah." "All of them." "Yeah." She said it evenly because Maddie was asking a specific question and deserved a specific answer, and Maddie received the answer the way she had received everything tonight, with a small nod that cost her nothing visible. Wren walked them down the main stairs, slow, her hand on Eli's shoulder for balance as much as for him. She took them past the foyer — there was nothing in the foyer — and into the living room and sat them on the couch and pulled the throw blanket off the back and put it over their laps. She turned the TV off the news channel it had drifted to and found cartoons. She did not remember turning the TV to the news channel. Somebody had, at some point. Maybe her. Maybe him. "I need you to stay here," she said. "I need you to stay on this couch and watch this and not get up. Not for anything. Not to look, not for water, not for the bathroom. If you need the bathroom you tell me and I'll take you." Maddie said, "Okay." Eli said, "Can Gary watch too." "Gary has to watch. Gary's in charge." "Okay." She went to the kitchen. She pulled the tray of cookie dough out of the fridge. She set the oven to three-fifty and waited for it to beep and in the waiting she stood at the sink and washed her hands and arms up to the elbow in water as hot as she could stand. The water went pink and then clear and then pink again from the forearm, which was still going. She retied the dishtowel. She washed her face. She did not look in the window above the sink at her reflection because she did not want to meet her own eyes yet. She put the cookies in. She called 911 at seven-fourteen. She told the dispatcher there had been a home invasion and that men had broken in and that she thought they were all down but she wasn't sure and that there were two children in the living room and that the neighbor had come over to help and the neighbor was upstairs and she didn't think the neighbor had made it. She said this in a voice that shook in the right places. Some of the shake was acting and some of it wasn't and by the end she had lost track of which was which. The dispatcher told her to stay on the line. She stayed on the line. The cookies came out at seven-twenty-two. She took them off the tray with a spatula and put them on a plate and set the plate on the coffee table in front of the kids. Eli took one and blew on it and broke it in half and gave half to Gary. Maddie did not take one. Maddie watched Wren. The first siren came at seven-twenty-six. She set the phone down on the kitchen island still connected and she went to the front door and unlocked it and opened it and stood on the porch with her hands where they could be seen. Two cruisers. Then a third. Then an ambulance. A young officer came up the walk with his hand near his holster but not on it and she told him, in short sentences, what he needed to hear to let the paramedics past her and into the house. They took her arm. They took her leg. They asked about the children and she pointed. Maddie was already telling a second officer, in her flat clear voice, about the men and the attic and the babysitter, and the second officer was writing it down and nodding, and at one point Maddie looked across the room at Wren and held her eyes for two seconds and looked back at the officer and said, "I didn't see anything. I was upstairs." The Mercers arrived at seven-fifty-one. Mrs. Mercer got out of the car screaming her children's names. Mr. Mercer stood on the lawn with his tie loose and his phone at his side and did not move for a long time. In the noise of it Wren stepped off the back of the ambulance with her arm wrapped and her leg wrapped and she told the paramedic she needed a minute and she walked around the side of the house and through the gap in the hedge she had noticed going in twelve hours ago, and nobody stopped her, because nobody was looking at her yet. She walked. Four blocks. Five. The sun was fully up. Her leg was working on borrowed time and she knew it. She sat down on a curb outside a house with a For Sale sign in the yard and put her head between her knees. There was something in her hand. She lifted her head and looked. Gary. The plastic triceratops. She didn't remember taking him. She tried to remember Eli putting him in her hand and couldn't. Somewhere in the couch or the porch or the walk to the ambulance he had pressed the dinosaur into her palm and she had closed her fingers around him without noticing and had carried him out of the house and through the hedge and four blocks down the road. She sat on the curb with Gary in her lap. She looked at him for a while. She did not know what to do with him.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with Wren on the stairs, forehead against the cool wall, and the prose is doing exactly what the plan called for: registering her state without announcing it. "She stayed there longer than she meant to" is the kind of flat, honest sentence that earns its place. The decision not to call a cleanup crew is handled well — the explanation is economical and reveals character (she chose not to be found, every morning, for four years) without over-explaining. The story-construction sequence is one of the chapter's best passages. "She was already, standing on the staircase, composing the face she would show a paramedic and deciding what would come off it, later, when she threw the face away." This is precise and unsettling in the right way — it shows us someone who has a professional relationship with performance, who thinks in terms of faces as tools. It doesn't editorialize. It just shows. The attic retrieval scene is handled with real restraint. Maddie's flat "Are they gone. All of them." and Wren's equally flat "Yeah" is exactly the right register. The plan called for Maddie to ask "Are they gone?" and say "I won't tell" — both are here, and both land. Eli's "You're red" is perfect: a child's observation, not a child's horror, and the repetition "A lot" / "I know" is economical and affecting. The cookie-baking sequence fulfills the plan's stated purpose — the house needs to smell like something else, and Eli will want one — and it works on both levels without the prose underlining either. The detail of the water going "pink and then clear and then pink again" is good, concrete, not overwrought. The decision not to look at her reflection in the window is a small, smart choice: it avoids the cliché of the mirror-confrontation while still gesturing at it. The 911 call is handled well. "She said this in a voice that shook in the right places. Some of the shake was acting and some of it wasn't and by the end she had lost track of which was which." This is the chapter's best line. It captures something true about trauma and performance and the way they blur, and it does so without any of the purple-prose inflation that would ruin it. Maddie's "I didn't see anything. I was upstairs" is the payoff of her character arc — the watchful, lying-for-practice girl who has decided to protect Wren — and it's delivered without fanfare. The two-second eye contact is the right amount of acknowledgment. The ending with Gary is where the plan was most explicit: "Not resonant. Just true." The chapter mostly achieves this. The detail of not remembering taking him, of trying to reconstruct when Eli pressed him into her hand, is good — it captures the dissociation of the aftermath. "She did not know what to do with him" is the right last line: genuinely unresolved, not reaching for meaning, not a poetic image that gestures at theme. However, there are some weaknesses. The chapter is perhaps slightly too clean in its execution — the plan called for something that would leave the reader "a little unsettled by how much they rooted for her," and while the Halvorsen death is referenced (she tells the dispatcher about the neighbor), the chapter doesn't quite make us sit with that weight. The plan said "His death is the worst thing she does tonight and she knows it and the story will not redeem it" — but in this chapter, Halvorsen is reduced to a line in the 911 call. The unredeemed wrongness of it is present but not felt. The prose is consistently controlled. There are no "the night pressed in" sentences, no poetic gestures that don't land. The dialogue is spare and functional throughout. The chapter follows the plan closely — the cookie-baking, the 911 call at 7:14, Maddie's "I won't tell," the escape through the hedge, Gary on the curb. The plan's ending image is delivered faithfully. One minor weakness: "borrowed time" in "Her leg was working on borrowed time" is a cliché that the rest of the prose's discipline makes more visible. It's a small slip. Similarly, "in the noise of it" as a transition is slightly soft. The chapter is not a masterpiece but it is very good work — disciplined, honest, resistant to the easy moves, and faithful to both the plan and the prompt's swagger-with-residue ambition. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of pulp revenge fiction that operates well above the genre's typical floor. The author demonstrates genuine craft across multiple dimensions, though not without some notable weaknesses worth examining critically. STRENGTHS: The prose style is distinctive and controlled. The author employs a clipped, declarative rhythm that suits the material — short sentences that accumulate pressure rather than release it. "She did not hurry. Hurry was how you forgot things." This is economical and effective. The style is consistent across all eight chapters, which is a significant achievement in a long-form piece. Character work is genuinely strong. Wren is established through behavior rather than exposition. Her "walk" through the house in Chapter 1, the detail about taking the Ativan from the medicine cabinet, the way she checks the sliding door latch — these establish her as someone with a specific, practiced history without ever stating it. The backstory (Yuma, the stepfather, the other name) is parceled out with discipline; we get enough to understand her without being told everything. The children are handled with particular skill. Eli's relationship with Gary the triceratops is a genuine piece of characterization — the dinosaur functions as both comic relief and emotional anchor, and the final image of Wren sitting on the curb with Gary in her lap is genuinely earned. Maddie's flat competence ("I didn't see anything. I was upstairs") is chilling and believable for a child who has processed something terrible by going very still. The Mr. Halvorsen sequence is the piece's most daring and successful moment. The mistake — killing the neighbor — is handled without sentimentality or melodrama. The detail of the slipper on the third step, the towel folded with rounded edges out, the way Wren files the moment away "on the shelf inside her where these things went" — this is the kind of moral weight that elevates pulp into something more serious. The author doesn't let Wren off the hook, and doesn't let the reader off the hook either. Ray's scene is well-constructed. The decision to let him go is morally complex and the author acknowledges the complexity without resolving it neatly. "She knew, watching them go, that she had just let a man who did this for a living walk back out into the world to do it for a living." This is honest. Cope's final scene has genuine menace. The sunflower seeds, the Members Only jacket, the paper towel — these are specific details that make him real. His disappointment rather than fear at the end is a nice touch. WEAKNESSES: The "forced poetry" problem appears occasionally. "Something in her stomach went cold and familiar, the way a room you haven't been in for years is still the shape of itself in the dark" — this is reaching. The simile is strained; rooms don't have shapes in the dark in the way the sentence implies. There are a handful of these moments where the author's ambition slightly outruns their execution. The Marco sequence is the weakest chapter. His crying before the fight feels slightly convenient — it establishes him as sympathetic enough that killing him costs something, but the setup is a bit mechanical. The fight itself is competent but the emotional beat of Wren hitting him "once more after that" and feeling "the thing she had carried out of Yuma" is the piece's most explicit statement of theme, and it's slightly over-explained compared to the restraint elsewhere. The backstory (Yuma, the stepfather, the other name, the organization hunting her) is gestured at but never clarified. This is largely a strength — the ambiguity suits the tone — but Cope's scene raises questions (who sent them, why now, what organization) that the story declines to answer. For a pulp revenge tale this is acceptable, but it does leave the plot feeling slightly incomplete. The pacing in Chapter 7 (Cope) is slightly rushed compared to the careful build of earlier chapters. The fight is over quickly and the emotional processing is compressed. The dialogue is generally excellent — spare, functional, character-revealing. "Gary says recipes are the minimum" is a perfect line. The Ray interrogation scene is particularly well-handled. Cope's monologue about the tie and Wren's mother is the one place where dialogue feels slightly expository, though it's saved by the specificity of the tie detail. The ending is genuinely moving and earns its sentiment. The image of Gary in her hand is not forced — it's been built across the entire story. The final line "She did not know what to do with him" works on multiple levels without announcing itself. OVERALL: This is a high-quality piece of genre fiction with genuine literary ambition. It mostly succeeds. The prose is controlled, the characters are real, the moral weight is handled with honesty, and the structure is sound. Its weaknesses are the weaknesses of ambition rather than incompetence. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 15 World Building: 14 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 80.7/100
+ Queer Neo-Noir Romance — Cigarette Smoke & Bad Ideas (Chapter Avg: 82.7 | Final: 86.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Prompt interpretation:** "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas" — a voice describing someone who's magnetic, dangerous, self-destructive. Classic noir archetype: the femme/homme fatale. Queer neo-noir romance means we lean into shadow, obsession, moral ambiguity, rain-slick streets, jazz, longing, and — critically — two men (or nonbinary characters) at the heart of it. **Possible narrators:** - A weary private investigator hired to track the man, falls for him - A bartender at a dive who watches him come and go - A down-on-his-luck journalist who gets pulled into a story - A young man new to the city, seduced by a seasoned grifter - A forger/fence who gets tangled up in the man's scheme - A cop who's chasing him but in love with him - A mortician, a doctor patching him up after fights **Possible "he" characters:** - A con artist running a long grift - A jewel thief / art thief - A widow's gigolo who murdered his last patron - A torch singer with a dark past - A drifter running from the mob - A boxer who throws fights - A political operative / blackmailer - A drug dealer with poetry in his pocket **Settings:** - 1940s LA — classic noir - 1970s NYC — grimy, disco-adjacent, pre-AIDS queer underground - 1980s New Orleans — humid, jazz, decay - Contemporary Berlin — neon, techno, cold - A fictional rust-belt city, timeless - Seattle in the rain, 1990s **Plot possibilities:** 1. PI hired to find a missing husband's lover; discovers the lover is a con man; falls in love; gets caught in a heist/murder plot. 2. Narrator meets "him" at a bar, begins affair, learns he's married/wanted/dying/planning something awful; chooses to help or betray. 3. Narrator is the mark of a long con — realizes it too late or too early and chooses love anyway. 4. Two men meet, become lovers and partners in crime, one betrays the other — told after the fact. 5. A dying man recounts his one great love, who ruined him. **Tone & style:** - First person, past tense, confessional - Lyrical, sensory, smoke and whiskey - Short sentences. Fragments. Longing. - Unreliable narrator - Lots of weather as mood **8-chapter structure possibilities:** *Option A — The Affair:* 1. Meet-cute in a bar (narrator hooked) 2. First night / intimacy 3. Deepening obsession, red flags 4. Narrator discovers secret (crime) 5. Complicity — joins in 6. Job goes wrong 7. Betrayal 8. Aftermath / coda *Option B — The Investigation:* 1. PI gets the case 2. Finds "him" — sparks 3. Affair begins despite ethics 4. Truth about the case twists 5. The real villain revealed 6. Plan to run away together 7. Double-cross 8. Survivor's lament *Option C — Retrospective:* 1. Present-day narrator at bar, remembering 2-7. Flashbacks of the affair, in fragments 8. Return to present — we learn what happened **Characters to consider:** - Name him something sharp: Sal, Dorian, Rook, Cass, Milo, Vince, Ezra - Narrator: Daniel, Joe, Arthur — an everyman name, or something sad - A third party: the jealous lover, the boss, the wife, the cop **Specific scenes/images:** - Kissing in the back of a car while rain streaks the windows - A gun in a nightstand drawer - A stolen ring - A wound being cleaned in a motel bathroom - Dancing alone to a record - A letter left behind - A body in the trunk - A train station goodbye that doesn't happen - Coffee at 4 AM in a diner - Payphone calls that go unanswered # Reflection What draws me most is the confessional, obsessive tone the opening line sets. "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas" is already retrospective — the narrator is looking back, which means the story probably ends badly. That framing is native to noir; we know from page one that love here is a wound. Option C (retrospective) is tempting but risks being too fragmented for 8 chapters of ~1000 words each. The flashback structure can dilute forward momentum. I think Option A or B works better — linear descent — with the opening line recurring or echoed at key moments. A hybrid: open in a moment of aftermath or foreshadowing, then tell the story forward, returning to that frame at the end. **What setting serves best?** I'm drawn to something slightly unmoored from specific historical reality — a city that could be anywhere, any-time-ish 20th century. That gives the queer romance room to breathe without having to footnote the specific dangers of the era, while still carrying noir's weight. A rust-belt or port city, perpetual rain, late autumn. Or maybe a specific setting like 1970s somewhere — grainy, pre-cellphone, payphones matter, cigarettes everywhere. The prompt line leans 1940s-50s but queer noir in that era is so freighted with closet-dynamics it could be either a feature or a distraction. **Narrator choice:** PI is almost too on-the-nose, but it provides built-in plot machinery. A more interesting choice might be something adjacent: a night clerk at a seedy hotel, a piano player, a pharmacist who sells off-the-books, a tattoo artist, a locksmith. The locksmith is interesting — he literally opens doors for "him." Or a pawnbroker. Or — I like this — a forger. Someone already half-crooked, whose complicity is the hinge of the story. He can love "him" AND help him AND be undone by him. **Plot engine:** I want something with stakes but that doesn't become a heist caper. The emotional core must be the romance. A plan the two men hatch together, that slowly reveals one was playing the other — classic, but it works. Or: the narrator thinks he's being conned but "he" genuinely falls, and the tragedy is that they can't escape what they've set in motion. That's more interesting — love that's real but arrives too late to save either of them. **Who is "he"?** A drifter with a dangerous past is good, but more specific: someone running from someone. Maybe a former henchman who stole from his boss. Or a man who killed his wealthy lover (ambiguous — accident? mercy? murder?) and is running from the dead man's family. The latter adds a queer-specific noir texture: the closeted rich older man, the younger lover, the inheritance, the family wanting revenge or silence. **The 8-chapter shape I'm gravitating toward:** 1. First meeting (bar, rain, the line itself lands here) 2. The affair begins; sensual, careful 3. We learn narrator's world — he's a forger; "he" asks for a favor 4. The favor done; deeper in; real intimacy 5. The past catches up — men come looking for "him" 6. They plan to run; stakes established; a betrayal hinted 7. Violence; one of them does something terrible 8. Aftermath — narrator alone, smoking, telling us this story The last chapter returning us to the voice of chapter 1 creates a frame. The opening line can be delivered in chapter 1 as present-tense memory, and echoed/recontextualized in chapter 8. **Tone commitments:** lean into sensuality without being gratuitous; the intimacy should ache. Keep prose tight, sentence rhythms varied, period-neutral vocabulary. Avoid pastiche — don't write Chandler-karaoke. Instead, channel writers like Patricia Highsmith, Denis Johnson, Ocean Vuong-ish sensory prose filtered through noir. Real noir is about loneliness more than crime. That should be the emotional engine: two lonely men who find each other and can't quite save each other.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a queer neo-noir romance built around a confessional, retrospective voice — a narrator looking back at the man who ruined him, and whom he perhaps ruined in return. The opening line ("He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas") sets a tone of rueful, sensual recollection; the whole piece should feel like it's being told to us across a bar at 2 a.m. by someone who hasn't gotten over it and never will. **Setting:** An unnamed port city, late autumn, vaguely late-20th-century (payphones, cigarettes indoors, no cell phones — likely early 1980s but kept loose). Perpetual rain, neon, cheap rooms above bars. The setting should feel specific in texture but untethered from dateable politics, so the queerness reads as atmospheric rather than issue-driven. **Narrator:** Daniel — a forger (documents, signatures, provenance papers for stolen art). Already half-crooked, lonely, precise with his hands. His complicity is structural: he opens doors for the man he falls for. **"He":** Milo. A younger, sharp-edged drifter running from the family of his late rich lover — a man who died in ambiguous circumstances. Charming, hungry, damaged, and — crucially — in love with Daniel by the time it counts, though it may be too late. Not pure femme fatale; a real person caught in his own trap. **Plot engine:** Milo arrives needing false papers; Daniel provides them; they fall into each other; Milo's past catches up; they attempt to escape together; a betrayal occurs (whose, is the question); violence ends it. The romance is the point. The crime is the frame that gives the romance its pressure. **Voice & style:** First person past tense. Lyrical but restrained — short sentences, occasional fragments, sensory specificity (the smell of lighter fluid, the click of a lock, the weight of a hand on a sternum). Avoid Chandler-pastiche wisecracks. Aim closer to Denis Johnson, Highsmith, with the ache of Ocean Vuong filtered through smoke. Intimacy written with tenderness, not titillation. **What I'm avoiding:** heist-caper mechanics dominating the emotional story; period-closet-politics as the main conflict; unreliable narration tricks; too-clever twists. The tragedy should be emotional, not plot-puzzle. **Frame:** Chapter 1 opens with the narrator in the present, at a bar, and we hear the title line. Chapter 8 returns to that frame. Chapters 2–7 are the story itself, linear. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Smoke"** Present-tense(ish) frame. Daniel, alone at a bar in this same city, years later. He sees a man light a cigarette a certain way and is pulled back. Establishes voice, atmosphere, and the line: "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas, and I loved him the way you love a house fire." Transition into memory: the night Milo walked into the back room of Daniel's workshop needing papers. **Chapter 2 — "Papers"** The first meeting in full. Milo arrives through a mutual contact, needing a new name. Daniel watches him across the desk; notes his hands, the cut on his jaw, the careful way he speaks. They talk longer than the job requires. Daniel agrees to make the documents. Milo returns two nights later to pick them up; they share a drink; the first kiss happens in the doorway, almost accidentally. Daniel lets him stay. **Chapter 3 — "Routines"** The affair settles into something like a life. Milo doesn't leave. Mornings, coffee, Milo reading at the window. Daniel works; Milo watches him work, asks about the craft. Small tendernesses. We learn Daniel's backstory in fragments — a father who was a printer, a lover who left years ago, a careful solitude Milo has undone. Milo gives Daniel pieces of his past — a rich older man, a house by the water, something that went wrong. He doesn't say murder. He doesn't have to. **Chapter 4 — "The Favor"** Milo asks Daniel for one more thing: papers for a second identity, and provenance documents for a painting he says he can sell. Daniel knows this means Milo is planning to run, and is deciding whether to take Daniel with him. Daniel does the work. A scene of him forging late at night while Milo sleeps — the most honest love scene in the story, rendered through craft. Milo wakes and tells him: come with me. Daniel says yes before he's finished the sentence. **Chapter 5 — "Visitors"** Two men come to the workshop asking about Milo — polite, well-dressed, patient. The dead lover's people. Daniel lies smoothly; they leave but the threat is planted. He finds Milo at their apartment and tells him. Milo's composure cracks for the first time. He tells Daniel what really happened with the rich man — not murder, but not innocence either. An argument; reconciliation; a night of frightened, specific tenderness. They set a date to leave: four days. **Chapter 6 — "Four Days"** Compressed time. Preparations. Daniel liquidating what he can. A scene at a pawnshop. A scene buying train tickets under the new names. Milo is twitchy, drinking more. Daniel notices a call Milo makes from a payphone he won't explain. A seed of doubt. But also: a perfect afternoon, the two of them in bed with the rain on the window, Milo asking Daniel what his mother was like. The reader should feel how close they are to getting away. **Chapter 7 — "The Station"** The night of the departure. Daniel at the train station with both tickets, waiting. Milo doesn't come. Daniel goes back to the apartment and finds it — not empty, but wrong. Signs of a struggle, or signs of a hurried exit? He searches. He finds a letter, or the beginning of one, in Milo's handwriting. What happened next unfolds in the present of the chapter: the dead lover's men found Milo first — or Milo ran alone to protect him — or Milo betrayed him for passage out. I'll keep it ambiguous but land on: Milo tried to draw the men away from Daniel, and it cost him. Daniel finds him, or finds what's left. A final scene, whether dying or already gone. **Chapter 8 — "Ash"** Return to the frame. Daniel at the bar, years on. He's finished telling us (or himself). The man across the room who lit the cigarette is not Milo; Milo is long dead. Daniel walks out into the rain. He still forges papers. He still lives in the same city because leaving would mean admitting Milo is not coming back. The final lines echo and invert the opening: he was cigarette smoke, and I have been breathing him in ever since. A small, precise image to close — the striking of a match, or a door being locked for the night.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This A human writer coming to "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas" would probably start with a feeling, not a plot. They'd have a specific person in mind — an ex, a crush, someone they watched from across a room once — and the story would be an attempt to exorcise or preserve that feeling. The prose would carry the grain of actual memory: weirdly specific details that don't serve the plot (the brand of his lighter, the way he mispronounced a word, a song that was playing), and big gaps where memory failed or where the writer didn't feel like explaining. A human writer would also probably be a little embarrassed by the prompt. It's a romantic, almost purple line. They'd push against it — undercut it with humor, or with an image so banal it grounds the noir. They'd be suspicious of their own lyricism and puncture it periodically. They'd let the narrator be petty, horny, mean, stupid. They'd let the love interest fart, or have bad breath in the morning, or say something dumb. Real noir has grime in it, not just rain. A human would also accept not knowing things. They might never fully decide what happened to the rich dead lover. They might not resolve who betrayed whom. They'd trust the reader to sit with uncertainty. # Telltale LLM Approaches and Why They Hurt This Piece **1. Over-explanation.** LLMs fill in causal logic, backstory, motivation. Noir demands ellipsis. The reader should feel like they walked in halfway through something and will leave before it ends. If I tell you exactly why Milo is running, exactly what happened with the rich man, exactly who betrayed whom — the mystery evaporates into procedure. **2. Symmetry and tidy callbacks.** LLMs love frames, echoes, inverted opening lines. "He was cigarette smoke, and I have been breathing him in ever since" — that's exactly the kind of satisfying-on-paper closure that feels designed. Real endings are often lopsided, anticlimactic, or stopped mid-gesture. **3. Lyrical over-reach.** LLMs reach for "beautiful" writing and produce prose that's uniformly elevated — every sentence load-bearing, every image doing work. Real prose has valleys. Characters say "yeah" and "I dunno." A flat, plain sentence between two gorgeous ones is often more powerful than three gorgeous ones in a row. **4. Safe emotional register.** LLMs write tender scenes that are tender and violent scenes that are violent and tense scenes that are tense — they modulate predictably. Humans let registers collide: a joke at the wrong moment, desire during a crisis, boredom during danger, absurdity in grief. **5. Universality / archetype-reaching.** LLMs default to types (the forger, the drifter, the dead rich man). They round characters toward recognizability rather than toward the specific idiosyncrasies that make fiction live. **6. Tell-don't-show-that-you-told.** LLMs often state the theme. "The tragedy was that he couldn't love me and run at the same time." A human writer would let you feel it and never name it. **7. Resolution bias.** LLMs want catharsis, closure, moral legibility. Noir traditionally refuses all three. The protagonist ends diminished, complicit, alive but not recovered. **8. Sentimentality creep.** The closer we get to the romantic core, the more LLMs will reach for conventional romantic imagery (rain on windows, hands through hair, whispered names). These images aren't bad but they've been used to death. A human writer would replace the expected tender image with something stranger and more specific. **9. Plot mechanics dominating.** LLMs like plot because it's tractable. The piece could quietly become "how they escape" rather than "who they are to each other." I've already flagged this, but it bears repeating. **10. Clean pacing.** LLMs pace chapters evenly — introduce, develop, complicate, turn, end. Humans write uneven chapters. One is 400 words and a blackout. The next is 2000 words of conversation. Uniformity is a tell. # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan **The frame is too clean.** Chapter 1 sets up the retrospective voice, Chapter 8 returns to it, the last line inverts the first line. This is a Creative Writing 101 structure and it's going to feel engineered. The opening line is already doing frame-work; doubling down on frame in chapter 1 and again in chapter 8 risks preciousness. The ending image I sketched ("the striking of a match, or a door being locked for the night") is a textbook "LLM-ending image" — small, precise, meaningful. Suspicious. **The arc is too legible.** Meet → affair → deepening → complication → crisis → plan → betrayal → aftermath. It's structurally flawless and therefore lifeless. Nothing surprises. I can predict every beat. A human writer would break the rhythm — skip the affair-deepening chapter entirely, or put the violence in chapter 4 and spend chapters 5-7 in grief, or start in the middle. **Milo is an archetype.** Beautiful damaged drifter with a dead rich lover. He needs a specific weirdness. Does he love a bad TV show? Does he have a stupid opinion about something? Is he bad at something he thinks he's good at? The "polite, well-dressed, patient" men hunting him are straight out of the noir prop closet. **Daniel is underwritten.** He's "a forger, lonely, precise with his hands." That's a function, not a person. What does he want besides Milo? What does he find funny? What's embarrassing about him? Does he have a cat, a bad habit, a grudge against someone boring? **The forging-as-love-scene idea is pretentious.** Chapter 4's "the most honest love scene in the story, rendered through craft" is exactly the kind of Writerly move that reads well in an outline and groans on the page. It's metaphor substituting for intimacy. Write actual intimacy. **The "four days" compression chapter (Ch 6) is generic.** Montages of preparation are a cliche. The payphone call is a cliche. The perfect afternoon before disaster is a cliche. All of these might still work, but I should be aware I'm stacking them. **Chapter 7 hedges.** "I'll keep it ambiguous but land on: Milo tried to draw the men away from Daniel." That's having it both ways — ambiguity as mystique plus a sentimental resolution. Pick: either genuine not-knowing, or a harder answer. Milo betraying Daniel for a way out and Daniel knowing it is much more noir than Milo heroically sacrificing himself. **The violence is vague.** "A final scene, whether dying or already gone." The more specific and strange I make this moment, the better. What if Milo isn't dead — what if he's just gone, and Daniel never learns? What if Daniel is the one who does something terrible? **Too much tenderness, not enough meanness.** The current plan is awfully romantic. Real noir romance has cruelty in it — small betrayals, petty lies, bad behavior on both sides. If Milo is all softness except for the Secret Past, he's not a fatale, he's a Disney prince with a backstory. **Setting is an evasion.** "Unnamed port city, vaguely late-20th-century." Noir works on specificity. The refusal to commit to a place and time is a way of avoiding research and responsibility. I don't have to name the city but I should know it — what the streetcar sounds like at 3am, what local beer Daniel drinks, what the specific smell of this harbor is at low tide. # Ideas to Make It More Human - **Let Daniel be unlikable in small ways.** Jealous, petty, a little cheap. Maybe he resents how much food Milo eats. Maybe he lies about his age. - **Let Milo be genuinely annoying sometimes.** He hums. He leaves cabinet doors open. He uses Daniel's toothbrush once and Daniel is disproportionately furious. - **Break the chapter rhythm.** Let one chapter be very short — 500 words, a single scene, something the narrator can barely bring himself to tell. Let another sprawl. - **Refuse one of the expected beats.** Skip the first kiss. Skip the sex scene. Skip the argument. Whichever one the reader most expects to be rendered, summarize in a line instead. - **Specificity passes.** Every chapter, find one weird concrete detail that has no narrative function and keep it in. - **Don't explain the dead rich man.** Let the reader never know if Milo killed him. - **Let Daniel do something bad.** He's complicit; make him actively, morally compromised in a way that isn't just "loving the wrong man." - **Undercut the lyricism.** Every time I write a beautiful sentence, follow it with something flatter. "The rain came down like it had a grudge. It was Tuesday." - **Kill the ending echo.** Don't mirror the opening line at the close. Find a different, worse, more interesting last beat. - **Let the ending be small and wrong.** Not cathartic. Not tragic-beautiful. Just — the next day. Someone has to buy groceries. - **Consider an unreliable detail.** Not full unreliable narrator — but let Daniel contradict himself once, casually, about some small thing, and never address it. - **Let humor in.** A single line of dry humor in the right place will do more for the voice than ten lyrical passages. - **Name the city, or at least let it have texture.** Baltimore-ish. Marseille-ish. Something. Get the specific streetcar, the specific bridge, the specific weather. - **The ending: what if Milo just vanishes?** Daniel never finds a body, never gets a letter, never knows. He lives the rest of his life not knowing. That's crueler and truer than either version I sketched.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a queer neo-noir romance told by a man who has not recovered and knows he won't. The opening line — "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas" — is the thesis: romantic, a little melodramatic, and immediately to be complicated by everything that follows. The voice is confessional but not confessing for absolution. Daniel isn't telling us this story to be forgiven or understood. He's telling it because he can't stop telling it. **Plot shape:** Forger meets drifter, makes him papers, falls in love, gets caught up in the drifter's past, tries to run with him, loses him. Standard noir skeleton — deliberately so. The piece earns itself through texture, specificity, and refusal, not plot invention. **Key commitments based on critique:** - **Specificity over archetype.** The city has a name — Varna Bay, a composite American-East-Coast port town, late-ish century but I won't date it precisely in-text. It has specific weather, specific beer, specific streets. Daniel has a specific apartment, specific tools, specific grudges. Milo has specific tics — he hums a song wrong, he can't sit still, he overtips. - **Let both men be difficult.** Daniel is petty, jealous, cheap about strange things. Milo is charming but also irritating, sometimes cruel, sometimes bored. The love between them is real but neither of them is a good man. - **Refuse the expected beats.** I'll skip at least one "required" scene (the first kiss; it happens off-page). I'll deflate at least one lyrical moment with something banal or ugly. I'll withhold at least one answer the reader wants. - **The ending: Milo vanishes.** No body, no letter, no closure. Daniel does not find out what happened. This is the cruelest and most honest version. - **The rich dead lover stays ambiguous.** I will not tell the reader whether Milo killed him. Daniel doesn't know either, or pretends not to. - **Break the chapter rhythm.** Some chapters sprawl conversationally; one is much shorter and spare; the last is stranger and smaller than expected. - **Kill the mirror-ending.** No echoed opening line. The last beat is domestic, off-key, and unresolved. - **Undercut lyricism.** Every beautiful sentence earns its place. Flat sentences, ugly details, and dry humor are structural, not decorative. - **Moral weight on Daniel.** He does something genuinely bad — not just "loves the wrong man." A small, real betrayal of his own, something he never tells Milo about. **Voice:** First person past tense, conversational more than ornate. Short paragraphs. Some fragments. Contractions. Occasional deadpan. Avoid Chandler-isms; avoid purple noir-karaoke. Closer to Denis Johnson, mid-period Highsmith, a bit of James Salter's restraint. **What I will not do:** frame-symmetry, mirrored opening/closing lines, a thematic summary sentence, a tidy redemption, a cathartic violent climax, explanations of Milo's past beyond what Daniel himself gets to know. No heist mechanics foregrounded. No telegraphed foreshadowing. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Varna Bay, November"** Open inside the memory, not a frame. Daniel in his workshop above a laundromat, late evening, doing a small forging job for a regular. The line lands within the first paragraph, casually, almost self-mockingly: "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. I want that on record before I start lying." Establishes city, trade, Daniel's voice, his solitude, his minor meannesses (he overcharges a widow he dislikes). A knock at the door. Milo enters with a name from a mutual contact, a cut on his eyebrow, and asks for papers. They talk. Daniel quotes him a price. Milo counters. Daniel agrees for less than he should. End of chapter. **Chapter 2 — "The Job"** Milo comes back three nights later to pick up the documents. He stays to drink. A conversation that sprawls — Milo asks about Daniel's work; Daniel asks nothing he wants to know and several things he doesn't. Milo is funny, a little mean, evasive about himself but specific about useless things (a diner in a city he used to live in, a girl who taught him to drive). Daniel notices him notice the workshop's exits. They end up on the roof smoking. No kiss on-page. Cut to: morning, Milo in Daniel's bed, already awake, looking at him. Two lines of dialogue. End. **Chapter 3 — "House Rules"** Three weeks compressed. Milo doesn't leave. Small domesticity rendered with mess: Milo hums the wrong melody to a song Daniel likes, leaves cabinet doors open, uses Daniel's razor. Daniel is irritated and besotted and catches himself mid-sentence about to be cruel. They fight once, stupidly, about laundry. They reconcile without discussing it. Daniel's backstory arrives in a single paragraph — a father, a previous man, a reason he lives alone. Milo gives nothing back but a name that might be false and a story about a house by the water where someone died. He says it like it was weather. Daniel doesn't press. **Chapter 4 — "Provenance"** Milo asks for a second set of papers — a different name, and provenance documents for a painting. Daniel understands what this means. He agrees before Milo finishes asking. Scene of Daniel working late, Milo asleep on the couch. Resist the temptation to make this scene mystical. Keep it workmanlike: ink, solvent, a cramp in his hand, the radio playing something stupid. Milo wakes, makes coffee badly, says *come with me when I go.* Daniel says yes. Later that week: Daniel does the bad thing. A regular client of his — a man he dislikes — asks about Milo, having seen him leave. Daniel, without thinking it through, passes along a lie that will cost that client money. Small, petty, protective, unnecessary. He tells no one, including us fully. It will not return as plot. It's just who he is. **Chapter 5 — "Callers"** *(the short chapter — ~600–700 words)* Two men come to the workshop. They are not "polite and well-dressed" — one has a head cold and is annoyed about it; the other is younger than Daniel expected and chews gum. They ask about a man matching Milo's description. Daniel lies with the steadiness of his trade. They leave a card. After they go, Daniel sits for a long time without moving. He doesn't tell Milo that night. He tells him the next morning, and badly. Chapter ends with Milo quiet, then laughing — not a nice laugh — and saying something Daniel will think about for years: a single line, throwaway, that doesn't explain anything but changes the shape of what Daniel thought he knew. **Chapter 6 — "Five Days"** Preparations for leaving. Resist montage. Instead: one long scene at a pawnshop where Daniel sells a watch that was his father's and is surprised by how little he feels; one scene of Milo packing, throwing away things Daniel didn't know he'd acquired; one argument about money that is really about trust. A good afternoon in the middle — they go to a movie, Milo falls asleep against him. Daniel notices Milo making a phone call from a booth and not explaining it. He does not ask. He decides not to ask. This is the chapter where the reader should feel they might actually get out. **Chapter 7 — "The 11:40"** The night of the train. Daniel at the station with two tickets, a bag, forty minutes early. He waits. The train comes. The train goes. He waits for the next one. He goes home near dawn. The apartment is empty in a way that is not staged — not ransacked, not cleaned, just empty the way a place is when someone has left in a hurry and wasn't thinking about what they were doing. Milo's coat is gone. His cigarettes are on the table. No note. Daniel looks for a note for a long time. He does not find one. He goes to the rooms of people who might know things; no one knows anything, or no one says. He files no report, makes no calls. He walks the harbor road at noon, which is something he used to do before Milo. The chapter should not resolve. It should just stop. **Chapter 8 — "After"** Not a frame-close. A short chapter set some indeterminate time later — months, a year, he won't say. Daniel is still in Varna Bay. Still forging papers. He has a new regular, a woman who makes him laugh. He has not heard from Milo. He has heard a rumor, maybe two, none confirmable; he has decided to believe none of them and half of one. A small scene: he is at the grocery store buying things for one person. He sees someone at the end of an aisle who, from behind, could be. It isn't. He buys what he came for and walks home. Last image: something domestic and slightly wrong — a detail that doesn't summarize anything, just sits there. End.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Daniel Veitch **Background.** Thirty-eight, born in Varna Bay, never really left it. Son of a printer — his father ran a small legitimate press that did wedding invitations, funeral cards, the occasional municipal contract, and, quietly, better things for worse people. Daniel learned ink from him and also learned to keep his mouth shut at dinner. His mother left when he was eleven. He does not talk about her and gets irritated when asked, even gently. His father died six years ago of a stroke at the press; Daniel found him, finished the job on the machine, called the ambulance. He's told two people this story. Milo is one of them. He was with a man named Reuben for four years in his late twenties. Reuben left him for a woman, which Daniel treats as a joke now and was not a joke at the time. Since Reuben he has had arrangements, not relationships. He convinced himself he preferred it. He works alone in a second-floor room above a laundromat on Clare Street. He does documents — passports occasionally, but mostly the dull stuff: backdated leases, signatures on wills, provenance for objects that need to appear older or cleaner than they are. He is very good and knows it. He resents clients who don't appreciate the craft and overcharges them a little out of spite. **Personality & flaws.** Watchful, dry, patient in a way that's half professional and half depressive. Funny in a deadpan register when he chooses. Cheap about odd things — he will reuse tea bags but buy expensive shoes, on the theory that shoes touch the ground. Petty. He holds grudges against people who have no idea he's holding them. When he's hurt, he gets quieter, not louder, which is worse. He lies easily about work and badly about himself. He has a streak of cruelty he doesn't like to acknowledge. He has, on at least two occasions, passed along information about clients to other clients in ways that cost them money, and told himself it was just business. It wasn't just business. He does it when he feels small. **Idiosyncrasies.** Drinks coffee black but keeps a bottle of cheap vanilla syrup in the cabinet that he denies owns. Reads paperback mysteries and mocks them as he reads. Can't stand the sound of someone eating an apple. Hums, badly, without knowing he's doing it. Keeps every receipt. Has never owned a pet. **Physicality.** Tall-ish, narrow through the shoulders, slightly stooped from twenty years at a work lamp. Dark hair going grey at the temples earlier than he'd like. Clean-shaven because he distrusts beards. Long hands, ink-stained at the cuticles no matter how he scrubs. Pale eyes, a face that goes blank when he's thinking, which people sometimes mistake for coldness. Dresses plainly and well — good shirts, worn collars, a wool coat he's had for a decade. He moves carefully, like he's used to working with small things. **Motives & allegiances.** He wants to be left alone and he wants to be found. He doesn't believe in love the way he once did, which is why he falls so fast when it comes for him. His only real loyalty before Milo was to his father's memory and to the trade, in that order. He owes small favors to a handful of people in the city, collects on none of them. **Voice samples.** > "I'd tell you I knew better. I did know better. Knowing better has never once stopped me from doing a thing I wanted to do, and I don't trust anyone who says otherwise." > *(to a client)* "The date you want is a Tuesday. The date you're giving me is a Sunday. If you want to sign a lease on a Sunday in 1974 I can do that, but someone is going to ask, and you are going to have to have an answer, and I can tell by your face you do not have answers in you." > *(internal)* I watched him sleep and thought: this is the part where you decide. Then I got up and made coffee, because I had already decided and didn't want to look at it straight on. --- # Milo (Milo Kearns, probably not his real surname) **Background.** Twenty-seven or twenty-nine depending on which story he's telling. Grew up somewhere inland — a small city with a paper mill, he's said once, and refused to name. Left home at sixteen after something with his stepfather he won't describe. Drifted east. Worked in restaurants, on a fishing boat for one bad summer, as a driver for a man who sold antiques and other things. Learned to read people quickly because he had to. He spent the last three years with a man named Aldous Finch — wealthy, fifty-something, owned a house on the coast an hour north of Varna Bay. It was not exactly a secret and not exactly public. Milo was the live-in something; what Aldous called him in front of other people changed depending on the room. Aldous died nine weeks before Milo walked into Daniel's workshop. The death was ruled accidental. There was an inquest. There were questions. The family — two adult children, a brother, lawyers — did not like Milo and liked less that Aldous had changed a provision in his will six months before he died. Milo left the house the week of the funeral with a suitcase and a painting that may or may not have been his to take. Whether he killed Aldous, Milo does not say. He does not say he didn't, either. It is possible he does not fully know. Daniel never asks directly, which Milo notices and is grateful for and resents. **Personality & flaws.** Charming without effort, which has taught him to distrust his own charm. Quick, funny, often mean in a way he passes off as a joke. Restless — genuinely cannot sit still for more than twenty minutes; paces, taps, fiddles with things on the table. Sentimental in flashes about strange objects (a lighter, a matchbook, a song) and cold about things that should matter. Lies for practice more than for profit. Will occasionally tell the truth in a way designed to sound like a lie, which he finds funny. He is afraid in a way he has mostly learned to hide, and the fear comes out as appetite — for food, for sex, for leaving. He eats like he's been hungry before. He has been. **Idiosyncrasies.** Hums songs with the wrong melody, confidently. Overtips waitresses and underpays cab drivers on some private moral system. Can't swim. Reads the obituaries in any newspaper he picks up, first. Good with his hands in surprising ways — can fix a watch, can't hang a picture straight. Takes cream in his coffee and two sugars and pretends to be embarrassed about it. **Physicality.** Shorter than Daniel by a few inches, wiry, built like someone who's done real work and also sat in bars for long stretches. Dark blond hair he cuts himself badly. A face that photographs better than it looks in person, or the other way around, depending on the light. A small scar through one eyebrow from a fight he won't describe, and a newer cut on his jaw the night he arrives at Daniel's that he says came from a door. Moves quickly, touches things without thinking — other people's sleeves, the rim of a glass, a railing as he walks past. Smokes constantly. Holds a cigarette like he's thinking about holding it. **Motives & allegiances.** Wants to get out of this city and this country. Wants money, not for what it buys but for what it prevents. Wants, against his own instincts, to be known by someone who isn't going to die on him or sell him out. He is capable of love and also capable of leaving in the middle of the night; he does not consider these contradictions. His only loyalty before Daniel was to Aldous, and that loyalty was complicated and is now the thing he can't put down. **Voice samples.** > "You're the third forger I've met and the first one who isn't stupid. That's a compliment. Take it." > "He was kind to me. I want that on record. People will tell you otherwise, and some of them will even believe it. He was kind to me and I think I ruined him a little, and he died, and here we are." > *(after a fight, quietly)* "Don't be good to me when I'm being like this. I'll get used to it." > *(lighting a cigarette, grinning)* "Bad idea. That's me, professionally. Put it on my card."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — Varna Bay, November He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. I want that on record before I start lying. I'd been at the desk since four, working on a lease for Hanne Gebler — widow, two cats, a grudge against her dead husband that had outlived the husband by eleven years. She wanted the lease backdated by six months so her stepson couldn't touch the apartment. I charged her seventy dollars more than I should have because she talked through her nose and I'd decided, the first time I met her, that I didn't like her. This is the kind of man I am. I'd like to say it's the worst of me. It isn't. The rain had started sometime in the afternoon and was doing the thing Varna Bay rain does in November, which is settle in like a tenant. Below me the laundromat was still going; I could feel the dryers through the floorboards, a warm mechanical hum I'd stopped hearing years ago and still sometimes noticed when I was tired. I was tired. I drank the end of my coffee cold, put the seal on Mrs. Gebler's lease, and was thinking about the heel of bread in the kitchen and whether it was worth eating when the knock came. Three soft, one hard. Janusz's knock. Janusz had my number for a reason and did not use it tonight for a reason, which meant he was sending someone up and didn't want his voice on the line. I stood, tucked Mrs. Gebler's papers into the drawer, and opened the door. "Evening," the man said. He had a cut above one eyebrow, not fresh, not old either — the scab at that shiny stage where it's deciding whether to become a scar. He was shorter than me.
