The four seconds of not-moving, the body knowing before the mind does, and crucially the realization that it wouldn't have mattered if it were him — "I would not have gone down the aisle" — this is the chapter's real emotional revelation. It's not about whether Milo is alive or dead or findable; it's about what Daniel has become in the aftermath. This is showing rather than telling at its best. **The Cigarette** The final image — the cigarette on the sill, moved twice and replaced both times — is the "domestic and slightly wrong" detail the plan called for. It doesn't summarize anything. It just sits there, as Daniel says. The self-awareness of "I don't know why I am telling you about the cigarette" is a risk that pays off: it acknowledges the potential sentimentality of the image while refusing to either lean into it or deflate it. The final three sentences are spare and correct. **Weaknesses** The chapter is almost too well-executed — there are moments where the craft is slightly visible, where you can see the plan being followed. "I have decided to believe half of the third one" is a little too neat as a formulation, a little too aphoristic. The Janusz paragraph, while doing necessary work (establishing Daniel's continued life, his friendship), is the chapter's weakest section — it tells more than it shows and the observation "He was always the closest thing I had to a friend. I had not noticed" is the one moment that feels slightly like a thematic summary sentence, which the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The dialogue is minimal (one line from the woman messenger) and it works — it's not a chapter that needs dialogue. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The grocery store scene, the three rumors, the domestic ending, the refusal to resolve — all present. Daniel's character is consistent: the efficiency of buying for one person "in a way I am not proud of," the decision not to go west, the cigarette kept and replaced. The plan's instruction to "kill the mirror-ending" is honored — there's no echoed opening line, no thematic closure. **Overall Assessment** This is a very strong piece of literary fiction. It's controlled, emotionally honest, and refuses easy comfort. The occasional moment of visible craft and one slightly over-neat formulation keep it from being a masterpiece, but it's close. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of neo-noir queer romance fiction. The narrator's voice is established immediately and maintained with remarkable consistency throughout all eight chapters — a dry, self-aware, morally compromised man who tells us he's going to lie and then proceeds to tell us a kind of truth. The opening line is used as a title/prompt and then immediately complicated: "I want that on record before I start lying." This is a sophisticated move that sets the epistemological tone for the entire piece. CHARACTER WORK: Daniel Veitch is one of the more fully realized first-person narrators in this genre of writing. His self-awareness is calibrated precisely — he knows he's cheap, he knows he's petty (the Arendt scene), he knows he's falling, and he watches himself fall with the detachment of a man who has learned to observe his own life from a slight remove. The detail about finishing the print job before calling the ambulance for his father is devastating and perfectly placed. Milo is harder to assess because he's deliberately opaque, but the opacity is earned — we see him through Daniel's eyes, and Daniel is both perceptive and willfully blind. The canvas bag that never gets fuller, the obituaries, the unfinished paperbacks — these are character details that accumulate into a person without ever being explained. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely excellent. It has the rhythm of people who are smart and guarded, where what's said and what's meant are always slightly offset. "I've been a James." "That's your real name." "It's a name I answer to." "Those aren't the same." "No, they aren't." This is economical and revealing. The fight about laundry that's really about trust, the argument about money that's really about abandonment — these are handled with real craft. The dialogue never over-explains. PACING: The pacing is one of the piece's strongest elements. The story moves through time with confidence — three weeks compressed into a paragraph, five days given individual scenes, the year after given a chapter of fragments. The decision to slow down for the grocery store scene at the end, after a year of summary, is exactly right. WORLD BUILDING: Varna Bay is evoked through texture rather than exposition. The laundromat below, the harbor, Clare Street, the flat grey light — these accumulate into a place without ever becoming a travelogue. The world feels lived-in because Daniel has lived in it. PLOT: The plot is deliberately understated, which is appropriate. This is a character study with a noir skeleton. The two men who come looking for Milo, the painting provenance, the question of what happened to Aldous — these are kept deliberately vague, and the vagueness is mostly earned. The ending — Milo's disappearance, the year after, the three pieces of information, the cigarette on the sill — is genuinely moving and avoids sentimentality. POTENTIAL WEAKNESSES: The piece occasionally tips toward the self-consciously literary in ways that feel slightly performed. "Chekhov can go to hell; things were in that apartment that simply sat there and did nothing, like the rest of us" is clever but also a little too pleased with itself. The Arendt section, while revealing character, slightly disrupts the momentum of Chapter 4. The woman who delivers the message in August is a touch convenient as a plot device, though the narrator's response to it is handled well. Some of the aphoristic observations ("Boring is the goal. Interesting is what got you here") are very good; a few others feel like they're reaching for profundity. The "I'm not going to tell you who decided it" line about the roof is a slight coyness that doesn't quite earn its withholding. METAPHOR AND POETRY: The metaphors are largely earned. "The rain had started sometime in the afternoon and was doing the thing Varna Bay rain does in November, which is settle in like a tenant" is excellent. "He had the kind of face that rearranges itself depending on what you bring to it" is precise and true. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard — "the sky was the color rain-cities get at night, which is not black, which is some brown-orange thing made of streetlights bouncing off low cloud" is good but slightly labored. Overall, the poetic riffing lands far more often than it doesn't. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The final chapter is genuinely moving. The cigarette on the sill is a perfect closing image — not explained, not over-weighted, just there. The grocery store scene with the wrong man is the emotional climax of the piece and is handled with real restraint. The three pieces of information Daniel receives over the year are calibrated beautifully — the body at the river mouth, the sighting, the message — each one doing different emotional work. QUEER CONTENT: The queerness of the romance is handled with complete naturalness — it's never announced, never made into a theme, simply present. This is exactly right for the genre and the period implied. COHERENCE: The piece is remarkably coherent across eight chapters. The voice never wavers. The details accumulate consistently. The emotional logic holds. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Pacing: 17 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 86.2/100
+ Fantasy Erotica — The Bounty (Chapter Avg: 84.6 | Final: 82.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Fantasy Erotica Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Just three days out from Irongate. The red headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king's ransom -- that is, if she didn't talk him into an early grave first.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Fantasy Erotica Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Just three days out from Irongate. The red headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king&#x27;s ransom -- that is, if she didn&#x27;t talk him into an early grave first. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Premise & Setting The prompt gives us: a bounty hunter/slaver transporting a red-headed tiefling to Irongate, three days out. She's valuable ("king's ransom") and she's a talker — enough to drive him mad. This immediately sets up a classic "captor and captive" dynamic with erotic tension built into the premise. **Setting ideas:** - Irongate as a fortress-city, possibly a center of anti-magic or anti-demon prejudice - A wagon road through wilderness — forest, plains, mountain passes - A grim fantasy world with slavery as a legal institution - Could be post-war, where tieflings are persecuted as relics of a demonic incursion - Roadside inns, hidden bandits, cold nights by fire **The tiefling captive:** - Red hair, horns, tail — classic infernal heritage - Why is she worth a king's ransom? Options: she's a noble's daughter, a fugitive cult priestess, a mage, a princess in exile, has stolen something valuable, is wanted by an archdevil, is the key to some prophecy - Her weapon is her tongue — seduction, persuasion, needling, information warfare - Could be bound in magic-suppressing chains **The bounty hunter:** - Grizzled human man, veteran, possibly an ex-soldier - Could have a dark past, debts, family to support - Needs to be disciplined but tempted — that's the core tension - Maybe an ex-paladin who fell from grace (adds spice) - Needs moral complexity — not a cartoon villain ## Plot Arcs to Consider **Arc A: Straightforward seduction-to-freedom** She talks/seduces him into freeing her over three days. Classic but may feel predictable. **Arc B: Twist — she's the real threat** He thinks he's the captor; she was letting herself be caught for access to something in Irongate. He becomes complicit. **Arc C: Pursuit & alliance** Day 2, someone comes after them (bandits, rival hunters, her real enemies). They must ally. Trust builds. Ends with him choosing her over the bounty. **Arc D: Internal collapse** He's been told she's a monster. Over three days, she reveals the truth: she's innocent, or the bounty is corrupt. His worldview crumbles, along with his resistance. **Arc E: She breaks him, then saves him** Dark version where she psychologically dismantles him, then when he's truly broken something tender emerges. ## Character Dynamics & Tension - Power inversion: physically he has control; verbally/emotionally she dominates - Growing sexual tension that neither will admit - She reads him accurately — names his fears, his past - He resists but she gets under his skin - Physical intimacy born of necessity (a wound, cold night, needing to bathe her, etc.) - The bounty notice vs. the woman he's coming to know ## Possible Story Beats Across 8 Chapters **Version 1 (Romance/Escape arc):** 1. Establish the journey, his weariness, her chattering defiance 2. She starts probing — learns about his past, begins working on him 3. A night encounter — physical proximity, first real crack 4. External threat (bandits/wolves) — forced cooperation 5. Aftermath — wounds tended, barriers lowered, first real conversation 6. She tells her "truth" — why the bounty exists, her innocence (or partial truth) 7. Arrival near Irongate — he must decide 8. Decision & consequences — he frees her / they run together / bittersweet end **Version 2 (Darker, with twist):** 1. Setup — he's confident, she's maddening 2. Day 1 — needling, small provocations 3. Night 1 — dream/vision, maybe demonic 4. Day 2 — she escalates, real seduction attempts 5. Attack — bigger threat reveals stakes 6. Night 2 — intimate scene (sex or near-sex under duress of circumstance) 7. Day 3 — Irongate visible, reveal of twist 8. Ending — he chooses; her true nature shown ## Tone & Style - First-person from him? Third-limited from him? Alternating? - Third-limited from him probably best — lets us feel her as the mystery/force - Tone: tense, wry, with heat building. Think Joe Abercrombie meets a romance - Sensory: smell of horses, leather, woodsmoke, her perfume or sweat - Dialogue-heavy since her weapon is words ## Erotica Considerations - The prompt is "Fantasy Erotica" so we need genuine heat, not just implied - Building tension across chapters with payoff in chapters 6-7 - The erotic content should feel earned, not gratuitous - Could be one major scene or escalating encounters - The chains/bondage aspect is already there — play with it - Consent under unequal power is tricky — the tension should be that he wants her to want him, and vice versa; neither will admit it first ## Specific scene ideas - She asks him to loosen her bonds to piss; mundane intimacy - Washing blood off her after an attack - Sharing a bedroll in the cold - A moment where she could escape but doesn't - Him dreaming of her - Her singing a lullaby that shouldn't be so affecting - Him having to check her gag / feed her — mouth play - A bath scene at a hidden spring - A moment where he realizes she's been truthful about something checkable ## Character names - Him: Corven, Tharn, Edrik, Aldric, Kell, Vance - Her: Zaela, Ixelle, Sable, Vermeil, Saskia, Nyx, Rhovanna # Reflection The prompt is rich but compact: three days, bounty hunter, red tiefling, high value, verbal sparring as core dynamic. The "talk him into an early grave" line is doing a lot of work — it signals both her danger and the comedic/flirtatious tension. That tells me the piece shouldn't be grim-dark; it should have bite and wit. The best version will have her genuinely funny and sharp, not just sultry. Eight chapters at 1000 words each = 8000 words. That's enough room for genuine character development but not enough for a sprawling plot. I should resist the urge to add too many external events. The story needs to be fundamentally about the two of them in the wagon/on the road, with maybe one or two external pressure points rather than a full action plot. **What works:** - Three-day structure maps beautifully onto 8 chapters if I use 2-3 chapters per day, or use a roughly day-per-chapter-cluster pattern - The captor/captive dynamic with her as the one really in control (verbally) is the juiciest thing the prompt offers - Her being a legitimate threat (not just a damsel) makes her sexier and the stakes real - A twist or reveal about why she's worth a king's ransom gives the story a spine **What doesn't work as well:** - Too much external action (bandits, multiple attacks) would dilute the core two-hander intimacy - Making her simply innocent and him a redeemable guy is too saccharine — loses the edge - A full twist where she was manipulating him the whole time risks making the intimacy feel hollow; readers want emotional payoff - Alternating POV might muddy things; staying in his head keeps her mysterious and the tension tight **My instinct for direction:** Stay in his POV (third-limited). Three days, eight chapters. Use Day 1 for setup and initial sparring (2 chapters), Day 2 for escalation including a disruptive external event that forces intimacy (3 chapters), Day 3 for the emotional/erotic payoff and the approach to Irongate with a decision point (3 chapters). The "king's ransom" should have a specific, surprising reason — perhaps she's the illegitimate daughter of a powerful figure, or she carries information, or she's bait in a larger game. The truth should emerge gradually. For the erotic content: I want the tension to build genuinely, with small intimacies (touching her to adjust bonds, tending a wound, sharing warmth) escalating to a real encounter around chapter 6, perhaps with an aftermath/second encounter that deepens it. The sex should be complicated by the power dynamic — the thrill is in him still being technically her captor when she takes control of him. Chains/bondage play naturally into this. For her character: she needs to be genuinely witty, genuinely dangerous, and genuinely wounded underneath. If she's only seductive, she's flat. She should needle him about specific things, read him scarily well, but also reveal cracks — moments where her bravado falters. He should be drawn to the cracks more than the seduction. For him: he shouldn't be a villain, but he also shouldn't be too soft. He's hard, practical, a man who's made peace with doing unpleasant work. What breaks him isn't moral argument but recognition — seeing himself in her, or seeing her see him. Ending: I lean toward an ending that's neither clean escape-together nor tragic betrayal. Something more interesting — maybe he delivers her but the delivery itself becomes an act of defiance (to whoever hired him), or he frees her at the gate and walks away, forever marked. Or the twist: she wanted to reach Irongate, and he delivered her exactly where she wanted to be, to do what she came to do. That last option has real teeth and fits the "talk him into an early grave" line — maybe she does, in a sense, damn him. But with erotica expectations, some tenderness and mutual want must land. I think the winning structure is: she is genuinely dangerous and has her own agenda, but her attraction to him becomes real despite herself. He falls for her knowing she's playing him, and chooses her anyway. The ending leaves them bound to each other in a new way — not romantic fairy tale, but a dark pact with heat and consequence.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing an 8-chapter fantasy erotica piece (≈8000 words total) built around a three-day journey between a bounty hunter and his red-headed tiefling captive. The core engine is verbal — she is dangerous precisely because she talks, reads him, needles him, and won't shut up. Her seduction is intellectual before it's physical. **Voice & POV:** Third-person limited, staying inside the bounty hunter's head throughout. This keeps her as mystery and pressure — we experience her the way he does. Prose will be lean and muscular with wry, tactile sensory detail (leather, woodsmoke, iron, sweat). Dialogue-heavy, with her getting the sharpest lines. Tone: tense, witty, heat-building — closer to Abercrombie-with-a-pulse than grimdark or bodice-ripper. The title-phrase "talk him into an early grave" is the tonal north star: she's funny and frightening at once. **Plot choices made:** - Stay intimate. One external disruption on Day 2 (not a full action setpiece — more of a pressure valve that forces physical contact). Otherwise the story is the two of them on the road. - She is genuinely dangerous and has her own agenda. She is not a simple innocent. The reveal of *why* she's worth a king's ransom is doled out gradually, with the final truth emerging at Irongate. - Her attraction to him becomes real despite her scheming. His to her is the ruin of him and he knows it. The story's emotional spine is: he falls for her knowing he's being played, and chooses her anyway. - Ending: he delivers her to Irongate — but on her terms, not the bounty's. She wanted to be brought there. His "early grave" is metaphorical (the old version of him is dead) but the line's danger is kept literal-adjacent — he's now hers, bound to whatever she is about to do. **Erotic pacing:** Small physical intimacies escalate across chapters (bonds adjusted, a wound cleaned, shared warmth, a near-kiss). First significant encounter in Ch 6, with deeper consummation/aftermath in Ch 7. The chains/bondage dynamic is played with — the fact that he is technically her captor during their encounters is the heat. Consent is real but complicated by mutual manipulation; both know the game and want it anyway. **What I'm avoiding:** - External action overwhelming the two-hander - Making her a pure victim OR a pure manipulator — she must be both dangerous and genuinely wounded - Alternating POV (keeps her mysterious) - A saccharine "ride off together" ending - Gratuitous sex that hasn't been earned by the tension **Character names:** Him — **Kell Aldric Vance** (he goes by Vance, surname-use is the professional distance he clings to). Her — **Ixelle** (later, possibly a truer name revealed). # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Three Days Out** Day 1, afternoon. Establish the wagon, the road, Vance's weariness, Ixelle strung up in back — wrists bound above her head to the crossbeam, mage-iron collar at her throat. She has been talking for six hours. He has not answered once. She is witty, crude, needling — trying to find a seam. He's a professional; he's done this before. Establish his backstory in glimpses (ex-soldier, debts, this job will set him up for life). Establish the bounty: a sealed writ from a consortium in Irongate, payment in gold weight, no questions. End with her asking a question so precisely aimed at a wound of his that he finally turns and looks at her. She smiles. First crack. **Chapter 2 — Campfire, Night One** Evening of Day 1. He makes camp. The intimate logistics of a captive: feeding her, letting her piss, checking the collar. He keeps professional distance. She keeps talking — now more varied: stories, a filthy joke, a question about his name. Hints at what she is: she names the kind of binding on her wrists correctly, she knows Irongate too well. He wonders for the first time if the consortium wants her for something other than punishment. He dreams of her that night — not overtly sexual, but unsettling. Wakes to find her awake, watching him. "You talk in your sleep, Vance." He didn't tell her his name. **Chapter 3 — Day Two, Morning Road** Day 2. Rain. The wagon slows. She is quieter today, which unsettles him more than chatter. When she does speak, it's surgical: she tells him what his commander's name was in the war. She knows things she shouldn't. He finally engages — asks her how. She tells him she reads people; she doesn't need magic for it (the collar would stop that anyway — she reminds him of that, pointedly). A genuine conversation. He catches himself enjoying it. Small intimacy: her bound hands have gone numb; he adjusts them. His fingers on her wrist. Neither comments. End the chapter with the sound of hoofbeats behind them on the road. **Chapter 4 — The Riders** Midday Day 2. Three riders overtake them — not random bandits; they are hunting *her*, and they are not the consortium's men. Ixelle, still bound, calls out a warning to Vance in a single clipped sentence that saves his life. Short, sharp violence. He kills two; the third flees. He takes a cut along the ribs. Reveal: these were sent by someone who does *not* want her to reach Irongate. Which means Irongate is where she wants to be. He realizes the bounty may not be what he thinks. He looks at her, bloodied and bound and unsurprised, and she says: "Now we talk properly." **Chapter 5 — The Spring** Late Day 2. He pulls off the road to a hidden spring to clean his wound and water the horses. Here, the first sustained physical intimacy: he uncuffs her (collar still on) to let her bathe — practical necessity, but fraught. She tends his wound. Their hands, close. She gives him a partial truth: she is the natural daughter of an Irongate high-seat, and the consortium is her father's enemies — but she wants to reach Irongate because her arrival, as their prisoner, will trigger a political collapse her father needs. She's been letting herself be transported. He is a pawn. She tells him this because she has decided she likes him, which is inconvenient for both of them. They don't touch beyond the wound-tending. The tension is unbearable. He re-cuffs her. His hands shake. **Chapter 6 — Night Two** Cold night. He cannot sleep. Neither can she. She asks him to come closer — for warmth, she claims, shivering. He does. Close quarters under a shared cloak. Talk becomes touch. The first real kiss, initiated by her despite her bound hands — she uses her teeth, her tongue, the whole of her dangerous mouth. He unfastens her wrists from the post but not from each other. She remains bound; this is deliberate on both their parts. The first sexual encounter — heated, quiet, complicated. The bondage is part of the heat; so is the fact that he is still, technically, her captor. Afterward she doesn't speak, which is its own revelation. He holds her. He is doomed and knows it. **Chapter 7 — Day Three, the Long Road** Dawn. A changed man in the driver's seat. She is back in her bonds but everything is different — looks exchanged, small touches when he feeds her, a second encounter at midday when he pulls the wagon off the road without quite deciding to. This one is slower, more tender; he lets her hands free this time and she traces his scars. Afterward, the harder conversation: he asks what happens when he delivers her. She tells him the rest of the truth — her arrival is a signal; people will die; she will not be freed quickly; he should leave the gold and ride. He asks what she wants from him. "Deliver me," she says. "And then wait." She can't promise more. He says nothing, which she correctly reads as yes. Irongate's walls appear on the horizon at dusk. **Chapter 8 — Irongate** Day 3, evening into night. The city. He drives the wagon to the consortium's gate. Tension: will he go through with it? He does. The handover is cold and transactional; she is taken into a stone hall, bound still, not looking back — which is how he knows she meant what she said. He collects the gold. It is every coin promised, a king's ransom in truth. He walks out into the city. Close with him in a rented room above a tavern, gold bag on the bed, the sound of bells beginning somewhere in the high quarter — something is starting. He thinks of her mouth, her voice, the word *wait*. He pours a drink. He waits. Final line echoes the prompt: she talked him into an early grave after all — the man he used to be is dead. What comes next, he doesn't yet know. He waits.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer coming to this prompt — "three days out from Irongate, tiefling strung up in back, king's ransom, talk him into an early grave" — would likely notice first that it's *already* a complete tonal package. The voice in that prompt is wry, weary, with an implicit grin. A human would probably write *toward* that voice rather than building elaborate scaffolding around it. They might draft the opening first, find the rhythm, and let the plot emerge from character. A human would likely be much more willing to: - **Leave things unexplained.** Not every lore detail needs a backstory. Irongate can just *be* Irongate. The consortium can just exist. The mage-iron collar doesn't need a paragraph. - **Let scenes breathe or end abruptly.** Not every chapter needs a tidy dramatic arc. Some chapters might be mostly one conversation. Some might end mid-thought. - **Write ugly sentences on purpose.** Clipped, blunt, ungrammatical when the character's mood calls for it. - **Let the erotic content be weird and specific.** Real desire is not tasteful. It's got elbows and embarrassment and particular fixations. - **Resist the moral arc.** A human writer might let Vance remain morally compromised at the end — not redeemed, not damned, just different. - **Commit to a specific, limited sensibility.** Not trying to hit every beat (funny AND tragic AND sexy AND philosophical) — picking 2-3 things and doing them well. # Common LLM Failure Modes **1. The Everything-Balanced Problem.** LLMs tend to hit every note: some humor, some tragedy, some tenderness, some action, some world-building, some interiority. A human writer often sacrifices half of these to make the other half sing. Our plan currently tries to do all of them. **2. Thematic Over-Explanation.** LLMs tend to name themes aloud. A character will literally think "the old version of me is dead" or "I am doomed." A human writer would show this through action or absence, not naming. Our final chapter has him thinking "the man he used to be is dead" — that's an LLM tell. **3. The Pristine Arc.** LLMs love cause-and-effect cleanliness: setup → escalation → climax → resolution. Every chapter has a function. Every detail pays off. Real fiction has dangling threads, scenes that exist just for atmosphere, details that don't go anywhere. Our plan is suspiciously well-engineered. **4. Reflexive Resolution.** LLMs tend to wrap things up, even when ambiguous. Our ending — Vance waiting with the gold, bells ringing — is *structured* ambiguity, which is different from real ambiguity. It feels like a movie ending. **5. Safe Erotica.** LLMs, when writing erotic content, tend to default to either: flowery romance-novel language, tasteful fade-to-black, or a weirdly mechanical anatomy inventory. Human erotic writing has rhythm, obsession, specific fixations, awkwardness, and often a touch of embarrassment or absurdity. It's embodied, not aesthetic. **6. Over-Characterization Through Backstory.** LLMs explain characters by giving them pasts: ex-soldier, debts, war trauma. Our Vance has "ex-soldier, debts, war" stamped on him. A human writer might give him *one* specific, weird detail (he hums a particular merchant's jingle when nervous; he can't stand the smell of juniper) instead of a Backstory Package. **7. Dialogue-as-Delivery-System.** LLM dialogue tends to deliver information or theme efficiently. Real dialogue interrupts itself, repeats, misses the point, goes in circles, drops non-sequiturs. Ixelle's "witty needling" risks becoming a series of zingers that always land. **8. The Show-Not-Tell Overcorrection.** LLMs have been trained to show, not tell, which can lead to bloated sensory description at the expense of propulsion. Sometimes *telling* is the right move. "He was tired." Done. **9. Adjective Layering.** LLM prose loves tricolons and paired adjectives ("tense, witty, heat-building"; "leather, woodsmoke, iron, sweat"). Human prose is more willing to pick one word and trust it. **10. The Competence Cascade.** Every character is good at their job. Vance is competent. Ixelle is brilliant at reading people. The attackers are skilled. Nobody fumbles, panics, or is bad at anything except in scripted moments. Real people are inconsistent. # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan **The structure is too symmetrical.** 2 chapters for Day 1, 3 for Day 2, 3 for Day 3 — plus the "one external event at the midpoint" is textbook screenplay architecture. This is the kind of neat plan an LLM loves because it maps cleanly. A human writer might have one day eat four chapters while another passes in a single scene. The rhythm should be uneven. **The attacker chapter (Ch 4) is suspicious.** It exists because the plan decided it needed an "external pressure valve" at the midpoint. That's a craft-book reason, not a story reason. It introduces violence and information-reveal in one efficient package. It's also the most generic chapter — ambush, fight, wound, reveal. A human writer might not need it, or might handle it very differently (offscreen, aftermath-only, or much weirder). **Ixelle's "layered reveal" is over-structured.** She reveals: (a) she's valuable, (b) she's political, (c) she's her father's pawn, (d) her arrival is a trigger. This is an LLM's idea of good pacing — information doled out in tidy increments. A human might have her just *tell him* the whole thing in chapter 3 in a flat voice, and the tension becomes "does he believe her" rather than "what is she." **The sex is in the expected places.** Ch 6 first encounter, Ch 7 second, tender vs. heated. This is exactly where an outline-generator would put them. A human writer might place the first encounter weirdly early (end of Ch 3?) or late (Ch 7 only), or split it across moments. The two-encounter structure with a "tender daylight reprise" is Romance Novel 101. **"He is doomed and knows it."** This line appears in the plan and reflects the core LLM trope: the character narrating their own arc to the reader. The whole "he falls for her knowing he's being played" framing is an intellectualization that risks making Vance's interiority too self-aware. Real people rationalize and avoid; they don't narrate their own descent with clarity. **Ixelle risks becoming a Manic Pixie Femme Fatale.** Clever, wounded-underneath, reads him perfectly, makes filthy jokes, has an agenda but genuine feelings. This is a composite of every "dangerous woman" LLM trope. She needs specificity — a weird fixation, an ugly habit, a blind spot, a moment she's just plain wrong. **Vance is a stock type.** Grizzled, weary, professional, ex-soldier with debts. The only distinguishing feature is that he chooses her in the end. He needs a strangeness. A habit. An obsession. A belief that doesn't fit. Something that makes him *him* and not "Bounty Hunter #3." **The ending is trying too hard.** Gold on the bed, bells ringing in the high quarter, him waiting with a drink. This is atmospheric *cinema*. The final line "What comes next, he doesn't yet know" is pure voice-over. A human writer might end mid-scene, mid-sentence, on an image without commentary, or on something small and weird (he thinks about her laugh; he eats a pear; he doesn't drink the drink). **The title-phrase callback is a classic LLM move.** Echoing the opening prompt at the end ("talked him into an early grave after all") is satisfying in a neat way, but it's also the most obvious structural choice. A human might deliberately *not* callback, or callback earlier so the ending can be something else. **The world is too coherent.** Mage-iron collars, consortium writs, political factions, signal-arrivals triggering collapses — this is building out the world to justify the plot. A human writer might leave 80% of this blurry. Vance doesn't need to understand the politics. Neither does the reader. The gold is gold. Irongate is where it happens. # Ideas to Make It More Human **On voice/prose:** - Let some sentences be ugly. Fragments. Repetitions. - Cut adjective pairs. Pick one. - Let Vance's interiority be evasive, not clarifying. He should avoid thinking about things we want to know. - Put in at least one weird, specific sensory detail per chapter that doesn't mean anything (a particular bird call, the smell of a cold pipe). **On character:** - Give Vance one odd specific: he collects buttons off the dead. He prays to a god he no longer believes in. He reads when he can. - Give Ixelle a moment she's wrong, or boring, or petty. She gets a song stuck in her head and hums it for an hour. She cries from pain, not artfully. She misreads him once. - Let both characters have silence that isn't loaded. Sometimes people don't talk because they don't feel like it. **On plot:** - Consider cutting or radically changing the attacker chapter. What if the "external event" is a stranger at a crossroads inn who recognizes her, and Vance has to decide in a moment? What if nothing attacks them and the threat is purely internal? - Consider whether Ixelle's full truth comes out earlier, flatly, so the tension is belief rather than revelation. - Consider an uneven chapter-to-day mapping: Day 1 takes 3 chapters because it's where the slow work happens; Day 3 is just Ch 8. **On erotic content:** - Make it specific and a little off. Not aesthetic. The bondage shouldn't be sexy in the abstract — it should be awkward, complicated, and hot *because* of the awkwardness. - Let there be a failed moment. A near-encounter that stops for a real reason (fear, pain, something unsexy). - Don't sanitize aftermath. Bodies are strange. People say dumb things after sex. **On theme:** - Resist the urge to have Vance *realize* anything aloud (or in thought). Let the reader do the work. The "old self is dead" theme should never be named. - Don't have the story be "about" transformation. Let it be about three days and then end. **On structure:** - Unbalance the chapters. Maybe one is 500 words. Maybe one is mostly dialogue with no narration. - Consider not having every chapter end on a hook or image. Some just end. - Cut the bells and the gold on the bed. Find a weirder final image — or no final image, just a line of dialogue. **On the ending specifically:** - What if he doesn't wait? What if he does something unexpected — goes to the temple, gets drunk poorly, writes a letter he won't send? - What if the story ends before Irongate is reached? At the gate, on the road in sight of it, mid-approach? - What if Ixelle, at the handover, does something tiny and human — a look, a word — that doesn't resolve anything but stays with him? - What if the final line isn't about him at all, but about the horse, or the weather, or a stranger passing? The core directive for making this more human: **be willing to be less impressive, less complete, less balanced, and more specific.** Trust the reader. Leave things broken.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention An 8-chapter fantasy erotica piece (~8000 words) about a bounty hunter and the red-headed tiefling he's hauling to Irongate. The engine is her mouth — she talks, and talking is the weapon. The prompt's voice is wry, weary, grinning at its own danger. I'm writing toward that voice, not around it. **Voice & prose.** Third-limited, stuck to Vance. Lean sentences. Fragments when the rhythm wants them. One unnecessary, specific sensory detail per chapter that doesn't serve plot (a particular bird, a smell, a horse's habit). I'll cut paired adjectives and trust single words. Vance's interiority is *evasive* — he avoids thinking about what the reader wants to know. He rationalizes, dodges, notices the wrong things. I will not have him name his own arc. The phrase "early grave" will not be echoed at the end. **Character specificity.** - *Vance* has one odd thing: he reads. He carries a water-damaged book of someone else's letters that he didn't buy, won't explain, and reads at night. He is not especially clever, not especially kind. He is tired and he is good at his job. - *Ixelle* is sharp but inconsistent. She gets a drinking song stuck in her head and hums it for hours. She cries from pain without dignity. She is wrong about one thing, and petty about another. Her "reading people" is real but not infallible. **Plot compression.** I'm cutting the ambush. No riders. The external pressure is a stranger at a roadside inn on Day 2 — one uneasy encounter, no fight. This keeps the story a two-hander. Ixelle's truth comes out earlier and flatter than the previous plan had it — she tells him most of it in Ch 3, and the remaining tension is *does he believe her* and *does it matter*. **Erotic content.** Specific, a little awkward, not aesthetic. The bondage is real — it chafes, her arms go numb, the collar leaves a mark. The first real physical encounter happens earlier than expected (end of Ch 5, carrying into Ch 6). There's a failed moment too — something stops, not artfully. Aftermath is unflattering. I want heat that comes from particularity, not from choreography. **Structure.** Uneven. Day 1 takes chapters 1–3 (slow build, this is where the work is). Day 2 takes chapters 4–6. Day 3 is chapters 7–8. Some chapters end on images; some just stop. **What I'm avoiding.** - Any line where Vance names his own transformation - Callback to "early grave" at the close - A violent setpiece - Layered political reveal; I'm giving the truth early and letting the tension be human - A tidy ending with gold on the bed and bells ringing. The ending will be smaller, weirder, and off-center. - Ixelle as a composite femme-fatale; she must have at least one moment of being simply a person - World-building paragraphs about Irongate, the consortium, mage-iron, tieflings. References stay glancing. **The ending.** He delivers her. He gets paid. He does not wait for her in a rented room with a drink. He does something smaller and less explicable. The final image is off to the side of the story — not the story's climax restated, but a detail that refuses to resolve. Ixelle is not seen again on the page after the handover. Her absence is the last thing. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Talker** Day 1, mid-afternoon on the road. Wagon, two horses, Vance, Ixelle bound upright to the back crossbeam. She has been talking since midmorning. Establish her voice before his — she gets the first line. He ignores her. The chapter is mostly her talking (filthy jokes, observations about the horses, a surprisingly good impression of a minor noble, a drinking song) and him not answering. One paragraph establishes the job: sealed writ, consortium's gold, three days out. A single line about his book (he's read the same letter twice this morning without meaning to). She asks nothing personal the entire chapter. She is working up to something and he knows it and doesn't want to know what. End: she finally asks a question, and it's mundane — what the horses' names are. He answers without thinking. Then regrets it. **Chapter 2 — Camp** Night 1. Logistics of a captive: water, food, piss. Unflattering. He unties her hands from the beam but leaves the collar and her wrists cuffed. She is stiff, ungraceful, swears at him when her legs give out. She eats the stew he makes and says it's bad, which it is. By firelight she asks about the book. He doesn't answer. She guesses, wrongly, what it is (a treatise, a ledger). He almost corrects her and doesn't. He ties her back. She hums the drinking song as she falls asleep. He lies awake listening to her breathe, annoyed with himself for listening. One weird detail: a fox watches the camp for a while from the tree line and then leaves. **Chapter 3 — The Truth, Cheaply Given** Day 2, morning. Rain starts and stops. She is not chatty today — which he notices, which annoys him. When she does speak, she tells him, flatly, most of the truth: her father is someone in Irongate, the consortium is his enemy, her being delivered to them is the point, she has let herself be caught. She says this the way you'd describe the weather. He doesn't believe her — or he tells himself he doesn't. He asks why she's telling him. She says: "Because you've read the same page of that book four times." The chapter's emotional core: he adjusts her cuffs because her hands have gone blue. His fingers, her wrist. Neither speaks through the adjustment. After, she thanks him in a small voice that sounds like a different person. That voice unsettles him more than all the cleverness. **Chapter 4 — The Inn** Day 2, late afternoon. A roadside inn, just for hot food and grain for the horses. He leaves her in the wagon with a blanket thrown over her head and shoulders — the horns would draw notice. Inside, a stranger at the bar recognizes the wagon. Short, wary exchange. The stranger doesn't threaten Vance; he just looks at him a long time and says, "She got you talking yet?" and leaves. Vance eats his stew. The stew here is also bad. He buys a pear for Ixelle on the way out with no clear reason. Back in the wagon he gives it to her without comment. She eats it while he drives. The sound of her eating it fills the chapter's second half. He does not ask her about the stranger. She does not ask about the pear. **Chapter 5 — The Spring, and What Happens There** Evening Day 2. He turns off the road to a spring he knows, a mile off the track. Practical reasons: water, wash the horses' legs, she needs to clean herself, so does he. He unlocks her cuffs but not the collar. She bathes at a distance, her back to him, not performing it. He looks anyway and is ashamed of it. She catches him looking. She says something — not clever, just tired: "You could come over here." He does. The first physical encounter begins here. It is awkward. She's cold, he's clothed, there is a long moment of logistics (the collar's chain, his belt, where to kneel). It is not elegant. It is specific. They don't finish here — she stops, actually stops, because her shoulder cramps from the cold and how she's been bound all day. She laughs at herself. He's never heard her laugh at herself. End chapter with them not touching, both half-dressed, the horses cropping grass nearby. **Chapter 6 — Night Two** Back at the fire, later. He has not re-cuffed her. The collar is still on. They eat. They don't talk about what happened at the spring. She asks about the book finally, really asks, and he tells her: letters between two people he never knew, found in a house he was sent to burn. He's been reading them for six years. She doesn't say anything clever about it. She says, "That's sad, Vance," and it lands wrong and right at once. They move under the same blanket for warmth, or say that's why. The second encounter, quieter, unhurried. Not narrated as romance — narrated as two specific bodies doing a specific thing. Afterward she falls asleep on his arm and he can't move it and doesn't want to. He thinks: *I should cuff her again.* He doesn't. He stays awake, arm numb, watching the fire. **Chapter 7 — Day Three** Morning. Irongate is a smudge on the horizon by noon. She is quiet. So is he. He has re-cuffed her — she watched him do it without comment, and the watching was worse than protest. On the road he asks her what happens when he delivers her. She tells him the part she didn't tell in Ch 3: she will not walk out of the consortium's hall. Her arrival is a death, not a trigger. Her father needs a martyr, not a daughter. She says this plainly. He asks why she's doing it. She gives an answer that isn't satisfying — something about a brother, or a debt, half-sentence, she doesn't finish it. She doesn't ask him to save her. He doesn't offer. They drive. The city gets closer. She hums the drinking song again, softer. He finds he knows the tune now. **Chapter 8 — Irongate** Dusk. The city gate, the consortium's compound, the handover. Cold, transactional, brief. She doesn't look at him. He doesn't look at her. The gold is weighed. He signs a thing. He walks out. Outside, he stands on the street not knowing what to do. He doesn't go to a tavern. He doesn't drink. He walks the wagon back to the stables, unharnesses the horses, gives them names aloud to the stable boy — the names she used for them in Ch 1, which were not their names. The stable boy writes them down wrong. The last paragraph is small: Vance in the stable yard, the smell of straw, a pear in his pocket he doesn't remember buying, the sound of bells somewhere — not explained, not punctuating anything, just bells. He stands there. End.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Kell Aldric Vance **Background.** Forty-one. Born in a river town called Hesk that nobody south of the mountains has heard of. Conscripted into the Duchy's Fifth Regiment at nineteen, the kind of conscription that's optional in the way a cliff is optional. Served eleven years — mostly border work, a few real battles, the sort of campaign that makes men either drunks or quiet. He went quiet. Mustered out at thirty with a bad knee, a bounty-hunter's license (which was a thing the Duchy handed out in lieu of pensions to veterans they wanted off the payroll), and no family to speak of. His mother was dead by then and he hadn't written anyway. He's been working writs for eleven years. He's good at it because he doesn't ask questions and doesn't lose nerve. He's made enough, over the years, to live. This Irongate job is different — it's the first one that would let him stop. He hasn't let himself think about what stopping would look like. **Personality and flaws.** Not clever. Not stupid either. Methodical. He's the kind of man who checks a knot twice and then a third time and gets annoyed at himself for the third. Not cruel, but has done cruel things and squared them away somewhere he doesn't visit. He drinks moderately. Eats badly. Doesn't pray, but catches himself sometimes saying *gods willing* and it bothers him. He rationalizes. He is, specifically, a man who tells himself small lies to make the day work — *she's just talking*, *the knee is fine*, *I'll read the letters through to the end one of these days* — and he knows he does it and doesn't stop. **Idiosyncrasies.** He carries a book of letters. He found it six years ago in a merchant's house he'd been hired to clear out — a woman who'd defaulted on debts, whose things were being sold to settle them. The letters were between her and someone named Arem. He doesn't know who Arem was. Husband, brother, lover, uncertain. He took the book because it was going to be burned with the rest of the paper goods, and something about it stopped him. He's read it through twice and is on his third pass. He won't finish it. When he gets near the end he starts over. He cannot explain this to anyone including himself. He hums when he's nervous but does not know he does it. He eats the ends of loaves first. He has a superstition about crossroads — won't camp at one — and pretends he doesn't. **Physicality.** Tall-ish, heavy through the shoulders, going soft in the middle but not badly. Brown hair cut short, grey coming in at the temples. A face that people describe as *serious* because they can't think of anything else. Beard trimmed when he remembers, which is not often. One front tooth chipped diagonally — a campaign thing, not a fight. Moves carefully because of the knee, which he favors without noticing. Hands are big and scarred in the small unpoetic ways hands get scarred: a white line from a horse bite, a crooked little finger from a door. When he listens he tilts his head slightly to the left — old hearing damage on that side, a cannon at Varris. **Motives and allegiances.** The gold. He'll tell himself the gold, and it'll be true, and it won't be the whole truth. Beneath the gold is a flat exhaustion — he has been doing this too long and the thought of another year of it sits on him like wet wool. The Irongate job is a door. He is not loyal to the consortium, or to any authority. He was loyal to his regiment once and that got beaten out of him slowly. He has no lovers, no debts of the heart, no one who waits for him. He tells himself this is freedom. **Voice samples.** *Internal:* He'd said the horses' names out loud and she'd heard. That was stupid. That was the kind of stupid that gets men killed, and he'd done it without thinking, because she'd asked like a person asks, and he'd answered like a person answers. He'd have to watch that. He was already not watching it. *Dialogue:* "Eat it or don't. It's the same to me." *Dialogue:* "I didn't ask." *Dialogue (later, quieter):* "Letters. Just letters. Two people I never met." --- # Ixelle **Background.** Twenty-six. The natural daughter of a man who holds a high seat in Irongate's inner council — she will not say his name aloud and hasn't for years. Her mother was a courtesan of some reputation, tiefling, dead of a fever when Ixelle was eleven. Her father kept her in the household as a kind of open secret — educated, fed, ignored by the legitimate children, tolerated by his wife because tolerance cost less than scandal. She grew up in libraries and kitchens, which is a specific kind of education. At nineteen she left — not dramatically, just walked out — and spent five years in the south doing things she mostly won't discuss, though some of them left marks. She came back a year ago because her younger half-brother — the legitimate one, the one she was supposed to resent — wrote her a letter. She came. He was dead by the time she arrived. The consortium killed him, or had him killed; her father cannot prove it and cannot move against them without proof. Ixelle can. She is the proof, or will be, when she is delivered to the consortium and they do what they have already decided to do. **Personality and flaws.** Quick-mouthed, and knows it's her best weapon, and occasionally it runs her when she means to be running it. She talks when she's afraid. She talks when she's angry. She talks when she's bored, which is often. She reads people well but not perfectly — she misreads loyal ones as weak ones, and has been hurt by this twice. She is vain about her voice and not vain about her face, which is unusual. She is petty in small ways: holds grudges against people who've spelled her name wrong, prefers one brand of soap and pouts when she can't get it. She cries when she's in pain and does not do it prettily. She laughs with her whole body. She has a drinking song she picked up from a barmaid in Dovrin eight years ago and has not been able to get rid of; it surfaces when she stops paying attention. **Idiosyncrasies.** She names things she has no business naming — horses, trees, rocks. The wagon horses she called Plum and Soldier within an hour of being tied in the back. She chews the inside of her cheek when she's thinking. She can do a surprisingly good impression of a minor noble she met once. She is afraid of rivers but not of the sea. She knows how to pick two kinds of lock and not a third, and the third has been a problem. When she's tired she drops into her mother's accent and doesn't notice. She thinks this is private; it isn't. **Physicality.** Small, not fragile. Red hair — not auburn, not copper, *red* — coarse and growing out of a bad cut. Freckles across the nose and shoulders. Skin a warm off-red, more pronounced at the hands and feet. Horns small, swept back, one chipped at the tip. Tail long and expressive; she has excellent control of it and uses it the way other people use their hands when they talk, which she finds out, too late, that Vance watches. Eyes a pale yellow that unsettles people who haven't seen a tiefling up close. A long white scar along the inside of her left forearm she does not discuss. She moves, when unbound, with the economy of someone who's had to leave rooms quickly. **Motives and allegiances.** She is going to Irongate to die, or something close to it, so that her father has what he needs to break the consortium. She is doing this for her brother, who was kind to her when no one else was, and because she has no one else to do it for. She does not especially love her father. She does not hate him. She is using him the way he is using her, and they both know it, and neither of them has said so. She did not expect Vance. She did not plan for Vance. He is not in the plan. She is, over three days, becoming a small, dangerous problem to herself. **Voice samples.** *Dialogue (Chapter 1):* "The bay one on the left is Plum. Don't argue, she looks like a Plum. The other one is Soldier, because he's got that look, like he's seen things. Tragic. A war horse, reduced to pulling a cart for a man who won't speak to a lady." *Dialogue (Chapter 3, flat):* "My father is Terev Halsin. The consortium killed my brother. I am going to Irongate to be killed in a way that will let my father kill them back. You don't have to believe me. I don't particularly care. Your hands are cold." *Dialogue (Chapter 5, at the spring):* "You could come over here." *Dialogue (later, quieter):* "That's sad, Vance." *Internal she'd never say:* She had decided, against her own interests and quite definitely against his, that she liked him. It was the worst thing that had happened to her on this road, and several bad things had happened to her on this road.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — The Talker "The bay one on the left is Plum. Don't argue, she looks like a Plum. The other one is Soldier, because he's got that look, like he's seen things. Tragic. A war horse, reduced to pulling a cart for a man who won't speak to a lady." Vance did not speak to her. He hadn't spoken to her since dawn and he wasn't going to start in the middle of the afternoon when the sun had finally worked through the cloud and was making the back of his neck itch under the collar of his coat. The road was bad here, rutted from the spring wagons, and he was giving the horses their heads on the easier patches and taking them back on the stones. He'd done this road nine times in eleven years. He knew where the ruts were. "Soldier's a bit lame on the off hind, have you noticed? No, of course you've noticed, you're the sort of man who notices. I just mean it sympathetically. Poor Soldier. All those battles, just to end up here, carrying me to my doom. You too, come to think of it." Behind him, in the covered back of the wagon, she was strung up to the crossbeam by her wrists, cuffs through an iron ring, the ring bolted. The mage-iron collar at her throat had a chain that ran to a second ring on the floor. She had enough slack to sit or to stand half-stooped. She had been standing half-stooped for about an hour now, which was the sort of thing she did to make a point he was determined not to hear. "I'll tell you what I've been thinking about. The shape of the word *ransom*. It's an ugly word, isn't it. You can hear the price in it. *Ran-som*. Like a coin being bitten. Did I ever tell you how much I'm worth?" She had not. She had alluded. She had joked. She had quoted, once, what she claimed was the sum on the writ, and her number had been close enough that it made his back go cold, and he had not looked around and she had not, he thought, seen him not look around, but he was not sure. "Fine. Be like that. Tell me about yourself, then. Your hopes. Your dreams. Your childhood pets. I bet you had a dog. A big one, with a sad name. Duke. Was he called Duke?" The writ sat in an oilcloth pouch in the chest under the driver's bench. Sealed with the consortium's mark, a pair of hands clasping a weight. Payment in gold on delivery, weighed at the compound, no questions. Three days out of Irongate now — two and a half, if the weather held. He had met the consortium's man at a public house in Harrow, and the man had not given a name, and Vance had not asked, and the coin advance had been enough to shoe both horses and replace the wagon's rear axle, and that was the kind of advance that meant the balance would be real. He didn't like the job. He hadn't liked it from the first hour. He'd taken it anyway. She'd been waiting for him, more or less — the arrangement was a hand-off at a farm two days out of Harrow, and she'd come out of the farmhouse already cuffed, looked him over, said *oh dear* in a tone he couldn't place, and let herself be walked to the wagon. She hadn't struggled then and she didn't now. What she did was talk. "Plum's got a fly on her ear. Just there. No, your other left. Well, she'll manage. Plum manages." He shifted his weight on the bench. The knee grumbled. He ignored it. Under the oilcloth with the writ was the book. He'd read two pages that morning before harnessing, and then he'd read the same page again at the noon halt without noticing, and he had not let himself read it a third time, which counted for something. It was a bad habit. He was aware it was a bad habit. He planned to do something about it in a nonspecific future that kept moving. "You know what I keep coming back to. You have a good face for silence. Most men, you put them in a silence, they start leaking out of it. You don't. You sit in it like it fits you. It's impressive, actually. I mean that." He didn't answer. "I could work with that face. In another life. I could have made you a fortune." He didn't answer. "Or spent one on you." A wood dove was calling somewhere off to the right, the three-note call, and then the four-note, and then the three again. He listened to it and pretended not to listen to her. The road bent around a stand of alders and straightened out again toward the pale line of the hills. She went quiet for a while. Long enough that he started to feel the quiet, which was, he suspected, the point. The wagon creaked. Soldier blew through his nose. The dove gave up and flew off across the field. "What are their names, really." He turned his head half an inch. He didn't mean to. The question was nothing. He'd been braced for something and this was nothing, just a woman asking after two horses, and his mouth opened before the rest of him caught up. "Bet and Harrow," he said. "Oh," she said. "Bet and Harrow. That's nice. That's — those are good names." He faced forward again. His ears were hot. He gripped the reins tighter than he needed to and watched the road and told himself it was nothing, a word, two words, names weren't anything. Behind him she made a small pleased sound and said, "Hello, Bet. Hello, Harrow. I'm Ixelle. He won't tell me his name but we'll get there." He didn't answer. But now he was listening.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through it systematically. **Voice and Prose:** The chapter establishes Vance's third-limited perspective with economy and precision. The prose is lean without being skeletal. Sentences vary in length naturally, and fragments appear where rhythm demands them ("He didn't answer." repeated as a structural beat). The voice is wry and weary, matching the prompt's register exactly. The detail about the wood dove — three-note call, four-note, three again — is the kind of specific sensory irrelevance the plan called for, and it earns its place without announcing itself. **Character Work:** Ixelle's voice is immediately distinctive. Her opening monologue about the horses is charming, slightly performative, and reveals her method: she names things, she anthropomorphizes, she talks at people rather than to them. The line "You too, come to think of it" is a small, well-placed knife. Vance's interiority is appropriately evasive — we get his rationalizations, his awareness of his own habits, his self-deception about the book. The detail about reading the same page twice without noticing is excellent character work, showing rather than telling his distraction. **The Ending:** The chapter's final beat — him answering without thinking, then regretting it, then her using the names to introduce herself to the horses — is genuinely good. It's small, it's earned, and it lands with the right weight. "But now he was listening" is a clean close that doesn't overstate. **Dialogue:** Ixelle's dialogue is the chapter's engine and it mostly works. The voice is consistent, the jokes land without being too clever, and the shift to the mundane question ("What are their names, really") is well-executed. The "Oh dear" callback in the backstory paragraph is a nice touch. One minor concern: some of her lines ("I could work with that face. In another life. I could have made you a fortune. Or spent one on you.") edge toward the archetype of the witty captive, though they don't quite tip over. **Plan Adherence:** The chapter follows its plan closely. Ixelle gets the first line. He ignores her. The job is established in one paragraph. The book appears. She asks nothing personal until the end. The horses' names are given and immediately regretted. The plan specified she'd hum a drinking song — this is absent, which is a minor deviation, though not damaging. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable in its competence. There's little that genuinely surprises — the dynamic of the talkative captive and the silent captor is well-worn, and while the execution is above average, it doesn't transcend the setup. The "early grave" from the prompt isn't echoed (as planned), but the chapter also doesn't find a truly unexpected angle on the premise. The writ description ("a pair of hands clasping a weight") is the one moment that feels slightly generic. **Purple Prose / Forced Metaphor:** Largely absent. The prose is controlled. "The kind of advance that meant the balance would be real" is functional rather than poetic. No strained metaphors. **Coherence:** Excellent. The spatial and temporal logic is clear throughout. The logistics of her bondage are established without belaboring them. **Overall:** This is accomplished, controlled opening chapter work. It does what it sets out to do with skill and restraint. The characters are alive, the voice is consistent, and the ending earns its quiet satisfaction. It's not transcendent, but it's genuinely good. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — Camp He pulled off the road at the usual place, a flat shoulder of packed earth between two fields where the ditch ran shallow and a person could see a good way in both directions. Not a crossroads. He watered the horses at the ditch and hobbled them and built the fire while she watched him from the wagon through the gap in the canvas. He could feel her watching. He made the fire anyway. When the fire was going he climbed up into the wagon and unbolted her wrists from the ring in the beam. She made a noise when her arms came down — not a word, just air — and he caught her under one elbow because she'd gone that stiff. Her wrists stayed cuffed. The collar stayed on. He walked her to the edge of the firelight and turned his back. "Gracious of you," she said. "Piss or don't." She pissed. It took a while. Her legs had gone to sleep, he could hear it in the way she shifted. When she was done he walked her back without looking at her and sat her on the log he'd dragged over, and she sat heavily, and said, "Ow," in a flat voice that had nothing clever in it. He gave her stew out of the pot with a wooden spoon. He'd made it from what he had — barley, a bit of salt pork, an onion past its prime. She ate it bent over the bowl with her cuffed hands because he'd cuffed her in front for the meal, which he didn't do for everyone. "This is terrible," she said, chewing. "Yes." "I mean genuinely. It's not even bad in an interesting way. It's just bad." "Eat it or don't." She ate it. She finished the bowl and held it out for more and he gave her more because he'd made too much, as he did every time, because he could not for the life of him learn to cook for one. She ate the second bowl slower. The fire popped. Somewhere out past the hobbled horses a fox was watching the camp — he'd caught the shine of its eyes twice now at the edge of the light. It wasn't coming in. It was just watching. He didn't point it out. "What's the book." He didn't answer. "In the chest. Under the bench. I saw it when you got the stew pot out. A book. What is it." "Nothing." "A treatise." "No." "A ledger, then. You keep accounts. Your receipts. Who you've hauled to which city, for how much. Would I be in it?" "No." "Pity." He took the bowl from her and set it by the pot. He sat down opposite her on the other log, not close. The fire was between them. Her face in the light was a warm red-brown and the horns caught the shine and threw a little of it back. He looked at the fire and not at her. "I'll guess," she said. "I like guessing. A book of poetry." He almost laughed. He didn't. "A book of maps." "No." "A —" "Stop." She stopped. She looked at him across the fire with a considering sort of look and he realized she had been watching him the way the fox was watching the camp. Not coming in. Just watching. "Alright," she said. "I'll stop. For now. Can I have some water." He gave her the skin. She drank, and handed it back, and their fingers touched and both of them pretended they hadn't. "Vance," she said, trying it out. "That's what he called you. The man at the farm. Aldric Vance." "Vance is fine." "Vance, then." "Don't wear it out." "I won't." He banked the fire and walked her back to the wagon, and she didn't argue when he bolted her wrists to the beam again, but she sighed, loud, performing it, and said, "These are going to leave marks, you know." "They leave marks on everyone." "Comforting." He pulled the canvas partway closed and went back to the fire. He sat with his back to the wagon and got the book out of the chest and held it in his lap without opening it. Two people he'd never met. A woman named, in the letters, only *yours*, and the man she wrote to, Arem, and the man who'd written back. The handwriting on one side was neat and small. The handwriting on the other side sloped and had ink blots at the ends of the lines, where the writer had sat too long thinking before lifting the pen. He'd been through the book twice. He was somewhere in the middle of the third pass and he knew — because he always knew — which letter was coming next. The one about the pear tree. He did not open the book. From the wagon, quietly, she began to hum. It was a drinking song. He knew the tune. Everybody on this road knew the tune. It was the one they sang in the public houses from Harrow to the coast, about a girl who would not be told and a boy who would not listen, and the verses got filthier as the night went on. She was humming the first verse, which was the respectable one. She hummed it slow, like a lullaby, and then she hummed it again. He sat with the book closed in his lap and listened to her hum until she stopped, and then he listened to her breathing until the breathing went even, and then he sat a while longer after that. The fox was gone. He hadn't seen it go. He put the book back in the chest without reading it. He lay down by the fire with his coat over him and stared up at the underside of the wagon and thought: *Bet and Harrow.* He had said it out loud. He had said it out loud and now she knew. He slept badly.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and discipline. The model has executed its plan with notable fidelity while maintaining the voice established in the prompt's conception. The prose is lean and controlled. Sentences are short where they need to be, and the fragments land rather than feeling like affectation. "Piss or don't" is exactly the kind of dialogue the character profile calls for — terse, not cruel, functional. The exchange about the stew is genuinely good: she's right that it's bad, he agrees, she eats it anyway, and the detail that he always makes too much because he can't cook for one is the kind of specific, unshowy character revelation that earns its place. The fox detail is handled well — it's the "unnecessary, specific sensory detail" the plan called for, and it's deployed with restraint. The model doesn't over-explain it or make it symbolic. It watches and leaves. The parallel to Ixelle watching Vance across the fire is implicit and not belabored, which is the right call. The book scene is executed with appropriate evasion. Vance holds the book without opening it. He knows which letter is coming next. He doesn't read it. This is exactly the kind of rationalization and avoidance the character profile specifies — he's a man who tells himself small lies, and not reading the book while holding it is a perfect small lie. The detail about the pear tree letter is specific enough to feel real without over-explaining. The drinking song landing as a lullaby is effective. The transition from her humming to her breathing to his lying awake is well-paced. The final line — "He had said it out loud and now she knew" — refers back to Chapter 1's ending (the horse names) and closes the chapter's emotional loop cleanly. The dialogue is consistently good. Ixelle's guesses about the book (treatise, ledger, poetry, maps) feel like her — she's probing, she's performing, she's also genuinely curious. His monosyllabic responses are right. The moment where their fingers touch on the water skin and "both of them pretended they hadn't" is shown rather than told, and it's earned. The chapter follows its plan closely: logistics of captivity (unflattering, ungraceful), the stew scene, the book inquiry, the fox, the drinking song, Vance lying awake. The one element from the plan that's slightly underplayed is Ixelle's wrongness about the book — she guesses treatise and ledger, he denies both, but the plan specified she'd guess wrongly and he'd "almost correct her and doesn't." The "almost" is present but subtle; he almost laughs at "poetry" but doesn't correct her. This is fine but slightly less developed than planned. The collar mark detail ("These are going to leave marks, you know" / "They leave marks on everyone") is a small, good exchange — it's not just about the cuffs. Weaknesses are minor. The line "Her face in the light was a warm red-brown and the horns caught the shine and threw a little of it back" is the closest thing to purple prose in the chapter, but it's not egregious — it's observational rather than decorative. The chapter is perhaps slightly low on tension compared to what the plan implied, but this is Day 1 Night, and the slow build is intentional. The ending — "He slept badly" — is exactly right. It refuses to be poetic about it. Overall, this is disciplined, specific, character-consistent work that earns its quietness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — The Truth, Cheaply Given It started raining an hour after dawn and stopped before he'd finished harnessing and started again when he was up on the bench with the reins in his hands. He pulled his hood up. He didn't pull the canvas closed on the back because the light was bad enough inside without shutting it off, and she hadn't asked him to, which was new. She wasn't talking today. He noticed it the way you notice a draft under a door — not immediately, and then all at once. He'd clucked to the horses and Bet had startled at a lapwing and he'd said *easy* aloud and waited for her to make something of it and she hadn't. He drove a mile. He drove another. The only sound from the back was the chain of her collar shifting against the ring when the wagon went over a rut. He found he was annoyed. He found he was annoyed at being annoyed. "You sick," he said, finally. "No." "You sound sick." "I sound quiet. You've mistaken them. It happens." He chewed that for a while. The rain came and went. The road climbed a slow grade through a stand of wet alder and came out on a rise where he could see the land rolling south in long grey folds. Somewhere off to the east a bell was ringing, slow, a church or a goat, it was too far to tell. "My father is Terev Halsin," she said, from the back. He didn't turn. He kept his hands on the reins. "He sits the fourth seat on the Irongate inner council. He has sat it for nineteen years. He has a wife and two legitimate children and a house on the hill with a green copper roof. You'll be able to see it from the wall when you ride in. It's the one with the green roof." "Alright." "The consortium is four houses — Verrin, Osk, Halladay, and the one that calls itself the Weights and Measures Guild, which it isn't. They have been trying to unseat my father for six years. Last autumn they killed my brother Piet. Piet was nineteen. They poisoned him at a supper he shouldn't have been at, and they did it in a way that could not be proved, and my father has been unable to move against them because he cannot prove it, and he cannot be seen to move without cause."
