Hattie had on Bernard's coat, which was too big for her and made her look smaller than she was, and her knitted cap with the pompom, and the mask with the cloth panel I had sewn last summer out of an old pillowcase. I had mine. We looked ridiculous. No one was watching. We went down the lane and then off the lane, along the track that went past the orchard to the back woods. Neither of us talked. The cart clanked. Once a crow went up out of a hedge and Hattie put her hand on my arm without looking at me, and we stopped, and we stood, and nothing came of it, and we went on. I had picked the place. I had seen it three weeks ago on a walk and chosen not to think about it. It was a clearing in a stand of birches a half-mile back from the house, where the ground dipped and a small spring came up in spring and was frozen now, and where the light, I remembered, had been odd. We came to the edge of it through hazel. Hattie stopped first. I stopped behind her. We looked. There were seven. They were in a rough circle, the way cows stand in a field in summer, facing no particular direction, not quite touching. Some were upright. One was tilted over against a birch and appeared to have grown into it, the shoulder fused to the bark in a slow pale seam. One was smaller than the rest, which I did not want to think about. The pale growths on them were larger than the ones I had seen on the porch — some of them plated like shelf-fungus, some fluted like gills, one spreading in a great scalloped fan down the length of an arm. In the cold they glowed, faintly — not like lamps, nothing that clean — more the way snow glows at dusk, holding a light that isn't there. They were not clicking. That was the thing. They were silent, all of them, and the silence was worse than the clicking had been. "Oh," said Hattie, quietly. "Oh, goodness." I recognized Delia by the collar. It was the plaid collar of the shirt she had been buried in. I had helped to bury her. I had folded that collar down myself, in April, because I could not stand how it was bunched up under her chin. The shirt was rags now and the collar was rags too but the pattern was there, red and green, what was left of it, and under the pattern was what used to be Delia's neck and above the collar was what used to be Delia's face, and was not. "Hattie." "I see her." "That's Delia." "I see her, love." We stood in the hazel. I could not move my feet. It was a stupid thing. It was a stupid thing to be stopped by, after everything, after the porch, after the kitchen — but it stopped me. The collar stopped me. The small folded-down collar of a woman who had put up pears. "We'll take from the others," Hattie said. "Hattie." "We'll take from the others, Mara. Look at me. We will take from the others." I looked at her. She was small in Bernard's coat. Her eyes above the mask were wet and absolutely steady. "All right," I said. --- We worked. We worked the way you work when you have decided not to feel a thing yet — efficiently, ugly, with short sentences. I took the handsaw. Hattie held the oilcloth open. We took from the one by the birch, because it was tilted and easy to reach, and then from the small one, because — I don't want to write about the small one. We took from the small one. I will not say more than that. They did not move. Not at first. Halfway through the second, one of the others — the tallest one, at the far side of the circle — turned its head. Not fast. Slow, the way a sunflower turns through a long afternoon. Its face came around and its face was pointed at me. I had the saw in my hand. I stopped. Hattie saw me stop and she turned and she saw it too and she did not move either. We stood like that, the three of us, for I don't know how long. Long enough for my hand to start shaking on the saw. Long enough for Hattie to take one breath, and another. Then it turned its head back. I don't know what that was. I am writing this down because I do not know what that was and I do not want to forget it. It turned its head back as if it had looked at me, and seen what I was doing, and decided not to mind. We finished. We loaded the cart. We did not speak. On the walk back, halfway across the field, with the cart going *clank clank* over the ruts and my shoulders burning and my mask wet with my own breath, Hattie said, without looking at me: "Bernard's buried in a grove like that. Three counties over. Probably." I didn't answer at first. There was no answer. Then I said, "Probably not exactly like that." "No. Probably not exactly." We walked the rest of the way in silence. Tomás was at the kitchen window when we came up the lane. He had his hand flat against the glass. I made pancakes. Flat ones. He ate four.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Dialogue and Voice** The opening argument about the dog is excellent — it's economical, character-revealing, and genuinely funny without trying too hard. "Since the thing with the crow." "Oh. The crow." This is exactly the kind of shorthand that signals a real relationship. Tomás's negotiation for flat pancakes is perfectly calibrated — he's a specific child, not a symbol. The dialogue throughout avoids the trap of being expository or on-the-nose. Hattie's "We'll take from the others" is devastating precisely because it's practical. **Emotional Engagement and Show-Don't-Tell** The collar detail is the chapter's centerpiece and it earns its weight. "I had folded that collar down myself, in April, because I could not stand how it was bunched up under her chin." This is exactly the kind of specific, physical grief that the plan called for — not "she felt complicated guilt" but a remembered gesture, a folded collar, a woman who put up pears. The emotion is entirely filtered through action and detail. The line "I will not say more than that" about the small one is a controlled withholding that works — it implies without dramatizing, and trusts the reader. **The Mutant Scene** The description of the grove is handled well. "Not like lamps, nothing that clean — more the way snow glows at dusk, holding a light that isn't there" is the chapter's most lyrical moment, and it's earned by the surrounding plainness. The head-turning scene is genuinely unsettling and the decision to leave it unexplained ("I don't know what that was") is exactly right. The plan called for "tense and mundane at once" and the chapter delivers. **Potential Weaknesses** The phrase "holding a light that isn't there" is slightly precious — it's the one moment where the prose reaches for effect in a way that feels slightly manufactured. It's minor. "We worked the way you work when you have decided not to feel a thing yet — efficiently, ugly, with short sentences" is a slight tell-don't-show moment. The self-commentary ("with short sentences") is a bit meta and self-congratulatory. The chapter would be stronger if it simply showed the efficient ugliness without announcing it. The ending — flat pancakes, four of them — is exactly right. It's small, it's earned, it doesn't reach for symbolism. The hand flat against the glass is a good image without being overwrought. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely: the argument about Bijou, the route, the weather, the grove of seven, Delia's recognition, Hattie's "We'll take from the others," the head-turning mutant, Hattie's comment about Bernard. All present. Mara's voice is consistent — wry, clipped, filtering emotion through detail. Hattie's steadiness under pressure is right. Tomás is briefly but accurately rendered. **Creativity** The chapter avoids the obvious moves. The mutants are not beautiful or melancholy — they're wrong, fused to bark, plated with shelf-fungus. The horror doesn't aestheticize itself. The exchange about Bernard ("Probably not exactly like that" / "No. Probably not exactly") is quietly brilliant — two people acknowledging the unspeakable through understatement. **Minor Issues** The "oilcloth" detail is good but the logistics of what exactly they're harvesting and how is slightly vague — the plan mentions a handsaw, and the chapter uses it, but the physical reality of the work is somewhat elided. This is probably the right call given the "I will not say more than that" approach, but it does mean the chapter is slightly less viscerally grounded than Chapter 3 presumably is. Overall this is accomplished, controlled work that honors both the horror and the cozy registers without cheapening either. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 — Things That Might Mean Nothing I started dreaming of standing. Not in any particular place. A field, sometimes. Once a room with a window in it and the window was open. Once nowhere — just the fact of standing, the weight distributed between the two feet, the small constant small adjustments a body makes to stay upright, and me doing none of them and not falling. The strange part was that I was not bored. I have stood in a restaurant kitchen for fourteen hours. I know what boredom feels like in the legs and I know what boredom feels like in the head. This was not that. I was standing and time was going past me and I was all right with it going past me. I was, in the dream, patient in a way I have never been patient in my life. I woke up and lay in the dark and thought: *that's nothing. That's a dream. Dreams are rearranged days.* Then I thought: *I haven't stood like that in a day. I haven't stood like that ever.* --- I told Hattie at breakfast. I told her because if I didn't tell her I was going to keep turning it over and turning it over, and she is the person I tell things to, for better or worse. "I've been dreaming of my mother," she said. "Since when." "Since nineteen sixty-one." "Hattie." "I'm saying. You ask a seventy-six-year-old woman if she's had strange dreams lately and she says yes and you don't know what you've learned. I dreamed about my mother the night before last. That is not new information." "Have you dreamed about anything —" "Standing still in a field." "Yes." "No." "Okay." "I dreamed about the house I grew up in. I dreamed about it with the wallpaper it had before my mother changed the wallpaper. That is also not new information. I have been dreaming about that wallpaper for fifty years." "Okay." She looked at me. She put her hand on the table, not on my hand, but next to it, which is the Hattie version. "It's not nothing," she said. "I'm not saying it's nothing. I'm saying one dream is one dream." "Okay." "We'll see if there's a second one." --- Tomás said, from his chair, reading his book upside down because he had decided that morning that the book was better upside down: "I dreamed I was a potato." "When." "Last night." "What was it like." "Dark. And I was a potato." "Did anything happen." "No. I was a potato." He turned a page. "I don't want to talk about it." Hattie and I did not look at each other. --- In the afternoon I found a soft patch on the back of my hand. It was below the knuckle of the middle finger, on the left hand, which is my dominant hand, and it was the size of a pea. It was not a bruise, exactly. It didn't have the color of a bruise. It was not raised and it was not sunken, it was only, when I pressed it, softer than the skin on either side, as if the flesh under it had thought about something else for a minute. I pressed it. It did not hurt. I pressed it again. It was still soft. I washed my hands. I dried them. I pressed it. Soft. I did not show it to Hattie. I do not know why I didn't. I think I was afraid she would say it was nothing and I would believe her, and I think I was afraid she would say it was something and I would believe her, and both of those were worse than keeping it to myself. I put on my gloves and went to bring in wood. --- In the evening I played cards with Tomás. We had a deck that was two cards short — the two of clubs and the jack of hearts — and we had agreed months ago to pretend the two of clubs was in the deck and the jack was the joker. It worked. Most games don't need every card. We were playing rummy. He is not good at rummy. I am not good at rummy either, but I am less bad than he is, and I usually lose on purpose because he cries when he loses. I lost three hands on purpose. On the fourth hand I looked down at my cards and realized I did not want to lose. I wanted to win. I wanted to win at rummy against an eight-year-old with a waterlogged book on his lap and one sock pulled up and one sock slouched, and the wanting was sudden and stupid and out of scale, and I did not stop it. I won. I laid down my cards and he looked at them and his mouth went and he said, "You did that on purpose," which was the opposite of the truth. "I didn't." "You did." "I didn't, Tomás." "You *always* let me win." "I didn't this time." He put his head down on his arms on the table. He didn't cry loudly. He made a small sound that was worse than crying loudly. Bijou got up from under the stove and came over and pressed her body against his leg. I sat there with my hand of cards face-up on the table. I wanted to undo the last three minutes and I also wanted to have my dinner and I also wanted to lie down and I could feel the soft patch on the back of my hand throbbing a little, which was probably in my head. "I'm sorry," I said. He didn't answer. "I was hungry." "That isn't why," he said, into his arms, which was both unfair and correct. --- I put him to bed. I read to him from the book — the chapter on the deepest mines, which was his favorite — and he let me, because even eight-year-olds will take comfort from the person who has just wronged them, which is one of the few merciful facts about human children. By the end of the chapter he was asleep with his mouth open. I went downstairs. Hattie was in her chair with the knitting. She did not look up. She said, "I heard." "I know." "It was only cards." "It wasn't only cards." "No," she said, after a moment. "It wasn't only cards." I sat down across from her. I put my hands on my knees, palms up, and looked at them. The patch on the back of my left hand was gone. I turned the hand over. I turned it back. I looked at it under the lamp. The skin was the skin I had always had. The knuckle was the knuckle. There was nothing there. I did not tell Hattie. I did not know if it had gone or if I had imagined it. I did not know which of those was worse. Outside, the wind was up. Bijou was already under the table. I put another log on the stove. I thought: *one dream is one dream. One patch is one patch. Rummy is rummy.* Then I thought: *we will see if there is a second one.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. **Voice and Character** Mara's voice is exceptionally well-realized. The flatness, the clipped sentences, the way emotion leaks through action rather than declaration — all of this is consistent with the character profile and the stated intention. "I told her because if I didn't tell her I was going to keep turning it over and turning it over, and she is the person I tell things to, for better or worse" — this is exactly right. It's not sentimental, it's not cold, it's the voice of someone who has learned to manage herself. Hattie's dialogue is excellent. "Since nineteen sixty-one" is a perfect line — it's funny, it's characterful, it's also genuinely useful information about how to interpret the dream ambiguity. Her hand placed next to Mara's rather than on it is a beautiful piece of showing. The line "I'm saying one dream is one dream" does real work. Tomás's potato dream is the chapter's best joke and it lands cleanly. "I don't want to talk about it" is perfect. The card game scene is the chapter's emotional center and it's handled with real skill — the wanting to win being "sudden and stupid and out of scale," the small sound worse than crying loudly, Bijou pressing against his leg, and then the devastating "That isn't why" from Tomás, which is both unfair and correct. That last phrase is the kind of observation that earns its place. **The Soft Patch** The soft patch on the hand is the chapter's central horror element and it's handled with admirable restraint. The description is precise without being overwrought: "softer than the skin on either side, as if the flesh under it had thought about something else for a minute." That's a genuinely strange and effective image. The decision not to show Hattie, and the reasoning given for that decision, is psychologically true. The disappearance of the patch at the end — whether imagined or real — is properly ambiguous and properly unsettling. The chapter plan called for "a soft patch on the back of her hand that might be a bruise" and the execution exceeds the plan. **Structure** The chapter uses white space and section breaks well. The drift from the dream discussion to the potato joke to the soft patch to the card game to the aftermath is organic. The ending — returning to "one dream is one dream" and "we will see if there is a second one" — creates a satisfying circular structure without being too neat. The repetition of Hattie's phrase in Mara's internal voice is earned. **Weaknesses** The chapter is almost entirely successful, but there are minor issues. The opening section on the standing dream is slightly longer than it needs to be. "I was standing and time was going past me and I was all right with it going past me" — the repetition of "going past me" feels slightly clumsy rather than intentional. The phrase "the small constant small adjustments" has an accidental double "small" that reads as an error rather than a stylistic choice. The chapter plan mentioned that Mara "plays cards with Tomás and loses on purpose and then stops losing on purpose and beats him and he cries and she feels terrible. Cozy and raw." The execution is faithful to this but the chapter plan also said "Nothing is resolved" — and indeed nothing is, which is correct. The plan also mentioned "Cozy and raw" as a tonal target, and the chapter achieves this. One small concern: the chapter plan says Mara "finds a soft patch on the back of her hand that might be a bruise." The execution makes it clearly not a bruise — "It was not a bruise, exactly. It didn't have the color of a bruise" — which is actually better than the plan, more specific and more unsettling. The chapter follows its plan faithfully, hits its tonal targets, maintains character consistency, and contributes meaningfully to the larger story's ambiguity. The horror is present but not overwrought. The comedy lands. The emotional beats are earned. This is skilled work. **Minor quibble on dialogue:** The dialogue is generally excellent, but the exchange "Did anything happen / No. I was a potato" is perhaps the one moment where the comedy is slightly too easy — though it still works because of Tomás's character. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 — A Bad Night He was hot in the evening and by midnight he was burning. Hattie felt his forehead with the back of her hand and said a word I had not heard her say before, and then she said, "Cool cloth," and I went and got the basin and the cloth, and we did not talk about what was causing it because neither of us wanted to be the one to say it out loud. His cheeks were very red. The rest of him was very white. He was talking in his sleep in the language we didn't have, the quick soft language, and then he was not talking, and then he was. Bijou was on the bed. She had come up on the bed without being invited, which she does not do, and she had laid herself across his feet, and she was not going to move. I did not make her. Hattie sat on one side of the bed. I sat on the other. I wrung out the cloth and laid it on his forehead and it was warm in a minute and I wrung it out again. "What did he eat today," Hattie said. "The same as us." "The same." "The same, Hattie." "All right." "Don't." "I'm not." "You are." "I'm not, Mara. I'm asking because I'm frightened." I didn't answer. I wrung the cloth. --- Around two I made tea. I made it without thinking about it, which was how I made tea these days, a small automatic kindness my hands knew. I brought two cups upstairs. Hattie took hers and held it and did not drink it. I drank mine and did not taste it. "He had a fever in March," she said. "I remember." "It broke." "I remember." "So." "So." She drank the tea then. She drank it in three swallows like a woman taking medicine. "My younger boy had a fever when he was seven," she said. "Matthew. Not Paul. Paul never got sick. Matthew used to get sick if you looked at him sideways. I sat up with him for three nights once and by the third night I was seeing things in the corner of the room. Little things. Bernard said it was the lack of sleep. It was the lack of sleep. But I saw them." "Hattie." "I'm telling you because I want to tell you." "All right." "He's a very alive boy," she said, looking at Tomás. "He is very alive. Do you see what I mean." "I see what you mean." She nodded. She didn't cry. She would not cry with him in front of her. She had a rule about it, I think, that she had never stated and would never state. --- Around three I almost told her about Iris. The words came up in my throat and sat there. *My sister's name is Iris. We had a fight at our mother's funeral and I said something cruel. I don't know if she's alive. I think about her every day. I think about her when I cook.* The words were all there, lined up, waiting. I opened my mouth. What came out was, "The stove needs another log." "Mm," said Hattie. I went down and put a log in the stove and I stood in the kitchen in the dark with my hands on the warm iron and I did not cry because Hattie had not cried, and because if I started I was not sure where I would stop, and because there was nothing to cry about yet — he was breathing, he was turning his face toward the cloth, he was a very alive boy. When I came back up, Hattie was asleep in the chair with her mouth slightly open and her hand on the edge of the bed near his foot. I put the blanket over her. I took her place on the edge of the bed. I almost said something to Bijou then, some small nothing, and I almost said something to Tomás, and I almost said something to the ceiling, to whoever was listening, if anyone was listening — not a prayer, I do not pray, but the shape of a prayer, which is what people who don't pray have instead. What I actually said was: "Don't." I said it to him. I said it quietly. I said it once. --- The fever broke near dawn. It broke the way fevers break, which is without drama — one minute his forehead was wet because of the cloth and the next minute it was wet because he was sweating, a clean clear sweat, and his breathing changed, and Bijou, who had been still as a stone all night, let out a long breath through her nose and put her head down. Hattie woke up. She didn't ask. She put her hand on his forehead and she took it away and she sat back in the chair and she closed her eyes for a second and then opened them. "Soup," she said. "I'll make soup." "Onions." "I have onions." "And the —" "Yes," I said. "And the." --- I made it from the smoked strips and the last two onions in the cellar and the end of the beans, with salt and some thyme from the windowsill pot that was mostly sticks. I brought a bowl upstairs. He was awake. He was sitting up against the pillows. He looked small and hot-eyed and surprised to be awake. "I had a weird dream," he said. "Yeah." "I was a potato again." "Yeah." "But I was a bigger potato." "That's growth." He ate. He ate slowly. Halfway through he stopped and looked at the bowl and looked at me. "Mara." "Yeah." "Can monsters get sick." I thought about it. I thought about it honestly, which is what you owe a child asking a serious question at six in the morning after a bad night. "I don't know," I said. "I don't think anyone knows." "Oh." "Is that okay." "Yeah," he said. He ate another spoonful. "I just wondered." He finished the bowl. He gave it to Bijou to lick. He lay back down and was asleep again in about a minute, cleanly, like a child who has remembered how. --- I went to the window. The sky was grey. The yard was empty. The line of the woods was empty. There was nothing at the fence and nothing under the birches and nothing by the shed and nothing, as far as I could see in any direction, that was not supposed to be there. I had been ready for something to be there. I had been ready for it all night, I realized — a small constant readiness at the back of my head, like an ear cocked. I had been ready to look out and see a ring of them at the treeline, or one of them on the porch, or a slow pale shape moving across the field, come for the boy or come in answer to the boy or come for no reason at all. There was nothing. The horror of it — and I know this is a strange thing to write — was that there was nothing. That the world was not arranging itself around him. That a child could nearly die in a house in the middle of a field and the field would not notice. That whatever we were doing, whatever we had become, whatever was or wasn't happening in our dreams, the woods did not care. Life kept going. Life was going to keep going whether he did or not, and the only thing between him and that indifference was a woman asleep in a chair and a woman at a window and a dog on his feet. Hattie stirred. "Anything." "Nothing." "Good." "Yeah." "Come away from the window, love." I came away from the window.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft across nearly every dimension. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with economy and precision: "He was hot in the evening and by midnight he was burning." This is not purple prose — it's a clean escalation that earns its rhythm. The chapter then proceeds through the night in a way that feels genuinely lived-in rather than constructed. The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. The exchange "What did he eat today / The same as us / The same / The same, Hattie / All right / Don't / I'm not / You are / I'm not, Mara. I'm asking because I'm frightened" — this is exceptional. It conveys the unspoken accusation (that the mutant meat caused the fever), the guilt, the defensiveness, and the genuine fear, all without naming any of it. The dialogue does exactly what the plan called for: it filters emotion through action and exchange rather than stating it. The "Don't / I'm not / You are" rhythm is particularly good — it sounds like two people who have had this kind of argument before, who know each other's rhythms. Hattie's monologue about Matthew is well-handled. It's specific (Matthew, not Paul; Paul never got sick), it reveals character (she saw things in the corner from sleep deprivation, Bernard explained it away), and it ends with "He is very alive. Do you see what I mean" — which is doing a lot of emotional work without sentimentality. The line lands. The Iris section is the chapter's most delicate moment, and it's handled with real restraint. The words "lined up, waiting" and then what comes out being "The stove needs another log" — this is show-don't-tell at its best. We understand everything about Mara's emotional withholding without being told it. The plan called for Mara to "almost tell Hattie about her sister, and doesn't" and this is executed with precision. "Not a prayer, I do not pray, but the shape of a prayer, which is what people who don't pray have instead" — this is the chapter's one moment of genuine lyricism, and it's earned by the surrounding plainness. It doesn't feel forced. The "Don't" said to Tomás is perfect. One word, said quietly, once. The plan called for this kind of restraint and the execution delivers. The fever breaking "without drama" is well-observed. The Bijou detail — "let out a long breath through her nose and put her head down" — is exactly the kind of specific animal behavior that earns its place. The potato dream callback ("But I was a bigger potato / That's growth") is the chapter's best joke. It lands cleanly, it's in character for both of them, and it provides genuine relief after the tension without undercutting it. "Can monsters get sick" is a good child's question. Mara's response — thinking about it honestly, "which is what you owe a child asking a serious question at six in the morning after a bad night" — is one of the chapter's finest lines. It characterizes Mara perfectly: her competence, her care, her refusal to condescend. The final window section is where the chapter risks overreach. "The horror of it — and I know this is a strange thing to write" — the parenthetical is a slight wobble, a moment where the narrator becomes self-conscious in a way that slightly breaks the spell. The meditation on indifference is thematically sound and consistent with the story's project, but it runs slightly long and becomes more explicitly philosophical than the rest of the chapter's mode. "Whatever we were doing, whatever we had become, whatever was or wasn't happening in our dreams" — this is the chapter gesturing at its own themes in a way that's a touch too on-the-nose compared to the restraint elsewhere. The plan called for "the horror is that life keeps going" and this section delivers that, but slightly over-explains it. The ending — "Come away from the window, love" / "I came away from the window" — is excellent. The "love" as warning signal (established in the character profiles) here functions differently, as genuine tenderness, and the chapter earns that shift. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Tomás's fever, Hattie sitting up, Mara sitting up with Hattie, the almost-told Iris story, the almost-told husband story (though this is lighter than planned — Hattie's Matthew story substitutes somewhat), Bijou on the feet, the soup, the "can monsters get sick" exchange, the empty treeline. The plan's note that "the horror is that life keeps going" is delivered, perhaps slightly too explicitly in the window section. Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Hattie's "love" usage, her refusal to cry in front of Tomás, her certainty-as-coping. Mara's withholding, her competence, her love for Bijou and Tomás expressed through action. Tomás's food rules (the bowl given to Bijou to lick), his specific child-logic questions. One small weakness: the plan mentioned Hattie "almost tells Mara something about her husband, and doesn't" — this is present only in the Matthew story, which is about her son, not Bernard. The husband thread is somewhat absent. This is a minor deviation. The chapter is not amateurish. It does not reach for false profundity. Its darkness is well-earned. Its lightness (the potato joke, "That's growth") is genuinely light without being cheap. This is accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 — A Thursday It was a Thursday, probably. We had lost the calendar in October and had not bothered to make another. Hattie kept a notional week going in her head and would announce the day at breakfast, and we had agreed, without discussing it, to believe her. "Thursday," she said, pouring tea. "All right." "My knee thinks Friday." "Your knee is wrong." "My knee has been right before." "Your knee has been right once." "Twice." "Once, Hattie." She smiled at her tea. She had been better-tempered since the fever broke, in the way some people are better-tempered after weather has passed through them. I went out to do the chores. The chores were short now — no chickens since the autumn, no goat since before that — and amounted to bringing in wood, checking the shed, breaking the crust on the water barrel, and walking the fence. I did them slowly. The cold was the serious cold now, the cold that went through two shirts and looked for a third. The sky had the flat white quality of a sky that is not going to do anything today. At the fence I stopped at the big birch the way I always stop at the big birch. I looked at the woods. The woods looked at nothing. I stood there a while. Not dreaming-still. Just standing. Then I went back. --- Hattie was at the table with the knitting. It had become, over the weeks, a shape — a square, more or less, grey, with a bias to one side that she was blaming on the light. Tomás was on the rug with Bijou, reading aloud. "The oldest living person," he was saying, "as of the date of this book, is Yone Minagawa of Japan. She is one hundred and fourteen." "Good for her," said Hattie. "Do you think she's still alive." "The book is from two thousand and seven, love." "So." "So probably not." "Oh." He thought about it. He turned a page. "The second-oldest is Edna Parker of the United States. She is one hundred and fourteen also." "A tie." "It says Yone is older by four months." "Then it isn't a tie." "It's almost a tie." "Almost isn't a tie, Tomás. That's the whole point of ties." He considered this. He did not argue. He went on to the next entry, which was about a man in India with very long moustaches. --- I made stew. I made it in the heavy pot, on the back of the stove, slowly. I took four strips down from the line over the stove and I chopped them into small pieces, and I took a jar of the tomatoes — not the bulging one, we had eaten the bulging one a month ago and survived — and I tipped that in, and I took two handfuls of beans from the soak and a good spoon of honey, which Hattie allowed without comment, and a bay leaf from the twig she had brought in from the hedge in October and hung by the door. It smelled the way a stew smells. I mean that as praise. It smelled like stew. It did not smell of the woods and it did not smell of bread gone over and it did not smell of anything tired. It smelled like supper. "That smells like supper," said Hattie, from the table. "I know." "Just saying." "I know, Hattie." --- We ate at six because by six the light was almost gone. Three bowls on the table, a little apart, nothing touching anything else. Bread from the last of the flour, a little sour, the way the last of the flour goes. Water in the blue jug. Bijou under the table, waiting. Hattie told a story about a man named Whit Lauder who had lived down the road from her as a girl and had been very tall and very bad at dancing. She had told the story before. I listened to it again. It was not a good story. It was the shape of a good story without the substance, which was what Hattie had instead of a substance, most of the time, and which was one of the things about her I had come to love. Tomás finished before us and asked if he could draw. I said yes. He took his pencil and his brown paper and went to the other end of the table and drew with his tongue out, the way he drew. I ate slowly. I took my time with it. The food was good. It was as good as it had been the first night, and I did not think, anymore, about what it was. I thought about whether there was enough salt. There was enough salt. --- After supper I went out for wood. The cold was very clean. The sky had started to let down a little snow, the first of it, slow enormous flakes coming straight down because there was no wind. I stood on the step a minute before going to the pile. I looked at the yard. I looked at the fence. I looked at the woods. I did not see anything in the trees. I will be honest about this, because I have been honest about the rest: I did not *not* see anything, either. There was a place at the edge of the birches where I could not quite tell, in the dusk, whether there was a shape or whether there was only the shape of my expecting a shape. I looked at it for a long time. It did not move. It did not clarify. It remained the kind of thing you cannot be sure about, which is what most things are. I brought in the wood. --- I latched the door. I put the bar across. Hattie looked up from her knitting. "All in." "All in." "Good girl." "Do you want tea." She thought about it. She looked at me over her glasses. "Yes," she said. "I want tea." I put the kettle on. Tomás was still drawing. I went and looked over his shoulder. He had drawn the three of us at the table with our three bowls, and Bijou under the table, and the stove with its smoke coming out the top of the page, and, at the side of the paper where the trees were, some small pale upright shapes with lines coming off them that I chose to read as light. "That's us," he said. "I see that." "That's you. That's Hattie. That's me. That's Bijou. Her eye is on this side because she's facing the other way." "I see." "And that's them." "Yeah." "Is that okay." I thought about it. I put my hand on the top of his head. His hair needed cutting. It would need cutting forever; that was going to be a constant of my life now, his hair needing cutting, and I would keep cutting it badly, and he would keep not minding. "Yeah," I said. "That's okay." The kettle whistled. I went and took it off. I poured two cups. I brought Hattie hers and I sat down across from her with mine and I wrapped my hands around it. The heat went into my fingers. Bijou came out from under the table and put her chin on my foot. Outside, it was snowing. Hattie sipped her tea. I sipped mine. "Thursday," she said. "Thursday."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that demonstrates genuine mastery of the cozy-horror register the prompt demands. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Craft** The first-person voice is exceptionally well-maintained. Mara's narration is clipped, wry, and emotionally restrained in exactly the way the character profile demands. The humor lands — the exchange about Hattie's knee being right "once" versus "twice" is genuinely funny, delivered with perfect timing. The Tomás/Hattie exchange about Yone Minagawa and whether "almost a tie" is a tie is both funny and characterful. These are real laughs, not strained ones. The prose style is disciplined. Short sentences. Fragments used purposefully. The occasional longer sentence earns its length. "It smelled like stew. I mean that as praise." is a small masterpiece of tonal control — it acknowledges the horror context without belaboring it, and the self-commentary ("I mean that as praise") is exactly Mara's voice. **Character Consistency** All four characters are rendered faithfully to their profiles. Hattie's repetitive story about Whit Lauder, her knee-as-weather-forecaster, her "Good girl" — all consistent. Tomás's food rules (nothing touching), his Guinness Book obsession, his tongue-out drawing — specific and right. Bijou's chin on Mara's foot rather than Hattie's is a small, correct detail (Mara is demonstrative with the dog). The drawing scene at the end — Tomás including "them" in the picture — is handled with restraint. Mara's response ("Yeah. That's okay.") is exactly right: not sentimental, not dismissive. **Following the Chapter Plan** The plan called for: ordinary day, chores, Hattie's knee complaint, Tomás reading from Guinness Book (the oldest living person entry — exactly as planned), Mara making stew, eating, going out for wood, the ambiguous treeline moment, coming inside, latching the door, asking Hattie about tea. Every single beat is hit. The plan said "no spoon raised, no symbolic image. Just a woman asking another woman if she wants tea" — and that's exactly what's delivered, with the addition of Tomás's drawing, which is the one creative addition that slightly complicates the ending. **The Ambiguity** The treeline moment is handled with exceptional care: "I did not see anything in the trees. I will be honest about this, because I have been honest about the rest: I did not *not* see anything, either." This is the chapter's best passage. It refuses to resolve, refuses to symbolize, and the meta-commentary ("I will be honest about this") is both in-character and structurally smart — it acknowledges the reader's expectation and declines to satisfy it cleanly. **Tomás's Drawing** This is the one moment that risks tipping into symbolism. "Some small pale upright shapes with lines coming off them that I chose to read as light" — Mara's choice to read them as light rather than what they probably are is a beautiful, quiet moment of self-deception or self-protection. "Is that okay" / "Yeah. That's okay." This works. It doesn't over-explain. The plan warned against "the ring of mushrooms, the candlelit final spoon, the visitation-at-the-treeline as a big symbolic scene" — and this drawing scene walks right up to that line without crossing it, because Mara's response is so flat and the scene moves on immediately to the kettle. **The Hair Detail** "His hair needed cutting. It would need cutting forever; that was going to be a constant of my life now, his hair needing cutting, and I would keep cutting it badly, and he would keep not minding." This is the emotional core of the chapter, and it's delivered through a completely mundane observation. It's the closest the chapter comes to sentimentality and it earns it by being so specific and so unadorned. **Minor Criticisms** The "flat white quality of a sky that is not going to do anything today" is slightly overwritten — it's trying a little hard. The phrase "the shape of a good story without the substance, which was what Hattie had instead of a substance, most of the time" is a bit convoluted and slightly condescending toward Hattie in a way that doesn't quite fit Mara's affection for her. The ending repetition of "Thursday" is earned but perhaps slightly too neat — it's the one moment that feels like it's reaching for a bow. The stew section is excellent but the "I mean that as praise" line, while good, is the kind of move that could become a tic if overused. Here it's used once and lands. **Overall** This is a chapter that does exactly what it sets out to do, does it with genuine skill, and resists almost every temptation toward easy sentiment or easy horror. The cozy and the dark coexist without either canceling the other. The characters are alive. The ending is small and slightly unsettled, exactly as planned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of post-apocalyptic cozy-horror fiction. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. **Strengths:** The opening inventory list is a masterstroke — it establishes character, stakes, and tone simultaneously without a word of exposition. The voice is immediately distinctive: dry, precise, emotionally restrained in a way that makes the emotional moments land harder. Mara's chef background is woven organically into her perspective and actions rather than announced. The characters are genuinely nuanced. Hattie is not simply "wise elder" — she's stubborn, occasionally infuriating, privately grieving, and her relationship with the broken radio is a perfect piece of characterization. Tomás is rendered with real specificity (the Guinness Book, the upside-down reading, the potato dreams) without being preciously quirky. Bijou functions as an emotional barometer without becoming a device. The absent Iris and the unsent letters add genuine depth to Mara. The dialogue is exceptional. Lines like "Your knee has been right before" / "Your knee has been right once" / "Twice" / "Once, Hattie" feel genuinely lived-in. The exchange about naming the carcass is both funny and quietly devastating. The dialogue never over-explains and never performs emotion — it consistently reveals character through indirection. The horror elements are handled with real craft. The spore mutants are genuinely unsettling — the clicking, the rearranged faces, the slow head-turn in the grove — but the piece never lingers grotesquely. The discovery of Delia in the grove is the emotional climax of the piece and it earns its weight completely. The plaid collar detail is devastating precisely because it's so specific and so small. The cozy-horror balance is maintained throughout. The piece understands that coziness is not the absence of horror but its counterweight — the tea, the cards, the flat pancakes, the knitting — these are not escapism but survival strategies, and the story treats them as such. The soft patch on Mara's hand is a genuinely unsettling detail that is introduced and then apparently resolved (or perhaps imagined away), which is more sophisticated than most horror writing. The standing dreams are similarly handled — suggestive without being declarative. The final chapter achieves something rare: genuine warmth that has been fully earned by everything preceding it. Tomás's drawing with the pale shapes at the edge is the right note to end on — neither falsely hopeful nor grimly conclusive. **Weaknesses and criticisms:** The piece occasionally tips into self-conscious literary style. Phrases like "the shape of a prayer, which is what people who don't pray have instead" are slightly too neat, too aphoristic — they feel written rather than thought. There are a handful of moments where the prose becomes aware of its own elegance. The "long pig" exchange in Chapter 4, while funny, slightly undercuts the horror of what they've done. The laughter feels a touch convenient as an emotional release valve. The soft patch subplot is introduced with real menace and then essentially dropped — it disappears and Mara decides not to investigate. This is either a sophisticated choice (ambiguity as horror) or an unresolved thread. Given the story's overall control, it reads as intentional, but it may frustrate readers expecting development. The pacing in Chapters 4-6 is slightly episodic — the "housekeeping" chapter works as a structural choice but the individual vignettes (the sock, the letter to Iris) vary in their payoff. The Iris thread is introduced and never developed beyond the letters burned in the stove, which feels like setup without resolution. The transformation — from horror at eating the mutants to matter-of-fact acceptance — is handled well but perhaps slightly quickly. The moral weight of eating what may have been human is acknowledged ("we ate Delia's neighbor, maybe") but not fully wrestled with. The metaphors almost universally land. "Like a woman at a grocery store" is genuinely brilliant. "Life was going to keep going whether he did or not" is earned. The occasional misfire ("the shape of a good story without the substance, which was what Hattie had instead of a substance") is slightly muddled. Overall, this is a piece of genuine literary quality — controlled, emotionally intelligent, formally inventive, and faithful to its unusual prompt. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Pacing: 16 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 84.9/100
+ Victorian Romance — The Upper Window (Chapter Avg: 84.4 | Final: 83.5)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **On the prompt itself:** "One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows." This suggests a narrator with experience — someone who has done this repeatedly. The tone is wry, knowing, almost conversational. It implies a practiced criminal, a lover, a spy, or someone who must avoid being seen. The Victorian Romance genre suggests there should be a love story at the heart, likely transgressive, with class tension. **Character possibilities:** - A cat burglar who falls in love with the woman of the house. Classic but rich. - A woman who disguises as a man to enter men's spaces — though "upper windows" is more literal. - A governess or lady's maid who meets her lover via window. (The lover climbing in.) - A jewel thief — female — who encounters a man in the bedroom she's meant to rob. - A young gentleman who has been disinherited, visiting his childhood love who is now married off. - A suffragist or radical who uses burglary skills for political ends. - Two thieves — rivals — who keep encountering each other. - A woman posing as a man, a professional burglar, in love with the daughter of the house she's been hired to rob. - A resurrectionist or occult investigator entering homes to retrieve cursed objects. - A bastard son returning to see his half-sister, or to reclaim something stolen from his mother. **Plot directions:** 1. **The Rival Thieves**: Two burglars — a gentleman-thief and a lady-thief — keep crossing paths in the bedrooms of London's great houses. They flirt, compete, eventually team up, fall in love. Risk of it being too light/caper-y. But could be romantic. 2. **The Returning Love**: A man forced abroad comes back and must visit his former fiancée, now wed to a cruel earl, through her window. A story of forbidden renewal. Darker, more melancholic. 3. **The Thief and the Lady**: A burglar slips into a bedroom and finds the lady of the house awake, unafraid, perhaps even welcoming. She is lonely, trapped, sees in him an escape. Gothic-tinged. 4. **The Lady Burglar**: A woman, perhaps a disgraced gentlewoman, becomes a burglar. She enters a house, meets the son — who recognizes her from society. He doesn't turn her in; he becomes complicit. 5. **The Window-lover**: A long-running romance conducted entirely through the upper window of the lady's bedroom — her suitor forbidden by her father. Tests the limits of what can be accomplished in 8 chapters. I think option 4 or 1 has the most potential. Let me develop option 4: **Detailed plot for Option 4 (Lady Burglar):** - Ch 1: Our narrator, a woman, enters a manor through the upper windows. She establishes her voice, her skill, hints at how she came to this life. She is surprised by a young man awake in the bedroom — not the master but a guest, the son of the house. - Ch 2: The confrontation. He recognizes her — they met once, at a ball, before her fall. He doesn't raise the alarm. They speak. She leaves empty-handed. - Ch 3: Backstory — how she fell. Father ruined, died. She had skills (climbing trees as a girl, a governess's lessons in locks and servants' backstairs). She took to theft, starting with what had been her own. - Ch 4: He seeks her out. Finds her — perhaps through a pawnbroker. He wants to understand. A confession of mutual attraction. - Ch 5: He proposes a scheme — help him recover something that was stolen from his mother by his cruel stepfather. Collaboration begins. Tension. - Ch 6: A burglary together. Rooftops. Intimacy in close spaces. First kiss? Or first real moment of connection. - Ch 7: Betrayal or complication — they are nearly caught; she is injured or he must choose between protecting her and his social standing. Perhaps a servant or rival thief is threatening exposure. - Ch 8: Resolution. They escape to the continent, or she refuses his proposal of marriage (too respectable!), or they agree to a strange, irregular life together. Bittersweet or triumphant. **Setting:** London, perhaps 1870s-80s. Mayfair, Belgravia. Rooftops. Gaslight. Rain-slick slate. **Tone:** First-person, wry, intelligent female narrator. Literate, observational. Think Sarah Waters (Fingersmith) meets a lighter touch. The prose should savor physical detail — the texture of stone, the smell of rain, the specific weight of pearls in the palm. **Love interest qualities:** He should not be a rescuer. He should be interesting in his own right — perhaps a younger son, a scholar, someone who resents his world. Something between Lord Peter Wimsey and a more melancholy character. He should admire her, not pity her. **Key images:** the upper window, always. Ivy. Drainpipes. Slates. The sash lifted. The candle guttering. The cold sill against the stockinged foot. The gleam of a mirror in moonlight. A half-open letter. **Title possibilities:** "The Art of Entering"; "Upper Windows"; "The Lady in the Eaves" # Reflection The prompt's voice — "One quickly learns the art" — is the anchor. It's a practiced voice, wry, almost tutorial in its opening, which hints at a first-person narrator who has committed this act many times. This strongly suggests a burglar narrator. The word "art" implies skill, aesthetics, even pride. This is not a fearful person. And the Victorian Romance framing means this skill must ultimately serve or collide with love. Option 4 (Lady Burglar) appeals to me most because: - The voice fits naturally — a woman who has fallen from society, speaking with ironic remove about a skill no lady should possess. - It allows for a genuine Victorian Romance arc (forbidden love, class complications, the question of reputation). - It gives the narrator agency rather than making her a damsel. - It allows for the physical texture of burglary — the climbing, the rooftops — which is viscerally satisfying and distinctive. - The meet-cute is inherent in the prompt: climbing through the window, she finds him. Option 1 (rival thieves) is more conventional caper — risks feeling like pastiche. Option 2 is melancholic and good but doesn't use "upper windows" as actively; it might be a one-off scene rather than a recurring motif. Option 3 is Gothic and tempting but might feel too static. The challenge with Option 4 is avoiding two pitfalls: (a) the man "saving" her and making her respectable — which would betray the voice of the prompt; (b) making it too modern-feeling, too anachronistically feminist. The answer to both is to keep her agency intact throughout, to have her choose her life at the end, and to ground the language and constraints firmly in the period. I want to avoid: a straightforward heist plot that overshadows the romance; a tragic ending that feels unearned; a conventional marriage ending that undermines her autonomy. What I want to preserve: the wry, knowing voice; the specific sensory texture of Victorian London at night; the slow-build romance where each encounter is freighted; the idea that "entering through upper windows" becomes a metaphor — for entering a life, a heart, a world one has been locked out of. That metaphor is what elevates this from caper to Victorian Romance proper. I think eight chapters at ~1000 words each will require discipline. Each chapter should do one thing well: establish voice, the first meeting, backstory, second meeting, alliance, first heist together, crisis, resolution. The romance must be the spine; the burglary the rhythm. Pacing-wise, the first kiss should not arrive until chapter 6 or so — the restraint itself is Victorian. The ending that feels right: not marriage, not tragedy, but a shared irregular life. He joins her, perhaps. They choose a liminal existence — rooftops, travel, a kind of freedom. That honors both the romance and the voice of the opening line, which values the skill, not the respectability one might have it renounce.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I am writing a Victorian Romance in the voice of a fallen gentlewoman turned burglar — a first-person narrator whose wry, practiced tone opens with the prompt itself. The piece will be a chamber romance rather than a heist story: the burglary is the scaffolding, the rhythm, but the spine is the slow mutual recognition between two people who both live, in different ways, at the edge of their world. **Voice and style:** First person, past tense, literate and observational, period-appropriate in diction without pastiche. I want sentences that savor physical texture — slate, ivy, rain, candle-smoke, the small mechanics of a sash lifted without sound. Think Sarah Waters, a touch of Wilkie Collins, restraint over melodrama. The narrator should be self-aware, ironic about her own position, but capable of real feeling — which she approaches obliquely, as a Victorian would. **Plot choices:** Clara (narrator) is a gentlewoman fallen after her father's financial ruin and suicide. She has become a thief, specializing in upper-window entries. In the first chapter she breaks into a Mayfair bedroom and finds James — a younger son of the house, awake, unafraid — who recognizes her from a season years ago. He does not turn her in. Over subsequent chapters they meet, speak, and eventually conspire to recover something stolen from his late mother by his cruel stepfather. The romance builds through restraint: long looks, hands touched in climbing, one kiss late in the story. The ending is not marriage and not tragedy, but a chosen, irregular life — they leave England together, her terms, not his. **What I will aim for:** - The motif of "upper windows" as both literal skill and metaphor — entering a life, a heart, a world one has been locked out of. - The narrator's agency preserved throughout. He does not save her. If anyone is rescued, it is him, from the gilded stasis of his life. - Period-accurate constraints (reputation, inheritance, marriage law) that make the stakes real. - Rooftops, gaslight, fog — the sensory London of the 1880s. - Each chapter as a self-contained scene or movement, with the arc of acquaintance, alliance, and commitment built across them. **What I will avoid:** - Caper tone or comic-heist beats. This is not witty banter; it is two serious people finding each other. - Respectability as the goal. She will not be made a lady again. - Anachronistic feminism — her defiance is shaped by, not separate from, her era. - Overloading backstory; it must be woven, not dumped. - A first kiss or declaration too early. Restraint is the Victorian engine of desire. - Tidy marriage ending; the resolution must honor the voice of the opening line, which values skill and knowing, not the renunciation of either. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Art of Entering** Clara enters a house in Grosvenor Square via the roof and an upper window. The chapter is a tutorial in her craft: how to read ivy, how to judge a sash, how to muffle a step. Sensory, confident, wry. She slips into what she believes to be an empty bedroom — and finds a young man, fully dressed, reading by a single candle. He looks up. He does not shout. "I wondered when you'd come," he says, or something like it. She recognizes him, barely. End on the held breath of that recognition. **Chapter 2 — A Gentleman Who Does Not Ring the Bell** Dialogue chapter. He is James Ashcombe, second son of the house. He recognizes her from a ball six years ago — one of her last. He does not ring for the servants. They speak, carefully, obliquely. He offers her brandy. She refuses, then accepts. He lets her leave, empty-handed. At the window he asks her name. She gives him a false one. He says: "I know your name." She goes out through the window as she came in. **Chapter 3 — How One Falls** Backstory, woven through a present-tense return to her lodgings in a shabby street near King's Cross, where she lives under an assumed name with an older woman (a former actress, now her fence and only friend, Mrs. Pell). How her father failed, how he died, how she had climbed trees as a girl, how she learned locks from a disgraced butler. The first things she stole — her own. The shape of her new life. She tries not to think of James. She thinks of him. **Chapter 4 — Second Meeting** James finds her. Not through cleverness — through Mrs. Pell, who knows everything. He comes to a coffee-house in Holborn. He tells her he has a proposition. His mother, dead three years, left jewels — a specific pearl set — that his stepfather, Sir Horace, has appropriated and intends to give to his new young wife. James cannot recover them through law. Will Clara help him? She is suspicious. He is earnest. She agrees, and tells herself it is for the fee. **Chapter 5 — Reconnaissance** They meet in daylight — a park, a museum — to plan. He sketches the house's floor plan; she corrects him. The chapter is about the strange intimacy of two people planning a transgression together. A moment: he reaches across a table to stop her hand from trembling over the teacup. They do not speak of it. She tells herself again it is for the fee. She begins to believe herself less. **Chapter 6 — The Pearl Set** The burglary. Night, rain, roof-slate, the stepfather's country house (or a London house while the family is out — I'll decide in draft). They go in together. The physical closeness of climbing, of shared silence. They find the pearls. A noise — a servant. They hide in a closet, pressed close. First kiss here, brief, silent, almost involuntary. They escape with the pearls. The chapter ends on the rooftop, breath visible in cold air, both changed. **Chapter 7 — The Cost** Crisis. Sir Horace has seen enough to suspect James. A letter arrives — not to Clara, but in the papers, a notice of a burglary, a description vague but too close. Mrs. Pell warns Clara to leave London. James comes to her lodgings — the first time he has seen where she lives. He proposes — not marriage, exactly; he says he will follow her wherever she goes. She refuses at first. She has built a life that does not need rescuing; she will not exchange one cage for another, even a loving one. They part badly. She thinks she will not see him again. **Chapter 8 — Upper Windows** Some days later. She is packing to leave for the Continent alone. A sound at the window — the upper window of her shabby rooms. She opens it. James, clumsy, laughing quietly, has climbed the drainpipe. He has left his name, his inheritance, a letter to his brother. He has come in her way, through the upper window. He asks to go with her, not to save her, but to be saved — to learn her art. The final image echoes the first line: one quickly learns, she thinks, watching him — though he, poor man, will learn slowly. They leave together for the night boat. Ending is quiet, chosen, and open.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer — especially one drawn to Victorian Romance — would likely start from a single vivid image or a voice they can't shake. The prompt itself hands us a voice: that wry, practiced "one quickly learns" opening. A human would sit with that voice, maybe write a few pages just to hear it, and let the plot emerge from who that person is. They would not begin by plotting eight chapters and reverse-engineering character to fit. A human writer would also likely have specific loves and obsessions bleeding into the work: a particular kind of rain, a particular London street they've walked or read about, a line from Browning they can't get out of their head, a painting that haunts them. The piece would have fingerprints — small, idiosyncratic fixations that don't serve the plot but give the prose texture and weight. A human would also be willing to be **messy**. To leave a question unanswered. To have a chapter that's just a strange mood, or a scene that doesn't pay off cleanly. To let a character be unlikeable in a way that isn't redeemed. To let the reader work. # Telltale LLM Approaches and Why They Hurt This Piece **1. Symmetry and structural tidiness.** LLMs love mirror structures — the ending that echoes the opening, the motif that recurs exactly three times, the chapter that closes with a reprise of its opening image. My plan literally ends Chapter 8 with a callback to the first line. That's not wrong per se, but it's the *obvious* choice and risks feeling engineered rather than inevitable. **2. Wrapping things up with a bow.** LLMs reflexively resolve. My Chapter 8 has James climbing through her window — a pat, satisfying, thematically perfect moment. Real Victorian romances leave more unspoken. Think of the ending of *Villette*, which refuses closure. A human writer might end with Clara alone on the boat, not knowing if he'll come. **3. Writing impressively instead of truthfully.** LLMs reach for the florid adjective, the triple-clause sentence with rhythm but no weight, the Sarah Waters-adjacent lushness. The prompt has a flat, wry, almost offhand voice. An LLM will tend to inflate it. I should be worried about prose that sounds like a Victorian novel rather than prose that *means* something from inside one. **4. Explaining emotions rather than dramatizing them.** LLMs like to name feelings. "She felt a reluctant tenderness." "He looked at her with something that might have been hope." A human writer shows the averted eyes, the untouched tea, the sentence left unfinished. **5. Safe characters.** LLMs soften. They give the cruel stepfather a reason. They make the heroine noble in her criminality. They make the love interest decent. The result is flattening. A human writer might let James be slightly ridiculous, slightly useless, genuinely besotted in a way that's not entirely flattering. Clara might steal from people who don't deserve it. She might not miss her father. **6. Over-explaining backstory.** LLMs love a Chapter 3 that's "the backstory chapter." My plan has exactly this. Real novels tend to drip backstory in obliquely, through objects, through gaps, through what the narrator *refuses* to say. **7. Motif-mongering.** LLMs identify a theme and then drive it home — "upper windows" as metaphor for "entering a life," etc. I literally wrote this in my Intention. The danger is that it becomes a thesis the story is constantly illustrating rather than something the reader stumbles on. **8. Dialogue that lands too cleanly.** LLM dialogue tends toward epigram. Every line is quotable. Real dialogue — especially Victorian dialogue — has misfires, deflections, people speaking past each other, sentences that don't finish. **9. The balanced gender-politics ending.** LLMs want to thread the needle: she keeps her agency, he joins her world, nobody loses. Life is rarely so equitable. A harder, more interesting ending might involve real cost — she gives up something, or he does, and it's not made up for. **10. Sensory checklists.** LLMs dutifully include sight, sound, smell, touch in each scene. A human writer notices one thing intensely — the specific way candlewax pools on a bookcover — and lets other senses lie fallow. # Critique of the Intention and Plan **The voice ambition is strong but the execution risks falseness.** I've said I want a Sarah Waters–ish voice, but Waters writes from deep immersion in the period. An LLM imitating her will produce something glossier, more self-consciously "lovely." I need to police the prose hard: cut the pretty sentence that doesn't earn itself. **The plan is too clean.** Eight chapters, each with a job: meeting, backstory, reunion, alliance, reconnaissance, heist, crisis, resolution. This is a screenwriting beat sheet. Real novellas breathe differently — a chapter might be all atmosphere and no plot, or might telescope months into a paragraph. The uniformity is an LLM tell. **Chapter 3 is the worst offender.** A whole chapter of backstory woven through a present-tense scene is a classic LLM move. It will feel like a Wikipedia entry with gaslight. The backstory should leak throughout — a sentence here, an image there, something she won't name until Chapter 6. **James is too palatable.** "Earnest," "scholarly," "resents his world," "doesn't pity her." He's a composite of love-interest virtues. He should have a flaw that matters — maybe he's vain, or actually a bit cowardly, or his "earnestness" is a form of naivety that costs Clara something. Maybe he's not as interesting as she is, and she knows it, and loves him anyway. **The stepfather as villain is thin.** "Cruel stepfather" with "new young wife" is a stock Victorian baddie. This is the plot's weakest point — it's too convenient that the object of theft is also morally righteous to steal. Making the moral calculus cleaner makes the romance less interesting. What if the jewels aren't clearly his mother's? What if Clara isn't sure whether they're doing the right thing? **The closet kiss.** This is a *classic* romance trope. It's also what an LLM would confidently pick as the "right" first-kiss moment. A human writer might put the first kiss somewhere stranger — in broad daylight, or through a window, or after a fight, or not where anyone would predict. **Mrs. Pell is a convenience.** The wise older woman / fence / confidante is a stock figure. She risks being a plot function. She needs her own oddness — maybe she disapproves of James, maybe she's not quite kind, maybe she has her own reasons for wanting Clara to stay. **"She thinks of him. She tries not to think of him."** This is exactly the LLM-rendered interiority that tells instead of shows. I should cut interiority that simply names the feeling. **The ending is too thematically legible.** James climbs through her window — we get it, she taught him the art, they're equals now, full circle. A human might resist that completeness. What if he arrives at the door like an ordinary man, embarrassed? What if she leaves without him and the last image is her on the boat, the coast receding, and she does or doesn't see a figure running on the pier? **The "chosen irregular life" resolution** is something I've intellectually identified as the "right" ending. But it's also very legible — bohemian, modern-friendly, easy to like. The more uncomfortable ending might involve real renunciation or real compromise. # Ideas to Make It More Human - **Let Clara be harder.** Not a gentlewoman in reduced circumstances with a heart of gold. Let her enjoy stealing. Let her be somewhat contemptuous of the houses she enters. Let her have stolen from genuinely sympathetic people before and not cared. - **Let James be less.** Less handsome, less brave, less useful. Let her love be partly pity, partly exasperation, partly real tenderness — not admiration of a noble soul. - **Muddy the moral premise.** The pearls may or may not be rightfully his mother's. The stepfather may not be a monster, just an ordinary bore. The romance doesn't need a righteous heist to survive. - **Withhold a backstory chapter.** Let Clara's fall come out sideways — an object she won't look at, a name she changes the subject from, a habit she has of counting exits. - **Let dialogue be uncomfortable.** People interrupting each other, failing to say what they mean, saying the wrong thing. - **Refuse a motif when it's asking to appear.** If "window" is the obvious word for a sentence, reach for a different one. - **Have a chapter that is mostly weather, or mostly waiting.** Not every chapter has to advance plot. - **Let something go unresolved.** Mrs. Pell's feelings. What happened to Clara's mother. Whether James is being followed. - **Commit to an uglier or smaller or stranger ending.** Clara alone. Or Clara with James but not happily. Or Clara never seeing James again and the last chapter being years later, a glimpse of him across a room in Paris, neither acknowledging. - **Trust the reader.** Don't explain the ending. Don't make the final line do thematic work. End on an image, or a small action, or a sentence that doesn't close. - **Let the narrator be wrong about herself.** The most alive first-person narrators have blind spots the reader sees around. Clara might think she's colder than she is, or braver, or more free. The voice can be knowing and still be self-deceived. - **Specific period texture over general Victorianness.** Not "gaslight" generically but a specific street, a specific scandal in the papers, a specific music-hall song. Weight comes from specificity. - **Kill one darling in the plan.** Something I've identified as "the right choice" — the closet kiss, the Chapter 8 echo, the heist climax — should probably go, replaced by something I haven't thought of yet.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention A Victorian Romance in the voice of a woman who has made a profession of climbing into other people's houses. The prompt gives me the voice — wry, practiced, slightly cold — and I will trust it. The opening line is tutorial; the narrator knows things. She is not going to be redeemed into a lady, and she is not going to be saved by love. What she may do is allow another person to climb, badly, after her. **Voice:** First person, past tense. Dry, specific, occasionally cutting. Clara should be capable of cruelty in observation. She should be wrong about herself in places the reader can see. I will resist the lush Sarah-Waters register and aim for something sparer and odder — shorter sentences where the LLM instinct is to roll out long ones, concrete nouns where the instinct is abstraction. The prose should sometimes be plain to the point of flatness; the feeling comes from what she notices and refuses to notice. **Plot commitments:** - Clara is a gentlewoman's daughter turned thief, but she does not grieve her old life. She likes what she does. - James Ashcombe is a younger son who is not especially brave or clever. He is kind in small, slightly useless ways. She loves him anyway, and is a little ashamed of it. - The "job" that brings them together — pearls, a cruel stepfather — I am deliberately muddying. The moral premise is ambiguous. The pearls may or may not be his mother's; James believes they are; Clara is not sure she believes him and proceeds anyway. - Mrs. Pell is not a kindly fence. She is sharp, self-interested, and disapproves of James not because he threatens Clara but because he isn't worth her. - The backstory of Clara's fall is withheld, leaked in fragments, and never given its own chapter. **What I am cutting from the earlier plan:** - The "backstory chapter" (old Chapter 3). - The closet kiss in the heist — too neat. First kiss will come earlier, in daylight, wrongly. - The clean Chapter 8 echo of the opening line. The ending will refuse that symmetry. - James as noble scholar. He is a decent, slightly foolish young man who has been bored his whole life. - The "chosen irregular life" as triumphant resolution. The ending will be smaller, more ambiguous, and not fully settled. **What I am aiming for:** - Specific period texture: named streets, a real scandal in the papers, the smell of a particular kind of coal. - A chapter that is mostly mood, not plot. - Dialogue that misses, deflects, stops short. - A first-person narrator with blind spots. - An ending that doesn't do thematic work but lands on an image. **What I am avoiding:** - Caper tone; heist quippery; triumphant reveals. - Thematic legibility in the final lines. - Any sentence that exists to be pretty. - Tidy resolution of James's world versus hers. - Wrapped-up emotional arcs — hers or his. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Art of Entering** Opens with the prompt line. A working chapter: Clara enters a house in Grosvenor Square by ivy, drainpipe, and a sash window she has judged from below. The prose is specific and unhurried — how to test slates, how to silence the sash, which servants sleep where. She likes this. She enters what she takes for a disused bedroom. A candle is lit. A young man — dressed, fully awake, reading — looks up. He says: "Don't go. Please." End on her hand still on the window-sash, uncommitted. **Chapter 2 — A Civil Conversation** They talk.
