He does not ring. She does not flee. She learns he has been sitting up most nights lately, for reasons he does not quite give. He asks her, almost plaintively, what she came for. She tells him, truthfully — she had meant to take whatever was small and unloved. He offers her a pair of cufflinks he says no one will miss. She declines. He asks her name; she lies. He says, "I saw you dance once," and names the house and the year. She leaves without the cufflinks, angry at having been recognized, angrier at being pleased by it. **Chapter 3 — Brewer Street** A chapter of lodging and weather. Clara in her room off Brewer Street, a fog settling. Mrs. Pell comes up with tea and the evening papers. A notice: a small burglary in Belgravia last week, no suspect. Pell watches Clara read it. They talk about everything except the man in the bedroom. The past leaks in obliquely — a pair of mother-of-pearl buttons Clara keeps but does not wear; a letter she does not open. She does not go out that night, or the next. She tells herself this is prudence. **Chapter 4 — An Approach** A week later, James appears at the coffee-house Mrs. Pell favours. Pell has sold him the address, and tells Clara so, unapologetically. James is awkward in daylight, worse than he was by candle. He makes his proposition badly: his late mother's pearls, now worn by his stepfather's new wife, a country house in Hertfordshire, a fee he names too high because he doesn't know what the work is worth. Clara watches him flounder. She agrees before she has decided to. When he leaves, Mrs. Pell says only, "He is not clever enough for you." Clara does not answer. **Chapter 5 — Daylight** They meet to plan — a bench in Regent's Park, a table at the South Kensington museum. The chapter is about the strangeness of seeing him in weather, among people. He draws the house's plan badly; she corrects it. He knows almost nothing useful about his own family's property, which both charms and unsettles her. Walking back through the park at dusk, he stops her under a plane tree and kisses her — clumsily, without asking, and then apologises so thoroughly she has to laugh. She kisses him back properly, to stop him talking. Nothing is said after. She walks home alone, furious with herself and not sorry. **Chapter 6 — Hertfordshire** The burglary. Night train, a walk across wet fields, the house dark but for one window. Roof-work in rain. She goes in alone; he waits below as agreed, because he is not good enough to come up. She finds the pearls in a dressing-case in the new wife's room. She also finds, not looked for, a bundle of letters in the writing-desk — from James's mother, to a man not his father. She takes the pearls and leaves the letters. Climbing down she slips, badly, and lands hard; James catches some of her, not well. They run. On the train back, bruised, wet, she does not tell him about the letters. She watches him sleep against the window and understands she has decided something without noticing. **Chapter 7 — The Papers** Days later. A notice in the *Times*: a burglary in Hertfordshire, description of a man and a woman seen near the station. Sir Horace has not gone to the police, quite — but has let it be known. Mrs. Pell tells Clara to leave England within the week. James comes to Brewer Street for the first time. He is frightened and trying not to show it. He tells her he means to follow her wherever she goes. She tells him about the letters. He is silent a long time. He says, "I don't want to know what was in them." They quarrel — not about the letters, but about what he thinks he is doing. He leaves without resolution. She begins to pack. **Chapter 8 — Dover** She goes to Dover alone. The chapter is mostly waiting — a boarding-house, a grey sea, a ticket for the night boat. She does not look for him. At the pier, in the last hour before sailing, she sees him — not climbing, not heroic. He is standing at the landward end of the pier with a small case, uncertain, as if he has not decided either. She does not wave. He does not see her yet. The boat's bell rings. She watches him a moment longer. The final image is hers: the bell, the gulls, his figure against the lamps, her hand on the rail. Whether he comes aboard is not told.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Clara Westlake (formerly Clarissa Aylmer) **Background.** Daughter of a Shropshire banker who married late and badly — her mother died when Clara was nine, her father in 1879 when Clara was twenty-one, by his own hand after the Glasgow Bank failure swept through the smaller country houses. She had a half-season in London in 1877, enough to have stood in the Ashcombe ballroom once. The house at Hanbury was sold to meet creditors; she went to stay with an aunt in Bayswater who made her understand, without quite saying so, that the stay was not indefinite. She left without explanation some months later and has lived under her mother's maiden name — Westlake — ever since. She has been a thief for four years. She is twenty-six. **Personality and flaws.** She is not warm. She notices people as one notices furniture: where they are, what weight they will bear, whether they can be moved. This is partly temperament and partly trade. She enjoys her work in a way she would not admit out loud, and is occasionally cruel about the houses she enters — she despises bad taste, worn carpet on a good stair, pretentious oils. She is vain about her competence. She has almost no sense of pity for the rich and a faintly unfair contempt for the poor, whom she thinks ought to be cleverer. She is unsentimental about her father, whom she nursed through one ruin too many, and about whom she will not be questioned. She has a habit of counting exits. Her flaw, which she would not recognise: she is more easily flattered by attention than she believes. She thinks she is immune to the sort of look James gives her. She is not. **Idiosyncrasies.** She keeps four mother-of-pearl buttons in a twist of paper in her workbox; they came off a dress of her mother's and she has never sewn them onto anything. She does not drink spirits but will drink dark beer. She cannot bear the smell of lilies. She reads the agony column of the *Times* every morning, for fun and for work. She has taught herself to sleep in any position, anywhere, for a quarter of an hour at a time. **Physicality.** Tall for a woman, narrow in the shoulder, long-handed. Dark hair she keeps pinned low. A face not pretty but arresting — broad across the cheekbones, thin through the mouth, grey eyes. She carries herself very still; she has learned stillness as a professional skill and now cannot unlearn it. She dresses well when she goes into houses: a decent walking-dress, mended invisibly. In her rooms she wears men's trousers cut down, and a dark jersey, and finds this more comfortable than she would have thought possible at twenty. **Motives and allegiances.** She wants to keep working, keep her rooms, not be caught, not be married. She is loyal to Mrs. Pell in the way one is loyal to a useful instrument one has grown fond of. She believes she has no other attachments. **Voice:** > *The house was three stories and the ivy went up two, which was enough. I have taken worse odds for worse prizes. A woman in Eaton Square once made me climb a copper drainpipe in December for a brooch I could have bought at a pawnbroker's for thirty shillings.* > "I don't mind being looked at. I mind being recognised. There is a difference, and you have just demonstrated it." --- # James Ashcombe **Background.** Second son of the late Sir Edmund Ashcombe and his first wife, Eleanor; twenty-eight; Eton, Oxford (no degree taken, something unclear involving a tutor and a term abroad), an abortive attempt at the Bar. His mother died when he was twenty-five, of a long illness he was not often at her bedside for, which he has not forgiven himself. His father remarried within the year — Sir Horace is his stepfather in name only, being in fact the second husband his mother had taken; the first husband, Ashcombe père, had died when James was twelve. (I am keeping the family arrangements deliberately muddled; James himself gets them wrong in speech.) He has an allowance. He has never earned a shilling. **Personality and flaws.** Kind in the small, slightly useless way of young men who have never been asked to be otherwise. He is not stupid but he is not quick, and he has been told he is clever all his life by people who did not mean it seriously. He reads — Tennyson, Ruskin, a little Browning — and is moved by what he reads in a way that does not always survive contact with the world. He is vain about his mother's memory and has constructed her, posthumously, into a figure she probably was not. His real flaw is a kind of moral laziness disguised as sensitivity. He wants to do right but prefers the wanting to the doing, and when pressed he tends to defer to whoever is nearest. This is why Clara finds him infuriating and also why she finds him restful — after Mrs. Pell, a man who will not argue with her is a holiday. **Idiosyncrasies.** Fiddles with a signet ring he does not wear but carries in a waistcoat pocket. Knows the names of trees. Writes unsent letters. Hums — unmusically — when anxious. Cannot tie a parcel. **Physicality.** Medium height, slight, fair hair already thinning at the crown though he is only twenty-eight. A long, clean-shaven face; a chin not quite firm. Blue eyes set rather close. Well-dressed by his tailor; badly-dressed by any eye that notices the way clothes sit. He stoops a little when listening, which people mistake for attentiveness. His hands are soft. **Motives and allegiances.** He wants to be interesting. He wants his mother avenged for things she may not have suffered. He wants Clara in a way that has surprised him and that he is frightened of because it requires him to do rather than think. His allegiance to Sir Horace is nil; to his elder brother Edmund, fond but distant; to his mother's memory, fierce and unexamined. **Voice:** > "I thought — I don't know what I thought. That you would come, and I would — say something. And you would understand it. I hadn't got further than that." > "I'm not brave. I want you to know that, before anything else. I've been told I am, by people who wanted me to be. It isn't the same." --- # Mrs. Pell (Harriet Pell) **Background.** Born 1830 in Southwark; on the stage from fourteen — chorus, then small parts in melodrama at the Surrey and the Adelphi — until a throat illness in '62 ended the singing. Married a man named Pell who was useful to her and died conveniently. Has run a lodging-house in Brewer Street since '71; fences jewellery through a cousin in Hatton Garden; takes a cut from three or four working women besides Clara. Fifty-six and looks sixty. **Personality and flaws.** Sharp, amused, untender. She likes Clara in the way one likes a clever dog — with real affection and some proprietorship. She is not kindly. She does not give advice unless asked and sometimes not then; when she does, it is usually correct and never warm. She has contempt for young gentlemen in general and has cultivated it into a principle. She drinks gin in the afternoons, neatly, and is never drunk. Her flaw: she mistakes her own clear sight for wisdom and does not allow that other people might want things she thinks foolish. **Idiosyncrasies.** Keeps a canary. Reads novels and pretends not to. Calls Clara "Miss Westlake" in public and "girl" in private. Has a scar on the back of her left hand she will not explain. **Physicality.** Short, broad, still handsome in the bones. Dyed black hair, papery skin, very good teeth for her age and class. Moves without hurry. **Motives and allegiances.** Income, comfort, Clara's continued usefulness, her own dignity. She would not protect Clara against her own interest, but her interest and Clara's have so far aligned. **Voice:** > "He's not clever enough for you, girl. That is not a compliment to you. It is a warning about him." > "I have known a dozen of him. They start by bringing flowers and they end by bringing policemen. Not on purpose. That is the aggravating thing." --- # Sir Horace Venn **Background.** Clara's and James's adversary, though barely present on the page. Late fifties, minor baronet, second husband of James's mother Eleanor, now married again to a woman of twenty-three. Made his money in railway shares and a little shipping. Not cruel in the storybook sense; merely ordinary, acquisitive, and unimaginative, which amounts in practice to the same thing. **Personality.** He believes himself fair. He believes his second wife was grateful to marry him. He believes the pearls are his because his wife gave them to him on what he understood to be her deathbed, though the understanding was his alone. He is the kind of man who will not go to the police over a burglary because it embarrasses him, but will make sure the right clubs know. **Physicality.** Heavy, florid, mutton-chopped. Carries himself as a man who has never been refused a chair. **Motives.** Not to look foolish. To recover what is his. To punish, if he can do it without appearing to. **Voice:** > "I do not propose to involve the constabulary. I propose to have the matter understood."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — The Art of Entering One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. The ground floor is for callers and constables; the servants' door is for the tradesmen and the unlucky; but the sash above the portico belongs, if one has the patience for it, to oneself. The house in Grosvenor Square was not a hard one. I had watched it three evenings from the area railings of the house opposite, a coal-smut-smelling spot where no one looks twice at a woman waiting with a basket. I had seen the gas go down in the drawing-room at a quarter past eleven and the candle go up in the front bedroom at half past. I had seen the second-floor windows remain dark. The third floor is always servants, and servants sleep badly, but they sleep away from the street. The front of a great house is a kind of stage, and no one watches the stage in the dark hours. The ivy went up to the second storey. Above that, the stucco was worked in a pattern of leaves that would hold a foot if one knew where the leaves were deepest. I knew. I had made it my business, on the second evening, to walk around to the mews and look at the back of the house, which is where one learns the front. The drainpipe on the east corner was sound. The sash of the small dressing-room window, three along from the drainpipe, had been painted open last summer and never properly closed; I had seen the housemaid fight it for a quarter of an hour one morning and give up. I went in after one o'clock, when the last hansom had gone down South Audley Street and the watchman in the square had passed on his second round. I took off my boots at the foot of the ivy and tied them round my neck by the laces. One climbs better in stockinged feet. The ivy is a liar — it looks sturdy and is not — but it will take a woman's weight for the first fifteen feet if she climbs close to the wall and does not trust any single stem for longer than it takes to reach the next. After that, the stucco. After that, the drainpipe, which was cold enough through my stockings to make me breathe in sharply and remind myself not to. After that, the sill. The sash gave as I had known it would. I lifted it by the inch, because any faster and the wood speaks. When the gap was a hand's breadth I put my ear to it and listened for the sound of a breath, and heard none, and lifted it the rest of the way. The room smelled of old lavender and a little of mice. A dressing-room, as I had thought: a wardrobe, a washstand, a narrow bed made up but not slept in, a door on the far side standing half open into a larger room beyond. Through the gap of that door I could see the edge of a rug, the corner of a chest of drawers, and, unexpectedly, a thin slice of yellow light. I stayed on the sill a long moment. A candle left burning is not, in itself, unusual — the rich are careless with flame — but a candle at half past one, in a bedroom whose occupant I had watched take himself to bed two hours since, is a fact to be considered. I considered it. I climbed in anyway. The rug muffled me. I put my boots down by the sash and crossed the dressing-room on the outsides of my feet, which is quieter than the flats, and laid my hand on the edge of the inner door, and looked through. He was sitting in a button-backed chair by the fire, which had gone out. There was a book open on his knee. He was not reading it. His head was tipped back against the chair and his eyes were open and fixed on the ceiling, and he was so still that for a foolish half-second I thought he was dead, and was annoyed about it. Then he moved — just his thumb, turning the corner of the page — and I saw that he was entirely awake, and had been awake, I thought, a long time. I could have gone back through the sash. He had not seen me. The rug was thick; I had made no sound; I was standing in shadow; the candle was on the other side of the room. I counted the steps back to the window. Four. I did not take them. I have wondered since what stopped me. It was not pity; I felt none. It was not fear; he was slighter than I am and further from the bell. I think it was vanity — I wanted to see his face when he saw mine — and that is a poor excuse for a professional, and I have tried to be honest about it. He turned his head. He did not start. He did not rise. He looked at me across the room as if I were the next expected event of a long evening, and he put his thumb in the page to keep the place, and closed the book, and said, very quietly, "Don't go. Please." I had my hand on the jamb. I did not answer him. I did not move. The candle guttered and steadied. Somewhere in the square a horse shifted in its traces and a man coughed. He did not take his eyes off me. He was perhaps six-and-twenty, fair, in evening dress without the coat; his collar was undone and his cuffs were turned back as if he had been going to write a letter and had not. "Please," he said again. I stood on the threshold with my boots at my feet and considered the distance to the window.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of opening chapter writing. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Execution** The narrator's voice is exactly what the plan promised: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and spare. The prose resists the lush Victorian register and instead delivers something more like a technical manual with feeling underneath. "The ivy is a liar — it looks sturdy and is not" is the kind of sentence that earns its place: concrete, characterful, and revealing of a mind that distrusts surfaces. The instruction-manual quality of the opening ("One quickly learns...") is sustained without becoming a gimmick. The narrator teaches us her craft and, in doing so, teaches us herself. The plan called for "shorter sentences where the LLM instinct is to roll out long ones" and the chapter largely delivers. Sentences like "I considered it. I climbed in anyway." are exactly right — the gap between those two sentences does real work. The rhythm is controlled throughout. **Character** Clara comes through with precision. Her vanity is shown rather than told: "I think it was vanity — I wanted to see his face when he saw mine — and that is a poor excuse for a professional, and I have tried to be honest about it." This is a perfect moment — self-aware but not self-forgiving, and the phrase "I have tried to be honest about it" carries a slight irony that suggests she may not be fully succeeding. The profile's note that she "counts exits" is rendered literally and naturally. Her professional coldness is established without being announced. James's brief appearance is well-handled. "He looked at me across the room as if I were the next expected event of a long evening" is a strong characterization in a single image. His physical description — fair, collar undone, cuffs turned back "as if he had been going to write a letter and had not" — is specific and suggestive without over-explaining. The detail of keeping his thumb in the page is excellent: it shows a kind of composure that is also slightly absurd, slightly touching. **Dialogue** The dialogue is minimal — just "Don't go. Please." repeated — and this is the right choice. The repetition of "Please" lands because it's so undefended. The plan called for dialogue that "misses, deflects, stops short," and this chapter wisely gives us almost none, saving the conversation for Chapter 2. What's here is perfectly calibrated. **Plot and Structure** The chapter does exactly what it needs to: establishes the world, the protagonist's competence and voice, and ends on a moment of genuine suspension. The ending — "I stood on the threshold with my boots at my feet and considered the distance to the window" — is strong because it refuses resolution while being completely concrete. The boots at her feet are a nice detail: she has already put them down, already committed more than she intended. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly flawless at what it attempts, but a few minor observations: "a coal-smut-smelling spot where no one looks twice at a woman waiting with a basket" is slightly awkward in rhythm. The phrase "the rich are careless with flame" is a touch aphoristic in a way that slightly breaks the texture — it's the one moment where the narrator sounds like she's performing wisdom rather than thinking. The description of James as "perhaps six-and-twenty" when he is established as twenty-eight in the character profile is a minor inconsistency (though this could be Clara's deliberate uncertainty or a small error). The "foolish half-second I thought he was dead, and was annoyed about it" is a wonderful moment — the annoyance is perfectly in character and the self-awareness of calling it "foolish" is exactly right. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan almost exactly: ivy, drainpipe, sash window, the dressing-room, the unexpected candle, the young man awake and reading, the "Don't go. Please." The plan's commitment to specificity and unhurried prose is honored. The ending on "her hand still on the window-sash, uncommitted" is rendered as "I stood on the threshold with my boots at my feet and considered the distance to the window" — slightly different but arguably better, more concrete. **Overall** This is genuinely strong literary fiction writing. The voice is distinctive and sustained, the craft is evident in every sentence-level choice, and the chapter accomplishes its structural purpose while leaving the reader wanting the next one. It avoids the caper-tone trap, the heist-quippery trap, and the purple-prose trap. It is not a masterpiece — there are small rough edges — but it is well above the median of what this kind of prompt produces. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — A Civil Conversation "If I stay," I said, "you will not ring." "No," he said. "If you ring, I shall be out of the window before the wire pulls. You will have raised the house and caught no one, and your servants will not thank you for it at this hour." "I know," he said. "I am not going to ring." He had not moved from the chair. The book was still in his lap, his thumb still in the page, as though he meant to go on reading when I left. I stepped forward into the candle's throw and stopped at the foot of the bed, which put the width of the room between us, and a good deal of upholstered furniture, and a fire-iron if I needed one. "Sit down, if you like," he said. "I do not like." "No," he said again, and looked down at the book, and after a moment set it on the little table at his elbow. "Forgive me. I am behaving as if this were a morning call." "I should prefer that you did not." "What should you prefer?" "That you tell me why you are awake at half past one in the morning in a house I have watched three nights, and why you did not put the candle out when you heard the sash." He was silent. I had not known whether he had heard the sash. Now I did. "I have not been sleeping," he said. "For some weeks. I sit up. I thought — I don't know what I thought. That you would come, and I would — say something. And you would understand it. I hadn't got further than that." "You knew I was coming?" "I knew *someone* was coming. A house was done in Wilton Crescent a month ago, and one in Chesterfield Street the week after, and both by the window, and both — forgive me — rather well. I thought this house would be noticed sooner or later. I have been hoping for it." "Hoping." "Yes." I did not answer that. There are a number of things a man may mean by such a word, and I did not at that moment wish to choose between them. I looked instead at the room. A gentleman's bedroom of the usual kind, too much mahogany, a set of hairbrushes with his monogram in silver, a photograph on the chimneypiece of a woman no longer young in a dress ten years out of date. The cufflinks on the washstand were good but not extraordinary. A gold watch on the dressing-table. A signet ring beside it. Nothing one could not replace in a morning on Bond Street. "You are looking at my cufflinks," he said. "I am calculating." "Take them." "No." "They were a gift from a man I disliked. No one will miss them. The watch also, if you want it — it keeps poor time." "I do not take gifts," I said. "I take what I have decided to take. It is a different trade." He smiled at that — a slow, tired smile that did not make him handsomer but did make him, for the first time, interesting. "I beg your pardon. I did not mean to patronise you." "You did not succeed." "No," he said. "I see that." We stood — he sat, rather; I stood — in the quiet of the room for what was probably half a minute and felt longer. I had not put out a hand to anything. I had not taken a step toward the dressing-table. This was already the longest I had ever spent in a stranger's bedroom, and every minute of it was a minute in which a floorboard might creak elsewhere in the house and a servant come up to ask if the master wanted anything, as they sometimes do when they hear a voice. "I am going now," I said. "Will you tell me your name?" "No." "May I tell you mine?" "I would rather you did not." "James Ashcombe," he said, as though I had not spoken. "I am the younger son of this house. My brother is in Italy. My — my stepfather is at his place in Hertfordshire. I am alone here except for four servants, who sleep at the top and are deaf, all four of them, my mother used to say, from preference." "Mr. Ashcombe," I said, "you are telling me a great deal of what I came to find out. I do not want to know it from you. It spoils the pleasure." He looked at me then in a way that made me wish I had not used the word *pleasure*, and I turned toward the dressing-room door. "I saw you dance once," he said. I stopped. I did not turn. I should have gone on through the door and put my boots on and been at the sill before he had finished the sentence, and I did not, and that is the first mistake I made in that house, though not the last. "At Lady Carmichael's," he said, behind me. "In seventy-seven. You wore green. You danced with a man called Everett and then with my brother and then with no one, because the music stopped. Your name was not what you have not told me tonight." "You are mistaken," I said, to the door. "I am not." "You are mistaken, Mr. Ashcombe, and you will continue to be mistaken about it, or I shall be sorry I did not take your cufflinks after all, and a good deal more besides." I went through into the dressing-room. I put on my boots sitting on the edge of the unslept-in bed. My hands were steady; I was pleased about that, afterwards. I went out through the sash without looking back at the inner door, and down the drainpipe, and the stucco, and the ivy, and into the square. I walked home by a long way. I was angry at having been recognised. I was angrier, by Oxford Street, that I was pleased.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft and control. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** The first-person voice is exactly what the plan promised: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and spare. Clara's narration is consistently in character — she counts exits, she calculates, she notices the room as inventory ("A gold watch on the dressing-table. A signet ring beside it. Nothing one could not replace in a morning on Bond Street"). The voice never slips into sentimentality or self-pity. The final two sentences — "I was angry at having been recognised. I was angrier, by Oxford Street, that I was pleased." — are a perfect encapsulation of her character's blind spot, delivered with the flatness the plan called for. The plan specified she would be "wrong about herself in places the reader can see," and this is executed without being underlined. James is rendered with equal precision. His line "I thought — I don't know what I thought. That you would come, and I would — say something. And you would understand it. I hadn't got further than that." is exactly the voice sketched in the character profile — the moral laziness disguised as sensitivity, the wanting without the doing. He is not stupid but not quick. His offer of the cufflinks is both kind and slightly useless, which is his defining quality. **Dialogue** The dialogue is exceptional. It misses, deflects, and stops short exactly as the plan intended. The exchange about the cufflinks — "Take them." / "No." / "They were a gift from a man I disliked." / "I do not take gifts. I take what I have decided to take. It is a different trade." — is sharp and character-revealing without being showy. The dialogue does not explain itself. The recognition scene ("I saw you dance once") is handled with restraint; Clara's response ("You are mistaken, Mr. Ashcombe, and you will continue to be mistaken about it, or I shall be sorry I did not take your cufflinks after all, and a good deal more besides.") is a perfect blend of threat and wit that stays in character. **Prose Quality** The prose is genuinely spare without being thin. Sentences like "He had not moved from the chair. The book was still in his lap, his thumb still in the page, as though he meant to go on reading when I left" do real work — they establish his stillness, his deliberate non-threat, his odd calm. The observation about the photograph ("a woman no longer young in a dress ten years out of date") plants the mother without announcing her. There is no purple prose, no forced metaphor, no decorative language that doesn't earn its place. The one moment that slightly strains is "a fire-iron if I needed one" — this is good, but the parenthetical rhythm of the sentence ("and a good deal of upholstered furniture, and a fire-iron if I needed one") is slightly more self-conscious than the surrounding prose. It's a minor note. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and improves on it. The plan called for: they talk, he doesn't ring, she doesn't flee, he asks what she came for, she tells him truthfully, he offers cufflinks she declines, he asks her name she lies, he recognizes her, she leaves angry at being recognized and angrier at being pleased. All of this is executed, and the execution is better than the plan's summary — the recognition scene is more charged, the cufflink exchange more revealing, the final lines more precise. **Coherence and Plot** The chapter is coherent and advances the plot efficiently. We learn James has been waiting, that he knows the house will be targeted, that he recognizes Clara from her former life, and that Clara is already compromised by the encounter. The chapter plants the Hertfordshire/stepfather information naturally through James's exposition without it feeling like an info-dump. **What Doesn't Quite Work** The chapter is very close to excellent throughout, which makes the minor issues more visible. The line "This was already the longest I had ever spent in a stranger's bedroom" is slightly too self-aware, slightly too much of a wink at the reader. It's the one moment where the narrator seems to be performing her own coolness rather than simply being cool. Also, the plan mentioned she would be "angry at having been recognized, angrier at being pleased by it" — the chapter delivers this, but the final two sentences are so clean they risk being too neat, almost aphoristic. The plan warned against "any sentence that exists to be pretty," and these final sentences exist partly to be clever. They work, but they're the most calculated moment in an otherwise unselfconscious piece. **Overall Assessment** This is writing of genuine quality — controlled, specific, character-driven, with dialogue that does real work and prose that resists decoration. It follows its plan faithfully while improving on it in execution. The voice is consistent and distinctive. The chapter does what it sets out to do without announcing that it's doing it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — Brewer Street The fog came down on the Tuesday and did not lift until the Friday. It was the kind that tastes of the river and leaves a yellow mark on a white collar by noon. I did not go out in it. I had three good reasons and one bad one, and I told myself the three. Brewer Street at that end is not what it was. Mrs. Pell's house is the last of a row of four that lean companionably against one another, the plaster going at the corners, the fanlights cracked in patterns that I have come to know as one knows the veining of a leaf. My rooms are at the top: a bedroom with a sloping ceiling, and a sitting-room with a window that looks over the slates of Wardour Street and, in clear weather, as far as the spire of St. Anne's. There was no clear weather that week. There was the fog, and the slates beaded with it, and now and then the shape of a chimney darker than the grey it stood in. I sat by the window on the Wednesday afternoon with a book I was not reading and waited for Mrs. Pell to come up. She came at four, as she does, with a tray: tea, the evening *Globe*, and a plate of the stale seed-cake she buys cheap on a Tuesday and makes last the week. "You've not been out," she said. "No." "Two days." "Three, by the evening." She set the tray down on the little table and straightened, and looked at me with the look she uses on women who owe her money, which I do not. "Are you ill?" "I am prudent." "Prudent," she said, as if tasting the word, and found it wanting, and went away down the stairs without comment. I read the *Globe*. There was a column of burglary notices on the fourth page, as there is every evening — one in Pimlico, one in Bayswater, one on the Edgware Road which was plainly a servant and not our trade at all — and among them, four lines: > *A house in Grosvenor Square was entered on the night of the 14th instant. Nothing is known to be missing. The police have no description.* I read the four lines twice. I folded the paper so that they were uppermost, and then I unfolded it again and folded it the other way, so they were not. Mrs. Pell came back at six with the gin. She brought two glasses, which she does not always, and set one down at my elbow without asking, and sat in the other chair with her own, and drank off half of it in one pull and set it down. "Grosvenor Square," she said. "Yes." "Nothing missing." "No." "That is unusual for you, girl." I did not answer. She drank the rest of her gin and looked at the fire, which was a poor one, the coal being the cheap sort that burns blue and gives off no heat. "I have been on the stage," she said, after a while, "with a woman who had been on the stage with a woman who had known Mrs. Siddons. That is how near I am to the great Mrs. Siddons, which is to say, not near at all, but nearer than you are to being a fool, Miss Westlake, so I will ask you once. Did you see someone in that house?" "Yes." "Did he see you?" "Yes." "Does he know you?" "He thinks he does." She looked at me a long moment. Then she got up, and took her empty glass, and said, "Well. Drink your tea. It's getting cold," and went out, and shut the door behind her with unnecessary care. I did not drink the tea. I sat at the window until the gas was lit in the street below and the fog took the light and made globes of it, each lamp a soft yellow moon in the murk, and the slates above Wardour Street went from grey to black and were gone. I went to the cupboard where I keep my work-things and I took out the box at the back, which is a plain japanned box that was my mother's, and I opened it. There is not much in it. A prayer-book I do not use. A length of black ribbon. A twist of paper containing four mother-of-pearl buttons from a dress my mother had worn in the year she died, which I had cut off before the dress was given away and had never sewn onto anything else; I have meant to, sometimes. I took the twist of paper out and held it in my palm, and felt the small hard shapes of the buttons through it, and put it back without unwrapping it. Underneath the prayer-book there was a letter in my aunt's hand, addressed to me at a place I no longer lived, and forwarded twice. I had not opened it. I did not open it then. I closed the box and put it back behind the work-things and shut the cupboard door. He had said *I saw you dance once*. I had been seventeen and a half and my dress had not in fact been green; it had been a pale grey-green that one of my aunts had called *eau de Nil* and the other had called dirty water. I had danced with a man called Everett, though, and with two others whose names I could no longer have given under oath. I had not known I was being watched from a doorway by a boy of twenty who had not asked to be introduced. I did not know why a boy of twenty would remember a grey-green dress for seven years. I did not want to know it. I wanted him not to have said it. I went to bed early, and lay awake, and listened to the fog blunting the sounds of the street, and considered, without coming to any conclusion, whether prudence and cowardice are in fact different things or only the same thing spoken of in different rooms.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of prose that demonstrates considerable craft and restraint. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Character** The first-person voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Clara's narration is exactly as planned: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and wrong about herself in ways the reader can see. The line "I had three good reasons and one bad one, and I told myself the three" is an excellent opening gambit — economical, self-aware in the wrong direction, and immediately characterizing. The voice never slips into sentimentality or self-pity, which is a real discipline given the material (the mother's buttons, the unopened letter). The characterization of Clara is consistent with her profile throughout. Her vanity about competence, her habit of counting exits (metaphorically here — she folds the newspaper away from the incriminating lines, then unfolds it), her unsentimental relationship with her past, and her susceptibility to flattery that she refuses to acknowledge — all present and handled with subtlety. The final paragraph, where she lies awake wondering whether prudence and cowardice are different things, is exactly the kind of self-examination the profile calls for: she reaches a question but refuses an answer. Mrs. Pell is rendered beautifully in a small space. Her dialogue is sharp and economical. "I have been on the stage with a woman who had been on the stage with a woman who had known Mrs. Siddons" is a wonderful piece of characterization — it establishes her age, her theatrical background, her self-deprecating wit, and her method of approaching a subject obliquely, all in one sentence. Her exit — shutting the door "with unnecessary care" — is a perfect small detail. She is consistent with her profile: sharp, amused, untender, not giving advice unless she has to. **Prose Quality** The prose is genuinely spare in the way the plan demands. Sentences are short where the instinct would be to extend them. Concrete nouns dominate: the japanned box, the mother-of-pearl buttons, the twist of paper, the seed-cake bought cheap on Tuesdays. The fog is used well — it's atmospheric without being overwrought, and the detail about it tasting of the river and leaving a yellow mark on a white collar is specific and earned. The lamp-globes in the fog ("each lamp a soft yellow moon in the murk") is the one moment that edges toward the conventionally poetic, but it's brief and doesn't overstay. The "veining of a leaf" comparison for the cracked fanlights is slightly more literary than the surrounding prose but not egregiously so. The passage about the dress — "a pale grey-green that one of my aunts had called eau de Nil and the other had called dirty water" — is excellent. It's funny, period-specific, and characterizing without announcing itself as any of those things. **Dialogue** The dialogue is notably strong. It's elliptical, deflecting, and stops short in exactly the way the plan specifies. The exchange about prudence ("Prudent," she said, as if tasting the word, and found it wanting) is handled with real economy. The Grosvenor Square exchange — monosyllabic, each line a beat — is well-paced. Mrs. Pell's theatrical digression before her real question is the best piece of dialogue in the chapter. **Plot and Structure** The chapter does what it needs to do: it establishes Clara's state of mind after the encounter, develops Mrs. Pell as a character, and plants the emotional seeds (the buttons, the letter, the memory of the dress) without resolving anything. The four-line newspaper notice is handled well — the folding and unfolding of the paper is a nice piece of physical business that shows rather than tells Clara's agitation. The chapter is mostly mood, as planned, and it earns that. The plot is thin by design, and the chapter doesn't try to compensate with false incident. **Weaknesses** The chapter is very close to excellent but has a few small issues. The final paragraph's explicit question — "whether prudence and cowardice are in fact different things or only the same thing spoken of in different rooms" — is slightly too neat, too aphoristic. It does thematic work in a way the plan says to avoid. It's the one moment where the narrator seems to be performing insight rather than experiencing it. The rest of the chapter trusts the reader more. The gin scene is slightly underdeveloped — Mrs. Pell brings two glasses "without asking," which is a nice detail, but the scene ends before it quite lands. The transition from the newspaper to Mrs. Pell's return could be slightly more surprising. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the fog, the lodging, Mrs. Pell with tea and papers, the burglary notice, the oblique conversation, the mother-of-pearl buttons, the unopened letter, the memory of the dress. All present. The plan's note about "the past leaks in obliquely" is well-executed. **Overall** This is a high-quality piece of literary fiction that demonstrates real control of voice, restraint in prose, and genuine character work. Its weaknesses are minor and mostly confined to one slightly over-written final sentence. It is not a masterpiece — the chapter is somewhat static and the emotional range is deliberately narrow — but it is very good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — An Approach The fog lifted on the Friday and I went out. There is no use pretending one is not a creature of habit; I went to the coffee-house in Gerrard Street that Mrs. Pell patronises, because it was where I went on Fridays, and because if I had gone anywhere else she would have remarked on it. He was at the second table from the door. I saw him before he saw me, which was a small mercy and the last of the day. He was turning a coffee-cup in its saucer — round, and round again, and a quarter-turn back — and looking at the door, and not seeing it, in the way of a man who has been looking at a door for some time and has stopped expecting it to produce what he is waiting for. The moment I came in he saw me, and half-rose, and sat down again, which is a thing men do when they have forgotten whether they are to stand for a woman of a given kind. I went to the counter and ordered coffee and carried it to a table by the window, which was not his. I drank half of it. Then I got up, and crossed to his table, and sat down opposite him without being asked. "Mr. Ashcombe." "Miss — " "Don't." He shut his mouth. He had tried three times, I think, to decide what to call me, and was relieved to be spared the choice. His hair was less tidy than it had been in the candlelight; there was a greyish half-moon under each eye; he had not, I thought, been sleeping any better for having got what he had been waiting for. "Mrs. Pell told you this house," I said. "Yes." "How much." "Two pounds." "She overcharged you." "I thought perhaps she had." "You did not bargain." "No." "You should have. She thinks less of you for it. I should tell you that, because she will not." He took this in with a small wincing movement of the mouth, and nodded as though I had told him a thing he had already suspected and was pleased to have confirmed, which was not the effect I had intended, and which disarmed me in a way I did not choose to examine. "I have come," he said, "to ask you to do me a service. Which I shall pay for. I do not know what to pay. I should like you to tell me." "Tell me the service." He had rehearsed it. I saw that at once. He had rehearsed it well enough to have broken the rehearsal into pieces that no longer fitted. He began with his mother — *my mother, who died three years ago, who had a set of pearls that came to her from her own mother* — and then with his stepfather — *Sir Horace, who is not — who was not her first husband, nor my father, which I should say, since I myself get it wrong* — and then with a country house in Hertfordshire, and then with a wife of twenty-three who wore the pearls last month at a dinner at which James had not been present but of which a cousin of his had written to him, and then, doubling back, with the pearls again, which he had seen as a child on his mother's dressing-table, and which were not — he was at pains — not a thing of enormous value, as jewels went, but which ought to have come to his brother's wife, and had not. He did not say *stolen*. He said *kept*. He said, *kept from the family*. I noticed it and did not remark on it. "How much are they worth?" "I don't know." "A guess." "Five hundred. Perhaps seven." "To a pawnbroker, two hundred. To my cousin of Mrs. Pell's, three, if he is feeling generous, which he is not. You are offering me pearls I cannot sell for what they are worth, from a house I have never seen, belonging to a man who will go straight to the police the moment he finds them gone." "He will not go to the police." "Why." "Because he will not wish it known that he has them." I looked at him. He looked back. He had said the one clever thing of the afternoon without knowing, I think, that it was the clever thing. "Fifty pounds," he said, "and the pearls to do with as you like." "A hundred." "A hundred." "You agreed to that too quickly. You are making every mistake a man can make in this conversation, Mr. Ashcombe. I am going to take your hundred pounds and I am going to get your pearls, but I want you to understand that I am doing it on my own terms, and that if at any point between now and the end of it you attempt to make it otherwise, I shall leave you in whatever room I am standing in and not come back. Is that clear?" "Yes." "Say it." "It is clear." "Good." He looked down at the coffee-cup, which he had not turned since I sat down, and then up at me again. "May I ask you one thing." "You may ask." "Why are you doing it." I had known he would ask. I had not decided what to answer. I said, "For the hundred pounds," which was a lie, and which he took for one, and which he did me the kindness of not challenging. He paid for my coffee on the way out, over my objection. At the door he said, "When shall we — ", and I said, "I shall send word," and left him on the pavement, turning up his collar against a wind that was not cold enough to need it. Mrs. Pell was in the hall when I came in. She looked at my face and said only: "He is not clever enough for you, girl. That is not a compliment to you. It is a warning about him." I went up without answering her.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft in nearly every dimension. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Character** Clara's voice is exactly what the plan promised: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and wrong about herself in ways the reader can see. The observation about James half-rising and sitting down again — "which is a thing men do when they have forgotten whether they are to stand for a woman of a given kind" — is precisely the kind of socially acute, slightly cold notation the character profile demands. She notices people as furniture. The line "He had rehearsed it well enough to have broken the rehearsal into pieces that no longer fitted" is excellent: it shows Clara's observational precision while also being genuinely true to how rehearsed speeches collapse in practice. James is rendered with equal fidelity. His one clever observation — that Sir Horace won't go to the police because he won't want it known he has the pearls — lands correctly, and Clara's recognition that he said it "without knowing, I think, that it was the clever thing" is a fine character beat. He is not stupid but not quick, exactly as specified. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The staccato exchange — "How much." / "Two pounds." / "She overcharged you." / "I thought perhaps she had." — is genuinely good. It establishes power dynamics, character, and situation simultaneously without any of it feeling constructed. The rhythm is right. The moment where Clara tells him to say "it is clear" and he complies is excellent: it shows her need for control and his willingness to be controlled, which is both character-consistent and slightly ominous about what she's getting into. The one weak moment in dialogue is "Why are you doing it" / "For the hundred pounds" — not because it's badly written, but because it's the most expected beat in the scene. The plan acknowledges she lies, and the lie is good, but the question itself is a little on-the-nose. That said, the follow-through — "which was a lie, and which he took for one, and which he did me the kindness of not challenging" — rescues it by making the mutual acknowledgment of the lie into its own kind of intimacy. **Show vs. Tell** The chapter is almost entirely showing. The characterization of James's nervousness comes through the coffee-cup turning, the half-rise, the untidy hair, the grey half-moons under his eyes. Clara's attraction to him is never stated but is present in the precision of her observation of him and in the line "which disarmed me in a way I did not choose to examine" — which is the right amount of acknowledgment without over-explanation. **Plot and Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely and well. Mrs. Pell selling the address, James's awkwardness in daylight, the proposition made badly, the fee named too high, Clara agreeing before she has decided to — all present. The plan's note that "she agrees before she has decided to" is rendered through the speed of the negotiation: she names her price, he accepts, and it's done before either of them has quite processed it. The final Mrs. Pell line is deployed exactly as planned. **Prose Quality** The prose is spare without being thin. "He paid for my coffee on the way out, over my objection. At the door he said, 'When shall we — ', and I said, 'I shall send word,' and left him on the pavement, turning up his collar against a wind that was not cold enough to need it." This is excellent. The detail of the collar — unnecessary, slightly performative — tells us something about James's self-consciousness without stating it. The em-dash interruption of "When shall we — " is exactly right. There is no purple prose. There are no forced metaphors. The one slightly risky move — "which was a small mercy and the last of the day" — earns its slight archness because it's in Clara's voice and she would say something like that. **Minor Criticisms** The chapter is almost too controlled. The plan called for dialogue that "misses, deflects, stops short," and while the dialogue does stop short, it rarely misses — Clara and James are both performing their roles quite competently. The plan also noted Clara should be "wrong about herself in places the reader can see," and while this is present (the disarming she doesn't examine, the lie about the hundred pounds), it could be pushed slightly further. The chapter is excellent but perhaps slightly safer than the plan's most ambitious intentions. The question "Why are you doing it" is the one moment that feels like a conventional beat in an otherwise unconventional scene. **Overall** This is a high-quality chapter that delivers on its plan with genuine craft. The voice is consistent, the characters are rendered with precision, the dialogue is strong, and the prose is controlled without being cold. It is not quite a masterpiece — it lacks the slight wrongness, the productive failure, that would push it to that level — but it is very good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 — Daylight I sent word on the Monday, by the boy who runs errands in Brewer Street for a penny and who cannot read, which is a qualification in that trade. The note said only *Regent's Park, Tuesday, three o'clock, the bench east of the bandstand*, and was not signed. He was there before me. I had meant to be there before him and had allowed myself an extra quarter of an hour, and he had allowed himself, I suppose, an extra half, and was sitting with his hat on his knee and his gloves laid across the hat, looking at the gravel. He rose when he saw me. This time he did it properly. "You are early," I said. "I was afraid of being late." "Sit down." He sat. I sat at the other end of the bench, with perhaps two feet of painted slats between us, which was the distance a governess and her charge might keep, or two strangers who had found themselves with the same view. A nurse went past with a perambulator. A small boy in a sailor suit ran after a hoop and fell over it, and got up, and ran on. I watched the boy until he was out of sight round the curve of the path, because it gave me something to do with my eyes other than look at James Ashcombe. "You have brought the plan," I said. "I have tried." He produced from an inner pocket a folded sheet of paper, and unfolded it on his knee, and I leaned across the slats to look. It was a ground plan of the Hertfordshire house, done in pencil, and it was not good. He had got the staircase on the wrong side of the hall. He had drawn in a conservatory that he then admitted, unprompted, might not still be there, his uncle having spoken once of pulling it down. He had put the new wife's bedroom on the first floor above the library and was not sure of it. "You grew up in this house," I said. "I visited it. In the holidays. My brother and I — we were more at Aston, which was my father's." "You do not know where your stepfather's wife sleeps." "No." "You do not know where the pearls are kept." "I do not." "Mr. Ashcombe," I said, "you are going to be of very little use to me." "I know." He said it without resentment. He said it as a man states the weather. I had meant the remark to wound him a little — not much, only a touch, to keep him in his place — and had succeeded only in stating what he had already told himself, probably several times, in the course of the weekend. I was finding it harder, from one meeting to the next, to wound him at all. This was itself a difficulty, and not one I had foreseen. I took the pencil from him and corrected the staircase. I put the conservatory in with a question mark. I wrote *MAID?* in the back passage where I thought the maid would sleep, and *GUN-ROOM?* at the end of the east wing, because men of Sir Horace's sort always have one and it is always at the end of a wing. He watched me draw, with the particular attention of a man who is being shown something he ought to have known. "You have done this before," he said. "Drawn a plan?" "Corrected one." "Yes." We sat with the paper between us. The wind lifted one corner of it and he put his glove on the corner to hold it. His hand was next to mine on the paper. Neither of us moved the hand. "Walk with me," I said. "I cannot think sitting down." We walked. We walked north, round the Inner Circle, and then west, and out through Hanover Gate, and down Park Road, because I wanted the noise of traffic to talk under. I asked him about the household — how many servants, how old, which of them had been his mother's, which his stepfather's, which new. He knew some of it. He knew the butler's name, and the cook's, and nothing of the housemaids. I asked him when the family would be away from the house, and he said, uncertainly, that his stepfather and the wife were to be in Scotland from the fifteenth, and that the house would then be only the servants, and I said *Then we go on the sixteenth*, and he said *Yes*, and nothing more. We came back into the park by Clarence Gate and walked under the planes, which were beginning to drop their first leaves, although it was not properly autumn. The light was gone already out of the tops of the trees, and the air had that blue quality it has on an October afternoon in London that is about to turn to evening. He stopped under one of the planes and put his hand on my sleeve — lightly, only the fingers — and I stopped because he had stopped, and he bent his head and kissed me. He did it badly. He missed the centre of my mouth and had to correct, and when he had corrected he apologised — *I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I did not mean to — that is, I did mean to, but not — * until I put my hand on the front of his coat to stop him talking, and then, because I had stopped him and he was looking at me with the expression of a man waiting to be struck, I kissed him back, properly, to end the matter. It did not end the matter. Nothing was said about it. He walked me as far as the corner of Wigmore Street, where I told him I could go on alone, and he raised his hat and went the other way without looking back, which I was grateful for and irritated by in equal measure. I walked home by a long way. By Oxford Circus I had decided I was furious with myself. By Soho Square I had decided I was not sorry. The two decisions did not contradict each other, which I found, on reflection, the most troubling thing of all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine literary craft. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** Clara's voice is exactly what the plan promised: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and wrong about herself in ways the reader can see. The observation that she "had meant to be there before him" and failed is a small, perfect character note — she is competitive even in punctuality, and she loses. The line "I had meant the remark to wound him a little — not much, only a touch, to keep him in his place — and had succeeded only in stating what he had already told himself" is excellent: it shows Clara's cruelty, her self-awareness about that cruelty, and her failure simultaneously. This is nuanced characterization executed through action and interiority rather than description. James is rendered with equal precision. "He said it without resentment. He said it as a man states the weather." This is exactly the character profile's "moral laziness disguised as sensitivity" — he has already absorbed his own uselessness and made peace with it, which is both charming and slightly troubling. His botched kiss and excessive apology are perfectly calibrated to his character: well-intentioned, slightly useless, requiring Clara to do the actual work. **Prose Quality** The prose is genuinely spare without being thin. "The light was gone already out of the tops of the trees, and the air had that blue quality it has on an October afternoon in London that is about to turn to evening" — this is specific and earned, not decorative. The plan warned against sentences that exist to be pretty, and this sentence exists to do atmospheric work while also marking the transition to the kiss. It earns its place. The final paragraph — the walk home, the two contradictory decisions that don't contradict each other — is the chapter's best moment. "The two decisions did not contradict each other, which I found, on reflection, the most troubling thing of all." This is Clara being wrong about herself in a way the reader can see: she thinks the troubling thing is the logical compatibility of the two feelings, when the troubling thing is that she has feelings at all. The chapter earns this ending. **Dialogue** The dialogue is excellent. It misses, deflects, and stops short exactly as planned. "You do not know where your stepfather's wife sleeps." / "No." / "You do not know where the pearls are kept." / "I do not." This staccato exchange is both functionally expository and characterologically revealing — Clara's interrogation style, James's undefended honesty. The dialogue never exists merely to convey information; it always reveals character simultaneously. **Plot and Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the bench, the bad plan, the corrections, the walk, the kiss under the plane tree, the apology, the return kiss "to stop him talking," the walk home with the two contradictory decisions. The plan called for the kiss to be "clumsily, without asking, and then apologises so thoroughly she has to laugh" — the chapter delivers this almost exactly, though Clara doesn't quite laugh (she acts instead, which is more in character). The plan's note that "nothing is said after" is honored. The chapter ends on the emotional note specified. **What Doesn't Quite Work** The chapter is very good but not quite a masterpiece. A few minor observations: The small boy with the hoop is a slightly conventional period-texture detail — it does its job but doesn't surprise. The GUN-ROOM annotation is a nice touch of professional competence, but the MAID? annotation is slightly more predictable. The transition from the park back into the park via Clarence Gate is a little mechanical in its geography — it reads slightly like the author needed them back under the plane trees and walked them there efficiently rather than inevitably. The line "which was the distance a governess and her charge might keep, or two strangers who had found themselves with the same view" is the chapter's one moment of slightly strained simile — the governess/charge comparison is a touch too neat as a social observation, though it's not egregious. **Overall** This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with genuine skill. The voice is consistent, the characters are rendered with precision, the dialogue is strong, the emotional arc is earned without being announced, and the prose resists both prettiness and flatness. It is not a masterpiece — there are small mechanical moments — but it is very accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 — Hertfordshire We went down on the sixteenth by the five-past-seven from St. Pancras. He had booked second-class, which was sensible of him; first would have been remembered by a guard, and third by a passenger. I wore a dark walking-dress and a veil that was not heavy enough to be conspicuous, and he wore a tweed suit which he believed made him look like a man going shooting, and which in fact made him look like a man dressed by a valet to look like a man going shooting. We did not sit together. I had a novel; he had a newspaper he did not turn. We got down at a halt two stations before the village and walked across fields. It had rained in the afternoon and was not raining now, but the grass was wet to the knee by the time we came out on the lane above the park. The house was a long low thing of warm brick, set in a fold of the land, with a lime avenue going up to it and, at this hour, one window lit on the ground floor and no other. The servants would be in the kitchen wing at the back, which I could see from the lie of the chimneys. I had not needed James's plan for that. We went over the park wall by an elm that had grown too close to it and crossed the park keeping to the shadow of the hedges. He walked better in the country than he did in the town, which surprised me; he put his feet down as a man who had shot over the land as a boy, and I had not pictured him doing so. I filed the fact away with the other things I was learning about him and had not asked to learn. At the edge of the lawn I stopped him. "You will wait here." "I can come up." "You cannot. You have not climbed anything higher than a staircase in ten years and you will break your ankle on the roof and I shall have to carry you down. You will wait here, behind the yew, and if I am not out in forty minutes you will walk back to the halt by the way we came, and you will catch the twelve-twenty to town, and you will not enquire after me for three days. Is that clear?" "Clara — " "Is that clear." "Yes." He had used my name. I did not remark on it; I was not sure he had heard himself use it. I left him behind the yew and went across the lawn alone, keeping to the line of a flower-bed where the soft earth would take no print. The back of the house had a low service roof over the scullery that gave onto a flat lead gutter, and from the gutter a drainpipe went up to the first-floor windows, and from there the slates began. The slates were wet. Wet slate is the worst surface in the world short of ice, and I took it slowly, on my stomach where I could, on the outsides of my feet where I could not. The first-floor window I wanted was the third from the corner, which by my calculation was the new wife's dressing-room. The sash was latched. I took out the thin steel I keep for such sashes and slid it up the gap between the frames and lifted the catch, and the window slid open under my hand with a small dry whisper that was swallowed at once in the sound of a fox barking in the spinney. The dressing-room smelt of a woman who had overdone the scent-bottle. There was a great deal of ivory-backed brushwork on the table, and a little lacquered case that I opened first and found empty, and a rosewood case that I opened second and found the pearls, laid out on faded blue velvet — three strands, a brooch, a pair of drop ear-rings. They were good pearls. Not extraordinary. Good. I took them and put them in the soft bag under my jacket and closed the case and left it as I had found it. I turned to go. And then — this is the thing I cannot quite account for — I turned back, and opened the writing-desk. There was no call to. I had what I had come for. I opened it because the brass of its little key had caught the moonlight and because I have a weakness, long indulged, for writing-desks. Inside, under a blotter, was a bundle of letters tied with black ribbon. The top one had been opened, and folded back, and opened again; the fold was grey with handling. The hand was a woman's. The name on the first page — not my business, not then, not ever — was *Eleanor*, which had been the name of James's mother. The salutation was *My dearest*, and the signature, further down the page, was *yours, still, and always, H.* *H* was not Sir Horace. *H* was not any of the names James had spoken in my hearing. I stood a moment with the letters in my hand. I put them back. I retied the ribbon as it had been. I closed the desk. I went out through the window and across the slates and down the drainpipe and the gutter and the service roof, and I lost my footing on the last of the slates because my attention had gone, and I came down the last eight feet in a rush and landed badly on the gravel of the terrace. James was at me before I had got my breath. "You are hurt — " "Not much. Get me up. Walk." He got me up. He got an arm under mine and half-carried me across the lawn, and through the park, and over the wall, which I went over on my stomach because my left ankle would not take me, and down the lane to the halt. I did not tell him what was in the desk. On the train he fell asleep against the window, the newspaper slipping off his knee; I watched him sleep, and knew that I had made a decision without being consulted by myself about it, and did not know yet what it was.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on the ambitious intentions laid out in the writing plan. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Prose Style** The prose is exactly what the plan promised: spare, dry, specific, occasionally flat in a way that generates feeling through restraint rather than decoration. "He had a newspaper he did not turn" is a small, precise observation that does real character work without announcing itself. The instruction to Clara about what to do if she doesn't return in forty minutes — clinical, procedural, slightly cold — is excellent characterization. The sentence "I filed the fact away with the other things I was learning about him and had not asked to learn" is one of the chapter's best: it captures Clara's self-protective relationship to her own feelings without sentimentalizing it. The prose avoids the lush Victorian register the plan warned against. Sentences are short where they need to be. "Wet slate is the worst surface in the world short of ice" has the right professional flatness. The chapter earns its plainness. **Character Work** Clara is consistent with her profile throughout. Her competence is shown, not told. Her decision to open the writing-desk is motivated by a "weakness, long indulged, for writing-desks" — a small, specific, believable character detail that also serves the plot. Her withholding of the letters from James is consistent with her nature: she processes information as an instrument, not as a confessor. The final paragraph — "I had made a decision without being consulted by myself about it, and did not know yet what it was" — is a fine rendering of her blind spot about her own emotional life, exactly as the profile describes. James is handled well in his limited space. "He walked better in the country than he did in the town, which surprised me" — this is the kind of small discovery the plan called for, and it lands. His use of her name ("Clara —") without apparently hearing himself do it is a nice touch. He is not heroic in the rescue; he "got an arm under mine and half-carried me," which is adequate and slightly clumsy, consistent with his profile. **The Writing Desk Scene** This is the chapter's central creative achievement. The discovery of the letters is handled with real restraint. The narrator doesn't read them fully; she sees enough. The signature "H" — not Sir Horace, not any name James has spoken — opens a moral ambiguity that the plan explicitly wanted: the pearls may or may not be rightfully James's, and now the mother's memory he has constructed is complicated. Clara puts the letters back. She says nothing. This is the right choice, and the chapter earns it. The line "I opened it because the brass of its little key had caught the moonlight and because I have a weakness, long indulged, for writing-desks" is the chapter's best sentence. It's specific, slightly comic, and true to character. It avoids the trap of making the discovery feel fated or melodramatic. **Dialogue** The dialogue is minimal and functional. The exchange at the yew hedge is good — Clara's clipped commands, James's incomplete protest, the single word "Yes." The use of her name without comment is well-handled. There's no weak dialogue here; what exists is purposeful and in character. **Plot and Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The night train, the wet fields, the house, the pearls found in the dressing-case, the letters in the writing-desk, the slip on the slates, James catching her badly, the train home, his sleep against the window, her realization — all present and executed. The plan's note that "she does not tell him about the letters" and "watches him sleep and understands she has decided something without noticing" is rendered almost verbatim in spirit, though the prose finds its own phrasing. **Weaknesses** A few minor issues. "The house was a long low thing of warm brick, set in a fold of the land, with a lime avenue going up to it" — this is slightly more atmospheric than the plan's stated aesthetic, though it doesn't tip into purple prose. The phrase "a woman who had overdone the scent-bottle" is good; "a great deal of ivory-backed brushwork" is slightly awkward (brushwork usually refers to painting; she means hairbrushes with ivory backs). This is a small infelicity. The final sentence — "and did not know yet what it was" — is perhaps slightly too neat as a closing gesture, a little too consciously withheld. The plan warned against thematic legibility in final lines, and this one is close to the line. But it's not a serious failing. The chapter is not surprising in its broad strokes — the heist goes mostly as planned, the complication arrives as expected — but the execution is consistently above average, and the specific texture (the fox barking in the spinney swallowing the sound of the sash, the grey fold of the letter) elevates it. **Overall** This is accomplished, controlled literary fiction. It does what it sets out to do, avoids the traps it identified, and delivers genuine feeling through restraint. It is not a masterpiece — the plot is somewhat predictable in its beats, and the emotional revelation at the end is slightly too tidy for the aesthetic the plan aimed at — but it is very good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 — The Papers The ankle was not broken. It was the sort of thing that goes purple and yellow by turns for a fortnight and reminds one, on stairs, that one has been a fool. Mrs. Pell strapped it on the Thursday morning with a length of linen and a tongue-lashing delivered in a voice so low and even that I heard every word of it from the first landing up, and could not dispute a single one. "Thirty-one years old — " "Twenty-six." " — you have been climbing since you were four-and-twenty and you have never slipped on a slate in your life, and you will tell me, Miss Westlake, why you slipped on this one." "My mind was elsewhere." "Yes," she said. "I had worked that much out." She tied the linen off and sat back on her heels and looked up at me. "Where were the pearls while your mind was elsewhere?" "In my pocket." "Good," she said, and got up, and went out. I kept to the rooms for four days. On the fifth, the *Times* had it. It was a shorter notice than I had expected, and nastier. I read it twice. A house in Hertfordshire, no name given, had been entered on the night of the sixteenth; a quantity of jewellery had been removed; a man and a woman had been seen by a ticket-clerk at a country halt, boarding the twelve-twenty to town; the woman had walked with a limp, and the man had been fair, of middle height, in tweeds. It was not Sir Horace who had placed the notice, I was certain of that. It was someone who had wished Sir Horace to read it over his breakfast, and to understand from it that other people had read it also. Mrs. Pell came up at noon with the paper folded to the notice, although I had already seen it, and set it on the table between us. "You will be on a boat by Tuesday," she said. "Yes."
