"I have spoken to my cousin. He will take the pearls at two-sixty, which is less than they are worth, and more than you will get if you wait. I will bring you the money on Monday." "Thank you." "I have never liked being thanked." "I know." She looked at me a long moment. "The man is a difficulty." "Yes." "He will have been seen by the same ticket-clerk." "Yes." "He will be spoken of in a club before the week is out, if he has not been already. He will then be spoken to, by someone who has his interest at heart, and he will answer badly, because he does everything badly, and then he will come here." "Yes." "You will be gone before he does." "Yes." She nodded, once, and went down. She had not asked whether I wished to be gone before he came. It would not have occurred to her to ask it. It had not, until she spoke, occurred to me either. He came that evening. I heard his step on the stairs — he does not know how to come up a staircase quietly, and I was not surprised — and opened the door before he had reached the top. He was out of breath, and paler than I had seen him, and he had not shaved that morning or perhaps the morning before. He came in past me without speaking, and went to the window, and stood with his back to me and looked at the slates over Wardour Street for the length of time it takes to decide what one has come to say. "You have seen the paper," he said, without turning. "Yes." "My cousin called this afternoon. He did not say anything directly. He said a great deal that was not direct. He is under the impression that he has done me a kindness." "He has." "Yes. I suppose he has." He turned from the window. "I am coming with you." "Where." "Wherever you are going. I have thought about it. I have thought about very little else since Tuesday. I have a little money of my own — not much, but enough — and I have written to my brother, who is in Florence, a letter which I shall post on the way to the boat. I am not asking your permission, Clara. I am telling you." I sat down on the edge of the table, because standing was not comfortable, and because I did not want him to see that my hands had gone cold. "Mr. Ashcombe." "James." "Mr. Ashcombe, I found something in the desk at Hertfordshire." He did not speak. He had heard the change in my voice and understood, I think, that he was being offered a thing he could refuse. "A bundle of letters. To your mother. From a man whose name was not your father's and not your stepfather's. I did not take them. I read enough of the first to know what they were. I closed the desk and I left them where they were. I have not spoken of it until now." His face did a thing I had not seen it do. It did not change, exactly; it became, for a moment, empty, as though he had stepped out of it and would be back presently. Then he said, quite evenly: "I don't want to know what was in them." "They were — " "I don't want to know what was in them." "You are a fool." "Yes." "You have constructed a woman out of a photograph and a set of pearls and you are preparing to follow me to the Continent because of her, and I am telling you that the woman you have constructed is not the woman who wrote those letters, and you are telling me you would rather not know, and I am telling you that this is the whole of your difficulty, Mr. Ashcombe. You would rather not know. About anything." He stood very still. He said, after a long moment, "That is unjust." "It is not." "It is unjust tonight. It may be just another night. It is unjust tonight because I am here." I had no answer for that. He waited for one. When none came he picked up his hat from the chair where he had laid it, and said, "I shall be at Dover on Tuesday," and went down the stairs without looking back, and I sat on the edge of the table until I could no longer hear his step in the street below.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine literary craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** Clara's voice is exactly what the plan promised: dry, specific, occasionally cutting, and wrong about herself in ways the reader can see. The opening exchange with Mrs. Pell — "Thirty-one years old — " / "Twenty-six." — establishes the dynamic with economy and wit. The voice never slips into sentimentality or self-pity. When Clara sits on the edge of the table "because standing was not comfortable, and because I did not want him to see that my hands had gone cold," this is precisely the kind of self-revelation the plan called for: she notices her own reaction but frames it in terms of concealment, not feeling. This is excellent characterization. Mrs. Pell is rendered with the sharpness the profile demands. Her dialogue is clipped, functional, and reveals character without announcing it. "I have never liked being thanked" is a perfect line — it tells us everything about her without explaining anything. Her final observation, that she had not asked whether Clara wished to be gone before James came, and that it had not occurred to Clara either until she spoke, is one of the chapter's finest moments: a blind spot revealed through absence. James is rendered as the plan specified — not clever, not brave, but present in a way that matters. His "It is unjust tonight. It may be just another night. It is unjust tonight because I am here" is the chapter's emotional peak, and it earns its weight. It's the kind of thing a slightly foolish, decent man might actually say — not witty, not eloquent, but true in a way that stops Clara cold. The fact that she has no answer for it is the chapter's most honest moment. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It misses, deflects, and stops short exactly as the plan intended. The exchange about the letters is particularly well-handled: James's repeated "I don't want to know what was in them" is not weakness dressed as strength, but something more complicated — a man choosing the version of his mother he can live with, and knowing he is choosing it. Clara's attack on him ("You have constructed a woman out of a photograph and a set of pearls") is accurate and also slightly cruel in the way the plan specified she should be. The dialogue never exists to be pretty; it exists to do work. **Plot and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The Times notice, Mrs. Pell's instructions, James's arrival, the revelation of the letters, the quarrel, his departure without resolution — all present and correctly sequenced. The chapter ends without resolution, as planned, with Clara sitting on the table until she can no longer hear his step. This is the right ending: not an image that does thematic work, but a physical fact that carries emotional weight. **Prose Quality** The prose is spare and controlled. Sentences are short where the instinct would be to extend them. "He came in past me without speaking, and went to the window, and stood with his back to me and looked at the slates over Wardour Street for the length of time it takes to decide what one has come to say" — this is excellent: specific (Wardour Street), patient, and the final clause does the work of characterization without announcing it. There is almost no purple prose. The one moment that approaches it — "His face did a thing I had not seen it do. It did not change, exactly; it became, for a moment, empty, as though he had stepped out of it and would be back presently" — is actually quite good, and earns its slight poeticism by being grounded in observation rather than decoration. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly flawless within its own terms, which makes the few weaknesses more visible. The repeated "Yes" exchanges between Clara and Mrs. Pell, while effective as a rhythm, go on perhaps one beat too long — the technique is noticed. The revelation of the letters, while handled well, is slightly more expository than the rest of the chapter's show-don't-tell discipline would suggest; Clara explains the letters' significance rather than letting the scene carry it. These are minor complaints. The chapter is not surprising in the sense of doing something the reader could not have anticipated from the plan, but it executes its intentions with such precision and control that this feels less like a failure of creativity than a success of craft. The plan called for a quarrel not about the letters but about what James thinks he is doing — the chapter delivers this, though the quarrel is more about the letters than the plan suggested. This is a small deviation that actually improves the chapter. **Overall** This is sophisticated literary fiction executed with genuine skill. The voice is consistent, the characters are rendered with nuance, the dialogue earns its weight, and the prose serves the story without calling attention to itself. It is not a masterpiece — the chapter is somewhat contained within its own competence, and does not reach for anything that risks failure — but it is very good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 — Dover I went down on the Monday, not the Tuesday. Mrs. Pell brought the money at ten in the morning, in banknotes and a little gold, and a letter of introduction to a woman in Ostend who had once been on the stage with her and was now, she said, something less respectable and more useful. We did not embrace. She put a hand on my shoulder, briefly, and said, "Write to me if you find it dull," and went down the stairs before I could answer. I took the two o'clock from Victoria. I had one trunk and a carpet-bag. The trunk held what a woman takes who has decided not to come back; the carpet-bag held what a thief takes who has not yet decided whether she is one. I slept part of the way, with my head against the window-glass, and woke at Ashford with my neck stiff and the light going out of the fields. Dover in October is not a place one goes for pleasure. The boarding-house on Marine Parade was clean and cold and smelled of boiled cabbage and the sea in equal measure, and the woman who kept it asked no questions, having long since learned the commercial value of asking none. I took a room at the front. I could see the harbour from it, and the pier, and the lamps coming on along the front as the dusk came down. I did not sleep well. The ankle had gone from purple to yellow and ached in the cold. I lay and listened to the sea and to the sounds of a town that is always half awake because the boats go at all hours, and I thought about a number of things in no particular order, and came to no conclusion about any of them, which is the honest condition of most nights spent thinking. The Tuesday was grey. I walked in the morning — slowly, for the ankle — along the front as far as the castle and back. I ate a luncheon I did not want. In the afternoon I sat in the parlour of the boarding-house and read an old *Illustrated London News* from front to back, including the advertisements, which are sometimes the most informative part of a paper. The boat went at eight. At seven I put on my coat and took up the carpet-bag, the trunk having gone down an hour before with a boy from the boarding-house, and walked to the pier. The pier at Dover on a Tuesday evening in October is not a crowded place. There was a knot of people at the gangway — a family going out to an appointment in Brussels, a commercial traveller with a case of samples, two clergymen who were arguing about something in low voices — and beyond them the steamer, her lamps lit, breathing steam into the damp. I gave up my ticket and went aboard. I found a place at the rail on the landward side. I set down the carpet-bag. I had told myself on the train that I would not look for him. I told myself again at the rail. I looked for him. He was at the landward end of the pier. He had a small case at his feet. He was standing very still, in the way a man stands when he has been standing some time and has forgotten that he is standing. He had not started up the pier. He was looking, I thought, at the boat, and at the lamps, and perhaps at the rail where I was — though I could not have been more than a shape to him at that distance, in that light, among other shapes — and he was not moving. The boat's bell rang the quarter. There was a quarter of an hour. I watched him. I had not decided, even then, whether I wanted him to come aboard. This is the thing I find hardest to write; I ought by now to be able to say, *I wanted him*, or *I did not want him*, and I cannot say either cleanly. I wanted him to have decided. I wanted the decision to be his, because mine had been made without me in that train-carriage in Hertfordshire, watching him sleep, and I did not trust a decision I had not made. The bell rang the half. Ten minutes. He had not moved. I thought of a number of things, standing at the rail. I thought of the bundle of letters in the writing-desk and the black ribbon around them, and the woman he had constructed out of pearls and a photograph. I thought of Mrs. Pell, and the gin, and the canary, and the stage-woman in Ostend. I thought of my mother's buttons in their twist of paper in the box at the back of the cupboard in Brewer Street, which I had left behind because I had not been able, at the last, to carry them with me, and because I had not been able, either, to throw them away. I thought of the boy on the ivy in Grosvenor Square, in her dirty-water dress, who had not known she was being watched. The bell rang the three-quarter. Five minutes. He bent, and picked up his case. He did not come up the pier. He stood with the case in his hand, and looked once more at the boat, and turned — slowly, as a man turns who has thought a long time and is not certain even now — and walked back the way he had come, toward the town, and the lamps of the front, and the lights of the inns along it. I did not call out. I did not wave. I stood at the rail with my hand on it and watched him go, and he did not look back, and the gulls wheeled and cried over the harbour, and somewhere a sailor called something to another sailor in a language I did not know. The bell rang the hour. The gangway came up. I went below to find my trunk.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of prose fiction. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Execution** The chapter delivers on the plan's stated ambition: a spare, dry, first-person voice that earns its feeling through restraint rather than declaration. The opening paragraph is exemplary — "the trunk held what a woman takes who has decided not to come back; the carpet-bag held what a thief takes who has not yet decided whether she is one" is a clean, precise formulation that does character work without announcing it. The prose is consistently controlled. Sentences are short where they need to be. The flatness is deliberate and effective. The plan called for "an ending that doesn't do thematic work but lands on an image," and the chapter largely delivers this. James walking away rather than coming aboard is a genuine surprise — the plan says "whether he comes aboard is not told," but the chapter makes a bolder choice: he doesn't come. This is actually more interesting than the planned ambiguity, because it refuses the romantic resolution while still leaving Clara's emotional state genuinely unresolved. She watched him go; she didn't call out; she went below to find her trunk. The final line is excellent — mundane, deflating, and quietly devastating. **Character Consistency** Clara is rendered faithfully to her profile. The passage about her mother's buttons — left behind because she couldn't carry them and couldn't throw them away — is the best piece of character work in the chapter. It's specific, it reveals her without explaining her, and it connects to the established detail from the character profile. The line "I wanted him to have decided. I wanted the decision to be his, because mine had been made without me" is psychologically precise and consistent with a woman who is "more easily flattered by attention than she believes" and who has "almost no sense of pity" but is not without feeling. James's appearance is brief but consistent — the man who "has not decided either," standing still, picking up his case, and then turning back. His moral laziness disguised as sensitivity is rendered in action: he came to Dover, he stood there, and he left. This is exactly right for the character profile. Mrs. Pell's departure — "Write to me if you find it dull" — is perfectly in voice. The hand on the shoulder, the refusal to embrace, the exit before Clara can answer: all consistent. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows the plan closely. The boarding-house, the grey sea, the ticket for the night boat, the pier scene, the bell, the gulls, the hand on the rail — all present. The plan says "whether he comes aboard is not told"; the chapter makes the stronger choice of showing him leave, which is arguably better than the planned ambiguity. This is a productive deviation. **What Works Particularly Well** The paragraph beginning "I thought of a number of things, standing at the rail" is the emotional center of the chapter and it earns its weight. The list — the letters, Mrs. Pell, the stage-woman in Ostend, the buttons, "the boy on the ivy in Grosvenor Square, in her dirty-water dress, who had not known she was being watched" — is carefully constructed. Each item does work. The final item in the list is the most interesting: Clara thinking of herself as a "boy" in a "dirty-water dress" being watched is a moment of self-alienation that is psychologically rich and consistent with her character. The pacing of the bell-ringing — quarter, half, three-quarter, hour — is well-handled. It creates tension without melodrama. **Minor Weaknesses** "Dover in October is not a place one goes for pleasure" is a slightly weak sentence — it's the kind of generalizing observation that Clara's voice usually avoids. The chapter's middle section (the grey Tuesday, the walk, the luncheon) is necessary but slightly inert; it does its job of establishing waiting without being particularly interesting. The phrase "which is the honest condition of most nights spent thinking" is a small lapse into the generalizing mode that the voice otherwise avoids. It's not bad, but it's slightly less sharp than the surrounding prose. The sailor calling "something to another sailor in a language I did not know" is a touch that risks being too consciously literary — the alienation-through-foreign-language gesture — but it's brief enough not to become precious. **Overall Assessment** This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with considerable skill. It is not a masterpiece — there are small lapses in the voice, and the middle section is somewhat inert — but it is genuinely good literary fiction. The ending is earned, the character work is precise, and the prose is controlled throughout. The decision to have James leave rather than leave his arrival ambiguous is a better choice than the plan called for. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of Victorian-era fiction that demonstrates genuine mastery of voice, period atmosphere, and character. Let me examine it carefully across all dimensions. VOICE AND STYLE: The narrator's voice is the piece's greatest achievement. It is precise, dry, self-aware, and consistently maintained across all eight chapters. The opening paragraph establishes the register immediately — practical, observational, with a professional's eye for detail — and never wavers. The prose has the quality of someone who thinks in categories and hierarchies ("The ground floor is for callers and constables; the servants' door is for the tradesmen and the unlucky") which is both characterful and period-appropriate. This is not generic Victorian pastiche; it has a specific, earned texture. CHARACTER: Clara (whose name we learn only when James uses it, which is itself a fine structural choice) is one of the more convincing female protagonists in this genre. Her self-analysis is honest without being self-pitying: "I think it was vanity — I wanted to see his face when he saw mine — and that is a poor excuse for a professional, and I have tried to be honest about it." She is competent, guarded, and her emotional life is rendered through action and restraint rather than declaration. James Ashcombe is less fully realized but deliberately so — we see him through Clara's skeptical eye, and his function as a man who "would rather not know" about anything is thematically coherent. Mrs. Pell is a minor character who nonetheless feels fully inhabited. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is excellent. It is spare, functional, and character-revealing. The exchange in Chapter 2 — "Take them." / "No." / "They were a gift from a man I disliked." / "I do not take gifts. I take what I have decided to take. It is a different trade." — is genuinely good writing. The dialogue never over-explains and trusts the reader to infer. The negotiation scene in Chapter 4 is particularly strong. PACING: The pacing is deliberate and controlled. The story moves from professional competence (Chapter 1) through complication (Chapter 2) to backstory and interiority (Chapter 3) to the complication of the commission (Chapter 4) to the romantic development (Chapter 5) to the heist (Chapter 6) to consequence and resolution (Chapters 7-8). Each chapter has a clear function. The ending — James turning back from the pier, Clara watching — is earned and appropriately ambiguous. PLOT: The plot is elegant. The pearls are a MacGuffin that serves the real story, which is about a woman who has constructed a professional identity as armor against her past, and a man who has constructed a romantic ideal as armor against his present. The discovery of the letters in the writing-desk is the pivot — Clara learns something about James's mother that James refuses to hear, and this becomes the central conflict of the final chapters. This is genuinely clever plotting. WORLD-BUILDING: The period detail is meticulous and unobtrusive. The geography of London (Grosvenor Square, Brewer Street, Gerrard Street, Regent's Park), the social stratifications, the mechanics of burglary, the newspaper notices — all feel researched and inhabited rather than decorative. WEAKNESSES: The piece is not without flaws. The revelation about the letters in the writing-desk (Chapter 6) is somewhat underdeveloped — we are told they are significant but the nature of their significance (that James's mother had a lover whose name begins with H) is gestured at rather than fully integrated. The confrontation in Chapter 7 where Clara tells James he "would rather not know about anything" is the piece's most didactic moment — it tells us the theme rather than showing it. The ending, while emotionally appropriate, is perhaps slightly too neat in its ambiguity; James's decision to turn back is rendered with some uncertainty but the emotional logic could be sharper. The kiss in Chapter 5 is handled well — "He did it badly. He missed the centre of my mouth and had to correct" — which is exactly the right register. The piece consistently avoids sentimentality while remaining emotionally engaged. POETRY AND METAPHOR: The metaphors are generally earned. "The front of a great house is a kind of stage, and no one watches the stage in the dark hours" is apt. "The ivy is a liar — it looks sturdy and is not" works because it's in the voice of someone who has tested it. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too far — "the fog blunting the sounds of the street" is fine but the extended fog passage in Chapter 3 is the most self-consciously literary section and slightly less convincing than the rest. But these are minor. UNEARNED TRANSFORMATIONS: Clara's decision to go to Dover, and her ambivalence about James, is carefully prepared throughout. The ending does not feel unearned. James's decision to turn back is perhaps the least fully motivated moment — we understand it intellectually but feel it less than we might. OVERALL: This is a genuinely impressive piece of fiction — controlled, intelligent, period-appropriate, and emotionally resonant without being sentimental. It is the work of a writer who understands that restraint is a form of power. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 17 World Building: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 83.5/100
+ Romance — Love in the Limelight (Chapter Avg: 84.0 | Final: 84.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write a short story set in Wales. Task: write a short story over several chapters. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The story takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the first scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. The story will progress beyond this synopsis, and it's up to you where it goes.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write a short story set in Wales. Task: write a short story over several chapters. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The story takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the first scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor&#x27;s charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. The story will progress beyond this synopsis, and it&#x27;s up to you where it goes. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Setting possibilities:** - A small coastal town in Wales—Aberystwyth, Tenby, or a fictional place like "Llanbryn" or "Porth Cadwyn." Welsh language signage, slate roofs, the sea nearby, sheep-dotted hills. - The bookstore could be ancient, family-owned, cluttered, smelling of old paper, with a resident cat. Perhaps on a cobbled street. - Time of year: late autumn/winter. Rain-soaked, atmospheric. Mist rolling off the Irish Sea. - Why is the actor in Wales? Filming a period drama nearby (perhaps a BBC costume drama, or a high-profile adaptation). He's escaped set and paparazzi followed. **The Actor (protagonist 1):** - Name options: Callum Ashford, Rhys Llewellyn (if Welsh-born), Julian Hart, Sebastian Vane - Could be British, late 30s, known for rom-coms and period dramas. Recent tabloid scandal—a public breakup, rumored affair. - Rogue with fragile ego: relies on charm as currency. Used to being adored. Underneath: insecurity, loneliness, a manufactured persona. - Maybe his charm offensive is almost reflexive—he doesn't know who he is without it. - Could be Welsh-born but distanced from his roots—makes the setting personally resonant. **The Bookstore Owner (protagonist 2):** - Name options: Meredith "Mer" Pryce, Eluned Davies, Bronwen James, Iolo Morgan (if male) - Let's say female for straightforward romance, or consider male for queer romance—either works - I'll go with female: Bronwen or Eluned. Let's try Eluned Rhys or Mair. - Mid-30s, inherited the shop from a parent, literary, dry-witted, reads people well. Not impressed by fame. Maybe grieving, maybe solitary by choice. - Her immunity could come from: genuinely not recognizing him fully, or recognizing but being unimpressed by the mystique; or having been burned by charm before. - Inner flustered-ness: she notices his hands, his voice, catches herself smiling—but externalizes as deadpan. **Plot arc over 8 chapters:** 1. The meeting. Paparazzi chase, he ducks in. Witty standoff over a book. Tension. 2. He returns the next day under pretext. She's wary but curious. Reveals something real. 3. He invites her somewhere / she's drawn out. Or: a storm strands them. They talk. 4. Growing intimacy. She shows him something of her world—Welsh coast, poetry, something honest. 5. A setback: tabloid finds them, or his co-star/ex shows up, or his charm slips into its toxic mode and wounds her. 6. He has to confront his real self. Maybe visits family he's avoided. Or apologizes badly. 7. Reconciliation, risk, real declaration. 8. Ending—open or committed. Perhaps she comes to London, or he stays, or they negotiate. **Tone:** - Witty, dialogue-heavy, warm but with edges. Think Richard Curtis meets Sally Rooney's dialogue rhythm, but Welsh. Or Notting Hill crossed with something more textured. - Weather as character. Welsh language fragments. Sensory detail: wet wool, peat fire, old books. **Key conflicts/beats:** - His charm vs her dryness: a fencing match. - The "everyone must like me" wound—what triggers it? When she finally says something that cuts. - Her buried wound: maybe a previous relationship with a charming man who left. Or grief for parent. - Paparazzi/tabloid as external pressure. - The choice: does he go back to the glittering world, or recalibrate? **Possible ending directions:** - He stays/returns to Wales for her (traditional but satisfying). - She negotiates a shared life between worlds. - Bittersweet: they part but he's changed. (Less satisfying for romance prompt.) - Committed but realistic—a beginning, not a fairytale. **First scene specifics:** - Rain pelting. Bell above door jangles. He's out of breath, disheveled but gorgeous. She looks up from behind a counter piled with books. - He deploys charm. She doesn't bite. He tries a different angle. She's annoyed but observant. - A book he picks up becomes meaningful—maybe R.S. Thomas poetry, or Dylan Thomas, or something she loves. - He asks to hide. She makes him earn it—shelve something, or answer a question. # Reflection The setup has a lot of well-worn romance territory (Notting Hill is the obvious comparison) but the prompt's specific emphasis on the actor's fragile ego and the paradoxical chemistry from her immunity gives it a sharper psychological edge than a standard meet-cute. The challenge is making him likeable enough to root for while honestly portraying his neediness and self-centeredness. The answer is probably: he's charming AND his vulnerability is visible to us even when he's performing, so we understand the wound beneath the rogue. The Welsh setting is a real asset—it differentiates the piece from a generic London romance and gives texture. I should lean into specific details rather than tourist-brochure Wales. R.S. Thomas as a poet feels right—his poems are austere, spiritual, sometimes bitter about Welsh identity, which could parallel the bookstore owner's sensibility and resonate with the actor if he's Welsh-born and estranged. Having him be Welsh-born but anglicized gives the setting personal stakes and deepens the emotional content beyond "pretty location." For the bookstore owner, making her too cold risks unlikability; making her too quickly charmed undercuts the prompt. The sweet spot: she's genuinely sharp and not easily impressed, but we see her interior flustered-ness through small physical tells and a POV that lets us in. Dialogue does the visible work; interiority adds the undercurrent. The 8-chapter structure maps nicely onto a rising-action arc. The risk is Chapter 1 being self-contained and the rest feeling like stretching; I should plant seeds in Chapter 1 that pay off across the arc—the book choice, a piece of his backstory, something about her. Chapter 5 needs a real rupture, not a contrived misunderstanding—ideally something that arises from his actual flaws (the charm curdling, the ego cracking). The ending shouldn't be too neat. A romance promise with realistic uncertainty feels more earned than a full fairy-tale bow. He probably shouldn't abandon his career for her; she probably shouldn't uproot her life. Some negotiation, a genuine beginning. Dialogue-primary writing is specified for the first scene; I should maintain a dialogue-forward style throughout but allow interiority and sensory prose to deepen other chapters. Each character needs a distinct voice: his = quick, layered, performative, sometimes self-aware; hers = dry, economical, literary, occasionally barbed with warmth underneath. Welsh cadence in her speech would help differentiate.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a romance set in a small Welsh coastal town, centering on Callum Ashford—a London-based actor of Welsh extraction who has anglicized his name and his life—and Eluned Rhys, who runs her late father's bookshop. The story will lean into the tension between Callum's reflexive, fragile charm and Eluned's dry, sharp-edged resistance, with her interior flustered-ness legible to the reader through small physical tells and selective access to her POV. **Key choices:** - **Welsh identity as thematic spine.** Callum is secretly Welsh (born Cai Ashfield or similar, rebranded). This makes the setting personally charged rather than merely picturesque, and gives the story a real emotional stake beyond "famous man meets quiet woman." - **R.S. Thomas as a recurring touchstone.** A specific book in the first scene will echo through the arc. Thomas's austere, unsentimental poetry contrasts with Callum's polished surface and aligns with Eluned's sensibility. - **Voice differentiation.** Callum: quick, witty, layered with self-aware performance, occasionally slipping into something rawer. Eluned: economical, literary, barbed with warmth underneath, Welsh cadence in rhythm (not stage-Welsh dialect—just syntax and word choice). - **Dialogue-forward throughout**, with interior access especially to Eluned in early chapters (to show the charm is working underneath) and to Callum later (to show the wound beneath the rogue). - **A genuine rupture in Chapter 5** caused by Callum's real flaws—the charm curdling into something uglier when his ego is threatened—not by external contrivance. - **An earned, realistic ending.** Not a fairy-tale relocation. A beginning, with negotiation and risk. **What I want to avoid:** Notting Hill pastiche; Eluned being so cold she seems unlikeable; Callum's charm feeling gross rather than seductive-with-a-crack; tourist-brochure Wales; a tidy resolution that undoes the character work. I want to avoid misunderstanding-as-plot-device in Chapter 5. **Tone:** Warm but edged. Wry, sensory, weather-soaked. The romance should feel hard-won and specific to these two people. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Bell Above the Door** The meet-cute. Callum bursts into Eluned's bookshop in Porth Cadwyn fleeing paparazzi; rain, breathless, disheveled. Pure dialogue-forward scene. He deploys charm; she responds with deadpan economy. He picks up a book almost at random—R.S. Thomas's *Collected Poems*—and she tests him on it. He bluffs, badly. She makes him earn his sanctuary (shelving books; answering a question honestly). Undercurrent: her noticing his hands, his voice; his genuine surprise at not being recognized the way he's used to. Ends with him leaving into a rain-washed dusk, oddly unsettled. Seeds: the Thomas book; a moment where his accent slips almost-Welsh and she clocks it. **Chapter 2 — The Return** Next day. He comes back under transparent pretexts (wants to buy the Thomas book; wants a recommendation; needs a refuge from set). Her POV dominates. She's wary but curious. He's trying a different tack—less charm, more quiet. They talk about the film he's shooting (a period drama about Welsh tenant farmers, ironic given his background). A glimpse of his real accent, which he covers. She notices. Something brittle in him briefly visible. She closes up the shop in the evening; he lingers; they end up sharing tea in the back room surrounded by cardboard boxes of her father's unshelved library. **Chapter 3 — Storm** A proper coastal storm strands him in the village overnight—his driver can't make the road, the single inn is full with a wedding party. Eluned, reluctantly, offers the flat above the shop (her father's old room). Over a makeshift supper, defenses erode. He asks why she stayed in Porth Cadwyn. She asks—genuinely, not coyly—who he is when no one's watching. He half-answers, startled. Sleeps in her father's bed. She lies awake listening to the rain. **Chapter 4 — Llwybr** Morning after the storm. They walk the coast path (llwybr). He doesn't want to leave. The landscape pulls something out of him—he admits he grew up twenty miles up the coast, left at eighteen, reinvented himself. Cai became Callum. She receives this without sentimentality but with real attention. He is undone by being seen, not admired. First real kiss, initiated by her, to her own astonishment. Small, surprising, interrupted by his phone—his agent, his producer, his life. **Chapter 5 — The Crack** Back on set a few days. He's returned evenings; something tentative is building. Then a tabloid piece lands—"ASHFORD'S SMALL-TOWN FLING"—with a grainy photo and some quotes Eluned never gave. Worse: Callum, confronted, defends himself badly. Under pressure his charm curdles into something uglier—he suggests she leaked it, or at least benefits from the attention ("it's good for the shop, isn't it?"). His ego, threatened, lashes out. Eluned's quiet, surgical response cuts him down. He leaves. She closes the shop early and walks the cliff path alone. This is the rupture, earned from character. **Chapter 6 — Cai** Callum, alone in his rented cottage, cracks. A phone call with his mother (whom he rarely contacts). A drive up the coast to his hometown, unannounced. He sits in a pub where no one recognizes him and several do. He is forced to meet the version of himself he abandoned. Not a redemption-montage—more a slow, painful inventory. He writes Eluned a letter (not a text, not an email—a letter, on proper paper), honest and unpolished. Does not send it immediately. **Chapter 7 — The Letter** Eluned's POV. She is functioning, grieving a small loss, angry at herself for having let him in. A customer—someone local, affectionate—reminds her she has a life independent of this. The letter arrives. She reads it once, puts it away, reads it again. She doesn't decide anything quickly. She drives to the set the next day—not to forgive, but to say what she needs to say in person. They meet in his trailer. Real conversation, no performance. He isn't charming; he is uncertain, which is new. She tells him what she needs, and what she won't do. He listens. **Chapter 8 — Beginnings** Some weeks later. Filming has wrapped. The shop is still the shop. He is back in London for press, but has been in Porth Cadwyn more weekends than not. No grand gesture, no relocation. Small, concrete negotiations: he's using his real name in a new project; he's seen his mother twice; she has visited London once and hated it charmingly. Final scene back in the bookshop, closing time, the bell above the door. He's reading something aloud—Thomas, of course—and gets the Welsh pronunciation almost right. She corrects him. He accepts the correction. A kiss that is neither their first nor their last. Ends on a note of beginning, not arrival.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A thoughtful human writer tackling this prompt would probably start from a felt sense of the characters—maybe a single image or line of dialogue—rather than a structural outline. They'd likely have a personal stake: perhaps they've been to a Welsh coastal town, or they know a bookshop like this, or they've dated a charming narcissist, or they've been one. That particularity would show up in small details that feel observed rather than generated: the specific smell of a damp wool coat on a radiator, the way a till drawer sticks, the local joke about the one café that opens in winter. A human would also have opinions and blind spots. They'd have a favorite line they wouldn't cut even if it didn't serve the story. They'd get bored with their own cleverness halfway through and let a scene go quiet. They'd let a character say something ugly and not immediately redeem it. They'd probably write a scene where nothing much happens because they liked the weather that day. Crucially, a human would resist tidying things. They'd let the rhythm of dialogue be uneven—one character interrupting, another not finishing a sentence, a question answered three beats later. They'd leave jokes that don't land. They'd trust the reader to infer, rather than explain. # Telltale LLM Failings (General) 1. **Symmetry and closure fetish.** LLMs love to resolve every thread, name every Chekhov's gun, echo the opening image at the end. Life is messier. 2. **Epiphany-per-scene.** Every scene ends with a small insight or emotional shift, neatly articulated. Real scenes often end in stasis or ambiguity. 3. **Over-articulation of feeling.** Characters know what they feel and tell you (or the third person narration does). Real humans are opaque to themselves. 4. **Safety.** Avoiding the genuinely unpleasant, the genuinely unresolved, the genuinely ugly. Characters' flaws are performed rather than enacted. 5. **Aesthetic inflation.** Lush adjectives, rhythmic triples, metaphors that try too hard. "The rain fell like punctuation." (Probably wrote that last week.) 6. **Dialogue that's too articulate.** People in LLM stories speak in complete sentences with thematic coherence. Real people grunt, repeat themselves, change the subject, mishear. 7. **Thematic throat-clearing.** Characters noticing thematic resonance. "It was funny, she thought, how the book about loneliness had found her just when..." 8. **Google-maps texture.** Setting details that feel pulled from travel articles—lavender-hued dusks, cobblestones, "quaint." Welsh = sheep + slate + rain. 9. **Redemption arcs on rails.** The rogue learns, the wounded opens, everyone gets a little better. Every time. 10. **Workshopped prose.** Every sentence polished, no weird rhythms, no fragments, no unexpected word choices. Technically fine, aesthetically flat. # Deep Dive Critique of the Plan **The R.S. Thomas motif is a trap.** It's a very writerly choice—too writerly. Using a specific poet as a thematic anchor that threads through 8 chapters is exactly the kind of thing LLMs reach for because it creates the *appearance* of depth and gives an obvious ending (him reading Thomas aloud). It risks being pretentious and over-engineered. A human writer might plant the Thomas book in Chapter 1 and then *not return to it obsessively*—let it be a real book, not a symbol that has to work overtime. The closing scene with him reading Thomas aloud feels particularly engineered. **The "secret Welsh identity" reveal is neat to the point of tidy.** Callum being secretly Cai-from-up-the-coast is thematically convenient—it makes the setting personal, gives him an arc, explains his discomfort. But it's *so* convenient. It's the kind of twist an LLM generates because it ties up the prompt's disparate elements. A human might resist this and let him just be a London actor with no particular claim to Wales; the estrangement and discomfort would have to come from something more diffuse and harder to write. Or, if he is Welsh, perhaps he's from somewhere culturally unlike this village—North/South Wales divide, city Welsh vs. rural—and there's no romantic homecoming available. **Chapter 5's rupture is textbook.** Tabloid leak → misattributed quotes → man defends himself badly → says the wrong thing → woman cuts him down → he goes on redemptive solo journey. This is the well-trodden rom-com beat. The prompt specifies the rupture should come from his real flaws, which the plan acknowledges, but the *mechanism* (tabloid) is external and familiar. The rupture might be more interesting if it were smaller, quieter, not triggered by a newspaper—just a dinner where he was subtly cruel, or a night where his need for reassurance exhausted her. **Chapter 6 is a montage of growth.** Phone-with-mother + drive-to-hometown + pub-where-people-know-him + writing-a-letter-on-proper-paper. This is the "rogue learns humility" checklist. The letter on *paper* is especially LLM-ish—a tactile, self-consciously analog gesture that signals sincerity. A human writer might have him write something awful and send it, or not write anything and just show up badly, or have the growth not happen yet in this chapter at all. **Chapter 8's ending is too curated.** "No grand gesture, no relocation"—the plan congratulates itself on avoiding the fairy-tale ending, but then delivers a different kind of curated ending: the measured, thoughtful, negotiated, realistic-but-hopeful finale. The Thomas reading, the Welsh pronunciation corrected, "a kiss that is neither their first nor their last"—all of this is the *sophisticated* ending, which is still an ending on rails. A human writer might end in a weirder place: mid-conversation, mid-argument, with something unresolved, or on a day that seems inconsequential. **Every chapter has a clear emotional function.** Meet → return → intimacy → confession → rupture → reckoning → return → settlement. This is the Save-the-Cat beat-sheet applied to a short story. It's legible, which is good, but it's *too* legible. A human writer might let a chapter be about nothing much, or have the rupture come earlier/later, or not have a rupture at all. **Eluned is slightly overdetermined as The Quiet Grieving Bookshop Owner.** Inherited shop from dead father; quiet village; closed off; literary. This is a type. What makes her specifically herself? The plan doesn't yet have her contradictions, her embarrassments, the things about her that don't fit the archetype. Does she watch trashy TV? Is she bad at something? Is she in low-stakes conflict with a neighbor? Does she have a flirtation or friendship with someone else that complicates her read on Callum? **Callum is slightly overdetermined as The Charming Actor With A Wound.** The plan has him with a fragile ego, a hidden Welsh past, an estranged mother, a curdling charm. These are the right ingredients, but they're assembled into a recognizable Type. What's weird about him specifically? What's his actual taste? Does he like something embarrassing? Is he funny in a way that isn't just witty-charming? **The dialogue-forward instruction risks becoming "witty banter."** The prompt specifies a charged conversation, and the best version of this avoids Sorkin-esque volley. Real charged dialogue has awkward pauses, people talking past each other, non sequiturs, questions no one answers. The plan should guard against the dialogue becoming a fencing match in every chapter. # Ideas to Make It More Human - **Let Eluned have a specific contradiction or embarrassment.** She runs a bookshop but reads crime novels she's slightly ashamed of. Or she's writing something bad and hiding it. Or she's recently done something out of character. - **Let Callum have an actual, specific taste or interest that isn't cool.** He's obsessed with a mediocre video game. He has strong opinions about a soap opera. He's training for a marathon badly. - **Drop the Thomas-threading-through-all-8-chapters plan.** Use him once or twice, genuinely, then let him go. - **Reconsider the secret-Welsh-identity twist.** Either drop it, complicate it (he's Welsh from somewhere he also has complicated feelings about; coming here is not homecoming), or let him just be English. - **Replace the tabloid rupture with something smaller and more humiliating.** A dinner. A phone call she overhears. A thing he says at a party she attends with him. The ugliness is quieter and more specific. - **Let a chapter be quiet.** One chapter where not much happens: they do the shopping, or she shows him how to close the till, or they walk the dog of a neighbor. Romance built from small time spent, not event beats. - **Resist the letter-on-paper.** Let him text badly. Let him leave a voicemail. Let the reconciliation attempt be cringe. - **End somewhere ambiguous.** Not "a beginning, not an arrival" (which is still a tidy frame) but an actual scene whose meaning isn't clear, where the reader isn't sure if it's going to work out. - **Let some things not be explained.** Eluned's mother is never mentioned. Callum's previous relationships are alluded to but not detailed. Some of what makes them themselves is just atmosphere. - **Allow the Welsh language in without translating.** A word here, a phrase there, untranslated—trusting the reader or letting them not know. - **Let at least one joke not land.** Let one of them say something clever the other doesn't acknowledge. - **Avoid weather-as-mood-ring.** If it's going to rain (it probably is, it's Wales), don't have it rain because she's sad. Let the weather be indifferent. - **Give Eluned a moment of being unflattering.** Petty, or jealous, or small-minded. Not a flaw-as-cute-quirk but an actual unpretty moment. - **Let Callum's charm offensive succeed in places.** Even on Eluned. The prompt says she's inwardly flustered; let the charm sometimes *work* rather than always hitting her deadpan wall. The paradox is that she's drawn to him *despite* seeing through him, not because she sees through him and is therefore immune. - **Consider a minor character who isn't just a plot device.** A shop regular, a neighbor, someone from Callum's work, who has their own thing going on and isn't just there to reflect the leads. - **Resist the urge to write beautifully.** Plain sentences. Dull sentences, even. Let the prose breathe and not perform.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a romance in 8 chapters (approx. 1000 words each) between Callum Ashford, a charming, fragile-egoed actor, and Eluned Rhys, who runs a small bookshop in a Welsh coastal town. The piece will serve the original prompt's core—the charged first meeting, the paradoxical chemistry born of his charm hitting her deadpan, the unspoken flustered-ness underneath her composure—and extend it into a short relationship arc that feels earned rather than engineered. **Key choices, revised from critique:** - **Callum is English, not secretly Welsh.** Dropping the homecoming twist. His discomfort in the village is just the discomfort of a London actor in a place that doesn't care about him—simpler, truer, harder to write well. His fragile ego has no backstory shortcut. - **The R.S. Thomas book appears in Chapter 1 and maybe glancingly once more. It is not a motif. It's a real book he picks up because it was there.** - **The rupture in Chapter 5 is small and domestic, not tabloid-driven.** A dinner at his rented cottage with some of his industry friends down from London. He is subtly awful—not cartoonishly, just in a way she's seen before in other men. No newspaper. No misattributed quotes. - **Callum's reconciliation attempt is cringe, not a handwritten letter.** Voicemails, a bad text, showing up unannounced and saying the wrong thing first. Growth is halting, not montaged. - **The charm sometimes works on Eluned.** She's drawn to him *despite* seeing through him. Her immunity is partial, performed, and occasionally breached. This is closer to the prompt's stated paradox than total deadpan rebuff. - **Eluned has specific unflattering edges.** She's petty about a rival in the village. She reads a crime series she's embarrassed by. She's slightly jealous. She's capable of being small. - **Callum has embarrassing specifics.** A preoccupation with his own hair loss. A weird food thing. A friendship he's outgrown but keeps performing. - **At least one chapter is quiet.** Low event-count, high presence. - **The ending is genuinely ambiguous**—not "a beginning, not an arrival," which is still too neat, but a specific scene whose outcome isn't fully legible. - **Welsh language appears untranslated where natural.** The reader figures it out or doesn't. - **Weather is indifferent.** If it rains, it rains. - **Prose is plain-leaning.** Dialogue does the work. Interiority is spare and embedded, not announced. - **Voice differentiation:** Callum talks in quick, layered, performative bursts with occasional sincere slips. Eluned is economical, Welsh-syntax'd, drier. Neither is witty on demand. **What I'm avoiding:** thematic tidiness, epiphany-per-chapter, aesthetic inflation, the handwritten letter, the montage of growth, weather-as-mood, over-articulated feelings, the fairy-tale ending and its sophisticated cousin (the measured-realistic ending), Eluned as pure archetype, Callum as redemption-arc-ready, banter-as-intimacy-substitute. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Wednesday, Closing Soon** (~1000 words) Dialogue-led. Callum ducks into Eluned's bookshop in Porth Cadwyn with paparazzi outside. Rain, yes, but ordinary rain. Deploys charm immediately, reflexively. She is doing end-of-day sums and does not stop. He picks up the R.S. Thomas because it's near the till. She says he's holding it upside down (he's not, but she enjoys saying it). Several false starts of charm, small successes he doesn't notice—she smiles once, turns away. He asks to hide in the back; she says no; he stays anyway in the poetry corner until the photographers give up. Small crack: he says *thank you* in a way that isn't performed. She locks up. Closes with her alone, noticing her own hands are cold, irritated that she noticed. **Chapter 2 — What He Actually Bought** (~1000 words) Next afternoon. Callum returns, buys the Thomas and three other books at random. Eluned watches him pretend to browse. Some of the charm works now—she laughs once, briefly. He asks what she'd recommend; she gives him something he'd hate; he says he loves it; she clocks the lie and says nothing. He mentions the shoot (a film, doesn't matter what). An older local customer comes in—Mrs. Pritchard—recognizes him, fusses; Callum is perfect with her, and Eluned sees the machinery of it up close. After the customer leaves, Eluned says something cool about performance. He's stung. Leaves without his change. **Chapter 3 — Thursday Night, Later Than It Should Be** (~1000 words) He comes back the same evening, past closing. Apologises badly—partially—and she lets him in because she's curious and lonely and annoyed with herself. They end up drinking the last of a bad bottle of wine in the shop's back room. He talks too much; she listens more than she means to. He asks her questions that are almost right. She tells him one true thing about her father, which surprises her. He does not reciprocate in kind but he notices the gift. Small physical moment—hands near each other on a table, not touching. He leaves before either of them does anything about it. **Chapter 4 — The Quiet Chapter** (~1000 words) A Saturday. Not much happens. Callum has a day off from set. They walk into the next village for bara brith and he is recognized twice and handles it twice, differently. She watches. They argue about whether a particular crime novelist is any good (Eluned defends her guilty pleasure badly). He buys her a pasty. On the walk back they don't talk for a long stretch and it isn't meaningful, just a walk. At her door he hesitates; she doesn't invite him in; he looks disappointed in a way that isn't performed and she almost changes her mind. First kiss, initiated by him, brief, clumsy, at the threshold. She closes the door and leans on it. Small, domestic, unornamented. **Chapter 5 — The Dinner** (~1000 words) Two weeks in. Callum invites her to a dinner at his rented cottage with three friends down from London—an actress, a producer, the producer's partner. Eluned drives over in her father's old coat. The evening starts well; Callum is attentive. Then the London people take over and Callum becomes someone else with them—louder, meaner, performing a version of himself that requires making her slightly invisible. A joke at her expense that isn't quite a joke. She excuses herself early. He follows her to her car and says something defensive and unconvincing. She drives home. Not a tabloid; not a betrayal; just a view of who he is when an audience he prefers is watching. **Chapter 6 — Voicemails** (~1000 words) Callum's POV. He handles the fallout badly. Sends a text that's too flip. Leaves a voicemail that's too long. Leaves another that's worse. Drinks. Calls his agent instead of his mother. On set the next day gives a bad performance; the director notices. Sits in his trailer between takes doing nothing. A small, unsentimental conversation with a makeup artist who doesn't know anything about it but is kind. He drives to Porth Cadwyn at night, sees Eluned's light on, doesn't knock. Drives back. A sliver of something real: he is not used to being someone's disappointment rather than their delight, and he doesn't know what to do with it. **Chapter 7 — The Shop, Tuesday Morning** (~1000 words) Eluned's POV. She has been functioning. Mrs. Pritchard says something kind and unrelated that nearly undoes her. She has listened to the voicemails twice. She has not replied. Callum shows up in person mid-morning, no appointment, no speech prepared. He says the wrong thing first, then the second thing is better, then he stops trying to be eloquent and just says he was a coward at the dinner. She doesn't forgive him immediately. She tells him what she won't do—won't go to London, won't be his small-town girl, won't perform being uncomplicated for his friends. He receives this without defending himself, which is new. They don't resolve it. She says she has to open the shop. He leaves through the front door this time. **Chapter 8 — An Afternoon in November** (~1000 words) Some weeks later. Filming has wrapped. Callum has been back twice; today is the third time. Small, uneventful. He helps her move a bookshelf. They talk about nothing much. He says something about a job offer in Manchester and she says "that's closer"—a sentence that carries weight she doesn't fully acknowledge. They have an ordinary disagreement about where to eat. Walking to the pub in the dark, his hand finds hers and stays there. At the pub they run into someone she went to school with, and Callum is introduced simply as Callum. No resolution about what they are; no declaration. The chapter ends on a small moment in the pub—her laughing at something he said, him watching her laugh—that could be the beginning of something lasting or could be a good evening that doesn't survive the winter. The reader isn't told which.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Callum Ashford **Background.** Thirty-eight. Born Callum Ashford (his real name—no reinvention, no secret). Grew up in Reading, middle-class, father a solicitor, mother a part-time music teacher, one older sister who is a GP and faintly embarrassed by him. State school then a drama scholarship to Bristol Old Vic. Ten years of small theatre and television guest spots, then a breakout at thirty-two as the lead in a BBC/HBO period piece about a rakish MP. Since then: two films, a Netflix thriller, the ongoing shoot in Wales (a film about tenant farmers, ironically—he plays the English landlord). Divorced at thirty-six from another actress, Tamsin; the divorce was tidy in public and acrimonious in private. No children. He is not a superstar—British-famous, recognisable in London, scattered internationally, enough that paparazzi follow him in a slow week. **Personality & flaws.** His defining feature is that he cannot bear not being liked. He notices the one person in a room who isn't charmed and works on them until they are, at which point he loses interest. He is not cruel exactly, but he is cruel when his ego is threatened—not with intent, but with a sort of flailing. His charm is high-calibre: he listens, he remembers names, he asks good questions, he laughs at jokes in a way that makes the joke-teller feel funnier than they are. Some of this is genuine warmth. Most of it is a reflex so deeply installed he can't always locate where the warmth ends and the instrument begins. He drinks more than he should in specific weeks. He has an ongoing, undignified preoccupation with his hairline. He is on his phone too much. He is good at pretending to read books he hasn't read, a skill he's proud of and slightly ashamed of. He has a close male friend from drama school, Freddie, who he's outgrown but calls weekly because Freddie is the only one who knew him before. He is Freddie's most successful friend and Freddie is his least impressive one; the friendship runs on this unspoken exchange. He's not stupid. He reads scripts quickly and well. He can hold his own about politics in a room where no one's an expert. He cannot hold his own in a room of experts and so avoids them. **Idiosyncrasies.** Picks at the label on beer bottles. Puts hot sauce on almost everything (eggs, pizza, once, memorably, risotto). Has strong opinions about hotels. Cannot sleep without a fan on. Calls his mother on Sundays and lies mildly about how he is. Prefers his left profile. Knows this, and is ashamed of knowing. **Physicality.** Tall-ish, 6'1, lean in that actor-maintained way that requires effort he's starting to resent. Dark hair that he's begun to worry about; he's had it cut shorter this year to pre-empt the conversation. Good bones. A smile that works on cue but also a real one, rarer, that involves his eyes going slightly confused. Holds himself lightly, as if his body is a costume he's learned. In the shop he stands with his weight on one leg, one hand in his coat pocket, doing an unstudied lean that is in fact thoroughly studied. **Motives & relationships.** Wants to be loved; will settle for being adored; is terrified of being seen clearly. His agent (Priya) keeps him employed. His mother keeps him decent. Freddie keeps him nostalgic. He has not been in love since drama school, if then. He doesn't know this about himself. **Voice experiments.** > "I know what you're going to say. I'm holding it wrong. I'm holding it upside down. I'm holding it with the wrong hand, the *colonial* hand, the—no, go on, sorry, you say it. I've been talking for ninety seconds, it's your turn." > *She hadn't laughed. He catalogued this. She hadn't laughed and she hadn't pretended to laugh, which was worse in a way, or better, he couldn't tell yet; he needed another few minutes to tell.* > "My mother thinks I should do more Shakespeare. My mother has not seen me do Shakespeare. These two things are connected." --- # Eluned Rhys **Background.** Thirty-six. Born and raised in Porth Cadwyn, a fictional small town on the Ceredigion coast. Welsh-speaking household; English at school; bilingual and slightly more comfortable in Welsh when tired or angry. Father, Iestyn, ran the bookshop for forty years; mother, Nia, left when Eluned was eleven and now lives in Shrewsbury with a man called Gareth—contact is cordial, infrequent, unresolved. Eluned went to Aberystwyth University (English), then Cardiff for a year in publishing, then came home when her father got ill. He died three years ago. She kept the shop. She keeps saying she'll do something else eventually. She doesn't believe herself when she says this but she hasn't stopped saying it. **Personality & flaws.** Dry, observant, slow to warm, quicker to judge than she'd admit. She has the kind of competence that makes other people feel watched. She's kind in the specific, practical ways—will fix your bicycle brake, will remember your mother's name—and unkind in the diffuse ways, the small withholdings, the tone. She holds a long-standing, ridiculous grudge against a woman who runs a café up the road, Bethan, because of a perceived slight in Year 9. She is aware this is ridiculous and maintains the grudge anyway. She reads crime novels—a specific, mediocre Scottish series—and hides them under the counter. She doesn't know why she hides them; no one would care. She is writing a novel very badly in a drawer and will not tell anyone about it, ever. She dated a teacher called Owain for four years; it ended two years ago; she thinks about him approximately once a week, usually with mild irritation and occasional regret. She is flustered more easily than she looks. She hides it well. Her defence is deadpan, then, if that fails, silence, then, if that fails, leaving the room. She has never in her life said "I fancy you" out loud. **Idiosyncrasies.** Keeps the shop's till drawer shut with a specific shove from the hip. Makes tea too strong. Doesn't eat breakfast. Walks the coast path most mornings in an old green waterproof that used to be her father's. Sings—badly, privately—when no one's there. Knows the first names of three of the paparazzi photographers in London by reputation because she reads the tabloids online and is embarrassed about it. **Physicality.** Average height, solid rather than slight, mid-brown hair she cuts herself badly twice a year between proper cuts. Freckled. Wears her father's old jumpers in winter; they are too big and she doesn't care. A face that looks unremarkable until she's interested in something, at which point it becomes very watchable. Her hands are always cold. She moves economically; no wasted gestures; when she laughs it's a surprise even to her. **Motives & relationships.** Wants, she thinks, to be left alone. Actually wants to be known without having to perform being knowable. Allegiances: the shop, Mrs. Pritchard, a few old friends, a neighbour's dog. She is not lonely exactly but has been alone long enough that she has stopped noticing it's a choice she's still making. **Voice experiments.** > "It's not upside down. I just wanted to see what you'd say." > *She'd smiled. Once. He probably hadn't noticed. Good.* > "You can stay until they go. You can't sit in the children's section, you're too tall. You make it look sinister." > "Dad used to say the trouble with tourists was they thought a place was picturesque. A place is just a place. It's where you put the bins out."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — Wednesday, Closing Soon The bell above the door did its thing, which was jangle badly, and a man came in who was too tall for the doorway and did not duck quite enough. "Sorry," he said, touching the top of his head. "Sorry. Hello. Are you open." "Closing soon." "How soon." "Depends." He looked at her. She did not look up. She was doing the day's sums, which she could do in her head but preferred to do on paper because it felt like work. "On what," he said. "On you." He laughed, a short laugh, as if he'd been handed something unexpected. Through the front window she could see two men with cameras across the street pretending to read a menu outside the fish place, which had been closed since October. "Right," he said. "Yes. They've been at it all afternoon. I thought I'd lost them in Aberaeron and then they turned up at lunch. One of them's drinking an actual coffee, which is a new low." "The blue coat or the other one." "You—" He stopped. "You know which one's drinking coffee." "I can see him." "Right." He moved further in. He was wearing a good coat, wet across the shoulders. She carried on with the sums. The shop smelled, as it always did, of paper and damp wool and the electric heater she kept by her feet and didn't trust. He was drifting toward the poetry shelf, which was two shelves really, one of which was mostly Welsh-language and the other of which was mostly Heaney and Larkin and the kind of Faber hardbacks her father had liked. He picked up a book. She watched him in the angle of the window. "You're holding it upside down," she said. "I'm not." "Alright." He turned it over anyway. He opened it and pretended to read for a moment and then closed it and pretended he hadn't been pretending. "R.S. Thomas," he said. "Yes." "He's—" "Yes." "I was going to say something insightful." "Go on then." "I've forgotten it." "Shame." He smiled at her. It was, she noticed, a very serviceable smile. It had been used before. It was the kind of smile that expected a response and had a backup plan if one didn't come. She put a full stop at the end of a row of figures and underlined the total twice. "Look," he said. "I'm going to be embarrassingly direct. Can I hide in here for twenty minutes." "No." "Right." "You can stay until they go. You can't sit in the children's section, you're too tall. You make it look sinister." "Noted." "And I'm not making you tea." "I wasn't going to ask." "Yes you were." He laughed again, the short one. He stayed by the poetry. She went on with the sums. Outside, the blue coat said something to the other one and they both looked at the shop and then looked away, the way people do when they've been told off for staring. "Can I ask," he said, "what you're doing." "Sums." "You don't have a—" "I have a till. I do it on paper as well." "Why." "Because." "Because why." "Because I like it." "Fair enough." A minute passed. She had the sense, without looking, that he was watching her and then watching the door and then watching her again. He was turning a book over in his hands. She could hear the soft flap of a cover. "Are you going to buy that," she said. "Maybe." "It's twelve pounds." "I know. It says." "Alright." "I'll think about it." "You do that." He put the book back. He picked up another one. He put that one back too. He came over to the counter eventually, as she had known he would, because men of his type always needed the counter. He leaned on it in the way men of his type leaned on counters, weight on one leg, one hand in his coat pocket. She carried on with the sums. He waited. She did not look up. "I feel," he said, "like we've met before." "We haven't." "You're sure." "Yes." "You don't—" "No." "Alright." She put the pen down then, because the sums were done and because she could feel herself about to laugh and she did not want to laugh. She tidied the receipts into a clip. She closed the ledger. She pushed the till drawer with her hip and it did the thing where it didn't close and she did it again and it did. "Twenty minutes," she said. "Then I'm locking up whether they're there or not." "Understood." "Don't touch the rare books shelf." "Which is the rare books shelf." "The one that says *Rare Books*." "Right. Yes. Sorry." He went back to the poetry. She watched her own hands for a moment and noticed that they were cold, which they always were, and was annoyed that she had noticed. She put the kettle on despite herself and then, out of spite, did not offer him any. At twenty-four minutes past the hour the blue coat and the other one gave up and walked off toward the car park, and she saw them go, and he saw them go, and neither of them said anything for a moment. "Thank you," he said. He said it in a way she hadn't expected, without any of the earlier music in it, just the two words on their own with nothing decorative attached. "You're welcome." He put a ten and a two on the counter and picked up the Thomas, which he had in fact, at some point, taken down again. "I'll read it," he said. "No you won't." "I might." "Goodnight." "Goodnight." The bell did its thing again. She locked the door behind him and leaned on it for a second longer than she meant to, and then she went and turned the heater off, because the shop was closing, and because she was.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of short fiction that demonstrates a rare command of restraint, voice, and subtext. Let me work through it carefully. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The staccato exchanges—"How soon." / "Depends." / "On what." / "On you."—establish both characters immediately and distinctly without any authorial intrusion. Callum's voice is exactly as specified: layered, performative, with quick pivots and self-aware asides ("I was going to say something insightful." / "Go on then." / "I've forgotten it."). Eluned's voice is economical, Welsh-syntax'd, and dry without being cartoonishly so. The dialogue does not feel like witty banter for its own sake; it feels like two people genuinely negotiating space. The rhythm is controlled and confident throughout. **Character Consistency** Callum's fragile ego is rendered through behavior rather than statement: he turns the book over after she says he's holding it upside down, then "pretended he hadn't been pretending." His studied lean at the counter is noted as "thoroughly studied" in the character profile and appears here as "He leaned on it in the way men of his type leaned on counters"—the narratorial distance is perfect. His "I feel like we've met before" is exactly the kind of reflexive charm the profile describes. The crack at the end—"Thank you" without decoration—lands because it's been earned by contrast. Eluned is equally well-realized. Her hip-shove of the till drawer is a specific, physical, character-revealing detail. Her putting the kettle on "despite herself" and then not offering him any "out of spite" is a small, unflattering, entirely human moment that the profile promised. Her noticing her own cold hands and being "annoyed that she noticed" is the inward flustering rendered obliquely, exactly as planned. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: the paparazzi outside, the R.S. Thomas book, the upside-down joke (with the reversal—she admits she just wanted to see what he'd say, though here it's slightly different, she says it and he turns it over anyway), the children's section line, no tea offered, the genuine "thank you" at the end, and the closing image of her leaning on the door noticing her cold hands. The plan's note that "she smiles once, turns away" is rendered more subtly—she feels herself "about to laugh" and suppresses it, which is actually better than the plan. **Prose Quality** The prose is plain-leaning as intended, but not flat. "The shop smelled, as it always did, of paper and damp wool and the electric heater she kept by her feet and didn't trust" is economical and sensory without being showy. The final paragraph—"she went and turned the heater off, because the shop was closing, and because she was"—is the chapter's one moment of deliberate lyricism, and it earns it.
